6.4.text: We need to compare on a different basis. Instead of comparing what words mean, compare what one is LOOKING FOR vs what the other OFFERS. Example: 'love' is looking for: 'who is doing the loving?' (a subject, a person). 'I' OFFERS: 'I am a subject, a person' - HIGH MATCH! Not 'what do these words mean?' but 'what is one looking for vs what does the other offer?'
6.4.summary: Reframe attention as comparing what one word is looking for against what another word offers, producing high matches like love seeking a subject and I offering a subject.

6.6.text: Here's what confuses people: in the classroom analogy, every student is both an asker and an answerer simultaneously. Riya has a Query (her question), but she also has a Key (what she can offer others) and a Value (her actual info). Aman has a Key and Value, but also has his own Query (maybe he's looking for homework help). Each participant produces all three roles at the same time.
6.6.summary: Every student in the attention classroom plays asker and answerer simultaneously, producing Query, Key, and Value at once.

6.6.text: Chapter 6.6 - Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer. Core idea: in self-attention, every token produces a Query, a Key, and a Value simultaneously. Using the classroom analogy, Riya, Aman, and Priya each have their own question (Query), their own label of what they offer (Key), and their own actual information (Value). The same is true for words in a Transformer: in "The cat sat because it was tired", "cat" asks its own question while also advertising itself as a noun-subject and carrying its own semantic content. This dual role is what makes self-attention symmetric across tokens, and motivates the next chapter's question of why Key and Value must remain separate.
6.6.summary: Chapter-level summary: in self-attention every token simultaneously plays asker (Q), advertiser (K), and information-carrier (V).

6.5.text: Chapter 6.5 introduces the classroom analogy for Query, Key, and Value in attention. Imagine a classroom of 30 students. Student Riya has a question: 'who has yesterday's notes?' She looks around at everyone. Riya's question is the Query (what am I looking for?). Each other student's label or advertisement (e.g., Priya: 'I know the homework', Aman: 'I have yesterday's notes', Rahul: 'I know the canteen menu') is a Key (what can I be found for?). Aman matches, so he hands over the actual notes - physics equations, dates, diagrams - that real content is the Value (here's my actual info). Mapping back to attention: Query asks the question, Key advertises matchability, Value carries the real content delivered after a match.
6.5.summary: Classroom analogy maps Query to Riya's question, Key to each student's label, and Value to the actual notes handed over.

6.8.text: Chapter 6.8 frames Q, K, V through a Google Search analogy. Your typed search string is the Query. Each web page's keywords/tags are the Key. Google matching the query against page keywords is the dot product. The matched page's actual content (what you read) is the Value. The chapter then unifies three analogies covering the same Q/K/V concept: Classroom (question / label / notes), Restaurant (craving / menu description / actual food), and Google (search query / page keywords / page content). Next: compute full attention with real numbers.
6.8.summary: Google Search analogy maps Q=search query, K=page keywords, dot product=matching, V=page content; unified with classroom and restaurant analogies.

6.8.text: Another way to see Q, K, V is Google Search, broken into 4 steps. Step 1: Your search query, e.g. "best pizza near me" = Query. Step 2: Each page's keywords/tags, e.g. "pizza delivery NYC", "pizza recipes" = Key. Step 3: Google matches query against keywords to find the best-matching pages = Dot Product. Step 4: The matched page's CONTENT, the actual text you read = Value. So the user's typed search string acts as the Query, page metadata/tags act as Keys, the matching score is computed by a dot product, and what you actually consume (page body content) is the Value.
6.8.summary: Step-by-step mapping of a Google search to attention: typed query=Query, page tags=Key, matching=dot product, page content=Value.

6.7.text: Chapter 6.7 explains why Key and Value must be separate vectors in attention. Uses a restaurant analogy: the menu description is the Key (what helps you match your craving to a dish), the actual food on the plate is the Value (what you actually consume). If Key and Value were the same, you would get a plate with the menu description printed on it - useless. Connecting back to the Transformer with 'The cat sat because it was tired': cat's Key encodes 'I'm a noun, subject, animate' which helps 'it' find cat; cat's Value carries the rich semantic content (small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated) that 'it' actually absorbs. Key is optimized for matching, Value is optimized for content delivery.
6.7.summary: Why Key and Value must be separate: Key is for matching (menu description), Value is the actual content (food on plate).

This helped you decide - yes, this matches what I'm craving. It's optimized for matching your preference (your Query) to the right dish. The actual food on your plate = Value: the smoky charred paneer cubes, the creamy gravy, the aroma, the nourishment. This is the actual content you receive. What you actually consume.

6.7.text: Now connect this back to the Transformer. Take the sentence: 'The cat sat because it was tired.' The word 'it' needs to find what it refers to. It finds 'cat'. cat's Key (the menu description) encodes: 'I'm a noun, I'm a subject, I'm animate' - this is what helped 'it' find 'cat' in the first place. Optimized for matching. cat's Value (the actual food) carries: rich semantic content - small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated - this is the actual information that 'it' absorbs after finding the match. Key helped 'it' FIND 'cat' (menu description). Value is what 'it' actually GOT from 'cat' (the actual food).

6.12.text: Chapter 6.12 traces a complete attention example end-to-end with concrete numbers. Sentence: "The cat sat". Embeddings: "The"=[0.1,0.8,-0.2,0.5], "cat"=[0.7,-0.3,0.6,0.1], "sat"=[-0.4,0.5,0.2,0.9]. For "cat", multiplying its embedding by W_Q, W_K, W_V produces Q_cat=[0.4,0.8] (looking for verbs/actions), K_cat=[0.9,0.3] (offering: noun, subject, animal), V_cat=[0.6,-0.2] (semantic content). For "sat", Q_sat=[0.2,0.7] (looking for a subject). Attention score = Q_sat dot K_cat = (0.2x0.9)+(0.7x0.3) = 0.18+0.21 = 0.39, a high match. "sat" absorbs cat's Value and learns "I'm an action performed by a cat."
6.12.summary: Walks through a complete attention computation between "sat" and "cat" using real numbers for embeddings, Q, K, V, and dot-product score.

6.11.text: Chapter 6.11 — W Matrices: Learned During Training. The Q/K/V projection matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V are not designed by humans. They start as random numbers on day 1. Training repeats billions of times: model predicts next word, computes error, error flows backward through every grid entry via backpropagation, each number gets nudged a tiny amount (e.g. -0.09 → -0.07). After billions of nudges, the random noise settles into meaningful values: W_Q produces Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K produces Keys ("what do I offer?"), W_V produces Values ("what info do I carry?"). Nobody programmed the entries; they emerged from gradient descent.
6.11.summary: W_Q, W_K, W_V start random and are nudged by backpropagation over billions of examples until they produce meaningful Q, K, V projections.

But who decided what numbers go in those grids? Visual: three boxes labeled W_Q (search icon), W_K (tag icon), W_V (package icon), each filled with question marks indicating unknown entries. Answer: nobody decided. The numbers were learned during training.

6.11.text: Day 1: Start with random garbage. Before training begins, every grid is filled with random numbers from a random number generator. Visual shows three 4×4 grids labeled W_Q (random), W_K (random), W_V (random) with values like 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19. With random grids the model computes: random W_Q × embedding = garbage Query; random W_K × embedding = garbage Key; random W_V × embedding = garbage Value. Garbage Q, K, V → garbage attention → garbage predictions. The model is terrible at this point. And that's completely fine.
6.11.summary: Before training, W_Q, W_K, W_V are filled with random numbers, so the resulting Q, K, V vectors and predictions are nonsense — and that's the expected starting point.

6.11.text: The training loop: predict, fail, nudge. Model reads millions of sentences and tries to predict the next word each time. Steps every attempt: 1) Model sees: "The cat sat on the". 2) Uses its current (bad) grids to compute attention. 3) Predicts next word: "elephant" (wrong! should be "mat"). 4) Calculates error: how wrong was that prediction? 5) Error flows backward through every grid number. 6) Each number gets nudged slightly to reduce the error. This loop repeats billions of times. Every repetition nudges the numbers a tiny bit closer to useful values.
6.11.summary: Six-step training loop where the model predicts, computes error, propagates it backward, and nudges every W matrix entry — repeated billions of times.

how W matrices update each step

6.11.text: Zoom in: what does one nudge look like? Look at a single number in W_Q, row 3 column 2. Before: -0.09. After: -0.07. Change: +0.02. Reason: the error signal says "if this number were slightly larger, the Query for 'cat' would have paid more attention to 'sat' — and the prediction would have been a little better." That's it. Just +0.02. A tiny nudge. But multiply that by every number in every grid, across billions of training examples, and the grids slowly transform from random noise into something meaningful.
6.11.summary: Concrete example of one weight update: W_Q row 3 col 2 moves from -0.09 to -0.07 because the error signal says a slightly larger value would have improved the prediction.

6.11.text: After billions of examples: the grids have settled. The random numbers have been nudged millions of times into meaningful values. Visual: BEFORE (random) W_Q with values 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19 → AFTER (trained) W_Q with values 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. Result: W_Q now produces Queries that correctly capture "what am I looking for?"; W_K produces Keys that capture "what do I offer?"; W_V produces Values that capture "what info do I carry?".

6.11.text: The key insight: nobody programmed these numbers. No human sat down and said "row 3, column 2 should be 0.14." The numbers emerged from the nudging process (backpropagation) over billions of examples. This is what "learned" means in machine learning. The model figured out, on its own, what numbers to put in each grid so that the resulting Q, K, V vectors produce useful attention patterns. Random noise in → billions of nudges → meaningful grids out.
6.11.summary: Defines what "learned" means: the W matrix entries emerge from backpropagation, not human design — random noise becomes meaningful through billions of small updates.

6.9.text: Chapter 6.9 explains how Q, K, V vectors are created from a word's embedding. Each word starts as a single embedding vector (e.g., "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]). To get three different views (Query, Key, Value), the embedding is multiplied by three different W matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each W matrix is just a grid of numbers (a table/spreadsheet). Multiplying input by a table produces an output. Analogy: a photo with three filters - each filter highlights a different feature (eyes, nose, mouth). For real Transformers (GPT-style), embedding is 512 numbers, each W matrix is 512×64 = 32,768 numbers, producing 64-number Q/K/V vectors per head.
6.9.summary: How Q, K, V vectors are produced by multiplying a word's embedding through three learned weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V.

6.9.text: So far, Q, K, V are just concepts in our head. We know each word needs: Query - "what am I looking for?", Key - "what do I offer?", Value - "what info do I carry?". But each word starts as just one list of numbers - its embedding. For example: "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]. That's one list. We need three different lists from it (Query, Key, Value). How do you get three different things from one thing? You transform it three different ways. And the tool for each transformation is: a grid of numbers.

6.9.text: The grid of numbers is called a W matrix (W = "weights"). It's just a table of numbers. Like a spreadsheet. Rows and columns filled with numbers. Example: a tiny W grid: 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. When you multiply a word's embedding by this grid, you get a new, smaller list of numbers - the Query (or Key, or Value, depending on which grid). Multiply input by a table → get an output. Nothing more complicated than that.

6.9.text: Think of it like a photo with three filters. You have a photo of a person and want three different views. View 1: highlight only the eyes → captures where the person is looking. View 2: highlight only the nose → captures the nose shape. View 3: highlight only the mouth → captures the expression. How do you create these views? Apply three different filters. Each filter is a grid of numbers that, when multiplied with the photo, produces a different view. That's EXACTLY what W_Q, W_K, W_V are. Word embedding: [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] ← the "photo". Grid 1 (W_Q): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what am I looking for?". Grid 2 (W_K): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what do I offer?". Grid 3 (W_V): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what info do I carry?". Multiply the same embedding by Grid 1 → Query (2-number list). By Grid 2 → Key. By Grid 3 → Value.
6.9.summary: Photo-with-three-filters analogy: each W matrix is a filter highlighting a different aspect of the same embedding to produce Q, K, or V.

6.10.text: Chapter 6.10 explains why Q, K, and V are smaller than the embedding. W_Q is rectangular (e.g. 4x2, not 4x4) because it projects the d_model embedding down to a smaller d_k query space. The embedding keeps its full size through the network; Q, K, V are temporary compressed lenses used only inside attention. The smaller shape exists because multi-head attention splits a fixed budget across heads: d_k = d_model / num_heads. Real GPT scale: d_model=512, num_heads=8, d_k=64, W_Q per head is 512x64. After all heads, outputs concat back to d_model and W_O blends them.
6.10.summary: Why Q/K/V dimension d_k is smaller than embedding d_model and how multi-head splits the budget.

6.10.text: The puzzle: why is W_Q shaped 4x2, not 4x4? The embedding has 4 numbers, so it might seem the query should also have 4 numbers and W_Q should be 4x4. Short answer: no. Q (and K and V) are smaller than the embedding. W_Q's job is to project the 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query. The input and output sizes do not have to match. Visually: embedding (4 numbers) [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] times W_Q (4x2) equals query (2 numbers) [0.3, 0.7]. The matrix shape tells you exactly that: 4 rows = input size, 2 columns = output size.
6.10.summary: W_Q is rectangular (4x2) because it projects a 4-number embedding down to a smaller 2-number query.

6.10.text: The embedding stays big; Q, K, V are small views. Think of the embedding as the word's full profile - it stays 4 numbers long for the entire journey through the Transformer. Q, K, V are three compressed lenses on that profile, built only for the attention step. The 4-number embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] is multiplied by three different W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V) producing Query [0.3, 0.7], Key [0.9, 0.4], Value [0.6, -0.2] - each just 2 numbers. Three different W matrices give three different 2-number views. The 4-number embedding itself is untouched; it continues through the rest of the network in its full original size.
6.10.summary: Embedding keeps full size through the network while Q, K, V are three compressed 2-number lenses used only inside attention.

6.10.text: Why smaller? Multi-head attention splits the budget. The Transformer runs several heads in parallel on the same embedding. Each head gets its own set of W_Q, W_K, W_V and works independently, looking at the embedding through its own 2-number lens. Two heads, each with its own W_Q (4x2), produce different 2-number Queries (e.g. Head 1 Q1 = [0.3, 0.7], Head 2 Q2 = [-0.4, 0.2]) because W_Q1 and W_Q2 learned different things. After both heads do their attention, their outputs are concatenated back to 4 numbers total (2 + 2) and fed forward. Budget intuition: fixed budget of 4 output numbers per token. Options: one big head with a 4-number Q, or two small heads each with a 2-number Q. Multi-head picks the second: more heads, each specialized. Formula: d_k = d_model / num_heads.
6.10.summary: Q is small because multi-head attention divides a fixed d_model budget across heads, giving each head its own d_k = d_model / num_heads lens.

Right arrow labeled x W_Q2 (pink) goes to Head 2 Query [-0.4, 0.2]. 6.10.text: Real Transformer numbers (GPT-style). Scale everything up. The chapter's 4 becomes 512. The chapter's 2 becomes 64. The chapter's 2 heads become 8. This chapter (teaching): d_model = 4 (embedding size), num_heads = 2, d_k = 4 / 2 = 2 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 4 x 2. Real GPT (typical): d_model = 512 (embedding size), num_heads = 8, d_k = 512 / 8 = 64 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 512 x 64. 8 heads x 64 numbers each = 512 numbers total, which fits exactly back into the embedding size. The budget is preserved across heads.
6.10.summary: Real GPT uses d_model=512, 8 heads, d_k=64, W_Q per head 512x64; 8x64=512 fits back into the embedding.

6.10.text: Shapes recap. Embedding: shape d_model (4 here, 512 in real), lives through the whole Transformer. Q, K, V (per head): shape d_k (2 here, 64 in real), temporary, only inside attention. W_Q, W_K, W_V (per head): shape d_model x d_k (4x2 here, 512x64 in real), projects embedding to Q / K / V. Multi-head concat + W_O: back to d_model (4 here, 512 in real), returns to the main residual stream. Bottom line: W_Q is not square. It's a rectangular projection from embedding space into a smaller query space. The embedding keeps its original size; the query is a compressed view used only for the attention computation.
6.10.summary: Recap table of shapes: embedding d_model, Q/K/V d_k per head, W matrices d_model x d_k, concat+W_O back to d_model.

9.6.text: Chapter 9.6 walks through the complete encoder-decoder Transformer training (prefill) pipeline using 'The cat sat on the mat' as the encoder input and '<s> The cat' as the decoder input. All encoder tokens flow as a single 6x512 matrix through tokenization, embedding lookup, sinusoidal positional encoding, then encoder self-attention (Q=XW_Q, K=XW_K, V=XW_V, softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V, W_O), Add & LayerNorm, FFN (3->6->3 with ReLU), Add & LayerNorm. Encoder output (6 context-enriched vectors) is computed once and reused as K,V for cross-attention. The decoder runs all 3 target tokens in parallel via teacher forcing with a causal mask, applies masked self-attention, cross-attention to encoder K,V, FFN, and an output head that produces predictions at EVERY position simultaneously. Cross-entropy losses are averaged across positions (e.g. -log(0.78), -log(0.71), -log(0.72) -> 0.31). Self-attention mixes tokens; FFN and Add&Norm operate per-vector with no cross-token interaction.
9.6.summary: Full training-mode pipeline of an encoder-decoder Transformer with parallel processing, teacher forcing, causal mask, and per-position loss.

9.6.text: Step 0 - Raw Input. Training/Prefill Mode: all tokens flow as one matrix through every layer simultaneously. Raw input string: "The cat sat on the mat". This is the unprocessed text before any neural processing. Step 1 - Tokenization (lookup table, no neural network): split text into tokens, map each to an integer ID. The -> ID:1042, cat -> ID:5829, sat -> ID:3820, on -> ID:322, the -> ID:1042, mat -> ID:9382. Each word becomes one integer. Nothing neural yet. Note 'The' (capital, position 0) and 'the' (lowercase, position 4) here would actually map to different IDs in a real BPE; the diagram uses ID 1042 for both to illustrate that identical surface tokens map to the same ID.
9.6.summary: Raw text becomes integer token IDs through a non-neural lookup table.

9.6.text: Step 2 - Embedding Lookup (one matrix row per token). Each token ID selects a row from the embedding matrix (learned during training). For the 6 tokens we get 6 vectors (shown as 3-dim for clarity, really 512-dim): The=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], cat=[0.62,0.34,-0.18], sat=[-0.55,0.91,0.12], on=[0.08,-0.43,0.77], the=[0.21,-0.87,0.45], mat=[0.73,0.15,-0.61]. ALL 6 lookups happen at once - just row selection from a table. 'The' (ID 1042) and 'the' (ID 1042) get the SAME embedding - same word, same row. Position information is not yet encoded.
9.6.summary: Each token ID indexes a learned embedding matrix to produce a 512-dim vector, with identical IDs yielding identical embeddings.

9.6.text: Step 3 - Add Positional Encoding (pure math, not learned). sin/cos values based on position index, added element-wise to embeddings. Resulting position-aware vectors: 'The'(pos 0)=[0.21,-0.87,1.45], cat(pos 1)=[1.47,0.34,-0.17], sat(pos 2)=[-0.46,0.91,0.13], on(pos 3)=[0.16,-0.43,1.77], the(pos 4)=[0.30,-0.87,0.46], mat(pos 5)=[1.58,0.15,-0.60]. ALL 6 additions happen at once - element-wise, no dependencies. Now 'The'(pos 0)=[0.21,-0.87,1.45] vs 'the'(pos 4)=[0.30,-0.87,0.46] - now different! Position information is now baked into each vector so identical words at different positions become distinguishable.
9.6.summary: Sinusoidal positional encoding is added element-wise to embeddings so identical tokens at different positions become distinct.

9.6.text: Encoder Block (x6 in original Transformer, x96 in GPT-scale). Input: 6x512 matrix. Encoder Self-Attention is BIDIRECTIONAL - NO causal mask. Every token can attend to every other token (including future ones). Step A: Q = X * W_Q (one matrix multiply, 6x3 * 3x3 = 6x3) producing 6 Query vectors. Step B: K = X * W_K -> 6 Key vectors. Step C: V = X * W_V -> 6 Value vectors. Step D: Q . K^T -> Attention Scores (every token to every token). NO causal mask - 'The' can look at 'mat', 'sat' can look at 'cat'. Step E: Scale by 1/sqrt(d_k), Softmax, Weighted Sum of V. Formula: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V. Step F: x W_O (learned) -> Multi-Head Output. All operations on all 6 tokens happen simultaneously as batched matmuls.
9.6.summary: Encoder self-attention computes Q,K,V via learned projections, applies scaled dot-product softmax bidirectionally with no mask, then projects through W_O.

9.6.text: Add & LayerNorm 1: residual + normalize. Formula: LayerNorm(X + Attention(X)) - applied independently per vector, all at once. Then Feed-Forward Network (FFN): same weights applied to EACH vector independently. No cross-token mixing. All 6 go through simultaneously as a batched matrix multiply. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 dims + ReLU (expand). FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3 dims (compress back). Then Add & LayerNorm 2 (second residual + normalize). Encoder Output: 6 context-enriched vectors (6x512 matrix). These become K and V for the decoder cross-attention. Computed ONCE, reused every decoder step. Each vector now 'knows' about all other words thanks to bidirectional self-attention.
9.6.summary: After attention, residual+LayerNorm is added, then a per-token FFN expands and compresses, followed by another Add&Norm yielding context-enriched encoder output.

why do we expand then compress in FFN

9.6.text: Decoder Block (x6 in original Transformer). During TRAINING: all decoder tokens processed in parallel (teacher forcing). Showing decoder input: <s> The cat - predicting next token at each position. Decoder Input is the target sequence shifted right + positional encoding. Tokens used: '<s>'=[0.5,-0.1,0.8], 'The'=[0.95,0.42,-0.31], 'cat'=[1.47,0.34,-0.17] (already embedded + positional encoding applied). Then Masked Self-Attention (causal - can only look backward): 'cat' can see '<s>' and 'The', but NOT future tokens. Prevents cheating during training. Compute decoder Q, K, V via x W_Q, x W_K, x W_V. Apply Q . K^T with CAUSAL MASK (lower triangle only): position 0 attends to {0}, position 1 to {0,1}, position 2 to {0,1,2}. Softmax + Weighted Sum of V -> Add & LayerNorm.
9.6.summary: Decoder runs target tokens in parallel via teacher forcing, applying causal-masked self-attention so each position only sees past tokens.

9.6.text: Cross-Attention: Decoder asks questions, Encoder provides answers. Q = from decoder. K, V = from encoder output (computed once, reused). This is how the decoder 'reads' the input sentence. Decoder x W_Q -> Cross-Attention Query (3 vectors from decoder). K and V come from ENCODER OUTPUT (the 6 context-enriched vectors). No mask - full access. Cross-Attention computation: decoder Q . encoder K^T, weighted sum of encoder V. Each decoder token can attend to ALL 6 encoder tokens (no causal mask here). 'cat' can now see encoder 'The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on', 'the', 'mat' - the full input. Then Add & LayerNorm.
9.6.summary: Cross-attention uses decoder Q against encoder K,V so each decoder position can attend to every encoder token without masking.

why no mask in cross attention

9.6.text: Decoder FFN (same structure as encoder FFN). Each vector processed independently. Same weights for all positions. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 + ReLU. FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3. Then Add & LayerNorm (final decoder layer output). The FFN does not mix tokens - it applies the same expand-then-compress nonlinear transformation to each vector independently. Followed by the third Add & LayerNorm in the decoder block, completing one decoder layer. In the original Transformer this whole decoder block (masked self-attention, cross-attention, FFN with three Add&Norms) repeats x6.
9.6.summary: Decoder FFN mirrors the encoder FFN, applying a per-vector expand-ReLU-compress transformation followed by a final Add&Norm.

9.6.text: Output Head - TRAINING: Every Position Predicts the Next Token. During training, we compute loss at ALL positions simultaneously (not just the last one). WHY USE ALL POSITIONS? Position 0 (<s>) predicts 'The', position 1 ('The') predicts 'cat', position 2 ('cat') predicts 'sat'. This gives 3 training signals from 1 forward pass. Causal mask ensures each position only sees past tokens, so every prediction is fair. Linear + Softmax at each position. Position 0: probabilities 'The'=78%, 'A'=12%, 'cat'=4%. Target='The'. Loss = -log(0.78) = 0.25. Position 1: 'cat'=71%, 'dog'=12%, 'sat'=8%. Target='cat'. Loss = -log(0.71) = 0.34. Position 2: 'sat'=72%, 'is'=5%, 'ran'=11%. Target='sat'. Loss = -log(0.72) = 0.33. Total Loss = avg(0.25 + 0.34 + 0.33) = 0.31 -> backprop updates ALL weights. During PRODUCTION (generation), only last position is used because previous tokens already known. 1000 tokens = 999 free training signals.
9.6.summary: At training the output head produces predictions and cross-entropy loss at every decoder position, averaging losses for backprop and yielding many training signals per pass.

9.6.text: Summary - Training Phase Flow. Encoder: ALL 6 input tokens flow as one 6x512 matrix through every layer. Bidirectional attention (no mask). Decoder: ALL 3 target tokens (<s>, 'The', 'cat') flow together (teacher forcing). Causal mask prevents cheating. Cross-Attention: Decoder Q attends to encoder K,V. Each decoder token can see ALL encoder tokens. Self-Attention: MIXES information between tokens. Q.K^T creates cross-token connections. FFN: Does NOT mix tokens. Same weights applied independently to each vector. No cross-talk. Add & Norm: Per-vector operation. Adds residual, normalizes. No cross-token interaction. Output Head (train): ALL positions produce predictions. Loss computed everywhere. 3 signals from 1 pass. Output Head (gen): Only LAST position predicts next token. Others already known. Encoder runs ONCE - its output is reused as K,V for every decoder step. Decoder runs once (train) or N times (gen).
9.6.summary: Recap contrasting which components mix tokens vs operate per-vector, and how training differs from generation in encoder-decoder Transformers.

9.6.text: Progressive encoder-decoder training flow diagram showing complete pipeline: tokenization, embedding, positional encoding, encoder self-attention with Q K V projections, FFN, decoder with causal masking and cross-attention, and output head producing predictions at every position. Visual layers stack vertically with 6 colored encoder tokens (The/cat/sat/on/the/mat) and 3 decoder tokens (<s>/The/cat). Each transformation shows nodes for vectors with explicit numeric values, edges for fully-connected projections, callouts marking 'ALL 6 VECTORS IN PARALLEL', and boxes highlighting Add&Norm, FFN expand-compress, and per-position softmax with target labels and cross-entropy loss numbers.
9.6.summary: Progressive vertical diagram of the complete training-mode encoder-decoder Transformer with concrete token vectors at every step.

9.7.text: PRODUCTION / INFERENCE MODE (autoregressive generation). Only ONE new token flows through the decoder at each step. Previous K,V are cached. HOW INFERENCE WORKS - THE BIG PICTURE: 1. ENCODER runs ONCE on the full input "The cat sat on the mat" (6 tokens, all in parallel). 2. Encoder output (6 vectors) becomes K,V for cross-attention. Cached permanently. 3. DECODER runs in a LOOP, one new token at a time: Step 1: feed <s> -> predict "The". Step 2: feed "The" -> predict "cat" (KV cache has <s>). Step 3: feed "cat" -> predict "sat" (KV cache has <s>, "The"). Step 4: feed "sat" -> predict "on" (KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat"). ...until <end> token is produced. 4. At each step, ONLY the new token runs through all layers. Previous tokens' K,V are read from cache.
9.7.summary: Inference is autoregressive: encoder runs once, decoder loops one token at a time using cached K,V from prior steps.

Self-attention (bidirectional, no mask) -> Add&Norm -> FFN -> Add&Norm -> repeat x6. ENCODER OUTPUT CACHED - reused as cross-attention K,V for every decoder step. Output vectors shown for tokens "The", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat" with values like [-0.8,-0.5,1.3] etc.

9.7.text: PHASE 2: DECODER - showing Step 3: generating after "<s> The cat". Already generated: "<s>", "The", "cat". Now predicting the next token. DECODER STATE AT STEP 3: KV Cache (self-attention) contains K,V for: <s> (step 1), "The" (step 2). These were computed in earlier steps. 9.7.text: Masked Self-Attention (with KV Cache). Only "cat" computes Q. K,V for <s> and "The" come from cache. x W_Q -> Query (ONLY for "cat"): only 1 Q vector computed (not 3). x W_K -> Key: new "cat" K joins cached <s>, "The" K. Cached K vectors shown as dashed/faded; new K computed then ADDED to cache. x W_V -> Value: new "cat" V joins cached <s>, "The" V. New V computed, then ADDED to cache.

9.7.text: "cat" Q attends to ALL K (cached + new). "cat" Q dots with K_<s>, K_The, K_cat -> 3 attention scores -> softmax -> weighted V sum. Attention Output (1 vector, not 3). Single output vector for "cat" only. WHY IS THIS FASTER THAN RECOMPUTING? Without cache: compute Q,K,V for ALL 3 tokens, run attention on 3x3 matrix. O(n^2). With cache: compute Q,K,V for 1 token, read cached K,V, attention on 1x3. O(n). At 100K tokens, that is 100,000x cheaper per step. KV cache is essential for fast generation. 9.7.text: Cross-Attention (decoder Q, encoder K,V from cache). Only "cat" computes Q. K,V are the 6 encoder vectors (cached from Phase 1). Decoder x W_Q -> Cross Q (just 1 vector for "cat"). Encoder K,V: 6 vectors from encoder output (cached, never recomputed). ENCODER OUTPUT - computed once in Phase 1, reused every step. "cat" Q dots ALL 6 encoder K -> weighted sum of 6 encoder V. "cat" now incorporates information from ALL input words. Add & LayerNorm.
9.7.summary: Cross-attention uses the new decoder token's Q against the 6 cached encoder K,V vectors so the token absorbs full input context.

9.7.text: Decoder FFN (just 1 vector through - "cat" only). FFN processes each vector independently, so only the new token needs to go through. FFN Layer 1: 3 -> 6 + ReLU. FFN Layer 2: 6 -> 3. WHY ONLY RUN FFN ON THE NEW TOKEN? FFN does NOT mix information between tokens. It applies the same function to each vector independently. Previous tokens' FFN outputs are already "baked into" the K,V vectors stored in the cache. So running FFN on <s> or "The" again would produce the EXACT same output. No point recomputing. Add & LayerNorm (decoder block output for "cat").
9.7.summary: FFN runs only on the new token because it acts per-vector with no cross-token mixing; old token outputs are already baked into cached K,V.

9.7.text: Output Head ("cat" vector -> next word prediction). Linear Projection (3 -> vocab size). Vocab logits: "sat"=3.5, "is"=0.8, "ran"=1.2, "sees"=-0.3, "went"=0.5. Softmax -> Probabilities: "sat" 80.7%, "is" 5.4%, "ran" 8.1%, "sees" 1.8%, "went" 4.0%. Predicted next token: "sat". "sat" feeds back as decoder input for Step 4. WHY ONLY THE LAST POSITION? During generation, there IS only one position flowing through - the new token. <s> and "The" were processed in Steps 1-2. Their predictions are done. We already used them. The single vector for "cat" at the output is all we need to predict the next word "sat".
9.7.summary: Output head linearly projects the new token vector to vocab logits, softmax picks the next token (sat at 80.7%), which feeds back as next step's input.

9.7.text: What Happens Next: Step 4: "sat" enters the decoder. KV cache now has <s>, "The", "cat". Predicts "on". Step 5: "on" enters. KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat", "sat". Predicts "the". Step 6: "the" enters. KV cache has <s>, "The", "cat", "sat", "on". Predicts "mat". Step 7: "mat" enters. Predicts <end>. Generation stops. Final output: "The cat sat on the mat" (7 decoder steps, encoder ran once). KV Cache: Memory vs Speed Tradeoff. What is stored: K and V vectors at every attention layer, for every past token. Self-attention cache: Grows by 1 entry per step. At step N, holds N-1 K,V pairs per layer. Cross-attention cache: Fixed size (6 vectors K + 6 vectors V). Set once from encoder output. Real scale (GPT-4): 96 layers, 128 heads, 128 dims/head. Each token = 96*128*128 = 1.57M floats for K+V. At 100K context: 100K * 1.57M * 2 bytes (fp16) = ~300 GB just for the KV cache. Without cache: Each step would recompute ALL tokens. Step N costs O(N). Total: O(N^2). Impossibly slow. With cache: Each step costs O(1) for FFN + O(N) for attention lookup. Total: O(N). Much faster. Tradeoff: KV cache uses lots of GPU memory but makes generation feasible. No cache = no fast LLMs.
9.7.summary: Generation continues step by step until <end>; KV cache trades large GPU memory (~300 GB at 100K context for GPT-4) for O(N) total cost vs O(N^2) without cache.

9.7.text: Encoder-Decoder Inference Flow: encoder runs once on the full input and its output is cached as cross-attention K,V. The decoder generates autoregressively, one new token per step. At each step, only the new token computes Q,K,V; previous tokens' K,V are read from a self-attention KV cache. Cross-attention uses cached encoder K,V (fixed) and decoder Q. FFN runs only on the new token because it has no cross-token mixing. Output head projects the last position to vocab and softmax picks the next token, which feeds back as input. KV cache turns O(N^2) attention into O(N), at the cost of large GPU memory (e.g. ~300 GB for 100K context on GPT-4 fp16).
9.7.summary: Inference is one-encoder-pass plus a decoder loop where only the new token runs while past K,V are reused from cache, trading memory for O(N) speed.

2.1.text: Computers only understand numbers. Text is useless to an AI - no matter how eloquent. The first step is to convert 'I love cats' into [0, 1, 2]. This is Tokenization: slicing text into chunks and assigning each a number from a vocabulary.
2.1.summary: Tokenization converts text into numeric IDs because models only operate on numbers.

2.1.text: Three tokenization strategies exist. Character-level: 'love' -> ['l','o','v','e'] -> [108, 111, 118, 101]. Pros: works for any word, new letters rare. Cons: way too granular, sequences become huge. Word-level: 'unhappy' -> ['unhappy'] -> [5942]. Pros: human intuitive. Cons: 50K+ vocab needed, new words crash the model. Sub-word (BPE): 'unhappy' -> ['un','##happy'] -> [100, 6420]. Pros: sweet spot, ~50K tokens covers everything. Cons: need to learn splits first.
2.1.summary: Compares character, word, and sub-word tokenization strategies with tradeoffs.

2.1.text: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) - the gold standard. BPE learns which character pairs appear together most. It starts with characters, then merges the most common pairs repeatedly. Step 0: split 'I love cats' into characters [I, _, l, o, v, e, _, c, a, t, s]. Step 1: count adjacent pairs: l+o=1, o+v=1, v+e=1, c+a=1, a+t=1, t+s=1. Step 2: merge most common pair (all tied at 1, pick l+o -> lo). Step 3: repeat merging. Recount pairs. lo+v is highest. Merge -> lov. Then lov+e -> love. Then c+a+t+s -> cats. Final tokens with IDs: I=0, space=1, love=2, space=1, cats=3. The sequence [0, 1, 2, 1, 3] is what gets fed to the model.
2.1.summary: Walks through BPE step by step on 'I love cats', merging frequent pairs into tokens with IDs.

2.1.text: Real example: GPT tokenizer. Modern tokenizers like GPT-2 use learned vocabularies of ~50,000 tokens. The sentence 'The cat sat on the mat last week' becomes: The=464, cat=3797, sat=2906, on=319, the=262, mat=4416, last=1297, week=1285. Sequence: [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285]. Each token ID points to a learned embedding (a 768-dimensional vector in GPT-3). The model never sees text - only numbers.
2.1.summary: Real GPT tokenizer mapping a sentence to token IDs that index 768-dim learned embeddings.

2.1.text: Tokenization converts text into numeric IDs that a neural network can consume. Three strategies exist: character-level (too granular), word-level (huge vocab and out-of-vocabulary failures), and sub-word like BPE (the sweet spot at ~50K tokens). BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build its vocabulary. Real GPT models tokenize 'The cat sat on the mat last week' into IDs like [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285], where each ID indexes a 768-dim learned embedding fed into the model.
2.1.summary: Chapter-level overview of tokenization strategies, BPE training, and how token IDs feed into embeddings.

2.2.text: Self-supervised learning is how GPT pretrains: it learns from raw text without labels by predicting the next token. One sentence becomes many (input, target) training pairs. The model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary, cross-entropy loss measures the gap (Loss = -log(P_correct)), and gradient descent adjusts weights across billions of examples. Through next-token prediction alone, the model learns grammar, facts, reasoning, and style.
2.2.summary: Chapter 2.2 overview: GPT pretrains via self-supervised next-token prediction with cross-entropy loss on billions of free training examples.

2.2.text: Self-Supervised Learning is the trick behind GPT. GPT learns from raw text with NO labels. It doesn't need humans to annotate. Instead, it creates its own training signal by predicting the next token. One simple rule: given all the words so far, predict what comes next.

2.2.text: Example: 'The cat sat on the mat'. One sentence becomes FIVE training examples. Each one says: given these words, the next word is... Input 'The' -> target 'cat' [3797]. Input 'The cat' -> target 'sat' [2906]. Input 'The cat sat' -> target 'on' [319]. Input 'The cat sat on' -> target 'the' [262]. Input 'The cat sat on the' -> target 'mat' [4416]. From a tiny 8-word sentence we get 5 (input, target) pairs. From 1 billion words we get 1 billion training examples. For FREE.
2.2.summary: Shows how a single sentence is split into multiple (input, target) training pairs, demonstrating why self-supervised learning produces unlimited training data for free.

2.2.text: Inside the model: raw prediction. When we give the model 'The cat sat on', it outputs probabilities for the next token. Distribution: 'the' = 0.35 (correct, 35%), 'cat' = 0.15, 'mat' = 0.12, 'on' = 0.10, all others = 0.28. The model predicted 'the' with 35% confidence. The correct answer is 'the'. Good! But not perfect - 35% is not 100%.

2.2.text: Loss: how wrong was the prediction? We use cross-entropy loss to measure the gap. For this example: Loss = -log(0.35) = 1.05. Higher probability -> lower loss. If we predicted 1.0 (100%), loss = 0. If 0.1 (10%), loss approximately 2.3. Examples: P=1.0 -> L=0.0 (perfect). P=0.5 -> L=0.69 (so-so). P=0.35 -> L=1.05 (our example). P=0.1 -> L=2.3 (bad).
2.2.summary: Introduces cross-entropy loss formula Loss = -log(P_correct) with worked numerical examples showing how loss grows as confidence drops.
queries: how is loss computed for next token prediction
how does loss change with probability
what is cross-entropy in plain English

2.2.text: Why self-supervised works. The model learns to predict next words by trial and error on BILLIONS of examples. Through this alone, it learns: Grammar - predicting next word requires understanding subject-verb agreement and tenses. Facts - to predict 'The capital of India is ___', it must memorize that Delhi is the answer. Reasoning - to predict the output of a math problem, it must perform the computation internally. Style transfer - after SFT the model uses this learned knowledge to follow human instructions.
2.2.summary: Explains why next-token prediction alone teaches the model grammar, facts, reasoning, and style by forcing internal understanding to make accurate predictions.
queries: why does next-token prediction teach reasoning
what does GPT learn from pretraining
how does the model learn grammar
why does predicting next word work

2.2.text: During training: gradient descent. After each batch of examples, the model computes loss, then uses backprop to adjust weights to reduce future loss. Iteration 1: avg loss on batch = 4.2 -> adjust weights. Iteration 2: avg loss = 3.8 -> adjust weights. Iteration 3: avg loss = 3.1 -> adjust weights. ... After 1 trillion tokens: avg loss on batch = 0.8. The model gets better at predicting. This is 'pretraining' - GPT-3 trained for 300+ billion tokens.
2.2.summary: Shows the iterative gradient descent training loop where average batch loss steadily decreases from 4.2 down to 0.8 over a trillion tokens of pretraining.

2.3.text: How do we grade the model? The model predicts probabilities for every word in the vocabulary. We need to turn that into one number that says how good the predictions are. A loss function is a single score that tells us how wrong the model was. Lower loss equals better predictions. The loss function used by every LLM is called cross-entropy loss.
2.3.summary: Introduces the need for a single loss number to grade an LLM's vocabulary-wide probability output, motivating cross-entropy loss.
queries: what is a loss function for an LLM
how do we score language model predictions
how do we measure how wrong a model is
single number to grade model output
why not just compare predicted word vs actual word
what does loss mean in deep learning
why is loss needed during training
how does training know the model is improving

2.3.text: The formula: Loss = -log_e(P_correct). In plain English: take the natural log of how confident the model was in the right answer, then flip the sign. P_correct is the probability the model assigned to the actual next word; the negative sign flips it. The shape of -log(P): on a graph of loss vs probability, the curve is steep on the left (low probability gives massive penalty) and flat on the right (high probability gives tiny penalty). Annotated points: P=0.95 (confident & right) gives loss ~0.05; P=0.50 (coin flip) gives ~0.69; P=0.10 (wrong guess) gives ~2.30; P=0.01 (very wrong) gives ~4.60. Where does 'correct' come from? Not from inside the formula - from the training data. During training, the model sees real text like 'The cat sat on the mat'. We already know the next word is 'mat' because it is right there in the sentence. The model outputs 50,000 probabilities (one per word). We reach into that list, pull out the one probability assigned to 'mat', and plug THAT into the formula. Worked example: model output {the: 2%, cat: 5%, mat: 62%, dog: 1%, ...}. Correct answer (from training data): 'mat'. So P_correct = P('mat') = 0.62. Loss = -ln(0.62) = 0.48. The 'comparison' between actual and expected is implicit: we only look at the probability the model gave to the known correct word. There is no subtraction. The entire comparison is: how confident were you in the right answer? Tied to the LLM context: model gives 'mat' P=0.95 -> Loss 0.05, almost zero, barely any weight adjustment. Model gives 'mat' P=0.50 -> Loss 0.69, weights nudged. Model gives 'mat' P=0.01 -> Loss 4.60, massive correction. The model gets punished harshly for being confidently wrong, but barely punished when already doing well.
2.3.summary: Presents Loss = -log(P_correct), shows the -log curve's shape, and explains that the correct word comes from training data with a worked 'mat' example.

2.3.text: Why -log? The intuition. Imagine you're a weather forecaster. You say '70% chance of rain'. If it rains, you did decent (70% is not 100%, but ok). If it doesn't rain, you were wrong (you said 70%). Loss measures your mistake: Loss = -log(your prediction for what actually happened). Examples: You said 'It WILL rain (P=0.95)', actual IT DID RAIN, loss = 0.05, grade A+. You said 'Maybe rain (P=0.50)', actual it rained, loss = 0.69, grade C. You said 'Probably dry (P=0.10)', actual it rained, loss = 2.30, grade F.
2.3.summary: Uses a weather forecaster analogy to give intuition for why -log of the assigned probability measures prediction error.

2.3.text: Cross-entropy formula in detail. When predicting one token from a 50K vocabulary: Loss = -log_e(P_correct), where P_correct is between 0 and 1 (it's a probability). Concrete table: P=0.99 -> L=0.01; P=0.90 -> L=0.10; P=0.50 -> L=0.69; P=0.10 -> L=2.30; P=0.01 -> L=4.60. As probability increases, loss approaches zero. As probability drops, loss explodes. This penalizes confident wrong answers.
2.3.summary: Restates the loss formula and shows a P-to-loss table illustrating how loss explodes as the assigned probability drops.
queries: what's the loss for a 50 percent prediction
bar chart of loss vs probability

2.3.text: Why cross-entropy, not MSE? MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the default loss for predicting numbers - like predicting house prices. But language models predict probabilities over a vocabulary. MSE Loss formula: (P_pred - P_true)^2. Example: P_pred=0.1, P_true=1.0 -> (0.1-1.0)^2 = 0.81. Problem: treats small and large errors equally and doesn't penalize confidence enough. Cross-Entropy Loss formula: -log(P_correct). Example: P_pred=0.1 when true=1.0 -> -log(0.1) = 2.30. It heavily punishes confident mistakes - which is what we want.
2.3.summary: Contrasts MSE with cross-entropy and shows why cross-entropy's stronger penalty for confidently wrong probabilities makes it the right loss for LLMs.
queries: why not MSE for language models
why does MSE fail for probabilities
house price loss vs token prediction loss
why log loss for softmax outputs

2.3.text: Batch loss: averaging across examples. During training, we compute loss on a BATCH of examples, then take the mean. Example 1: Predict 'sat' -> model says P=0.4 -> loss = 0.92. Example 2: Predict 'on' -> model says P=0.8 -> loss = 0.22. Example 3: Predict 'mat' -> model says P=0.1 -> loss = 2.30. Batch Loss = (0.92 + 0.22 + 2.30) / 3 = 1.15. The model updates weights to reduce this batch loss. Then the next batch is loaded and loss is recomputed.
2.3.summary: Shows that during training, per-example losses are averaged into a single batch loss that drives weight updates.

2.3.text: Cross-entropy loss is how every LLM grades its own predictions. The formula Loss = -log_e(P_correct) takes the probability the model assigned to the actually correct next word (which comes from the training data, not the formula) and flips its log. The -log curve is steep at low probability (massive penalty for being confidently wrong) and flat near 1 (tiny penalty for being right). Cross-entropy is preferred over MSE because it punishes confident mistakes properly. During training, per-example losses are averaged across a batch to give one number that drives weight updates.
2.3.summary: Chapter recap of the cross-entropy loss formula, its shape, why it beats MSE, and how batch averaging plugs into training.

2.4.text: How the 'mat' output circle computes its score (logit). Values arriving from previous layer: [2.1, -0.8, 1.5, 0.3, -1.2, 0.9, 1.7, -0.4]. Weights for 'mat' circle: [0.6, 0.3, 0.8, 0.1, -0.5, 0.2, 0.9, 0.4]. Score = (2.1 x 0.6) + (-0.8 x 0.3) + (1.5 x 0.8) + (0.3 x 0.1) + (-1.2 x -0.5) + (0.9 x 0.2) + (1.7 x 0.9) + (-0.4 x 0.4) = 1.26 + (-0.24) + 1.20 + 0.03 + 0.60 + 0.18 + 1.53 + (-0.16) = 4.40. Score for 'mat' = 4.40. Same operation runs for all 20 output circles, each with its own weights.

2.4.text: Softmax conversion from logits to probabilities. Two steps: raise e (2.718) to the power of each score, then divide each by the total. Formula: probability = e^score / sum(e^score) across all 20 words. Example computations: 'the' score=+4.2 → e^4.2 ≈ 66.69, 'mat' score=+3.8 → e^3.8 ≈ 44.70, 'a' score=+2.1 → e^2.1 ≈ 8.17, etc. Sum of all e^score = EXP_SUM. Each probability = e^score / EXP_SUM. Result: every probability positive, all sum to 100%.
2.4.summary: Converts raw logits into a probability distribution using exponentiation then normalization (softmax).

2.4.text: Key insight: the entire pipeline is just multiply and add. (1) Take numbers in: word 'on' becomes [0, 0, 0, 1, ...] or an embedding. (2) Multiply by weights, add up, apply activation - each circle does this. (3) Repeat through every layer (GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-4 has ~100+). (4) Last layer has one circle per vocabulary word (20 for us, 50,000 for real GPT). (5) Softmax to get probabilities. The only 'smart' part is the weights - the numbers on each connection line. Training (gradient descent) adjusts those weights trillions of times until multiply-and-add produces good predictions.
2.4.summary: Wraps up the chapter: a language model is just stacked multiply-add layers; intelligence lives entirely in the learned weights.

2.6.text: Chapter 2.6 - Autoregressive Generation: One Token at a Time. Training and generation are two different modes. In training the model sees the whole sentence at once and predicts the next token at every position in one parallel pass. In generation (inference) the model only has the prompt and must build the answer one token at a time: run the full pipeline (hidden layers, output projection, softmax) on the current sequence, pick a token, append it, repeat. This is autoregressive generation. Sampling strategies decide how to pick: greedy (argmax), pure sampling (weighted dice roll), top-K (sample from the K highest-probability tokens). Temperature divides each logit before softmax: low T (0.2) sharpens to nearly greedy, T=1.0 is the model's natural distribution, high T (2.0) flattens the distribution for more creativity. T=0 means argmax (division by zero is undefined). It works because the model sees the full prior context at every step, training matched this exact task, and small mistakes are absorbed by the rich context. Generation stops when the model emits a special end-of-text token (e.g. <END> or <|endoftext|>) or hits a max length cap like 4096 tokens.
2.6.summary: Chapter-level summary of how trained LLMs generate text token by token via repeated forward passes, sampling strategies, and a temperature-scaled softmax until a stop token appears.

2.6.text: Two very different modes. The model has two lives. During training, it sees entire sentences and learns to predict the next word at every position. But when you actually use it (generation), it has to build the answer one word at a time. Training (learning phase): The model sees ALL words [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat] at once. At each position it predicts the next word and checks if it was right. Fast - one pass through the whole sentence. Generation (using the model): The model only has the prompt [The, cat, sat, on] plus a ??? slot. It must predict the next word, add it, then predict the next, add it... one word at a time. This is called autoregressive generation.
2.6.summary: Contrasts training (parallel next-word prediction across the whole sentence) with generation (sequential one-token-at-a-time prediction starting from the prompt).

2.6.text: Watch it build the sentence, word by word. Prompt: "The cat sat on". At each step, the full pipeline runs: tokens go in, hidden layers process them, output layer produces 50,000 probabilities via softmax, and the model picks one word. Step 1: input [The, cat, sat, on] -> hidden layers -> output layer -> softmax -> 'the' 72%, 'a' 15%, 'that' 8%. Pick 'the' and append. Step 2: input [The, cat, sat, on, the] -> 'mat' 68%, 'floor' 18%, 'sofa' 7%. Pick 'mat'. Step 3: input [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat] -> 'last' 45%, 'all' 22%, 'and' 15%. Pick 'last'. Step 4: input [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat, last] -> 'week' 91%, 'night' 5%, 'time' 2%. Pick 'week'. Final result: "The cat sat on the mat last week" - cyan tokens are the original prompt, green tokens were generated by the model one at a time.
2.6.summary: Worked four-step example showing how 'The cat sat on' is grown into 'The cat sat on the mat last week' by repeatedly running the full pipeline and appending the picked token.

2.6.text: How does the model pick a word? The model outputs a probability for every word. But how do we choose one? Three strategies. Greedy: always pick the highest probability. With probs the=72%, a=15%, that=8%, his=3%, greedy always picks 'the'. Same input always gives same output. Safe and consistent, but can get repetitive - the model might loop the same phrase. Sampling: roll the dice (weighted by probability). Imagine spinning a wheel where 'the' takes up 72% of the wheel, 'a' takes 15%, and so on. Wherever the wheel lands, that is the word we pick. More creative and diverse. But sometimes picks low-probability words that make no sense. Top-K: sample, but only from the best K options. Keep only the top 10 (or 40, or 100) most likely words; throw out the rest; then sample from those. Example: keep 'the' 72%, 'a' 15%, 'that' 8%; remove 'photosynthesis' 0.001%. The sweet spot: creative enough to surprise you, but never picks something completely absurd.
2.6.summary: Explains the three decoding strategies for choosing the next token: deterministic greedy argmax, full probability-weighted sampling, and top-K sampling that filters to the most likely K candidates before sampling.
queries: how does a language model choose the next token
why does sampling produce more creative output
why does greedy decoding repeat itself
does GPT always pick the highest probability word
why filter low probability tokens before sampling
sweet spot between determinism and creativity

2.6.text: Temperature: the creativity dial. Before softmax, we divide each logit by a number called temperature. This controls how 'spread out' the probabilities are. Same logits 'the'=4.2, 'a'=2.8, 'that'=1.5 give very different probabilities. Temperature = 0.2 (cold, nearly greedy): dividing by 0.2 makes logits huge (21, 14, 7.5). Softmax crushes everything onto the winner: 'the'=99%, 'a'=1%, 'that'=0%. Temperature = 1.0 (default, balanced): logits stay the same. Probabilities reflect the model's true confidence: 'the'=72%, 'a'=18%, 'that'=10%. Temperature = 2.0 (hot, creative): dividing by 2.0 makes logits small (2.1, 1.4, 0.75). Softmax spreads probability more evenly: 'the'=45%, 'a'=30%, 'that'=25%. Formula: softmax(logit / temperature). Low temperature -> winner takes all. High temperature -> more even distribution. Temperature = 0 can't go into the formula (division by zero), so in practice T=0 means argmax - just pick the highest logit, skip softmax entirely. Most APIs use T=0.2 as the lowest practical value.
2.6.summary: Defines the temperature parameter as a divisor applied to logits before softmax, with worked examples at T=0.2 (sharp), T=1.0 (default), T=2.0 (flat) and the special case T=0 = argmax.
queries: what is temperature in an LLM
why does high temperature produce creative text

does temperature change the logits or the probabilities

2.6.text: Why does this work? And when does it stop? Predicting one word at a time sounds fragile, but it works because of three facts. Full context at every step: at step 4 the model does not just see 'last' - it sees ALL previous words 'The cat sat on the mat last'. Every generated word gets the benefit of the entire history. The hidden layers process all of these words together, so the model never 'forgets' what came before. Training matches generation: the model was trained on exactly this task - given the words so far, predict the next one. Generation is not a new challenge but the exact thing the model practiced trillions of times. Self-correction is built in: even if the model picks a slightly unexpected word at step 2, it adjusts at step 3. The full context includes the 'mistake', and the model learned to continue coherently from any plausible word. Errors rarely compound. When does it stop? The vocabulary includes a special stop token (like <END> or <|endoftext|>). When the model assigns the highest probability to this token, generation stops. It is a learned behavior - the model saw thousands of sentences ending during training and learned to predict the stop token at natural endpoints. Step 5 example: input 'The cat sat on the mat last week', model predicts <END> with 85% confidence -> generation complete. In practice, there is also a max length limit (like 4096 tokens). If the model has not produced a stop token by then, generation is cut off.
2.6.summary: Explains why one-token-at-a-time generation is robust (full context, training/inference parity, self-correction) and how it terminates via a learned stop token or a hard max-length cutoff.

3.3.text: Chapter 3.3 Batch Training explains why LLMs update weights once per batch instead of after every example. Online (per-example) updates are slow and noisy; batching averages losses and gradients across many examples, giving one stable weight update per step that fully utilizes the GPU. Gradients from each example are computed independently, then averaged before the parameter update: weight -= lr * avg_gradient (typical lr 0.0001 to 0.001). Batch size is a key hyperparameter trading off speed, memory, gradient noise, and quality. Batch=1 is very slow with noisy gradients (research only); batch=32 fits small GPUs; batch=256 is standard with smooth gradients; batch=1024+ is used for large-scale training with very high memory cost.
3.3.summary: Chapter overview of why batch training averages many examples per weight update for speed, stability, and GPU efficiency.

3.3.text: Why batch training? Efficiency and stability. Imagine training on 1 trillion tokens. Do we update weights after every single token? No - that's wasteful and noisy. Instead, we group examples into batches, compute loss for all of them, then update once. This is faster and more stable.
3.3.summary: Introduces batching as a way to update weights once per group of examples rather than per single token, improving speed and stability.
queries: why do we use batches in training
is updating weights per example wasteful
do we update weights after every token
what is the motivation for batch training
efficiency of batch vs online training
why not online learning for LLMs

3.3.text: Online vs Batch training - a concrete example with 3 training examples. Online (update after each example): Ex1 loss=1.2 -> compute gradients -> update weights; Ex2 loss=0.8 -> compute gradients -> update weights; Ex3 loss=1.5 -> compute gradients -> update weights. Total: 3 weight updates. Slow, GPU not fully utilized. Noisy: one bad example can derail training. Batch (update after all examples): Ex1 loss=1.2; Ex2 loss=0.8; Ex3 loss=1.5; Avg loss = (1.2+0.8+1.5)/3 = 1.17 -> compute gradients ONCE -> update weights. Total: 1 weight update. Fast, GPU fully parallelized. Smooth: noise averages out.
3.3.summary: Concrete comparison of online vs batch training over three examples, contrasting update count, speed, GPU utilization, and gradient noise.
queries: how many weight updates with online training
how many weight updates in batch training
why does batch training use the GPU better
can one bad example derail training
average loss formula for a batch
how does GPU parallelism help batch training
compute gradients once vs many times
why batches give one update per step

3.3.text: Gradient averaging in batches. Each example computes its own gradient. With batching, we average them before updating. Ex1: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.05, -0.02, 0.08]. Ex2: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.01, 0.03, -0.01]. Ex3: gradient w.r.t weight = [0.02, 0.02, 0.04]. Batch avg gradient = sum / 3 = [0.027, 0.010, 0.037]. Update rule: weight -= lr * avg_gradient (usually lr = 0.0001 to 0.001).
3.3.summary: Shows how per-example gradients are summed and averaged into one batch gradient, then applied via weight -= lr * avg_gradient.

3.3.text: How batch size affects training. Batch size is a key hyperparameter with tradeoffs. Batch size = 1: very slow, minimal memory, high noise (noisy gradient), can get stuck, research only. Batch size = 32: slow, low memory, medium noise, reasonable quality, used on small GPUs. Batch size = 256: fast, medium memory, low noise (smooth), good quality, standard. Batch size = 1024+: very fast, high memory, very low noise, excellent quality, used in large-scale training.
3.3.summary: Compares batch sizes 1, 32, 256, and 1024+ across speed, memory, gradient noise, quality, and typical use case.
