4.3.text: Flaw #1: Vanishing Gradient (Information Loss). The hidden state is like a game of telephone - information gets corrupted with each pass. Example: 'The cat that I saw yesterday was sleeping.' When training, the gradient (error signal) flows backward through time. At step 7 ('was'), the gradient tries to adjust weights to make the model understand that 'cat' is the subject. But it has to flow back through 6 steps of gradient multiplication. Decay across the sentence: was 1.00 (full gradient), yesterday 0.80 (still strong), saw 0.64 (getting weaker), I 0.51 (weak), that 0.41 (very weak), cat 0.33 (nearly vanished). Result: the weights that should learn 'cat → subject' barely get updated. The model struggles to remember long-distance dependencies.
4.3.summary: RNN gradients shrink as they flow backward through time, so weights for long-distance dependencies barely update.
queries: what is vanishing gradient in RNN
why do RNNs forget long-distance dependencies
RNN information loss across time steps
how does gradient decay through RNN steps
why can't RNN link 'cat' and 'was' across many words
telephone game analogy RNN hidden state
why RNN struggles with long sentences
what is the fatal flaw of RNN training
why early RNN weights barely update

4.3.text: The math: why gradients vanish. In RNN backpropagation, the chain rule multiplies gradients through every time step: ∂L/∂W_h = (∂L/∂h_t) × (∂h_t/∂h_{t-1}) × ... Each ∂h_t/∂h_{t-1} term is often less than 1.0 (typical range: 0.1 to 0.9). Multiply seven of them together: 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 = 0.21. The gradient is now 79% lost before it reaches the early weights.
4.3.summary: Chain-rule multiplication of small per-step Jacobians (each <1) makes the gradient shrink exponentially with sequence length.
queries: chain rule applied to RNN backprop
why does multiplying values less than 1 cause vanishing
how much gradient is lost over 7 steps
partial derivative h_t with respect to h_t-1
what range do RNN gradients fall in
why long sequence training fails math

4.3.text: Flaw #2: It's SLOW (sequential processing). A modern GPU has 1000+ cores. If you're processing a sequence one word at a time, you're using 1 core and leaving 999 idle. Comparison: RNN takes 10 seconds, uses 1/1000 cores - one word per step, sequential. Parallel takes 0.01 seconds, uses 1000/1000 cores - all words at once. For a 1000-word document, RNN processes it 1000× sequentially. A parallel system could process all 1000 at once (with attention). That's potentially 1000× speedup.
4.3.summary: RNNs cannot parallelize across a sequence, wasting 999 of 1000 GPU cores and running ~1000× slower than a parallel architecture.
queries: why are RNNs slow on GPU
how many GPU cores does RNN use
why is attention faster than RNN

4.3.text: Partial solution: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory). LSTM added 'gates' to the hidden state - forget gate, input gate, output gate. These gates let the model decide what to remember and what to forget. Result: vanishing gradient is reduced (not eliminated). You can now model sequences up to ~100-200 words reliably instead of ~10-20. But: LSTM still processes sequentially. Still slow. Still has vanishing gradient for very long sequences (500+ words).
4.3.summary: LSTMs use forget/input/output gates to mitigate vanishing gradients up to a few hundred tokens but remain sequential and slow.
queries: forget gate input gate output gate
how long sequences can LSTM handle
limitations of LSTM for long sequences
why LSTM not enough for transformers

4.3.text: What we need: no sequence dependency. We need a model where: 1. Every word can directly look at every other word (no information loss through hidden states). 2. All words are processed in parallel (use all GPU cores). 3. Distance between words doesn't matter (word 1 and word 100 communicate equally well).
4.3.summary: Lists three requirements for the next architecture: direct any-to-any access, full parallelism, distance-independent communication.
queries: why every word must see every word

4.3.text: Chapter 4.3 RNN's Fatal Flaws: RNNs have two killers. Flaw #1 vanishing gradient - chain-rule multiplication of per-step Jacobians (each <1) shrinks the error signal exponentially across time, so 'cat' gets a 0.33 gradient seven steps later and long-distance dependencies barely train. Flaw #2 sequential processing - each step needs the previous hidden state, so RNN uses 1 of 1000 GPU cores and runs ~1000× slower than a parallel system on long documents. LSTM's gates (forget/input/output) help up to ~100-200 tokens but stay sequential and still vanish on 500+ word sequences. Conclusion: we need a model with direct any-to-any word access, full parallelism, and distance-independent communication.
4.3.summary: RNNs fail because gradients vanish through time and processing is strictly sequential; LSTM only partially patches this, motivating attention-based parallel architectures.
queries: LSTM partial fix and remaining issues
why RNN dies for long sequences

8.1.text: The original Transformer from the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" was built to solve machine translation. Translation needs two different operations: take an English input like "I love cats" and produce a Hindi output like "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". English and Hindi are different languages, so the model must fully understand the English input before it can generate the Hindi output. These are two separate tasks, which motivates splitting the architecture into two halves.
8.1.summary: Introduces the 2017 Transformer paper and why machine translation requires two distinct stages: understanding the source and generating the target.
queries: why was the original transformer built
what problem did the 2017 transformer paper solve
attention is all you need motivation
why does machine translation need two parts

why do we need separate understand and generate steps

8.1.text: Solution: two separate components. The Encoder reads the input and builds a deep understanding, outputting a compressed representation. The Decoder reads the encoder's output and generates the translation one word at a time. Encoder properties: processes input in parallel, all words attend to all words, builds context for each word, outputs rich representations of "I", "love", "cats". Decoder properties: reads encoder output, generates translation token by token, uses attention to look back at input, producing "Mujhe" then "billiyaan" then "pasand" then "hain".
8.1.summary: Splits the Transformer into Encoder (parallel understanding) and Decoder (token-by-token generation) and lists their distinct jobs.
queries: what does the encoder do in a transformer
what does the decoder do in a transformer
how does transformer encoder process input
why does decoder generate one token at a time
what is the output of the encoder
what builds context for each word
how is hindi translation produced word by word

8.1.text: Cross-Attention is the bridge between encoder and decoder. The decoder doesn't generate from nothing; it looks back at the encoder's output through a special connection called cross-attention. Flow: Encoder output (rich understanding of "I", "love", "cats") provides Keys and Values, which feed into the Cross-Attention Layer, where the decoder's Query asks the encoder "What's relevant now?". The decoder then generates "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". When generating a Hindi noun, the decoder's Query asks "what noun am I translating?" and the encoder's Keys/Values answer with the meaning of "love" and "cats", so the decoder picks the right Hindi word.
8.1.summary: Explains cross-attention as the mechanism where decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys/Values to ground generation in the source.
queries: how does decoder look back at encoder
where does the decoder get keys and values
cross attention queries keys values source
how does encoder pass info to decoder
how does decoder choose hindi word
encoder output as keys and values

8.1.text: The full encoder-decoder stack: the 2017 Transformer stacks both halves with 6 layers each. Encoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Decoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Masked Attention means the decoder can only look at past tokens; Cross-Attention means it looks at encoder output. The encoder feeds into the decoder.
8.1.summary: Lays out the 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder stacks and the sublayers inside each, including masked and cross-attention in the decoder.
queries: how many layers in original transformer
what is in each transformer layer
why is decoder bigger than encoder per layer

8.1.text: Why two halves? Different model types. The encoder-decoder design is great for translation, but other tasks need different shapes: Encoder-Decoder - Translation (T5, BART), input English, output Hindi. Encoder-Only - Classification (BERT, RoBERTa), input Sentence, output Sentiment score. Decoder-Only - Generation (GPT, Claude), input Prompt, output Continued text.
8.1.summary: Maps the three Transformer variants (encoder-decoder, encoder-only, decoder-only) to representative models and tasks.

8.1.text: Chapter 8.1 introduces the original 2017 Transformer's encoder-decoder design for machine translation. The Encoder reads input in parallel and builds rich contextual representations; the Decoder generates output token by token. Cross-Attention bridges them: decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys and Values. The full stack is 6 encoder layers (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) and 6 decoder layers (Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm). Three model families emerge from this design: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT, Claude) for generation.
8.1.summary: Summarizes the encoder-decoder Transformer, cross-attention, the 6+6 layer stack, and the three model-family variants.

9.1.text: Chapter 9.1 Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work explains why ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, and Gemini use only the decoder half of the original 2017 Transformer. Translation needs encoder + decoder because input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages. Chatting is one continuous English stream so encoder is unnecessary. Decoder-only relies on causal masking: token N attends only to tokens 0..N-1. The chapter compares three architectures: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini) for generation. Concludes the CNN -> RNN -> Transformer -> Decoder-only evolution.
9.1.summary: Modern LLMs use only the Transformer decoder half with causal masking because chatting is one continuous stream, not two-language translation.
queries: why did GPT throw away the encoder
evolution from CNN RNN Transformer to LLM

9.1.text: Plot twist: Modern LLMs are decoder-only. ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini all use only the Decoder half. They threw away the Encoder. Why throw away half the architecture? Because translation and chatting are fundamentally different.
9.1.summary: Modern LLMs like ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, and Gemini drop the encoder and use only the decoder.
queries: do ChatGPT and Claude use encoder
why drop the encoder in modern LLMs
what part of transformer does GPT use

9.1.text: Translation vs Chatting: different tasks. Translation: input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages - you must fully process the input before generating output. Chatting: input (your prompt) and output (my response) are both English. They are part of one continuous conversation, not two separate things. Translation has two separate sides: English input (complete) goes to Hindi output (generated). Chatting is one continuous stream: prompt 'What is 2+2?' to response 'The answer is 4.'
9.1.summary: Translation needs two-sided encoder-decoder because languages differ; chatting is one continuous English stream so a single decoder suffices.
queries: is chatting one stream or two
two languages vs one language transformer

9.1.text: Causal masking: the key difference. In a decoder-only model, when generating token N, you can only look at tokens 0 through N-1 (past and present). You cannot look at future tokens. This is called causal masking - causality: effect cannot precede cause. Processing 'What is 2+2?' sequentially: Step 1 generate 'What' looking at nothing []; Step 2 generate 'is' looking at [What]; Step 3 generate '2+2' looking at [What, is]; Step 4 generate '?' looking at [What, is, 2+2]. This is the fundamental constraint of LLMs: they generate one token at a time, and each new token is based only on what came before.
9.1.summary: Causal masking forces each generated token to attend only to previous tokens, never the future.
queries: why can't LLM see future tokens
token N attends to which tokens
why no future attention in GPT
why one token at a time

9.1.text: Why decoder-only wins. Decoder-only is simpler (half the architecture) but a large decoder trained on enough data can: 1) Read and understand your prompt (implicit encoder task), 2) Generate natural, coherent responses (explicit decoder task), 3) Reason, code, translate, summarize (with prompting/fine-tuning). The result: decoder-only models are more general-purpose than any other architecture. They are simpler, faster, and more scalable.
9.1.summary: A large decoder-only model implicitly handles understanding and generation, making it simpler, faster, and more general-purpose than encoder-decoder.

9.1.text: Three architectures: the complete picture. Encoder-Decoder (E + D): task Translation; models T5, BART, mT5; best for when input and output are different languages or modes. Encoder-Only (E): task Understanding; models BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT; best for classification, sentiment, similarity - no generation. Decoder-Only (D): task Generation; models GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini; best for chat, writing, reasoning, all modern LLMs.
9.1.summary: Three transformer flavors map to three task types: encoder-decoder for translation (T5/BART), encoder-only for understanding (BERT), decoder-only for generation (GPT/Claude).
queries: BERT vs GPT vs T5 architecture

9.1.text: You now understand the complete evolution: CNN (local patterns) -> RNN (sequential memory, slow) -> Transformer (attention, parallel) -> Decoder-only (the architecture of modern AI). You now have the full history. The upcoming sections go deep into each piece of the Transformer: how input enters, how attention works, and how the complete encoder and decoder blocks are built.
9.1.summary: Recap of the architectural evolution from CNN to RNN to Transformer to decoder-only LLMs.
queries: from CNN to RNN to Transformer

3.1.text: Chapter 3.1 covers scaling laws for LLMs. OpenAI (2020) found loss follows a predictable power law in three axes: parameters N, data tokens D, compute C, with Loss proportional to N^-alpha x D^-beta x C^-gamma. Scaling all three together yields smooth predictable improvement; scaling one alone gives diminishing returns. Real example progression: GPT-2 1.5B, GPT-3 175B, GPT-4 ~1.8T. Practical impact: train small model, extrapolate to predict large, save millions in compute. DeepMind's Chinchilla (2022) showed GPT-3 era models were undertrained: a 70B model trained on 1.4T tokens beat Gopher (280B params, 300B tokens) at the same compute budget. Chinchilla's 20:1 rule: ~20 tokens per parameter optimal. After Chinchilla, labs prioritized data quality and quantity alongside parameter count.
3.1.summary: Chapter overview of scaling laws, the three axes, Chinchilla's correction, and the 20:1 tokens-per-parameter rule.

3.1.text: Chinchilla (DeepMind 2022): Models Were Undertrained. Before Chinchilla, the AI world had one strategy: make the model bigger. GPT-3 had 175B parameters but was only trained on 300B tokens. Analogy: a brilliant student (175 billion parameters of brain capacity) only given two textbooks to read (300B tokens) is undertrained, not too small. The question DeepMind asked: if you have a fixed compute budget (say $10 million of GPU time), should you spend it on a bigger model with less data, or a smaller model with more data?
3.1.summary: Introduces Chinchilla's insight that pre-2022 models like GPT-3 were undertrained - too few tokens for their parameter count.

3.1.text: The Proof: Chinchilla vs Gopher. DeepMind trained two models with the same compute budget but split it differently. Gopher (old strategy): 280B parameters, 300B training tokens - giant brain, tiny reading list. Chinchilla (new strategy): 70B parameters, 1.4T training tokens - smaller brain, massive reading list. Chinchilla won on every benchmark. A model 4x smaller beat the big one because it saw 4.6x more data. The smaller model was actually better trained. This proved that data matters as much as size. Throwing parameters at a problem without enough training data is waste.
3.1.summary: Direct comparison: Chinchilla 70B/1.4T tokens beat Gopher 280B/300B tokens on every benchmark with the same compute.
queries: how many tokens did Chinchilla train on

3.1.text: The 20:1 Rule. Chinchilla's key finding: for optimal training, use roughly 20 tokens per parameter. 7B model: 7B x 20 = 140B tokens (LLaMA 7B used ~1T, well above optimal - even better!). 70B model: 70B x 20 = 1.4T tokens (Chinchilla hit exactly this ratio). 175B model: 175B x 20 = 3.5T tokens (GPT-3 only used 300B tokens, 10x too few!). Industry impact: After Chinchilla, labs stopped just making models bigger. LLaMA (Meta, 2023) proved a 7B model trained on 1T+ tokens per parameter could match much larger models. Today, training data quality and quantity matter as much as parameter count.
3.1.summary: States the 20 tokens-per-parameter rule with worked examples for 7B, 70B, 175B models and notes industry impact via LLaMA.

3.2.text: Chapter 3.2 Parameters at Scale: A parameter is one weight or one bias - a single floating-point number. LLaMA-7B = 7 billion parameters. A simple layer with 4 input neurons and 3 output neurons has a 4x3 weight matrix (12 weights) plus 3 biases = 15 parameters. Real models scale up: LLaMA-7B (7B), GPT-3 (175B), GPT-4 (~1.8T estimated). Parameters live in: embedding matrix (~20%), attention Q/K/V/O (~20%), FFN layers (~50%), output projection (~10%). Critical insight: more parameters does not equal smarter. Parameters are capacity. Need quality data, training time, good optimization. 1T params trained poorly is worse than 7B trained well.
3.2.summary: Overview of what parameters are, how to count them, where they live in an LLM, and why count alone does not equal intelligence.

3.4.text: The problem: big models are expensive. GPT-4 has 1.8 trillion parameters. Running it costs a fortune. It's slow. But your phone app needs instant replies. You can't run GPT-4 on a phone. Question: Can we make a small model that's almost as smart as the big one? Yes. The trick is called knowledge distillation - and it works like a teacher and student.
3.4.summary: Knowledge distillation lets a small, fast model approximate a giant expensive model like GPT-4 so it can run on phones.

3.4.text: Teacher teaches Student. Instead of training the small model from scratch (expensive), let the big model teach it. Teacher: GPT-4, 1.8 trillion params, slow, expensive, extremely smart. Student: GPT-4 Mini, 8 billion params, fast, cheap, learns from teacher. How? Show both models the same questions. The student tries to copy the teacher's answers - not just the final answer, but how the teacher thinks.
3.4.summary: The teacher model (e.g. GPT-4, 1.8T params) trains a smaller student model (e.g. GPT-4 Mini, 8B params) by showing it questions and having the student copy the teacher's reasoning.

do you train the small model from scratch
how do you copy a big model
what does the teacher do in distillation
why not train the small model from scratch

3.4.text: What does "copy how the teacher thinks" mean? Question: "What is the capital of India?" A lazy teacher just says the answer: "Delhi". Right or wrong. No nuance. A great teacher shares their full thinking - as confidence bars: Delhi 85%, London 8%, Mumbai 5%, Berlin 2%. This full set of confidences is called "soft probabilities" - and it's the key to distillation.
3.4.summary: Soft probabilities are the teacher's full confidence distribution across all options (Delhi 85%, London 8%, Mumbai 5%, Berlin 2%) instead of just the hard answer.
queries: why use full probability distribution for distillation
what does the teacher's full thinking mean

3.4.text: Why soft probabilities are magic. The wrong answers tell a story: Delhi 85% (correct), London 8% (wrong but a European capital), Mumbai 5% (wrong but an Indian city), Pizza 0% (not even a city). If you only said Delhi=right, everything else=wrong, the student would think London and Pizza are equally wrong. Soft probabilities teach: London is close, Mumbai is related, Pizza is nonsense. The student absorbs how concepts relate - for free. The temperature trick: divide logits by a temperature T before softmax: softmax(z_i / T). T = 1 (normal): Delhi 85%, London 8%, Mumbai 5%, Berlin 2%. T = 3 (soft): Delhi 45%, London 20%, Mumbai 18%, Berlin 17%. Higher temperature = softer distribution = more information about relationships between tokens. Same temperature slider in ChatGPT settings - higher T = more creative (spread out), lower T = more focused (peaked).
3.4.summary: The temperature trick softmax(z_i / T) with higher T flattens the distribution to expose richer inter-class relationships the student can learn from.
queries: what is the temperature trick in distillation
what does temperature do in softmax
why higher temperature gives softer distribution
high temperature creative low temperature focused

Mumbai is Indian city distillation example

3.4.text: This is everywhere. Most AI models you actually use daily are distilled students: GPT-4 → GPT-4 Mini (100x cheaper API calls). Claude Opus → Claude Haiku (fast enough for your phone). LLaMA 70B → LLaMA 7B (runs on a laptop). The big expensive model trains once. Then it teaches smaller, faster models. That's why you can chat with AI instantly on your phone - you're talking to a student who learned from a genius.
3.4.summary: Real-world distilled pairs include GPT-4 to GPT-4 Mini (100x cheaper), Claude Opus to Haiku, and LLaMA 70B to LLaMA 7B - one teacher trains many fast cheap students.

3.4.text: Knowledge distillation makes a small student model nearly as smart as a big expensive teacher model. Instead of training from scratch, the student copies not just the teacher's final answer but its full soft probability distribution over all options. Dividing logits by a temperature T (softmax(z_i / T)) softens the distribution and exposes how concepts relate, transferring rich knowledge for free. Real distilled pairs include GPT-4 → GPT-4 Mini, Claude Opus → Haiku, and LLaMA 70B → 7B - this is how AI runs fast and cheap on phones and laptops.
3.4.summary: Knowledge distillation transfers a teacher model's soft probability distribution (via temperature-scaled softmax) into a smaller student so it inherits nuanced knowledge cheaply.

3.5.text: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) by OpenAI teaches AI to connect images and text by encoding both into the same vector space. Two separate encoders (image and text) convert inputs into number lists. Training uses millions of image-caption pairs in a matching game (contrastive learning) where matching pairs must score high cosine similarity and non-matching pairs score low. Cosine similarity formula: cos(A,B) = (A·B) / (||A|| x ||B||), result between -1 and +1. This shared embedding space powers Google Image Search, DALL-E, Midjourney, and multimodal ChatGPT/Claude understanding photos.
3.5.summary: CLIP unifies images and text in one vector space via contrastive learning and cosine similarity, enabling search, generation, and vision in LLMs.

3.5.text: Setup question: you see a photo of a golden retriever catching a frisbee on a beach. Which caption matches? A) 'A dog catching a frisbee on a beach' B) 'A car parked in a garage' C) 'A person cooking dinner'. Obviously A. Your brain instantly matches image to words. But how do you teach a computer to do this? Images are pixels. Text is words. They are completely different things. How do you even compare them?
3.5.summary: Frames the core puzzle CLIP solves: how to compare pixels to words when they are fundamentally different data types.

3.5.text: Idea: translate both images and text into the same language of numbers. Two separate encoders do this. Photo (dog + beach) → Image Encoder → vector [0.8, 0.3, -0.5, 0.9, ...]. Caption ('dog on beach') → Text Encoder → vector [0.79, 0.31, -0.48, 0.88, ...]. If the image and text describe the same thing, their numbers end up almost identical. Notice how close the two vectors are. This system is called CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining), built by OpenAI.
3.5.summary: CLIP uses two encoders to map images and text into nearly identical vectors when they describe the same concept.

3.5.text: How CLIP learns: the matching game. Collect millions of image+caption pairs from the internet. Show 4 images and 4 captions in a grid: 'dog on beach', 'red car', 'sunset', 'birthday cake' across columns; dog photo, car photo, sunset photo, cake photo down rows. Only the diagonal cells are YES (correct matches), all off-diagonal cells are no. The model adjusts until matching pairs produce similar numbers and non-matching pairs produce very different numbers. This is called contrastive learning - learning by contrast (what matches vs what does not).
3.5.summary: CLIP trains via a contrastive matching game on a grid of image-caption pairs, pulling correct pairs together and pushing wrong pairs apart.

How does CLIP use image-caption pairs?
What data does CLIP train on?
How many pairs does CLIP need?

Why is it called contrastive learning?

3.5.text: Cosine similarity measures how similar two vectors are. Formula: cos(A, B) = (A · B) / (||A|| x ||B||). Where A · B is the dot product, ||A|| is magnitude of A = sqrt(sum of a_i squared), ||B|| is magnitude of B = sqrt(sum of b_i squared). Dividing by the magnitudes removes the effect of vector length - only DIRECTION matters. Result is always between -1 (opposite) and +1 (identical direction). Worked example: A = [0.8, 0.3], B = [0.79, 0.31]. A · B = (0.8 x 0.79) + (0.3 x 0.31) = 0.632 + 0.093 = 0.725. ||A|| = sqrt(0.64 + 0.09) = 0.854. ||B|| = sqrt(0.624 + 0.096) = 0.849. cos(A, B) = 0.725 / (0.854 x 0.849) = 0.9998 - nearly identical. Score examples: 0.95 dog photo + 'dog on beach' (almost identical), 0.40 dog photo + 'animal playing' (somewhat related), 0.05 dog photo + 'red sports car' (completely different). Training rewards high scores for correct pairs, low for wrong.
3.5.summary: Cosine similarity formula and worked computation showing how CLIP scores vector pairs between -1 and +1 based on direction only.
queries: What is the cosine similarity formula?
How do you compute cosine similarity?
What is the range of cosine similarity?
Why divide by magnitudes in cosine similarity?
Why does direction matter in vector similarity?
How to calculate dot product of vectors
Why use cosine instead of Euclidean distance?
How does CLIP score image-text pairs?

3.5.text: Once images and text live in the same number space, applications follow. Google Image Search: type 'fluffy cat sleeping' → encode text to numbers → find photos with closest numbers. No one manually tagged photos - CLIP just knows they match. DALL-E / Midjourney: type 'astronaut riding a horse on Mars' → text becomes numbers → image generator creates pixels that produce those same numbers. The shared space is the bridge. ChatGPT / Claude understanding photos: upload a photo → image encoder converts it to numbers → those numbers go into the same space as text → the model can 'read' the image as if it were words. Every time AI sees an image, contrastive learning is behind it.
3.5.summary: Real-world apps powered by CLIP-style shared embedding space: text-to-image search, generative art (DALL-E/Midjourney), and multimodal chat.

3.6.text: Zoom out. You've now learned every stage of how an LLM is built: tokenization, pretraining, loss functions, SFT, RLHF, scaling laws, distillation, and CLIP. Here's the full journey from a pile of internet text to ChatGPT answering your questions.

3.6.text: Phase 1: Pretraining. 2-3 months, $10-100 million. Feed the model trillions of words from the internet - books, Wikipedia, code, websites, forums, everything. The model learns by playing the prediction game millions of times: 'The cat sat on the ___' -> Model predicts 'mat', checks answer, adjusts weights, repeats. Gains: knowledge, language, reasoning, patterns. Lacks: can't answer questions - just autocompletes.
3.6.summary: Pretraining feeds trillions of internet tokens through next-word prediction; cost ~$10-100M, gives knowledge but only autocomplete behavior.
queries: what is pretraining of an LLM
how much does it cost to pretrain a model

why is base model only autocomplete
what does a base model know
what is the prediction game in LLM training

3.6.text: Phase 2: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning). 1-2 weeks, $1,000-10,000. Humans write 10,000-100,000 perfect Q&A examples. The model trains on these to learn. Example - Q: 'How do I boil an egg?' A: 'Put egg in water, boil, 6 min for soft, 12 for hard.' Gains: answers questions, follows instructions, right tone. Lacks: might still be rude, harmful, or confidently wrong.
3.6.summary: SFT teaches the base model to answer questions using 10K-100K curated human Q&A examples in 1-2 weeks for ~$1K-10K.
queries: how do you teach LLM to answer questions
human written Q and A training

3.6.text: Phase 3: RLHF (Human Feedback). 1-2 weeks, $1,000-10,000. Humans compare pairs of responses and pick the better one. A reward model learns their preferences, then the LLM optimizes for higher reward. Answer A: helpful, clear, safe -> Human picks this. Answer B: rude, misleading -> Rejected. Result: helpful, harmless, honest assistant. This is what becomes ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini.
3.6.summary: RLHF uses human preference comparisons to train a reward model, producing the helpful/harmless/honest assistant that ships as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini.
queries: reinforcement learning from human feedback explanation
how do humans rate LLM answers
what makes ChatGPT helpful and safe

3.6.text: After launch - optional upgrades. Distillation makes it smaller and cheaper: GPT-4 'teaches' a smaller model that learns 90% of ability at 1/100th cost (GPT-4 Mini, Claude Haiku). CLIP makes it understand images: plug in a CLIP-style image encoder so images convert into the same number space as text, letting the model 'read' photos. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) gives access to fresh info: the model searches a database or the web before answering, like an open-book exam, allowing web browsing and citing sources. Domain fine-tuning makes it an expert: train further on domain-specific data (e.g. medical records) to specialize in medicine, law, or finance while keeping general intelligence.
3.6.summary: Optional post-launch upgrades to a base LLM: distillation (cheaper), CLIP (vision), RAG (fresh info), and domain fine-tuning (specialization).

3.6.text: Parts 2 & 3 complete. Full LLM lifecycle: 1) Tokenization - how text becomes numbers. 2) Pretraining - learn language by predicting next words. 3) Cross-entropy loss - how the model measures its mistakes. 4) SFT - teach it to answer questions properly. 5) RLHF - teach it to be helpful, safe, and honest. 6) Scaling laws - bigger model + more data = predictably better. 7) Distillation - shrink the model without losing smarts. 8) CLIP - connect images and text in one shared space. Next up: the architecture that makes all of this possible - Transformers and Attention.

3.6.text: Chapter 3.6 walks through the complete LLM training pipeline. Phase 1 Pretraining (2-3 months, $10-100M) feeds trillions of internet tokens through next-word prediction to give knowledge and language. Phase 2 SFT (1-2 weeks, $1K-10K) trains on 10K-100K human Q&A examples so the model answers instead of autocompletes. Phase 3 RLHF (1-2 weeks, $1K-10K) uses human preference pairs and a reward model to make it helpful, harmless, honest - this is what ships as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini. Optional post-launch upgrades: distillation (smaller/cheaper students like GPT-4 Mini, Claude Haiku), CLIP (image understanding via shared embedding space), RAG (fresh info via search), and domain fine-tuning (medicine, law, finance experts). Closes with an 8-point recap of the entire training journey.
3.6.summary: Chapter-level summary of the three core LLM training phases (pretraining, SFT, RLHF) plus four optional post-launch upgrades (distillation, CLIP, RAG, domain fine-tuning).

8.2.text: Add & Norm is the stabilizer inside every Transformer block, appearing twice per block - once after Multi-Head Attention and once after the Feed-Forward Network. The Add (residual connection) preserves the original input by adding it back to the sub-layer output, creating a gradient highway. The Norm (Layer Normalization) rescales values to a stable range using mean, variance, epsilon, and learnable gamma/beta parameters. Together they prevent values from vanishing toward zero or exploding to thousands across deep networks like GPT-3's 96 layers, allowing both forward signals and backward gradients to flow without degradation.
8.2.summary: Add & Norm stabilizes deep Transformers by combining a residual skip connection with Layer Normalization, applied twice per block.

8.2.text: Inside one Transformer block, there are 4 steps. After attention inside a single Transformer block, the flow is: Input Embeddings -> Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN (Feed-Forward Network) -> Add & Norm -> Output. Add & Norm appears twice in every block - once after Attention, once after FFN. It's the stabilizer that keeps everything in check.

8.2.text: The Problem: Values Drift in Deep Networks. GPT-3 has 96 layers. Each layer multiplies values by weights. The result depends on the weights - if they're slightly below 1.0, values shrink. If slightly above, values explode. Keeping them perfectly balanced across 96 layers is nearly impossible. Take 0.7 from "cats" embedding. If weights average 0.9 (shrink): Start 0.7 -> Layer 10: 0.24 -> Layer 50: 0.004 -> Layer 96: 0.00003 (value vanishes to nearly zero). If weights average 1.1 (grow): Start 0.7 -> Layer 10: 1.8 -> Layer 50: 82 -> Layer 96: 4,838 (value explodes to thousands). Both are broken. Whether the value goes to 0.00003 or 4,838 - it's no longer a useful embedding. And gradients during backpropagation drift the same way, so the model can't learn either.
8.2.summary: Without stabilization, values either vanish toward zero or explode to thousands across 96 layers of a deep Transformer.

8.2.text: The "Add" - Keep the Original. The fix is beautifully simple: add the original input back to the output. The fancy name is "residual connection" - residual just means leftover. For "cats" with embedding [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]: original input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] goes through Multi-Head Attention to produce attention output [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]. The Add Step: [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] + [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]. Think of it as a highway: even if the attention layer learns nothing useful, the original embedding still passes through untouched. During backpropagation, the error can travel backward along this highway without shrinking to zero. Without the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output, if attention messes up, values are lost. With the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output + Input, original values always survive.
8.2.summary: The Add step (residual connection) adds the original input back to the attention output, preserving the signal even if attention contributes nothing useful.

8.2.text: The "Norm" - Layer Normalization. After adding, values can drift to unpredictable ranges. Layer Norm rescales them to a stable range. The Layer Normalization Formula: LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma * (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon) + beta. Parameters: x_i = each value in the vector (the raw input we want to normalize). mu (mean) = average of all values (centers the data around zero). sigma^2 (variance) = variance of all values (measures how spread out values are). epsilon = tiny number (1e-5), prevents division by zero if all values are identical. gamma = learnable scale (starts at 1.0), lets the model stretch or shrink the range. beta = learnable shift (starts at 0.0), lets the model move the center up or down. Gamma and beta are learned during training - they let the model undo the normalization if needed and stretch, shrink, or shift the normalized values to whatever range works best for each dimension.
8.2.summary: Layer Normalization formula rescales values using mean, variance, epsilon, and learnable gamma/beta parameters per dimension.

8.2.text: Computing Layer Norm - Step by Step. Normalizing [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] (the result from Add). Step 1: Compute the mean (mu). mu = (-0.2 + 0.2 + 0.9 + 1.0) / 4 = 0.475. Step 2: subtract the mean from each value (x_i - mu). -0.2 - 0.475 = -0.675; 0.2 - 0.475 = -0.275; 0.9 - 0.475 = 0.425; 1.0 - 0.475 = 0.525. Centered: [-0.675, -0.275, 0.425, 0.525]. Step 3: Compute variance, then divide by sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon). variance = mean of squared deviations = (0.675^2 + 0.275^2 + 0.425^2 + 0.525^2) / 4 = 0.2469. sqrt(0.2469 + 0.00001) = 0.4969. -0.675 / 0.4969 = -1.36; -0.275 / 0.4969 = -0.55; 0.425 / 0.4969 = 0.86; 0.525 / 0.4969 = 1.06. After this step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] - centered at 0, unit variance. Step 4: Scale by gamma, shift by beta. Initially gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0, so at first this step does nothing. With initial gamma=1.0, beta=0.0: 1.0 * -1.36 + 0.0 = -1.36; 1.0 * -0.55 + 0.0 = -0.55; 1.0 * 0.86 + 0.0 = 0.86; 1.0 * 1.06 + 0.0 = 1.06. Final output: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Why have gamma and beta if they start as identity? During training, the model may learn that some dimensions need more spread (gamma > 1) or a non-zero center (beta != 0). Without them, normalization would be too rigid.
8.2.summary: Worked example of Layer Norm on [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] showing mean 0.475, variance 0.2469, and final normalized output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06].

8.2.text: The Complete Add & Norm Pipeline. Watch "cats" flow through the entire Add & Norm after Attention. Input (cats embedding): [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] -> goes into Attention. Attention Output: [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] -> Add: input + output. After Add: [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] -> Normalize. After Norm: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] -> ready for FFN. To FFN: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. This exact same Add & Norm happens again after FFN. Every Transformer block does this twice - once after Attention, once after FFN.

8.2.text: Why It Matters - The Big Picture. Without Add & Norm: Layer 1: 0.7, Layer 10: 0.24, Layer 50: 0.004, Layer 96: ~0. Values drift to extremes. Model stops learning. With Add & Norm: Layer 1: 0.7, Layer 10: 0.67, Layer 50: 0.62, Layer 96: 0.57. Values stay stable. Gradients flow. Training works even at 96 layers deep. In one sentence: Add & Norm is the reason Transformers can be 96 layers deep without losing information. The "Add" preserves the original values, and the "Norm" keeps them in a stable range for the next layer.
8.2.summary: Side-by-side comparison shows values vanishing without Add & Norm versus staying stable with it, enabling 96-layer deep training.

8.3.text: Where does the Feed-Forward Network sit? Inside one Transformer block: Input Embeddings → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → FFN (Feed-Forward Network) → Add & Norm → Output. The FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers. It processes each token independently - no communication between words here. That's what makes it different from Attention.
8.3.summary: Locates the FFN as the third stage inside a Transformer block, processing each token independently between two Add & Norm layers.

8.3.text: FFN = Two Linear Layers with Activation. The FFN is two layers stacked from chapter 1.19 with an activation in between. Formula: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2. Where x = input from Add & Norm; W1, b1 = first layer weights & bias; GELU = activation function; W2, b2 = second layer weights & bias. No attention, no softmax, no multi-head - just two matrix multiplies with an activation in between.
8.3.summary: States the exact two-layer FFN formula FFN(x) = W2·GELU(W1·x + b1) + b2 with each component labeled.

why GELU between W1 and W2

8.3.text: The Expand-Then-Compress Shape. The first layer expands the dimension, the second layer compresses it back. In GPT-2 (d_model = 512): hidden layer has 4x more neurons than input or output. Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron (W1, 512x2048), GELU activates each hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron (W2, 2048x512). Data expands into a wider thinking space (512 → 2048), then compresses back (2048 → 512). Why 4x? Imagine spreading notes across a big desk (expand to 2048), working things out, then summarizing on a small card (compress to 512). The 4x ratio comes from the original 'Attention is All You Need' paper and is standard across GPT-2, GPT-3, and most Transformers.
8.3.summary: Explains the FFN's expand-then-compress shape with d_ff = 4 × d_model and the intuition for why widening creates a thinking space.
queries: why does FFN expand then compress
why is hidden dim 4x model dim
Attention is All You Need FFN ratio

8.3.text: Step 1: Multiply by W1 + b1 (expand 4 → 8 dims). Tracing 'cats' through FFN. Add & Norm output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] for 'cats'. We use a tiny 4-dim version (real models use 512). Compute [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] · W1 + b1. Each of the 8 output values is a dot product of the input with one column of W1, plus a bias - exactly like chapter 1.19. Result (8 dims): [0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61].
8.3.summary: Walks through Step 1 of FFN on the cats vector: matrix multiply by W1 plus bias to expand from 4 to 8 dimensions.
queries: how does W1 expand the input

8.3.text: Step 2: Apply GELU activation (each value independently, similar to ReLU but smoother). Inputs/outputs: 0.42→0.28, -1.80→-0.04, 0.95→0.77, 2.10→2.07, -0.33→-0.12, 1.47→1.37, -0.88→-0.17, 0.61→0.44. Result: [0.28, -0.04, 0.77, 2.07, -0.12, 1.37, -0.17, 0.44]. Step 3: Multiply by W2 + b2 to compress 8 → 4 dims. The 8-dim expanded vector compresses back to the original 4 dims. FFN output for 'cats': [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Same size in, same size out - but values transformed through a thinking space 4x wider. Output goes to the second Add & Norm.
8.3.summary: Continues the FFN trace through GELU and W2 to compress back to the original 4-dim output [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22].

8.3.text: GELU - The Activation Function (Gaussian Error Linear Unit). Modern Transformers use GELU instead of ReLU. Formula: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), where Φ(x) is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal: Φ(x) = (1/2)[1 + erf(x/√2)]. x = input value; Φ(x) = probability x is 'kept' (ranges 0 fully blocked to 1 fully passed); erf = Gauss error function (smooth S-curve mapping any number to [-1,1]). In plain English: GELU multiplies x by the probability that x would be considered 'large' under a standard normal bell curve. Worked examples: x=2.0, Φ=0.977, result=1.95 (large positive almost fully passed); x=0.5, Φ=0.691, result=0.35 (69% passed); x=0.0, result=0.00; x=-0.5, Φ=0.309, result=-0.15 (31% leaked); x=-2.0, Φ=0.023, result=-0.05 (almost fully blocked). ReLU vs GELU: ReLU has hard cutoff (negative=dead, gradient=0 forever). GELU has smooth transition - small negatives leak through, gradient never fully dies. During backpropagation, ReLU's gradient is exactly 0 for negatives - the neuron is dead and can never recover. GELU's smooth curve means slightly negative values get a small gradient, so neurons can recover. GPT-2 and GPT-3 both use GELU.
8.3.summary: Defines GELU's exact formula GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), works five example values, and contrasts the smooth GELU curve with ReLU's dead-region cutoff.

8.3.text: What FFN Stores - Knowledge vs Routing. Attention decides which words to listen to; FFN decides what to do with what it heard. Inside every block, Attention runs first, then FFN. FFN never sees the raw input - it always receives attention's output. Example 1: 'The area of this rectangle depends on its length' - notice 'breadth' is never mentioned but the model knows it matters. Attention runs first: 'area's Query asks 'what determines me?'; 'length's Key scores 0.55 after softmax; 'rectangle's Key scores 0.30. Weighted sum: Output = 0.55 · V_length + 0.30 · V_rectangle + ... 'area' vector now carries 'I'm the area of a rectangle and length is involved.' But 'breadth' is not in the sentence - attention can only mix what's there. Then FFN adds stored knowledge: W1 row 1203 ('rectangle-area' detector) was trained on millions of math texts: W1[1203] · x = 3.9. GELU(3.9) = 3.89, neuron 1203 fires strongly. Neuron 502 ('circle-area-pi') got -0.8, GELU suppresses it. W2 column 1203 shifts the vector toward a region encoding 'area = length × breadth, breadth is the missing factor.' The model implicitly knows breadth matters even though the word never appeared. During training, the FFN saw 'area = length × breadth' thousands of times - that relationship got baked into W1 row 1203 (detector) and W2 column 1203 (output).
8.3.summary: Contrasts attention's routing role with FFN's stored-knowledge role using the area/length/breadth example to show how FFN injects implicit facts.
queries: does attention or FFN store knowledge
how does FFN inject training data
attention as routing FFN as memory

8.3.text: Example 2: 'The capital of India is ___' - Attention gathers context, FFN recalls the fact. Attention runs first: '___'s Query asks 'what country am I about?'; 'India's Key scores 0.72 after softmax; 'capital' gets 0.18. Weighted sum: Output = 0.72 · V_India + 0.18 · V_capital + ... '___' vector encodes 'I need the capital of India.' But attention can only average existing vectors - it cannot look up a fact. It assembled the question, not the answer. Then FFN recalls: W1 row 847 ('capital-of-India' detector) trained on millions of geography texts: W1[847] · x = 4.2. GELU(4.2) = 4.19, neuron 847 fires. GELU(-1.3) = -0.05, neuron 200 ('capital-of-France') is suppressed. Of 2048 neurons, maybe 100-300 fire meaningfully for any given input. W2 column 847 was trained to shift vectors toward 'Delhi': output += 4.19 · W2[:,847]. The answer is now in the vector. Key insight: W1 rows = 'what to detect', W2 columns = 'what to output' - the key-value memory interpretation. The FFN is a lookup table: 2048 entries, each asking 'does this pattern match?' and outputting the corresponding knowledge.
8.3.summary: Uses 'capital of India is ___' to show FFN performing factual recall via a W1 detector row and matching W2 output column (key-value memory).
queries: W1 row detector W2 column writer

8.3.text: Attention + FFN Across Multiple Blocks. Example 3: 'She sat by the river bank' - does 'bank' mean money or riverbed? Block 1 (basic context): Attention - 'bank's Query matches 'river's Key (adjacent words). 'bank' absorbs ~40% of 'river's Value, ~20% of 'sat'. FFN sees 'bank + some river context'. W1 row 310 ('river-location') fires weakly at 1.2; W1 row 744 ('financial-institution') fires weakly at 0.8. GELU passes both. W2 nudges slightly toward both meanings. Ambiguity not yet resolved. Block 5 (deeper understanding): Attention - 'bank' attends to 'sat', 'by', 'river' with much higher scores than Block 1, because Blocks 1-4 FFNs already strengthened nature/location features. FFN: row 310 fires at 3.8 (strong match). Row 744 ('financial') fires at -1.1, GELU(-1.1) = -0.03, effectively dead. W2 column 310 shifts vector hard toward geography features; column 744 contributes nothing. 'Bank' now means 'riverbank' - ambiguity resolved. Why multiple blocks: each round, attention provides better context because FFN improved the vectors, and FFN makes better decisions because attention gathered more context. They bootstrap each other across all 96 blocks. Three types of FFN knowledge: (1) Implicit knowledge (area/breadth) - adds related concepts; (2) Factual recall (India/Delhi) - looks up stored facts; (3) Disambiguation (bank/river) - suppresses wrong meanings, boosts right ones.
8.3.summary: Traces 'river bank' disambiguation across Blocks 1 and 5 to show attention and FFN bootstrapping each other, then summarizes three FFN knowledge types.
queries: bank river vs bank money disambiguation
FFN refines vectors block by block
why one block is not enough

8.3.text: Deep Questions You Should Be Able to Answer. (1) Why can't attention replace FFN? Attention computes weighted averages of Value vectors - a linear operation. It can mix information from different tokens, but never create new features. 'If river AND bank are both present, output riverbank' requires non-linearity (GELU). Only FFN has that. (2) Why can't FFN replace attention? FFN processes each token in complete isolation. It never sees any other word. Without attention first injecting 'river' into 'bank's vector, FFN would process 'bank' identically in 'river bank' and 'investment bank' - same input, same output, unable to disambiguate. (3) What exactly does 'FFN stores knowledge' mean? Each row of W1 is a pattern detector trained during pre-training. Row 847 learned to fire when input looks like 'capital-of-India.' The corresponding column of W2 learned to output a vector that shifts toward 'Delhi.' This row-column pair is literally one stored fact. GPT-3 has 2048-49152 such detectors per block across 96 blocks. (4) How do researchers prove this isn't a metaphor? Ablation studies: zero out specific FFN neurons and watch 'The capital of India is' stop producing 'Delhi' while grammar stays intact. Activate those same neurons artificially and watch 'Delhi' appear in unrelated contexts. Knowledge is localized to specific neurons - not distributed across the whole network.
8.3.summary: Answers four deep questions about why attention and FFN are non-interchangeable and how ablation studies prove FFN neurons store localized facts.

8.3.text: Where Do the Parameters Live? Parameters are all the learnable numbers - every weight and bias tuned during training (chapter 1.4). They are the model's memory. More parameters = more capacity to store knowledge. Split between Attention and FFN in one Transformer block (~3.15M parameters total): Attention takes ~1/3 (~1.05M), FFN takes ~2/3 (~2.1M). Attention parameters: W_Q, W_K, W_V (3 matrices that create Queries, Keys, Values - chapter 6.9), W_O (matrix that blends multi-head outputs - chapter 7.12). These learn how to find relationships between words. FFN parameters: W1 (512 x 2048) expands to thinking space, W2 (2048 x 512) compresses back to model size. These learn facts and transformations - the actual knowledge. GPT-3 has 96 blocks: 96 × 3.15M = ~302M just in this layer pattern - and GPT-3's total is 175 billion because d_model is 12,288 (not 512), making each matrix vastly larger.
8.3.summary: Quantifies the per-block parameter split (~1/3 attention, ~2/3 FFN) and scales it up to GPT-3's 175B total.

8.3.text: Chapter 8.3 - FFN, the Feed-Forward Network. The FFN sits between two Add & Norm layers in every Transformer block and processes each token independently. Its formula is FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2 - just two linear layers with a GELU activation between them. The first layer expands d_model to d_ff (4× wider, e.g., 512 → 2048), and the second compresses back. GELU(x) = x · Φ(x) is a smooth alternative to ReLU that keeps gradients alive for negative inputs. While Attention routes information between tokens, FFN stores knowledge: W1 rows act as pattern detectors, W2 columns write corresponding outputs - the key-value memory view. This explains implicit knowledge, factual recall, and disambiguation. Each Transformer block has ~2/3 of its parameters in FFN, ~1/3 in Attention.
8.3.summary: Recaps the FFN's two-layer structure, expand-compress shape, GELU activation, knowledge-storage role, and parameter share.
queries: what is the feed forward network in transformer

8.3.text: Feed-forward network architecture diagram showing expand-then-compress structure: 512-dim input through W1 to 2048-dim hidden with GELU activation, then W2 back to 512-dim output. Three columns of neurons - Input (512, shown as x1, x2, x3), Hidden (2048, shown as h1, h2, h3, h2048) with + GELU, Output (512, shown as y1, y2, y3). W1 is 512x2048 between input and hidden; W2 is 2048x512 between hidden and output. Edges show full connectivity from each input neuron to every hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron to every output neuron.
8.3.summary: Diagram of the FFN's three-column expand-compress neural network with W1 (512x2048), GELU, and W2 (2048x512).

8.3.text: Line graph of the ReLU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is flat at y equals zero for all negative x, forming a horizontal line along the x-axis, then rises with a hard kink at the origin and continues linearly as y equals x for all positive x. Dead region for negatives where the gradient is exactly zero. Annotations call out the dead region (gradient = 0) on the negative side and the hard kink at the origin.
8.3.summary: ReLU plot showing zero output for negatives, hard kink at origin, and y=x for positives - illustrates the dead-gradient region.

8.3.text: Line graph of the GELU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is a smooth S-shape: nearly zero for large negative x, dips slightly below zero around x equals minus one forming a small leak region, passes smoothly through the origin with a continuous derivative, and asymptotically matches y equals x for large positive x. Smooth shape keeps gradients alive for negative inputs. Annotations call out the leak region (gradient alive) below zero and the smooth S curve at the origin.
8.3.summary: GELU plot showing the smooth S-curve with a small negative leak region and y≈x asymptote, contrasting with ReLU's hard cutoff.
queries: why GELU keeps gradient alive visual

8.4.text: Chapter 8.4 explains why the FFN handles any number of words with the same weight matrices. Words stack as rows of an input matrix; W1 (d_model x d_ff) and W2 (d_ff x d_model) only depend on model dimensions, not sequence length. One big matrix multiply replaces a per-word loop, enabling massive GPU parallelism. This works because FFN treats each token independently - rows never interact. Same property explains why the KV cache only stores attention K,V, not FFN outputs.
8.4.summary: Chapter recap: stacking words as matrix rows lets one shared W1/W2 process any sequence length in a single GPU-parallel matmul because FFN is per-token independent.

8.4.text: Recap: FFN with one word. Say 'cats' is a 3-dim vector [0.5, -0.3, 0.9]. The first FFN multiply: 1x3 (one word, 3 dims) times W1 of shape 3x6 (3 dims in, 6 dims out) equals a 1x6 hidden vector (expanded). W1 has 3 x 6 = 18 weights. These 18 weights are the same for every single word that ever passes through this FFN.
8.4.summary: One word as a 1x3 vector times W1 (3x6) yields a 1x6 hidden vector; W1 holds 18 shared weights.

8.4.text: The trick: stack words into a matrix. With 3 words ('I', 'love', 'cats'), don't run FFN three times. Stack the 3 word vectors as rows into a 3x3 matrix, then multiply once: 3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6. Same W1, still 18 weights. Row 1 of input only interacts with W to produce row 1 of output. No mixing between words - that is how matrix multiplication works.
8.4.summary: Stacking three word vectors as rows of a 3x3 matrix gives 3x6 output via one multiply by the same W1 with no row mixing.

8.4.text: 6 words: 'The cat sat on the mat'. Stack 6 words, each a 3-dim vector, into a 6x3 matrix: rows like [0.2, -0.9, 0.5], [0.6, 0.3, -0.2], [-0.5, 0.9, 0.1], [0.1, -0.4, 0.8], [0.2, -0.9, 0.5], [0.7, 0.2, -0.6]. Multiply by the same W1 (3x6, 18 weights) to get 6x6 output - one row per word. Row 1 ('The') hits W and produces its own output. Row 4 ('on') hits the exact same W and produces its own output. The rows never interact. Each word is independently transformed by the same 18 weights.
8.4.summary: Six stacked tokens form a 6x3 matrix; one multiply by the same 3x6 W1 yields a 6x6 output, each row transformed independently.

8.4.text: 10 words? 1000 words? Same 18 weights. The number of words is just the number of rows in the input matrix. The weight matrix never changes. Examples: 1 word -> 1x3 x 3x6 = 1x6, 3 words -> 3x3 x 3x6 = 3x6, 6 words -> 6x3 x 3x6 = 6x6, 10 words -> 10x3 x 3x6 = 10x6, 1000 words -> 1000x3 x 3x6 = 1000x6. The pattern: (num_words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (num_words x d_ff). The 'num_words' dimension is a free variable. W1 only cares about d_model. In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048): W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights. That number stays the same whether you feed it 10 words or 100,000 words.
8.4.summary: W1 depends only on d_model and d_ff, so num_words is a free variable; GPT-2's W1 is always 1,048,576 weights regardless of sequence length.

8.4.text: Scaling back down: W2 has the inverse shape. After GELU activation we compress back to original dim. For 6 words: input 6x3, multiply by W1 (3x6) = expanded 6x6, GELU keeps 6x6, multiply by W2 (6x3) = output 6x3. For 10 words: 10x3 x 3x6 = 10x6, GELU -> 10x6, x W2 (6x3) = 10x3. Same W1 and W2 in both cases. General pattern: Layer 1: (words x d_model) x W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (words x d_ff). GELU: (words x d_ff) -> (words x d_ff). Layer 2: (words x d_ff) x W2 (d_ff x d_model) = (words x d_model). Word count flows through as the row dimension; W1 and W2 only depend on d_model and d_ff.
8.4.summary: W2 has the flipped (d_ff x d_model) shape so output has same width as input; word count is just the row dimension across both layers.

8.4.text: Why this matters: massive GPU parallelism. Without stacking (for loop): 'for word in words: output = word x W' - 1000 words means 1000 sequential matrix multiplies, each tiny (1x3 x 3x6), GPU sits mostly idle. With stacking (one multiply): 'output = all_words x W' - 1000 words become one massive parallel matmul (1000x3 x 3x6) processed across thousands of GPU cores simultaneously. The deep insight: this parallel trick works because FFN treats each word independently. No word's output depends on any other word. That is the fundamental difference from Attention, which creates cross-word dependencies through Q dot K. You cannot parallelize Attention the same way because token 3's output depends on tokens 1 and 2. FFN is embarrassingly parallel - the GPU's dream workload. This is also why the KV cache during generation only caches attention K,V, not FFN outputs. FFN has no cross-word state to remember; run it on the new word, get the answer.
8.4.summary: Stacking lets one big GPU matmul replace a per-word loop because FFN is per-token independent, unlike attention's cross-word Q dot K dependencies, which is also why the KV cache stores only attention K,V.

8.5.text: Second Add & Norm sits after FFN in the Transformer block. Pipeline: Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm (first) -> FFN -> Add & Norm (second) -> Block Output. FFN just transformed each token's representation, but values drift in deep networks (problem from chapter 8.2), so we stabilize again. This second Add & Norm works exactly like the first - same mechanism - except its input is the FFN output instead of the Attention output.
8.5.summary: The second Add & Norm follows FFN in every Transformer block and stabilizes FFN's output the same way the first Add & Norm stabilizes attention's output.

8.5.text: The Add step around FFN works the same as in chapter 8.2: keep the original input by adding it back. The input to FFN was the first Add & Norm's output. Worked example for 'cats': FFN input = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Goes through FFN (chapter 8.3). FFN output = [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Add step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] + [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] = [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]. The residual connection ensures that even if FFN distorts values, the original signal from Add & Norm #1 survives by being added back.
8.5.summary: The Add step around FFN adds FFN's input vector to FFN's output element-wise, preserving the original signal as a residual.

8.5.text: Same Layer Normalization formula as chapter 8.2: compute mean, subtract, divide by standard deviation, scale by gamma, shift by beta. Worked example normalizing [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]: Step 1 mean mu = (-0.85 + -1.28 + 2.00 + 1.28) / 4 = 0.2875. Step 2 variance = 1.9172, sqrt(variance + epsilon) = 1.3846. Step 3 normalize each: (x - mu) / 1.3846 -> -0.85 -> -0.82, -1.28 -> -1.13, 2.00 -> 1.24, 1.28 -> 0.72. Block output for 'cats': [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72] with gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0 (initial values). The model learns optimal gamma and beta during training.
8.5.summary: Layer Norm rescales the post-Add vector using mean, variance, gamma, and beta - same formula as the first Add & Norm but applied to FFN output.

One block: Attention moves information between tokens, Add & Norm stabilizes, FFN processes each token's knowledge, Add & Norm stabilizes again. This is the heartbeat of every Transformer.

8.5.text: Add & Norm appears twice because each Transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each sub-layer gets its own Add & Norm because each can independently cause value drift. Sub-layer 1: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add & Norm #1 (stabilizes after attention changes). Sub-layer 2: From A&N #1 -> FFN(x) -> Add & Norm #2 (stabilizes after FFN changes). The Rule: in Transformers, every sub-layer that transforms values gets wrapped with its own residual connection and layer norm. This is what makes it possible to stack 96 blocks deep without the signal degrading. If you only normalized once at the end of the block, FFN's output could already be in a bad range before it gets stabilized.
8.5.summary: Each transforming sub-layer (Attention and FFN) needs its own residual + LayerNorm so that value drift from either sub-layer is corrected before stacking 96 blocks deep.

8.5.text: Chapter 8.5 covers the second Add & Norm in a Transformer block - the stabilizer that follows FFN. It mirrors the first Add & Norm: residual addition (FFN input + FFN output) followed by Layer Normalization with gamma and beta. Worked through with 'cats' embedding: FFN input [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] + FFN output [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] = [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28], then normalized to block output [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. The chapter ends by explaining why Add & Norm appears twice: each transforming sub-layer (Attention, FFN) gets its own residual + Layer Norm to prevent value drift, enabling stacks of 96 blocks.
8.5.summary: Chapter 8.5 explains the post-FFN Add & Norm, traces a full block on the 'cats' vector, and justifies why every sub-layer in a Transformer needs its own residual plus normalization.

8.7.text: Residual connections (skip connections) solve the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks by computing output = F(x) + x. The +x creates a shortcut path whose gradient is always exactly 1, so even when F'(x) shrinks toward zero across 96 layers, the gradient on the skip path arrives at layer 1 undiminished. Every Transformer block has two residual connections (one around Attention, one around FFN), giving GPT-3's 96 blocks 192 gradient highways. Without them, deep Transformers would be untrainable.
8.7.summary: Residual connections add input back to layer output so gradients flow through deep stacks via a skip path with derivative 1.

8.7.text: The Problem: Gradients Vanish in Deep Networks. From chapter 1.15, gradients shrink as they travel backward through layers because each layer multiplies the gradient by its derivative. If that derivative is below 1.0, the gradient shrinks exponentially. Multiplying through 96 layers: with F'(x)=0.99, 0.99^96 = 0.38 (lose 62% even with near-perfect derivatives). With F'(x)=0.9, 0.9^96 = 0.0000396 (effectively zero by layer 1). With F'(x)=0.5, 0.5^96 = 1.3 x 10^-29 (completely gone, no learning possible). GPT-3 has 96 layers. Without a fix, the gradient arriving at layer 1 from layer 96 would be essentially zero. Layer 1 would never learn. The model would be dead on arrival.
8.7.summary: Gradients in deep networks shrink exponentially through layer multiplications, killing learning in the early layers of 96-layer models.

8.7.text: The Residual Connection: output = F(x) + x. The fix is deceptively simple: add the input directly to the output. The +x creates a shortcut path that bypasses the entire layer. Path A (Without Residual): input x -> weight1 -> activation -> weight2 -> activation -> output. Gradient must travel through every layer and shrinks at each step. Path B (With Residual): input x -> weight1 -> activation -> weight2 -> activation -> F(x) + x. The +x adds a direct highway that bypasses all layers. The skip arrow runs alongside the layer stack and rejoins at the bottom.
8.7.summary: A residual block computes F(x)+x, adding the raw input back to the layer's transformed output to create a shortcut around the layer.
queries: what does output equals F of x plus x mean
how does a skip connection work visually
residual block diagram path A vs path B
what is a shortcut path in a neural network
why add input back to output of layer
residual function F of x explanation

8.7.text: Why It Solves Vanishing Gradients - The Math. Take the derivative of the residual output: d/dx [F(x) + x] = F'(x) + 1. F'(x) is the gradient through the layer weights and can shrink toward 0. The +1 is the gradient through the skip connection - ALWAYS exactly 1, no matter what. That +1 is everything. Even if F'(x) approaches 0, the total gradient is at least 1. The gradient through the skip connection is always 1, regardless of what the layer weights do. Without residual: gradient = F'(x); if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 0.01. With residual: gradient = F'(x) + 1; if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 1.01.
8.7.summary: The derivative of F(x)+x equals F'(x)+1, so the +1 from the skip path guarantees the gradient never vanishes even when layer derivatives approach zero.

8.7.text: The Gradient Highway visual. A 6-layer transformer with gradients traveling backward from Layer 6 to Layer 1 via two paths shown side by side at each layer. Through weights: gradient is 0.7^idx, decaying as 1.00, 0.70, 0.49, 0.34, 0.24, 0.17 - lost 83% of the signal across 5 layers. Through skip: always 1.00 at every layer, full signal preserved. Bars visualize the two channels. Conclusion: through weights 0.7^5 = 0.17, through skip = 1.00. The skip path delivers the gradient from layer 6 to layer 1 without any multiplication.
8.7.summary: Side-by-side visualization shows the weight path gradient decaying to 0.17 across 6 layers while the skip path stays at 1.00.

weight path vs residual path gradient

8.7.text: Why This Is Critical for Transformers. Modern transformers are absurdly deep. Without residual connections, training them would be impossible. GPT-2: 12 layers, 24 gradient highways. GPT-3: 96 layers, 192 gradient highways. GPT-4 (estimated): 120 layers, 240 gradient highways. TWO residual connections per block: every transformer block has two sub-layers (Attention and FFN). Each gets its own residual connection (chapter 8.2). That means GPT-3's 96 blocks have 192 gradient highways - one around every Attention layer and one around every FFN layer. Per-block flow: Input -> Attention + Residual #1 -> FFN + Residual #2 -> Block Output. Without residual connections, gradients would vanish by layer 3. With them, gradients flow freely through all 96 layers on the skip path, letting even the earliest layers learn from the loss signal. This is what makes deep transformers trainable.
8.7.summary: Each transformer block has two residual connections (around Attention and FFN), so GPT-3's 96 blocks contain 192 gradient highways enabling deep training.

8.8.text: Chapter 8.8 contrasts Post-Norm (the original 2017 'Attention Is All You Need' arrangement where LayerNorm comes after the residual Add) with Pre-Norm (the modern arrangement where LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer and the residual Add receives the raw, unnormalized input). Pre-Norm wins for two reasons: (1) cleaner gradient flow because the skip path is pure addition with derivative 1 at every step instead of passing through LayerNorm, and (2) better training stability that tolerates larger learning rates without careful warmup. Post-Norm appears in the original paper and early BERT; Pre-Norm is used by GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA family, Mistral/Mixtral, Claude, and virtually all modern LLMs since ~2019.
8.8.summary: Pre-Norm puts LayerNorm before sub-layers leaving a clean residual highway, replacing the original Post-Norm arrangement and now standard in all modern LLMs.

8.8.text: Post-Norm (Original 2017 Paper). In the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, LayerNorm comes after the residual Add. The exact order per sub-layer is: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add: x + Attention(x) -> LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add: prev + FFN(prev) -> LayerNorm. Key feature: LayerNorm sits after the residual add. The residual path passes through LayerNorm before reaching the next sub-layer.
8.8.summary: Post-Norm is the original 2017 transformer ordering where LayerNorm is applied after the residual Add, so the skip path itself flows through LayerNorm.
queries: How was layer normalization arranged in the original transformer paper?

How did Vaswani 2017 normalize transformer blocks?

8.8.text: Pre-Norm (Modern Approach). In pre-norm, LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer. The residual add gets the raw, unnormalized input - not the normalized version. Order: Input x -> LayerNorm(x) -> Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> Add: x + Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> LayerNorm(prev) -> FFN(LayerNorm(prev)) -> Add: prev + FFN(LayerNorm(prev)). Key difference: the Add step receives the raw input x, not the normalized version. LayerNorm only feeds into the sub-layer, not into the skip path.
8.8.summary: Pre-Norm places LayerNorm before each sub-layer so the residual Add operates on the raw input, leaving the skip path untouched by normalization.

8.8.text: Side-by-Side Comparison. Trace input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] (the 'cats' embedding) through the Attention sub-layer in both approaches. Post-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] -> Attention(x) = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] -> Add: x + Attn = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] -> LayerNorm = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Norm is last; the skip path passes through Norm. Pre-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] -> LayerNorm(x) = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] -> Attn(LN(x)) = [0.28, -0.12, 0.18, 0.38] -> Add: x + Attn = [-0.22, 0.18, 0.88, 0.98]. Norm is first; the skip path is just pure addition. Critical difference: in post-norm the skip path goes through LayerNorm; in pre-norm the skip path is pure addition - no transformations on the highway.
8.8.summary: Worked example tracing the 'cats' embedding through both pre-norm and post-norm Attention sub-layers, showing pre-norm leaves the residual highway as pure addition while post-norm routes it through LayerNorm.

8.8.text: Why Pre-Norm Wins. Two key advantages make pre-norm the clear winner for deep models. (1) Cleaner Gradient Flow: in post-norm, the gradient on the skip path must pass through LayerNorm at every block. LayerNorm has its own derivative that modifies the gradient - not as bad as vanishing, but it adds noise and makes optimization harder. In pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition. The gradient flows from the last block to the first block on a clean highway with derivative = 1 at every step. No LayerNorm interference on the residual stream. (2) Training Stability: post-norm requires very careful learning rate warmup - start with a tiny learning rate and slowly increase it over thousands of steps. Without warmup, gradients in early training are unstable and the model diverges (loss goes to infinity). Pre-norm is more forgiving. It works with larger learning rates from the start. The clean gradient highway stabilizes training even without careful warmup schedules. This makes training faster and less finicky.
8.8.summary: Pre-norm wins because the residual highway has derivative exactly 1 (clean gradient flow) and because training tolerates larger learning rates without delicate warmup schedules.

8.8.text: Who Uses What. Post-Norm: 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017), early BERT models, some early translation models - mainly historical, used in the original paper. Pre-Norm: GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA / LLaMA 2 / LLaMA 3, Mistral, Mixtral, Claude, virtually all modern LLMs - the industry standard since ~2019. The Field Converged: the shift happened because pre-norm trains faster, scales better to deep models, and is more robust. When you hear about a modern LLM with 96+ layers, it is almost certainly using pre-norm. The original paper used post-norm, but practice proved pre-norm superior for large-scale training.
8.8.summary: Post-norm appears in the 2017 paper and early BERT, while pre-norm is used by GPT-2/3/4, LLaMA, Mistral, Mixtral, Claude, and effectively every modern LLM since 2019.

8.6.text: Chapter 8.6 explains why Transformer blocks repeat N times. One block only learns simple patterns; deep models stack many identical blocks (called Nx) to build from grammar up to reasoning. Each block runs Attention then Add & Norm then FFN then Add & Norm. Every block has the same structure but its own unique learned weights (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, b1, W2, b2, gamma1, beta1, gamma2, beta2). Real model sizes: GPT-2 Small N=12 (d=768), GPT-2 Medium N=24 (d=1024), GPT-2 Large N=36 (d=1280), GPT-3 N=96 (d=12288). Research on internals shows depth-based specialization: blocks 1-4 surface patterns, 5-12 syntax, 13-24 semantics, 25-96 abstract reasoning. The encoder uses bidirectional attention (no masking).
8.6.summary: Transformer blocks stack N times with identical structure but unique weights per block, enabling deep models to learn from grammar to reasoning.

8.6.text: One block does not repeat enough. We've seen all 4 steps inside one Transformer block: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. But one block can only learn simple patterns. To understand language, the model needs to repeat this entire block many times. Think of it like this: 1 block learns basic word associations ("cat" relates to "sat"); 4 blocks learn grammar structure (subject-verb agreement); 12 blocks learn context and nuance (sarcasm, tone); 96 blocks learn deep reasoning, world knowledge, complex inference. Each repeat of the block refines the token representations further. The output of block 1 becomes the input of block 2, and so on.
8.6.summary: One Transformer block is too shallow; repeating it many times unlocks deeper capabilities from word associations up to reasoning.

8.6.text: The stack: how many blocks? Different models use different numbers of blocks. In the original paper and in practice, this number is called N (hence "Nx" in architecture diagrams). Real model sizes: GPT-2 Small = 12 blocks, d=768. GPT-2 Medium = 24 blocks, d=1024. GPT-2 Large = 36 blocks, d=1280. GPT-3 = 96 blocks, d=12288. What this means: in GPT-3, every single token in your prompt passes through 96 identical blocks in sequence. Each block runs the full Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm pipeline. That's 96 rounds of attention, 96 rounds of FFN, and 192 Add & Norm operations per token.
8.6.summary: Real model block counts and d_model sizes from GPT-2 Small (12 blocks, d=768) up to GPT-3 (96 blocks, d=12288).

8.6.text: Same structure, different weights. Every block has the same structure: Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm. But every learnable weight inside each block is unique - learned separately during training. Multi-Head Attention weights: W_Q creates Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K creates Keys ("what do I contain?"), W_V creates Values ("what info do I pass along?"), W_O blends all 8 heads back together. Add & Norm #1 weights: gamma1 (learnable scale per dimension), beta1 (learnable shift per dimension). FFN weights: W1, b1 (first layer expands 512 to 2048 dims), W2, b2 (second layer compresses 2048 back to 512). Add & Norm #2 weights: gamma2 and beta2 (separate copies learned independently). Every block has its own copy of ALL these weights. Block 1, Block 2, ... Block 96 each have completely different weights. If they shared the same weights, stacking 96 blocks would be no better than having one - you'd just repeat the same transformation. Each block's unique weights let it specialize: Block 1 learns different patterns than Block 50.
8.6.summary: Each block has identical architecture but its own unique copy of W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, W2, gammas, and betas, enabling per-block specialization.

8.6.text: What each layer learns - shallow to deep. Research on Transformer internals has revealed a clear pattern: early blocks learn simple things, deep blocks learn abstract things. Blocks 1-4 (Surface Patterns): part-of-speech (noun, verb, adjective), basic grammar and word order, simple co-occurrence ("New" near "York"). Blocks 5-12 (Syntax & Structure): subject-verb agreement across distance, clause boundaries and nesting, pronoun resolution ("she" refers to "Alice"). Blocks 13-24 (Semantics & Meaning): word sense disambiguation ("bank" = river vs money), sentiment and tone detection, entity relationships and facts. Blocks 25-96 (Abstract Reasoning): multi-step inference and logic, world knowledge and common sense, task-specific meaning and generation. This is why depth matters - you need many blocks to build from grammar all the way up to meaning and reasoning. Same principle as deep networks from chapter 1.21, but now each block has both attention (for context) and FFN (for knowledge).
8.6.summary: Empirically, early blocks learn POS and grammar, middle blocks handle syntax and semantics, deep blocks do abstract reasoning and world knowledge.

8.6.text: The complete picture - token to output through N blocks. Diagram shows the full journey of a single token from input to output: Token Embedding + Positional Encoding (Sections 5.2-5.7) -> Block 1 (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) -> Block 2 -> ... -> Block N -> Encoder Output (contextual hidden states for each token). The N blocks are bracketed with an Nx label on the right. Sizes: GPT-2 Small N=12 blocks, 117M parameters. GPT-3 N=96 blocks, 175B parameters. The full recipe of a Transformer encoder: Tokenize -> Embed -> Add positions -> Pass through N blocks (each: Self-Attention -> Add & Norm -> FFN -> Add & Norm) -> Rich contextual hidden states. The encoder uses bidirectional attention - every word can see every other word, no masking. This gives the encoder a full understanding of the entire input.
8.6.summary: Full encoder pipeline: embedding plus positional encoding, then N stacked blocks each with attention/Add&Norm/FFN/Add&Norm, producing contextual hidden states with bidirectional attention.
