PQ vs binary quantization vs scalar

11.14.text: Cut one fat vector into 96 small ones. A 768-dim embedding is too fat to compress as a single thing. PQ's first move is to chop it into 96 small pieces, 8 dims each. Each piece is called a slot. Why slots matter: every slot gets its own dictionary. Slot 0's dictionary only has to describe patterns appearing in dims 0-7 across the corpus - much easier than describing all 768 dims at once. Formula: d = 768, m = 96, d/m = 8 dims per slot. One vector becomes 96 mini-problems, each small enough to compress hard.
11.14.summary: Step 1 of PQ: split a 768-dim vector into m=96 slots of 8 dims each so each slot can be compressed independently.
queries: why split vectors into slots in PQ
what is a slot in product quantization
how many slots does PQ use
why 8 dims per slot in PQ
what is m in product quantization
why divide vector into pieces for PQ
why each slot needs its own dictionary
what does m=96 mean in PQ

11.14.text: Each slot learns its own 256-word dictionary. Pick one slot - say slot 0. Look at slot 0's sub-vector across every document in the corpus - billions of 8-D points. Run k-means on them with k = 256. k-means finds 256 prototype points called centroids that summarize this cloud. These 256 centroids are slot 0's codebook. Every future slot-0 sub-vector will be replaced by whichever of these 256 it is closest to. Why exactly 256? Because 2^8 = 256, and that fits in a single byte. The whole PQ design is built around squeezing one slot into one byte. Total codebook size: 96 slots x 256 centroids x 8 dims x 4 bytes = 786 KB total codebook (one-time cost, fits in L2 cache). 256 prototypes per slot, 96 codebooks, the whole dictionary is L2-resident.
11.14.summary: Step 2 of PQ: per-slot k-means with k=256 builds a codebook so each sub-vector can be encoded as a single byte (2^8=256).
queries: how is the PQ codebook built
what is k-means in product quantization
what is a centroid in PQ
how big is a PQ codebook 786 KB
does PQ codebook fit in L2 cache
how many centroids per PQ slot
what does codebook mean in PQ
why one byte per slot in PQ

11.14.text: Encode = snap each slice to its nearest prototype. After all 96 codebooks exist, encoding a new document vector is just lookup. For each of its 96 slots, find the closest centroid in that slot's codebook. Write down that centroid's index - a number 0 to 255. That index fits in one byte. Repeat 96 times. The whole 768-dim vector is now 96 bytes. The original floats are thrown away. The index stores only the byte codes. Example: Doc 1 code = [17, 203, 89, 142, ..., 71] - 96 centroid ids, 1 byte each, 96 bytes total. A vector becomes 96 byte-pointers into 96 tiny codebooks.
11.14.summary: Step 3 of PQ: encoding finds the nearest centroid per slot and stores its 1-byte index, turning a 768-dim vector into 96 bytes of centroid ids.

11.14.text: 3,072 bytes shrinks to 96 bytes per vector - 32x smaller. One float32 vector at d = 768 takes 3,072 bytes. The PQ code at m = 96 takes 96 bytes. Compression ratio = 32x per vector. At billion-vector scale the economics change: storage stops being the bottleneck. The whole corpus fits on one server with room for the graph index and a cache. float32: 768 x 4 = 3,072 bytes. PQ (m=96): 96 x 1 = 96 bytes. Storage table: N=1M float=3 GB PQ=96 MB; N=100M float=300 GB PQ=9.6 GB; N=1B float=3 TB PQ=96 GB. A billion vectors in 96 GB on one server.
11.14.summary: Step 4 of PQ: float32 to PQ-code yields 32x compression - 3 TB at 1B vectors becomes 96 GB.

PQ fits on one server billion scale

11.14.text: Search at query time uses Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC) in a 4-frame storyboard. Frame A (setup): query arrives as a float vector with 96 sub-queries; docs stored only as 96-byte PQ codes - no float doc vectors on disk. Frame B (build LUT once per query): each sub-query q_s feeds into one column of the lookup table; LUT shape 96 cols x 256 rows = 24,576 floats ~96 KB, fits L2, computed once per query. Frame C (score one doc): 96 lookups + 96 adds - each PQ code byte indexes its slot's LUT column, the cell value is read, all 96 cells summed for d(q, doc). No multiplies during scan - just byte reads, table lookups, adds. Frame D (rank all docs): repeat for every doc, sort ascending, return top-k. Cost comparison: float32 brute = 768 mul + 768 add per doc; SQ int8 = 768 byte ops, ~3-4x faster; PQ + ADC = 96 lookups + 96 adds, no multiplies, ~10x faster. Asymmetric: query stays precise, docs stay approximate.
11.14.summary: Step 5 of PQ: ADC search builds a per-query 96x256 LUT once, then scores each doc as 96 lookups + 96 adds with no multiplies.

build LUT once per query PQ

11.14.text: OPQ: rotate first, so the slots line up with the data. Real embedding dimensions are not independent - dim 0 and dim 200 might be highly correlated. When PQ chops the vector by raw position, correlated information gets split across slots and each slot's k-means sees a stretched, awkward cloud. OPQ (Optimized Product Quantization) fixes this: learn an orthonormal rotation matrix R alongside the codebooks. Apply Rv to every vector before splitting. The rotation decorrelates the dimensions, the per-slot clusters become tight and round, k-means fits them better, and recall goes up. Pipeline: v -> Rv -> split -> encode. Recall@10 at m = 96: plain PQ 0.89, OPQ + PQ 0.94. R is 768 x 768 orthonormal, learned alongside codebooks. Free recall bump for one extra matrix multiply. FAISS, ScaNN, and Qdrant all default to OPQ.
11.14.summary: Step 6: OPQ learns an orthonormal rotation R applied before splitting, decorrelating dims so per-slot k-means tightens and recall@10 lifts from 0.89 to 0.94.

11.14.text: New vector arrives: insert / update / delete drift the codebooks. INSERT: sub-vector lands far from every centroid; reconstruction error 6x baseline. UPDATE: re-encoded with stale codebooks, same error. DELETE: orphan PQ code, residual distribution shifts. Slot-0 2D scatter shows tight cluster of 32 representative gray X centroids (of 256 trained per slot) around the origin with gray training dots inside; a red new-vector dot at (2.1, 1.9) sits far outside the cluster connected to the nearest centroid by a dashed line labeled distance = 1.8, alongside training avg distance = 0.3. Faded strikethrough dots show deleted training entries. Codebook drift hurts both reconstruction quality and downstream recall.
11.14.summary: Step 7: insert/update/delete operations cause codebook drift - new vectors land far from centroids (6x error), deleted vectors leave orphan codes.
queries: what happens when PQ vectors get updated
why does PQ degrade over time
new vector far from centroids in PQ

11.14.text: Industry fix: oversample + tombstones + retrain when 95p error spikes. INSERT/UPDATE: re-encode with current codebooks; track 95p reconstruction error. DELETE: tombstone in posting list; background compaction reclaims and reshapes residuals. DRIFT: 95p error breach triggers training new codebooks on a fresh sample (~256 * k_per_slot rule of thumb), then hot-swap. A line chart of 95p reconstruction error over time shows flat baseline 0-10M vectors, spike between 10M-20M, crossing horizontal dashed retrain threshold at 15M, vertical dashed line marking retrain triggered at 20M, then return to baseline. Production implementations: FAISS uses ProductQuantizer.train(new_set) + index rebuild; Vespa does background re-quantization with hot-swap; Milvus runs scheduled IVF_PQ retraining + segment compaction; Qdrant exposes PATCH /collections/{name} quantization_config + optimizers_config.deleted_threshold.
11.14.summary: Step 8: industry fix is monitor 95p reconstruction error, tombstone deletes, retrain codebooks on fresh samples and hot-swap when threshold breached.

11.14.text: M is the only knob: bytes-per-vector vs recall. m controls everything. Higher m means smaller sub-vectors, simpler k-means, tighter approximation, higher recall - but also more bytes per encoded vector. Pick the smallest m that hits your recall target. Table: m=8 -> 8 bytes, 384x compression, recall@10 (OPQ) 0.81, extreme scale; m=48 -> 48 bytes, 64x, 0.91, web-scale search; m=96 -> 96 bytes, 32x, 0.96, the production sweet spot; m=192 -> 192 bytes, 16x, 0.98, recall-sensitive workloads. m=96 (32x compression, 0.96 recall) is the canonical setting in FAISS and Qdrant. Bytes-per-vector rises linearly with m while recall@10 climbs and flattens around m=96.
11.14.summary: Step 9: m is the bytes-vs-recall knob - m=8 is extreme scale, m=96 is the production sweet spot at 32x compression and 0.96 recall, m=192 is recall-sensitive.

11.16.text: Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) trains an encoder so that every prefix of the output vector is itself a usable embedding. The first 256 dims work, the first 512 work better, the first 1024 better still, the full 3072 best. This lets a team that already indexed 500M docs at d=3072 simply truncate to 512 or 1024 dims at index time for ~6x memory savings (6 TB to 1 TB) with only ~3% recall drop, avoiding a multi-day, ~$30,000 re-embedding job. It also enables coarse-to-fine adaptive retrieval: scan with the 256-dim prefix, rerank top 100 with the full 3072-dim vector. MRL ships in OpenAI text-embedding-3-large/small (dimensions API param), Cohere Embed v3, Jina v3. MRL stacks with SQ/PQ/BQ multiplicatively because MRL cuts dim count (width) while quantization cuts bits per dim (height); rules: MRL first then quantize, and keep d>=768 if BQ follows.
11.16.summary: Matryoshka embeddings make every vector prefix a valid embedding so you can truncate at query time for big memory wins, and they stack multiplicatively with SQ/PQ/BQ.

11.16.text: A team indexed 500 million documents a year ago at d = 3072 using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large. Latency and memory bills are getting painful. A smaller embedding would help, but re-embedding 500M docs is expensive: you need the original source text (often dropped from pipelines), API cost is roughly $30,000 (~2B tokens at $0.13 per 1M), wall-clock time is 3-5 days even with high concurrency and batching, and the index rebuild adds more days of HNSW reconstruction on top. Re-embedding is feasible at 1M docs. At 500M it is an expensive migration. Matryoshka avoids it entirely by letting you truncate the existing vectors instead.
11.16.summary: Re-embedding hundreds of millions of docs costs tens of thousands of dollars and days of compute, motivating a way to shrink existing vectors without recomputing them.

11.16.text: Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) is a training-time trick. Instead of training the encoder to produce one useful vector at d = 3072, it is trained so the first 256 dims are useful on their own AND the first 512 are useful (and better) AND the first 1024 AND the full 3072. Every prefix of the vector is itself a valid embedding. Truncating to the first K dims at query time gives a working smaller vector, with no re-encoding needed. The training loss is a sum over a chosen set of K values: Loss = Sigma over K in {256, 512, 1024, 2048, 3072} of L(embed[0..K]). By penalizing every truncation during training, the model is forced to pack the most important information into the early dimensions.
11.16.summary: MRL trains the encoder so that every prefix length of the embedding is a valid usable vector, by summing a loss over multiple truncation points.

11.16.text: Matryoshka dolls nest inside each other - the small one fits inside the medium, which fits inside the large, and each one is a complete doll on its own. That is exactly the structure of a Matryoshka embedding. The 256-dim prefix sits inside the 512-dim prefix sits inside the 1024-dim prefix sits inside the full 3072-dim vector. Each prefix is a complete, usable embedding at its own resolution. The Russian doll analogy is not metaphor; it is structural - nested concentric, each prefix complete.
11.16.summary: Matryoshka embeddings are literally nested like Russian dolls: each prefix is a complete embedding at its own resolution.
queries: Why are Matryoshka embeddings called Russian dolls
Russian doll analogy in machine learning
How does each prefix sit inside the next
What does nested mean for embeddings

11.16.text: Truncate to 512 dims for 6x memory savings. Keep shipping the full 3072-dim embedding (the richest version, useful for reranking) but only load the first 512 dimensions into the vector index. The index is 6x smaller (3072 / 512), HNSW graph distances are 6x cheaper to compute, RAM footprint drops 6x. Recall hit on long-context retrieval is about 2-4%. Memory math at N = 500M: full d=3072 means 3,072 * 4 = 12,288 bytes/vector and 500M * 12 KB = 6 TB. Truncated to 512: 512 * 4 = 2,048 bytes/vector and 500M * 2 KB = 1 TB. Net: 6 TB to 1 TB, recall@10 drops from 0.97 to 0.94. Full 3072-dim kept on cold storage for the reranking stage. The vectors already exist in their full form - truncation is a metadata change with zero re-encoding cost.
11.16.summary: Truncating each MRL vector from 3072 to 512 dims shrinks a 500M-doc index from 6 TB to 1 TB with only ~3% recall drop.
queries: How much memory does truncating to 512 dims save

11.16.text: Matryoshka enables a smarter two-stage retrieval than binary + float rerank. Stage 1 uses the 256-dim prefix to scan a large candidate pool cheaply - every document gets a coarse comparison (12x smaller than full, 6x smaller than truncate=512), returning top 100. Stage 2 reruns just the top 100 candidates with the full 3072-dim vector for fine-grained ranking, doing only 100 dot products. Both stages use the same source embedding; no separate models, no separate index. End-to-end recall ~0.98 at ~5 ms latency. The 256-dim prefix wears the cheap-filter hat; the 3072-dim full vector wears the accurate-rerank hat. One encoder pass at index time; both roles served thereafter.
11.16.summary: With MRL you can do coarse-to-fine retrieval - scan with the 256-dim prefix, rerank top 100 with the full 3072-dim vector, all from one embedding.
queries: Coarse to fine retrieval with Matryoshka
Two stage retrieval using MRL prefix
Why MRL beats binary plus rerank

11.16.text: Matryoshka went from research paper (ICML 2022) to production in about a year. OpenAI shipped text-embedding-3-small (d=1536) and text-embedding-3-large (d=3072) with Matryoshka training in January 2024; both expose a `dimensions` parameter in the API that does the truncation server-side before returning the vector. Cohere Embed v3 English (d=1024) supports K = 256, 384, 512, 1024 and adds int8 + binary. Jina Embeddings v3 (d=1024) is open-weights with MRL. Custom Matryoshka training is also available for self-hosted models via the sentence-transformers library. MRL is the compression lever production teams can turn on the same day the model provider ships it - smaller, faster index with zero re-embedding, available out of the box.
11.16.summary: Production-ready Matryoshka models include OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere Embed v3, and Jina v3, with the dimensions parameter as the API knob.
queries: When did OpenAI ship MRL embeddings

11.16.text: A vector takes bytes for two reasons: it has many numbers (dimensions), and each number takes many bits. MRL cuts the count of numbers (chops the row shorter). SQ, PQ, and BQ cut the bits per number (squish each cell flatter). Different scissors on different parts, so compression ratios multiply: 3x from MRL plus 4x from SQ gives 12x total, not 7x. Concrete stacks at d=3072 baseline = 12,288 bytes: MRL+SQ: 12,288 -> MRL to 1024 -> 4,096 -> SQ int8 -> 1,024 bytes (12x, recall ~96-97%). MRL+PQ: 12,288 -> MRL to 1024 -> 4,096 -> PQ m=128 -> 128 bytes (96x, recall ~93-95%). MRL+BQ: 12,288 -> MRL to 1024 -> 4,096 -> BQ 1 bit -> 128 bytes (96x, ~94% recall, ~98% with rerank). Two governing rules: (1) Order: MRL first then quantize. Reverse breaks because quantized codes have no semantic prefix structure to truncate; PQ codebook must be trained on already-truncated vectors. (2) Floor: BQ needs d >= 768 post-MRL (recall collapses below 768). SQ and PQ have no such floor. Real-world stack at N=1B: text-embedding-3-large -> MRL to 1024 -> SQ int8 -> HNSW. Hot RAM = 1B * 1024 B = 1 TB vs baseline 12 TB. 12x compression, ~96% recall, single beefy server territory.
11.16.summary: MRL cuts dimension count while quantization cuts bits per dim, so the two compression ratios multiply; always apply MRL first and keep d>=768 if BQ follows.

11.16.text: Four horizontal cell-rows showing the same embedding vector progressing through each compression stage. Row 1 is the float32 baseline with 24 tall cells representing 3072 dims at 32 bits = 12,288 bytes. Row 2 shows MRL truncation keeping the first 8 cells solid and fading the remaining 16 as dashed outlines, totaling 4,096 bytes. Row 3 shows MRL plus SQ where the same 8 cells remain but each cell is one-quarter the height for 8-bit storage, totaling 1,024 bytes. Row 4 shows MRL plus BQ where the same 8 cells become tiny 1-bit slivers totaling 128 bytes. Illustrates that MRL throws away cells from the right while quantization shrinks the height of each remaining cell.

11.17.text: IVF splits space, PQ shrinks each vector. IVF (chapter 11.5) and PQ (chapter 11.14) are the two workhorse compression tools of production vector search. IVF partitions the corpus into clusters so a query only scans a handful of cells instead of all N vectors. PQ replaces each float32 vector with a short byte-code, shrinking memory by 32x. Combined as IVF-PQ they become the dominant index for static billion-scale corpora, the recipe FAISS, Milvus, and many others ship as a default. IVF - cluster the space: k-means partitions the corpus into nlist cells; typical nlist ~ sqrt(N), 4096 at N=1M; at query time, probe the nprobe closest cells; nprobe=8 visits 8/4096 ~ 0.2% of the data. Cost per query ~ nlist + nprobe * (N/nlist). PQ - compress each vector: split a 768-dim vector into m=96 subvectors; k-means per slot gives 256 centroid codebooks; each subvector becomes a 1-byte code id; whole vector = 96 bytes (32x smaller than float32). m bytes per vector vs d*4 bytes for float32. IVF cuts vectors scanned. PQ cuts bytes per vector. Multiply savings: billion-vector query in single-digit ms while fitting in 20 GB RAM.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ combines IVF's cluster-probing with PQ's per-vector byte-coding to enable billion-scale similarity search in single-digit milliseconds and ~20 GB RAM.

11.17.text: Step 1: run IVF k-means on the corpus. Before any queries arrive, run k-means on all N vectors to produce nlist centroids and assign each vector to its nearest centroid. For the 10-doc cat corpus pick nlist=3. Five cat docs collapse into cluster A, two dog docs form cluster B, three other docs form cluster C. Each centroid C_A, C_B, C_C is a 768-dim float vector stored once in memory. Formula: cluster(v) = argmin_j || v - C_j ||. At production scale: N=1M -> nlist=4096; N=100M -> nlist=32768. Each vector now has an address: a single cluster id (4 bytes) telling the index which cell it lives in. The centroid is a reusable reference point, about to become the anchor for how each vector is stored in the cell.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ step 1 partitions the corpus with k-means into nlist cells, assigning each vector a 4-byte cluster id pointing to its nearest centroid.
queries: What does k-means do in IVF-PQ?
How many centroids in IVF for 1M vectors?

How is each vector indexed in IVF?
How big is a cluster id?
Walking through IVF k-means with cat corpus
What does IVF do before queries arrive?

11.17.text: Step 2: residual = vector - centroid. Instead of storing each vector as-is, IVF-PQ subtracts off the cluster centroid first. The leftover piece - the residual - captures how the document differs from its neighborhood. Because every doc in cluster A is already near C_A, its residual has much smaller magnitude than the original vector. Residuals across the cluster concentrate in a tight ball around zero, exactly the distribution PQ compresses well. Formula: residual_i = v_i - centroid_cluster(i). Worked example on three cluster-A docs with centroid C_A = [0.78, 0.15, 0.47, 0.25]: Doc 1 v=[0.81,0.12,0.45,0.22] -> residual [+0.03,-0.03,-0.02,-0.03]; Doc 3 v=[0.76,0.18,0.52,0.31] -> residual [-0.02,+0.03,+0.05,+0.06]; Doc 4 v=[0.72,0.22,0.48,0.26] -> residual [-0.06,+0.07,+0.01,+0.01]. Originals span 0.12 to 0.83. Residuals span only -0.07 to +0.07, about 10x tighter. Residuals cluster tighter than raw vectors, so PQ codebooks can pack the same accuracy into fewer bits.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ step 2 stores each vector as a residual (v - centroid), shrinking the value range ~10x so PQ can quantize more accurately.
queries: What is a residual in IVF-PQ?
Why subtract the centroid before PQ?
Why are residuals tighter than raw vectors?
How does residual coding improve PQ accuracy?
How tight is the residual distribution?
What is the leftover after centroid subtraction?
Why store v - C_A instead of v

11.17.text: Step 3: run PQ on the residuals. Apply product quantization to the residuals - not the raw vectors. Same PQ recipe from chapter 11.14 (m=96 subvectors, 256 centroids per slot, 8-bit codes), fit to the narrower residual distribution. Because every residual lives in a tiny ball around zero, per-slot k-means clusters are smaller and quantization error drops. Raw PQ (no residuals): encode v directly with m=96, 256 codes; codebook must cover wide spread 0 to 1; per-slot clusters loose, error accumulates. Recall@10 ~ 0.89. Residual PQ (IVF-PQ): encode (v - C_cluster) with m=96, 256 codes; codebook only covers the residual ball; tighter per-slot k-means, lower error per code. Recall@10 ~ 0.94. Each stored vector = cluster id (4 bytes) + residual PQ code (96 bytes). reconstruct(code) = C_cluster + decode_PQ(code) -> approximate original. FAISS, Milvus, Weaviate default to residual PQ when building IVF indexes: same byte budget, meaningfully higher recall, identical query path.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ step 3 applies product quantization to residuals (not raw vectors), lifting recall@10 from ~0.89 to ~0.94 at the same byte budget.
queries: Why does IVF-PQ quantize residuals not raw vectors?
Recall difference between raw PQ and residual PQ
How does residual PQ improve recall?
How is the original vector approximated?
Why FAISS defaults to residual PQ
Bytes per vector with cluster id and PQ code

11.17.text: Search: probe nprobe clusters, scan PQ codes. At query time the index does two passes. First it compares the query to every centroid (nlist dot products) and keeps the nprobe closest cells. Then for each probed cell it walks PQ codes: build an asymmetric-distance lookup table from the query, then for every code sum m table lookups to estimate distance. Bulk of work is second pass; scan is cache-friendly and code footprint tiny. Query walks two cells (nprobe=2): query in upper-left, orange arrow points to closest centroid C_A, cells A and B highlighted as probed, cluster C dimmed as skipped. Per-query cost at N=1M, nlist=4096, nprobe=8: Step 1 scan 4096 centroids; Step 2 build m*256 = 24576 entry asymmetric lookup table once per query; Step 3 scan nprobe*(N/nlist) = 8*244 ~ 2000 codes; each code is m=96 table lookups + adds. Brute force over 1M = 1,000,000 distances. IVF-PQ ~ 6,000 cheap code ops, ~150x faster, tuneable via nprobe. Raising nprobe from 8 to 32 scans 4x more codes but lifts recall a few points. Nprobe is the latency-vs-recall dial at query time.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ search probes the nprobe nearest cells, builds an asymmetric lookup table once, and scans ~2000 PQ codes for ~150x speedup over brute force.
queries: How does IVF-PQ search work at query time?
What is the asymmetric distance lookup table?
Why nprobe controls latency vs recall
How fast is IVF-PQ vs brute force at 1M?
How many codes are scanned per query?

Effect of raising nprobe from 8 to 32

11.17.text: 20 bytes per vector at N=1B. At billion-vector scale, production IVF-PQ drops residual PQ subvector count to m=16 (residuals are easier to quantize than raw vectors, so fewer subvectors still hit the recall target). Add the 4-byte cluster id every vector needs and per-vector footprint settles at 20 bytes. A billion vectors occupy 20 GB - comfortably one server - vs 3 TB for float32 or 96 GB for raw PQ at m=96. Bytes per vector: cluster id (4 bytes) + residual PQ code (m=16 * 1 byte) = 20 bytes per vector. Memory at N=1B (d=768): Float32 baseline 3 TB (d*4*N); Raw PQ m=96 96 GB (no cluster structure); IVF-PQ m=16 residual 20 GB (20*10^9 bytes). Where IVF-PQ ships as default: FAISS IndexIVFPQ (Meta's library, the original); Milvus IVF_PQ (distributed billion-scale); Weaviate PQ + IVF (toggleable on top of HNSW or flat); Vespa HNSW + PQ (same residual recipe on a graph). IVF for cluster structure. PQ for per-vector byte budget. Residuals for recall boost. Together: sweet spot for static, read-heavy, billion-scale search on a single machine.
11.17.summary: At billion-scale IVF-PQ uses m=16 PQ codes plus 4-byte cluster id for 20 bytes per vector, fitting 1B vectors in 20 GB on one server.

Production parameters for IVF-PQ at scale

11.17.text: IVF-PQ chapter summary: IVF-PQ layers IVF clustering on top of residual product quantization. Step 1 runs k-means with nlist centroids (~sqrt(N)) and assigns each vector to its nearest cell. Step 2 stores residuals (v - centroid) instead of raw vectors, concentrating values into a tight ball around zero. Step 3 runs PQ (m=96 subvectors, 256 centroids each, 8-bit codes) on the residuals, where the tighter distribution yields recall@10 ~0.94 vs 0.89 for raw PQ. Search probes only the nprobe nearest cells via an asymmetric distance lookup table, scanning ~2000 codes for a 150x speedup over brute force at N=1M. At N=1B with m=16, each vector takes just 20 bytes (4-byte cluster id + 16-byte PQ code), enabling 1B vectors in 20 GB on a single server. IVF-PQ is the default for FAISS IndexIVFPQ, Milvus IVF_PQ, Weaviate, and Vespa for static read-heavy billion-scale similarity search.
11.17.summary: IVF-PQ combines IVF cell-probing with residual product quantization to deliver billion-scale similarity search at 20 bytes per vector and ~150x speedup.

11.17.text: Diagram 1: Cat-corpus 2D scatter partitioned into three IVF clusters after k-means with nlist=3, arranged as a balanced inverted triangle. Five cat docs form cluster A around the purple centroid square C_A in the upper-left, two dog docs form cluster B around the yellow centroid C_B in the upper-right, three other docs form cluster C around the cyan centroid C_C in the lower-center. Illustrates step 1 of IVF-PQ which clusters the space first. Diagram 2: IVF-PQ search trajectory on the same 10-doc cat corpus inverted triangle. Query vector sits in the upper-left, an orange arrow points toward the closest centroid C_A, the two nearest cells A and B in the top row are highlighted as probed while cluster C at the bottom is dimmed as skipped. Illustrates nprobe=2 where only the two nearest clusters get their PQ codes scanned.

11.18.text: Chapter 11.18 covers HNSW + PQ: a production-default combo for 100M-1B scale similarity search. Keep the multi-layer HNSW small-world graph (M=16 edges, ef_search candidates, log N hops) but swap each node's float32 vector payload for a 96-byte PQ code. Memory drops from ~3 KB to ~196 bytes per vector (graph edges ~100 B + PQ code 96 B). Distance computation switches from float32 dot product to asymmetric PQ lookup: 96 table reads + 96 adds per pair, ~10x faster. Recall drops 1-5% vs float32 HNSW; raising ef_search from 50 to 150 recovers most of the gap. Shipped as a config flag in Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, FAISS.
11.18.summary: Chapter-level overview of HNSW + PQ as the modern production default for 100M-1B scale vector search.

11.18.text: HNSW gives fast search; PQ gives small vectors. IVF-PQ wins on static billion-scale corpora but many production systems want graph-navigation speed AND compressed vectors. HNSW (chapters 11.7-11.10) gives logarithmic-hop search over a multi-layer small-world graph. PQ (chapter 11.14) gives 32x compression per vector. HNSW + PQ keeps the HNSW graph structure unchanged and swaps out the float32 payload at each node for a PQ code. HNSW: multi-layer small-world graph over all N vectors, O(log N) hops from entry point to the beam, M = 16 edges per node at layer 0 (default), ef_search = 50 candidates at query time. PQ: m = 96 subvectors, 256 centroids per slot, each vector encoded as m bytes (96 bytes at m = 96), 32x smaller than float32 baseline, distances via asymmetric lookup table. HNSW graph structure (edges, layers, M, ef_search) = unchanged. Node payload: float32 vector becomes PQ code (96 bytes instead of 3,072). Search algorithm is byte-for-byte the same: enter at top hub, greedy-descend, beam-search at layer zero. Only distance function changes: it consults PQ codes instead of float32 vectors.
11.18.summary: Motivates HNSW + PQ as combining HNSW's fast graph navigation with PQ's 32x per-vector compression, leaving the search algorithm unchanged.
queries: what does HNSW + PQ change about search

production search at billion scale on one server

11.18.text: Three-layer HNSW graph with PQ codes at every node. Picture the 3-layer HNSW graph from chapter 11.7. In HNSW + PQ every node label changes from a float32 vector to a 96-byte PQ code like [c17, c203, c89, ...]. Edges, layer assignments, entry points - all untouched. Bottom layer (cyan) holds all eight docs with dense edges. Middle layer (yellow) holds three hub nodes with long-range edges. Top layer (red) has a single global entry-point node. Vertical green dashed lines are layer-to-layer links. Memory math: graph costs stay near 100 bytes per vector (M = 16 edges at layer 0 plus sparse upper layers), payload drops from 3,072 to 96 bytes. Per-vector memory = graph edges (~100 bytes) + PQ code (96 bytes) = 196 bytes. At N = 100M: 196 * 10^8 bytes = 19.6 GB - fits on a single server. Compare with float32 HNSW at 100M: 300 GB for vectors + 10 GB for graph = 310 GB, unworkable on one machine. HNSW + PQ drops the bill to 19.6 GB without touching the search algorithm.
11.18.summary: Visualizes the three-layer HNSW graph with PQ-coded nodes and computes the 196-byte-per-vector memory budget.

11.18.text: Distance = asymmetric PQ lookup. HNSW walks the graph exactly as before. At every step it has to score a candidate against the query - that distance call is the only piece that changes. Float32 distance baseline: dot(q, v) = Sum q[i] * v[i], d = 768 multiplies + 768 adds, AVX-512 about 48 fused-multiply-adds per pair. Asymmetric PQ distance: dist(q, code) = Sum LUT_slot[code[slot]], m = 96 table lookups + 96 adds, table built once per query (m * 256 = 24,576 entries), about 10x faster per distance. HNSW hop count ~= log_M(N) * ef_search. Per-hop cost: float32 -> ~10x cheaper PQ lookup. Net: end-to-end query latency drops by a factor near 10x at common settings. The lookup table holds query-to-centroid distances for every slot. Once it is built, every doc in the graph is just m table reads and m adds away from a full estimated distance. Graph hop count is identical, per-hop cost drops.
11.18.summary: Replaces float32 dot products at each HNSW hop with asymmetric PQ table lookups, yielding ~10x faster per-hop distance.

11.18.text: PQ adds distance error; bigger ef_search recovers recall. PQ distances are approximations. Most of the time the ranking is right but a small fraction of pairs get re-ordered - error is typically 1-4% at m = 96, more at smaller m. On a graph, a ranking slip means HNSW's greedy descent occasionally takes a slightly wrong hop. At fixed ef_search = 50 the recall drop is noticeable but modest. Raising ef_search widens the candidate beam at layer zero and most of the lost recall comes back. Recall@10 vs ef_search at M = 16: ef_search=50: HNSW float32 = 0.97, HNSW + PQ = 0.92 (PQ drops 5%). ef_search=100: 0.98 vs 0.94 (raising ef starts helping). ef_search=150: 0.99 vs 0.96 (nearly recovered). ef_search=200: 0.99 vs 0.97 (matches float within 2%). Formula: Recall_HNSW+PQ ~= Recall_HNSW - 1% to 5% (varies with PQ m). Dial: raise ef_search from 50 to 150 to recover most of the gap. Smaller m (more aggressive compression) widens the gap - tune m and ef_search together against your recall target.
11.18.summary: Quantifies PQ-induced recall loss in HNSW (1-5%) and shows raising ef_search from 50 to 150 nearly recovers float32-level recall.

11.18.text: Production deployments of HNSW + PQ. HNSW + PQ is now the default answer for 100M-1B scale production search when filtering, updates, and low-latency matter. Every major self-hostable vector database exposes it as a configuration flag on top of a vanilla HNSW index - same graph code, same search, a toggle for compression. Where HNSW + PQ ships today: Qdrant uses quantization_config.product (scalar, product, and binary on top of HNSW). Weaviate uses VectorIndexConfig.pq.enabled: true (PQ or BQ layered on HNSW, trainable centroids). Milvus offers HNSW_PQ / HNSW_SQ index types (both scalar and product quantization flavors). pgvector currently has scalar quantization + HNSW (SQ live today; PQ on the roadmap). HNSW + PQ is the modern production default for 100M-1B scale similarity search. One graph engine, one compression switch. Start with plain HNSW for quick prototyping, flip on PQ when memory bites. The API surface barely changes; the memory footprint drops an order of magnitude.
11.18.summary: Lists production vector databases shipping HNSW + PQ as a config toggle and frames it as the modern default at 100M-1B scale.

11.19.text: Five compression techniques were introduced over the prior chapters: scalar quantization, product quantization, binary quantization, Matryoshka truncation, IVF-PQ, and HNSW+PQ. Each works; none is universally right. The choice depends on four inputs. (1) Corpus size N is the primary driver - under 1M nothing helps, at 100M+ compression is not optional. (2) Embedding dimensionality d gates binary quantization, which needs d >= 768 to stay production-safe per chapter 11.15's recall table. (3) Database capability constrains the menu: pgvector offers halfvec only, Pinecone abstracts the choice, Qdrant/Weaviate expose the full suite plus rescore. (4) Recall tolerance is the override knob - high-stakes retrieval (medical, legal) downgrades one step from the default.
11.19.summary: Four inputs (corpus size N, embedding dim d, DB capability, recall tolerance) decide which of the five compression techniques to pick.
queries: how do I choose a vector compression method
which quantization should I use for my vector DB
SQ vs PQ vs BQ vs Matryoshka which one
how to pick between scalar product binary quantization
does corpus size matter for compression
why does embedding dimension matter for binary quantization
do I need to quantize at 500K vectors

11.19.text: The decision tree starts with one orthogonal pre-step: if the embedding model is Matryoshka-trained (OpenAI text-embedding-3 series, BGE-M3, some Cohere variants), request a smaller dim at the API call - truncating 3072 to 1536 or 1024 costs about 1% quality and halves every downstream memory number. Then walk the main tree, branching on N: N < 1M -> skip quantization, run HNSW + fp32. 1M - 10M -> Scalar Quantization (int8, 4x). 10M - 100M -> Binary Quantization + rescore, gated on d >= 768 AND DB supports it; otherwise downgrade to Scalar Q. N >= 100M -> HNSW + Product Quantization, the scale default. Override rule: if recall must exceed 99%, downgrade one step (BQ -> SQ; SQ -> skip; PQ -> raise m). Database capability gate: Qdrant and Weaviate support SQ/PQ/BQ+rescore fully. Milvus supports SQ/PQ fully and BQ partially. pgvector supports halfvec only - no PQ, no BQ. Pinecone abstracts compression entirely; the only knob is MRL at embed time.
11.19.summary: The compression flowchart: optional MRL pre-step, then branch on N (skip / SQ / BQ+rescore / HNSW+PQ) with d>=768 and DB-capability gates plus a recall-tolerance override.
queries: when to use HNSW plus PQ
what should I do under 1M vectors
what should I do at 100M vectors

11.19.text: Four worked scenarios walking the same tree with real numbers. Scenario 1 - startup skip path: Qdrant + OpenAI-3-large (d=3072) + N=500K. N < 1M gate hits immediately. Result: skip quantization, HNSW + fp32. Math: 500K x 12 KB = 6 GB RAM, fits any dev box. Scenario 2 - mid-scale SQ default: Qdrant + BGE-large-en-v1.5 (d=1024, not MRL) + N=5M. N in 1M-10M -> SQ default. Result: HNSW + SQ (int8). Fp32 baseline = 5M x 1024 x 4 B = ~20 GB. Final = 5M x 1024 B = ~5 GB. 4x smaller, ~1% recall loss. Scenario 3 - growing product high-leverage path: Qdrant + OpenAI-3-large (d truncated 3072 -> 1536 via MRL) + N=50M. MRL halves d up front; N in 10M-100M; d>=768; Qdrant supports BQ+rescore. Result: HNSW + MRL + BQ + rescore. Fp32 baseline = 300 GB. Final = 50M x 192 B = ~10 GB. ~30x smaller, <2% recall loss. Scenario 4 - massive scale HNSW+PQ default: Qdrant + OpenAI-3-small (d truncated 1536 -> 1024 via MRL) + N=200M. N >= 100M gate. Result: HNSW + PQ (m=96). Fp32 = 820 GB. Final = 200M x 96 B = ~19 GB. ~40x smaller. Scenario 1 is the do-nothing case engineers talk themselves out of - it's the right answer more often than the literature suggests.
11.19.summary: Four worked scenarios from 500K to 200M vectors apply the decision tree end-to-end, showing memory math at each scale.
queries: memory savings from MRL plus BQ
memory savings from HNSW plus PQ

11.19.text: Five rules of thumb plus four traps form the design-review checklist. Rules: (1) Don't quantize until memory bites - under 1M vectors complexity isn't worth it. (2) MRL is free; always apply it first if the model supports it - halves downstream memory. (3) DB first, compression second - pick DB for ops/filter/SLA reasons, then pick compression from that DB's menu. (4) Rescoring is nearly free; turn it on by default - BQ without rescore loses 5-10% recall, with rescore <1%, cost is one extra disk read per top-k. (5) Measure recall on your own data before committing - generic benchmark numbers assume generic distributions. Traps: (1) BQ at d <= 256 - recall collapses to ~0.82 or worse per 11.15's table. (2) Skipping MRL when available - leaves free compression on the table; OpenAI-3 at d=3072 with no truncation wastes half the budget. (3) Stacking SQ+PQ+BQ without measuring - tuning surface compounds. (4) Trusting recall numbers that silently disable rescoring - BQ-without-rescore is a different product than BQ-with-rescore.
11.19.summary: Design checklist: five rules of thumb (skip under 1M, apply MRL, DB first, rescore on, measure on your own data) and four traps (BQ at low d, skipping MRL, stacking without measuring, hidden rescore in benchmarks).
queries: why turn rescore on by default
measure recall on your own data
binary quantization recall collapse low d

11.19.text: Chapter 11.19 turns the prior compression chapters (SQ, PQ, BQ, Matryoshka, IVF-PQ, HNSW+PQ) into a single decision framework. Four inputs decide: corpus size N, embedding dim d, database capability, and recall tolerance. An optional MRL pre-step truncates the embedding at the API call. The main tree branches on N: under 1M skip; 1M-10M scalar quantization; 10M-100M binary quantization + rescore (gated on d>=768 and DB support, else downgrade to SQ); 100M+ HNSW+PQ. A recall-tolerance override downgrades one step if recall must exceed 99%. Four worked scenarios (500K, 5M, 50M, 200M) walk the tree with real memory math. Five rules of thumb (don't quantize early, MRL is free, DB first, rescore default, measure on your own data) and four traps (BQ at low d, skipping MRL, blind stacking, hidden rescore in benchmarks) finish the chapter.
11.19.summary: Chapter-level summary: a four-input decision framework folds the five compression techniques into one tree, validated by four scenarios and a checklist of rules and traps.
queries: MRL pre step plus N branch
rules and traps for vector compression
decision framework SQ PQ BQ MRL
picking compression for production vector DB

11.19.text: Compression-technique decision flowchart. A top input box lists the four inputs (N, d, DB, recall tolerance). An orthogonal MRL pre-step on the left truncates Matryoshka-trained embeddings at the API before the main flow. The main decision flow branches on N into four size buckets: under 1M skip (HNSW + fp32), 1M to 10M scalar quantization (int8, 4x), 10M to 100M binary quantization + rescore gated on d >= 768 and DB support (else downgrade to SQ), 100M or more HNSW + product quantization as the scale default. A fallback rule covers the BQ branch and a recall-tolerance override downgrades one step when recall must exceed 99%.
11.19.summary: SVG flowchart of the compression decision tree showing inputs, MRL pre-step, N-based branches, BQ fallback, and the recall override.

11.1.text: Chapter 11.1 introduces the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database: given N pre-computed embedding vectors and one query vector, return the top-k most similar. It establishes the running cat corpus (10 documents, 4-dim illustrative vectors, query 'Information about cats'), shows that brute-force scan costs N times d multiplications per query (about 1.5 trillion at N = 1 billion, d = 1536), surveys where vector search shows up (semantic search, recommendation, image retrieval, duplicate detection, anomaly detection, RAG), and reframes Section 11 as a systems problem - storage, indexing, retrieval - rather than a training problem. The embeddings already exist; the work now is the index.
11.1.summary: Overview of chapter 11.1: the top-k nearest-neighbor retrieval problem, the cat corpus, scaling pain, where it appears, and the systems-not-training framing.

How does a vector database find similar documents

What is top-k nearest neighbor search

What does a vector database actually do

Cat corpus example for vector search
Systems problem vs model problem in AI

11.1.text:
From embeddings to a retrieval problem. Back in chapter 5.2 the transformer turned every word into an embedding - a dense vector capturing meaning. Now picture a library: 10 documents, each pre-encoded into its own vector. A user types a query. How do we find documents whose meaning matches?

Query: 'Information about cats', vector [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21].

Stored database: 10 documents, each with a 4-dim embedding:
#1 Cats are small domesticated carnivores [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22]
#2 Dogs are loyal pets [0.33, 0.68, 0.29, 0.41]
#3 Lions are big cats that live in Africa [0.76, 0.18, 0.52, 0.31]
#4 My cat sat on the mat [0.72, 0.22, 0.48, 0.26]
#5 Tigers are striped cats [0.79, 0.15, 0.41, 0.28]
#6 Python is a programming language [0.09, 0.88, 0.12, 0.74]
#7 Kittens grow up to be cats [0.83, 0.10, 0.47, 0.19]
#8 The dog chased the cat [0.55, 0.44, 0.36, 0.39]
#9 Birds can fly [0.18, 0.31, 0.74, 0.62]
#10 Fish live underwater [0.21, 0.29, 0.68, 0.81]

Real embeddings have 768 or 1536 dimensions instead of 4, and production databases hold billions of rows instead of 10. The shape of the problem is the same.
11.1.summary: Sets up retrieval as finding documents whose embedding vectors match a query vector, using a 10-doc cat corpus with 4-dim vectors as the running example.
queries: How are documents stored in a vector database
What does a query vector look like
Why use 4 dimensional vectors as illustration
How many dimensions do real embeddings have
How does meaning become a number
What are the example sentences in chapter 11
How does query relate to stored vectors
What size are production embedding databases

11.1.text:
Find the 10 most similar documents. The core retrieval task: one query vector, N stored vectors, return the top-10 nearest. For the tiny 10-doc corpus, top-10 is everything, so the diagram highlights top-3 (docs 1, 3, 7 - all cat-related) to make 'closest' visible. The real problem scales: hundreds of millions of vectors, top-10 closest in milliseconds.

Formal statement:
  Given: N stored vectors V = {v_1, v_2, ..., v_N}
  Given: one query vector q
  Return: the k vectors in V most similar to q

This is the retrieval problem at the heart of every vector database. k is usually 10 or 20 - we want a shortlist, not the whole library.
11.1.summary: Defines the formal top-k retrieval task: given N stored vectors V and a query q, return the k most similar; k is typically 10 or 20.
queries: How do you define nearest neighbor search mathematically
What does k mean in top-k
Given N vectors return k closest
What is the query vector q
Set V of stored vectors notation
How big is k in production
Why highlight only top-3 in 10 doc demo

11.1.text: Retrieval diagram: yellow query box on the left labeled 'Query - about cats' with an arrow into a cloud of ten numbered document dots. The three dots closest to the query (documents 1, 3, and 7 - all cat-related sentences) are highlighted green as the top-3 nearest neighbors. The remaining seven dots are dimmed. Caption: 'Top-3 closest (highlighted): docs 1, 3, 7 - all cat-related.' In a 10-doc corpus, top-10 would be everything, so top-3 is highlighted to make 'closest' visible.
11.1.summary: SVG diagram showing query on the left with arrow into a cloud of 10 doc dots; the cat-related docs 1, 3, 7 are highlighted as top-3 nearest neighbors.
queries: What does the retrieval diagram look like
Yellow query box arrow into document cloud

11.1.text:
Now make it one billion. With 10 docs the naive approach - compare the query against every vector - is instant. Real systems have millions or billions of vectors and the cost of 'check every vector' explodes.

Toy corpus: N = 10, scan ~0.01 ms (this chapter)
Wikipedia-scale: N = 1,000,000 (all English Wikipedia chunked), scan ~500 ms
Internet-scale: N = 1,000,000,000 (web-scale search index), scan ~500,000 ms (~8 min)

Naive cost = N times d multiplications per query
At N = 1 billion, d = 1536: ~1.5 trillion multiplies per query

Checking every vector works for 10 docs. At 1B it is an 8-minute wait per search. Users expect answers in tens of milliseconds.
11.1.summary: Scales the brute-force scan cost from 10 docs (0.01 ms) through Wikipedia (500 ms) to 1 billion (8 minutes), giving the formula N times d multiplications per query.
queries: How long does brute force search take at 1 billion vectors
Naive cost formula N times d
Why does scanning every vector explode at scale
Internet scale 1 billion vectors search time
How many ms to scan 1 million vectors
Why is brute force 8 minutes at 1B
Toy corpus vs Wikipedia vs internet

11.1.text:
Where vector search shows up. The same retrieval setup - query vector, big pile of stored vectors, top-k - powers dozens of production systems.

Semantic search: User types 'how to fix leaky faucet'. System returns pages about plumbing repair even if those words never appear verbatim.

Recommendation: Spotify embeds every song. Given your listening history vector, find songs with nearby vectors. Netflix does it for shows, Amazon for products.

Image retrieval: Upload a photo of a red sneaker. CLIP encodes it, finds catalog images with similar vectors. Reverse image search, visual shopping.

Duplicate detection: Near-identical images on social platforms, plagiarized paragraphs in documents, duplicate bug reports. Vectors collapse minor differences.

Anomaly detection: Embed every credit-card transaction. Points far from any cluster are flagged as fraud. Works for network intrusions and medical outliers too.

RAG retrieval: An LLM is asked a question. Before answering, embed the question, find the top-5 most relevant documents, paste them into context. Grounded answers.

Different surfaces, identical plumbing. Every one of these systems runs a top-k nearest-neighbor query over a giant bag of vectors.
11.1.summary: Lists six production applications - semantic search, recommendation, image retrieval, duplicate detection, anomaly detection, RAG - all powered by the same top-k vector retrieval.
queries: What is vector search used for
How does CLIP do image retrieval
How does fraud detection use embeddings
Reverse image search how it works

11.1.text:
A systems problem, not a model problem. The earlier sections were about learning - teaching a model to produce good embeddings. That work is already done. Now the embeddings exist. The job is storage, indexing, and retrieval. No gradients, no loss functions, no backprop. Just systems design.

Training time (done): The model learned an embedding space.
  Forward pass
  loss = cross_entropy(...)
  Backward pass
  Weights -= lr times grad
Output: a function that maps text to vectors.

Query time (what this section is about): We search the space the model built.
  Encode query -> q
  index.search(q, k=10)
  return top-10 docs
  < 50 ms per query
The embeddings are fixed inputs. The work is the index.

This is a systems problem. Not a training problem. The rest of Section 11 is about storage layouts, index data structures, approximate algorithms, and the latency/recall/memory tradeoffs they force.
11.1.summary: Reframes Section 11: training built the embedding space; query time is a systems job - storage, indexing, retrieval - with latency/recall/memory tradeoffs, no gradients.

What is the work of the index

11.3.text: Chapter 11.3 introduces the three-way tradeoff triangle for vector databases: recall (fraction of true top-k neighbors returned), latency (wall-clock time per query), and memory (bytes to hold the index). Every algorithmic choice - indexing structure, similarity metric, compression, sharding, replication - moves along an edge of this triangle, improving one corner at the cost of another. Recall@k = |ANN_results ∩ true_top_k| / k. Production targets 0.95-0.99 recall, P50/P95/P99 latencies in tens of ms, and bytes-per-vector around 3 KB at d=768 plus ~100 B HNSW graph overhead. Concrete tradeoffs: raising ef_search lifts recall but adds latency; PQ compression cuts memory 32x but drops recall; replicas cut tail latency but multiply RAM. Indexing buys latency with memory, quantization buys memory with recall, sharding/filtering touches all three.
11.3.summary: Chapter overview: vector-database design balances recall, latency, and memory on a tradeoff triangle where every technique pushes one corner at another's cost.
queries: what is the three-way tradeoff in vector databases
recall vs latency vs memory tradeoff
how do vector databases balance speed accuracy memory
what does the vector database tradeoff triangle mean
why can't a vector index optimize all three of recall latency memory
