how do replicas affect memory and tail latency
why is memory the dominant cost in vector DB
what is P99 latency in vector search
how big is a 768-dim float32 vector
tradeoffs between indexing quantization and sharding

11.3.text: Every decision a vector database makes - which index, which similarity function, how aggressively to compress, how many machines - bends on three axes: Recall (top vertex, of true nearest neighbors how many we returned, 1.00 perfect 0.80 missed a fifth), Latency (bottom-left, wall-clock time per query, users feel 10ms vs 500ms as snappy versus laggy), Memory (bottom-right, bytes to hold the index, RAM is the single biggest cost in a vector DB bill). Pick any corner and the other two push back. Every tradeoff in the section is a move along one edge of the triangle.
11.3.summary: Introduces the three axes - recall, latency, memory - that every vector-DB design decision bends on.
queries: what are the three axes of vector database tradeoffs
what does the recall vertex mean
what does latency mean in vector search
what does memory mean in vector index
why is RAM the biggest cost in vector DB
intro to tradeoff triangle for ANN
three corners of vector database design
why every choice trades one axis for another

11.3.text: Recall@k is the formal score for how good an approximate search is. Run brute force to get the ground truth - the k documents actually closest to the query. Then run the fast method and ask: of the k it returned, how many matched the true set? Formula: Recall@k = |ANN_results ∩ true_top_k| / k. Size of the intersection divided by k. 1.0 means the ANN result is identical to brute force. Concrete example with query 'information about cats' and k=10: true top-10 are docs 1,3,7,5,4,2,8,9,6,10. ANN returns docs 1,3,7,5,4,2,8,9,6, and one impostor (doc 11). Nine of ten slots match. Recall@10 = 9/10 = 0.9 (90%). Production targets are usually 0.95 to 0.99. A recall of 0.90 means one neighbor in ten is wrong - often acceptable for search, rarely acceptable for fraud detection.
11.3.summary: Defines Recall@k as the intersection of ANN results with the true top-k divided by k, with a worked 9/10 example.
queries: how do you measure recall in ANN
how is approximate search accuracy measured
how to compute recall of vector search
why brute force gives ground truth
how to evaluate vector index quality
what is impostor in top-k results

11.3.text: Latency is the wall-clock time from query in to top-k out. On 1 million vectors, brute force takes about 100 ms - slow enough to feel. A tuned HNSW index on the same data answers in about 1 ms. Two orders of magnitude for the cost of a recall sliver. Query latency at N=1,000,000 (log scale): HNSW tuned ANN ~1 ms, brute force ~100 ms, disk-backed scan ~1000 ms (1 sec). Production cares about the tail not the mean. Engineers say P50, P95, P99 - the 50th, 95th, 99th percentiles. P50 is the typical query, P99 is the slowest 1%. Example latencies: P50 = 8 ms (typical), P95 = 25 ms (5% are slower), P99 = 80 ms (the tail). For a search API handling thousands of queries per second, P99 is what users notice when the page stalls. A good median with a bad P99 still feels broken.
11.3.summary: Latency is wall-clock query time; HNSW hits 1 ms vs brute force 100 ms on 1M vectors, and production tracks P50/P95/P99 percentiles, not means.
queries: what is query latency in vector search
why use percentile latency instead of average
how slow is brute force at 1 million vectors
what does the tail of latency look like

11.3.text: Memory is the cost that scales brutally. A single embedding at d=768 stored as float32 is 768·4 = 3,072 bytes = 3 KB per vector. bytes per vector = d · sizeof(float32). Multiplied by N the index can be bigger than the dataset it came from. Memory grows linearly with N: N=10 vectors 30 KB + HNSW graph 1 KB = 31 KB total; N=1,000,000 vectors 3 GB + graph 100 MB = 3.1 GB; N=100,000,000 vectors 300 GB + graph 10 GB = 310 GB; N=1,000,000,000 vectors 3 TB + graph 100 GB = 3.1 TB. HNSW overhead column matters because a vector is not the only thing the index stores - HNSW keeps a graph of edges between vectors, roughly 32 neighbor pointers per node, about 100 bytes of graph overhead per vector. At 1 billion that overhead alone is 100 GB. Indexing algorithms and compression schemes live or die on this number. 3 KB sounds tiny until you multiply by a billion.
11.3.summary: Memory at d=768 float32 is 3 KB per vector plus ~100 B HNSW graph overhead, scaling linearly to TBs at billion-vector scale.
queries: how many bytes per vector at 768 dimensions
how big is a vector index at 1 billion
why is memory the costly axis

11.3.text: The three axes are linked - tightening one corner loosens another. Three concrete production levers: (1) Raise ef_search to chase recall - HNSW knob controls how many candidate nodes to explore per query. Bigger number = more exploration = higher recall, but linearly more work so latency rises. ef_search=200 gives 0.99 recall, ef_search=50 gives 0.95 recall at 4x speed. Recall up → Latency up. (2) Add PQ compression to shrink memory - Product Quantization replaces each 3 KB vector with ~96 bytes of codebook indices (small integer codes that look up an approximate version of the real vector), 32x memory win. Stored vectors are approximate, distances are approximate, recall drops. Typical cost: 0.98 recall becomes 0.94. Memory down → Recall down. (3) Add replicas to cut tail latency - run three copies of the index on three machines, route each query to the least-loaded one. P99 drops because no single node gets a long queue, but you pay 3x the RAM. Caching frequent queries is a variant. Latency down → Memory cost up. Three different knobs, three different axes, same shape of answer. Nothing is free.
11.3.summary: Three production examples - ef_search, PQ compression, replicas - each push one triangle corner at the documented cost of another.
queries: how does ef_search trade recall for latency
what does PQ compression cost in recall
how do replicas reduce tail latency
why nothing is free in vector index tradeoffs
how to lower P99 with replicas
examples of axis tradeoffs in ANN
what knob to turn for higher recall

11.3.text: Every technique in a vector database is a labeled arrow on the tradeoff triangle. Indexing algorithms (Flat / IVF / HNSW) are different shapes of graph or partition - they buy latency at the cost of memory (the graph edges) and a small recall discount. Quantization (Scalar / Product / Binary) shrinks bytes per vector by 4x to 32x, buying memory at the cost of recall. Production concerns (filtering, sharding, replicas, cache) push on memory or latency depending on stacking, sometimes at the cost of recall when filters hide neighbors. The triangle is the whole architecture: every algorithm, every knob, every deployment choice is a move along one edge. When an engineer says 'HNSW with PQ32 behind two replicas', they are naming three specific moves on this triangle - one on each axis.
11.3.summary: Recap that every indexing, quantization, and production technique is a labeled move on the triangle, with the architecture's choices reading off one edge at a time.
queries: how do indexing algorithms move on the tradeoff triangle
what does quantization cost on the triangle
how do filtering and sharding push the triangle
HNSW with PQ32 behind two replicas meaning
map of techniques to tradeoff axes
why is the triangle the whole architecture
Flat IVF HNSW comparison on triangle
summarize ANN tradeoffs as triangle moves
how to read a vector DB architecture

11.3.text: Plain tradeoff triangle: equilateral triangle with Recall at the top vertex in cyan, Latency at the bottom-left vertex in orange, and Memory at the bottom-right vertex in yellow. Introduces the three axes every vector-database decision must balance.
11.3.summary: Bare triangle introducing the recall-latency-memory axes.
queries: what does the tradeoff triangle look like
latency bottom left memory bottom right

11.3.text: Annotated tradeoff triangle: equilateral triangle with Recall, Latency, and Memory at the vertices and interior labels mapping each family of techniques to its direction of movement - Indexing trades latency for memory (orange), Quantization trades memory for recall (yellow), Filtering and sharding push on all three corners (purple).
11.3.summary: Annotated triangle showing how indexing, quantization, and filtering/sharding push specific edges.
queries: which techniques map to which triangle edge
filtering sharding push all three corners

11.2.text: The naive approach: compare to everything. Before getting clever, start with the baseline algorithm. Given N stored vectors and one query, the dumbest thing that works is to compare the query against each stored vector, rank them, and return the best k. This is called brute-force k-nearest-neighbors, or brute-force kNN. Three steps: (1) Compute similarity - for every stored vector v_i, compute sim(q, v_i). With cosine similarity that is one dot product plus two norms per doc. (2) Sort - arrange all N scores from highest to lowest. Every document gets a rank from 1 (most similar) to N (least similar). (3) Return top-k - slice off the first k entries of the sorted list. Pseudo-code: scores = [cosine(q, v_i) for v_i in V]; ranked = sort(scores, descending=True); return ranked[:k]. No shortcuts, no index, no approximation. We literally touch every vector in the corpus. That is where the word brute-force comes from - it is the correctness-at-any-cost baseline.
11.2.summary: Brute-force kNN baseline: compute similarity to every vector, sort, return top-k.
queries: why is it called brute force

11.2.text: On 10 docs, brute force is instant. Run the algorithm on the cat corpus. For each doc, compute cosine similarity against the query 'information about cats', sort, take top-3. Cat-related docs float to top, off-topic ones sink. Total work: 10 dot products. Ranked results: #1 doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' cos=0.97 (top-3), #2 doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' cos=0.94 (top-3), #3 doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' cos=0.92 (top-3), #4 doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' cos=0.89, #5 doc 4 'My cat sat on the mat' cos=0.87, #6 doc 8 'The dog chased the cat' cos=0.68, #7 doc 2 'Dogs are loyal pets' cos=0.48, #8 doc 6 'Python is a programming language' cos=0.35, #9 doc 9 'Birds can fly' cos=0.30, #10 doc 10 'Fish live underwater' cos=0.27. This result is exact: we compared to every doc, no doc skipped. The top-3 returned (1, 3, 7) are guaranteed to be the true three most similar. That is the promise of brute force: perfect answers. Wall-clock time on a laptop: well under one millisecond. At N = 10, brute force is the right answer.
11.2.summary: Worked example: brute-force kNN on 10 cat docs produces exact top-3 (docs 1, 3, 7) in under 1 ms.
queries: cosine similarity ranked results cat corpus
wall clock time brute force search

11.2.text: At N = 1,000,000 it is slow but possible. Crank N up to one million vectors. The algorithm does not change - we still compare the query to every vector. But each comparison is no longer trivial because real embeddings are 768-dimensional, not 4-dim. Every dot product is 768 multiplies plus 768 adds. Ops per query = N * d = 1,000,000 * 768 = ~770 million multiply-add operations. Latency comparison: N = 10 (toy corpus) ~0.01 ms; N = 1,000,000 (Wikipedia-scale) ~500 ms. Half a second is slow for an interactive search but still technically possible. You could run this brute force on a single modern CPU and ship it for low-traffic workloads. The algorithm starts to creak, but does not break.
11.2.summary: At N=1M with d=768, brute force needs ~770M multiply-adds and ~500ms per query - slow but feasible.
queries: brute force kNN at 1 million vectors
ops per query N times d
how many multiplies per dot product 768 dim
is brute force feasible at million scale

11.2.text: First, what is FLOPS? FLOPS means FLoating-point OPerations per Second. In plain words: how many math steps (an add, a multiply, a compare) a chip can do in one second. It is the standard way people brag about chip speed. 1 FLOP = one floating-point math step. The expression a*b + c counts as 2 FLOPs (one multiply, one add). A modern GPU does about 10^12 FLOPS/sec - a trillion math steps a second. The chef and the warehouse analogy: chef = chip with chop speed ~10^12/sec (FLOPS, how fast math happens). Warehouse = memory with delivery speed ~50 GB/sec (memory bandwidth, how fast data arrives). Chopping is lightning-fast. Fetching the next vegetable from the warehouse is the slow part. So when we say 'the real killer is not FLOPS, it is memory,' we mean: the chip is waiting on deliveries, not on math.
11.2.summary: FLOPS = floating-point operations per second; chef-and-warehouse analogy shows memory bandwidth, not compute, is the real bottleneck.
queries: floating point operations per second meaning
how many FLOPs in a multiply add
chef and warehouse analogy memory bandwidth
why memory is the bottleneck not compute
chip speed vs data delivery speed
why FLOPS doesn't matter for vector search

11.2.text: Chef and warehouse diagram. A super-fast chef on the left (labeled chef = chip) stands next to a cutting board with chopping marks, connected by a long dashed red arrow labeled 'slow delivery' to a distant warehouse on the right (labeled warehouse = memory). The chef can chop in 1 second but has to wait 10 minutes for each delivery. Two cards underneath: Chop speed ~10^12/sec (FLOPS - how fast math happens), Delivery speed ~50 GB/sec (memory bandwidth - how fast data arrives). The illustration shows fetching ingredients is the real bottleneck, not chopping speed. This is the analogy for why memory bandwidth, not FLOPS, limits brute-force search at billion-vector scale.
11.2.summary: Diagram analogy: a fast chef waiting on slow warehouse deliveries shows memory bandwidth, not FLOPS, bottlenecks vector search.
queries: why FLOPS is not the bottleneck visual
DDR5 bandwidth vs GPU FLOPS diagram

11.2.text: At N = 1 billion it is hopeless. Push N to one billion. Compute cost scales linearly - a thousand times more work than a million. But the real killer is not FLOPS, it is memory. To touch every vector, we have to READ every vector from RAM. Every. Single. Query. Bytes of vector data to read per query: bytes = N * d * sizeof(float32) = 1,000,000,000 * 768 * 4 = 3,072,000,000,000 bytes = 3.072 TB per query. What we need: 3.072 TB read from memory per single user query. What RAM can deliver: ~50 GB/sec, a single DDR5 channel at peak bandwidth. NOT FEASIBLE: 3.072 TB / 50 GB/sec = ~60 seconds per query, just to stream the data past the CPU. Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not FLOPS. Even with infinite compute, brute force at 1 billion is hopeless for interactive search. The data does not even fit in a single machine. Google, Meta, and OpenAI all run indexes at billions of vectors. Brute force is off the table. Something has to give.
11.2.summary: At N=1B, d=768 float32, brute force must stream 3.072 TB per query; ~60 sec at 50 GB/sec - infeasible.
queries: why brute force fails at billion scale
bytes per query formula N d float32
Google Meta OpenAI vector index scale
why we need ANN at billion scale

11.2.text: Give up exactness for speed. The way out is to stop insisting on the EXACT top-k. Instead, return an approximate top-k: a list that is mostly the true neighbors, with the occasional miss. This is the family of algorithms called Approximate Nearest Neighbor, or ANN. How do we measure what we give up? Recall@k = |approx_topk intersect true_topk| / k. Of the k neighbors the approximate method returned, what fraction were in the true top-k? 1.0 = perfect (same as brute force), 0.0 = completely wrong. Comparison: Exact brute force - Recall@k 1.00, latency at 1B ~60 sec (perfect answers, unusable latency). ANN approximate - Recall@k 0.99, latency at 1B ~10 ms (misses 1 neighbor in 100, runs ~6000x faster). The tradeoff is wildly favorable: production ANN algorithms hit 99%+ recall while running 100 to 1000 times faster than brute force. You give up one neighbor in a hundred and gain orders of magnitude of speed. For search, recommendations, and RAG, that is an obvious win - the user never notices the missed result, but they notice the wait.
11.2.summary: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) trades exactness for speed; Recall@k measures the loss; 99% recall at 6000x speedup vs brute force.
queries: what is ANN approximate nearest neighbor
how is recall measured for ANN
tradeoff exactness vs speed vector search
what does recall at k mean

11.2.text: Production-uses-this arrow diagram. A rightward purple arrow labeled 'production uses this' points from the Exact (brute-force) card on the left to the ANN (approximate) card on the right. Left card: Exact brute force - Recall@k 1.00, Latency at 1B ~60 sec, perfect answers but unusable latency. Right card: ANN approximate - Recall@k 0.99, Latency at 1B ~10 ms, misses 1 neighbor in 100 but runs ~6000x faster. The arrow illustrates the tradeoff move from exact slow search to approximate fast search that production systems make.
11.2.summary: Diagram: production-uses-this arrow points from Exact (1.00 recall, 60s) to ANN (0.99 recall, 10ms) cards, showing the production tradeoff.
queries: exact vs approximate vector search comparison
brute force vs ANN tradeoff visual
exact brute force vs approximate diagram
side by side brute force ANN

11.2.text: Chapter 11.2 - Brute-Force kNN. Brute-force kNN is the correctness-at-any-cost baseline: compute similarity from the query to every stored vector, sort, return top-k. At N=10 it is instant (10 dot products, sub-millisecond, exact result docs 1, 3, 7). At N=1M with d=768 it costs ~770M multiply-adds and ~500 ms - slow but feasible. FLOPS (floating-point operations per second, ~10^12 on a modern GPU) is not the bottleneck; memory bandwidth (~50 GB/sec on DDR5) is. The chef-and-warehouse analogy: chip chops in 1 second but waits 10 minutes for each delivery. At N=1B, d=768 float32, every query must stream 3.072 TB of vectors past the CPU - ~60 seconds at 50 GB/sec, infeasible. The solution is to give up exactness: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms return a top-k that is mostly correct. Recall@k = |approx_topk intersect true_topk| / k measures the loss. Production ANN hits 99%+ recall while running 100-1000x faster than brute force. The chapter sets up why every later vector-database technique exists.
11.2.summary: Brute-force kNN is exact but memory-bound; at billion scale it needs 3 TB/query - infeasible - so production trades 1% recall for 6000x speed via ANN.
queries: why brute force fails at scale
FLOPS vs memory bandwidth ANN motivation
exact vs approximate top k tradeoff

11.4.text: Chapter 11.4 covers the three similarity metrics that dominate vector databases: cosine similarity (angle between vectors, range -1 to 1), L2 / Euclidean distance (straight-line distance, smaller is closer), and inner product (raw dot product, fastest to compute). Each measures something different and ranks documents differently. For text embeddings (SBERT, OpenAI, Cohere), cosine is default because the model encodes meaning in direction. For vision/face embeddings (FaceNet), L2 keeps magnitude signal. The key production identity: when vectors are L2-normalized to unit length (||q|| = ||d|| = 1), cosine, inner product, and L2 produce identical rankings. So normalize once at ingest, store unit vectors, and use inner product forever - the fastest possible similarity, no sqrt or divide in the hot loop.
11.4.summary: The three production similarity metrics, when each is appropriate, and the normalization trick that lets inner product replace cosine.
queries: what are the three distance metrics used in vector databases
cosine vs L2 vs inner product similarity
which similarity metric should I use for embeddings
how does cosine similarity compare to dot product
when are cosine and inner product equivalent
why normalize vectors before vector search
what metric for face recognition embeddings
fastest similarity metric for vector search
SBERT and OpenAI use cosine similarity
do all three metrics give same ranking

11.4.text: Three ways to measure similarity. So far everything has used cosine similarity. In production, three metrics dominate: cosine, L2 (Euclidean distance), and inner product (dot product). Each measures a different thing, and picking the right one is the first decision a vector database forces on you. Cosine: (q · d) / (||q|| ||d||) - angle between the vectors, ignores magnitude. L2 (Euclidean): sqrt(Σ (q_i - d_i)^2) - straight-line distance, smaller is closer. Inner product: q · d = Σ q_i · d_i - raw dot product, no normalization, no sqrt. Same corpus, same query - different metrics can rank documents differently. Which metric you pick is a statement about what 'similar' means for your embeddings.
11.4.summary: Overview of the three dominant similarity metrics in vector databases and what each one measures.
queries: what similarity metrics do vector databases support
difference between cosine L2 and dot product
which metric measures angle vs distance
does the metric choice change ranking
what is the simplest dot product
picking a similarity function for embeddings
what does similar mean for vectors

11.4.text: Cosine: the angle between vectors. Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring how long they are. Two vectors that point the same direction score 1.0 regardless of magnitudes. Vectors at 90 degrees score 0. Pointing opposite scores -1. Formula: cos(q, d) = (q · d) / (||q|| · ||d||). Range: [-1, 1]. Concrete values on the cat corpus with query q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21]: Doc 1 (Cats are small...) cos = 0.9993; Doc 7 (Kittens grow up to be cats) cos = 0.9984; Doc 10 (Fish live underwater) cos = 0.6229; Doc 6 (Python is a programming language) cos = 0.3554. Cat docs hug 1.0, random docs slide toward 0. For text embeddings - SBERT, OpenAI, Cohere - cosine is the default. These models are trained so that semantic similarity shows up in the angle, not the length.
11.4.summary: Cosine similarity formula, range, geometric meaning, and concrete scores on the cat corpus showing why text embeddings default to cosine.
queries: what is the cosine similarity formula
what does the angle between vectors measure
semantic similarity in angle not length

11.4.text: Diagram in sub-step 1: Two vectors drawn from the origin - a cyan query vector q and a green document vector d - with a small yellow angle theta between them. The tight angle illustrates that cosine similarity near 1.0 means the vectors point almost the same way. Same trick scales from 2D to 768 dimensions: cos(theta) close to 1 means high similarity.
11.4.summary: Geometric picture of cosine similarity as the angle theta between two vectors from the origin.
queries: what does small theta mean for cosine
arrow diagram of q and d

11.4.text: L2: straight-line distance. L2 distance (also called Euclidean) is the straight-line distance between two points - Pythagoras, but in d dimensions. Unlike cosine, L2 cares about magnitude: two vectors with the same direction but different lengths are far apart. Formula: L2(q, d) = sqrt(Σ_i (q_i - d_i)^2). Smaller is closer (it is a distance, not a similarity). Concrete values on the cat corpus, query q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21]: Doc 1 L2 = 0.047; Doc 7 L2 = 0.057; Doc 10 L2 = 0.922; Doc 6 L2 = 1.228. Cat docs are closest (smallest L2), random docs are far. Magnitude matters - some vision embeddings encode confidence or saliency in the vector's length, and L2 keeps that signal. Face embeddings and image retrieval systems often live in L2 land.
11.4.summary: L2 / Euclidean distance formula, example values on the cat corpus, and why vision/face embeddings prefer L2 over cosine.
queries: when to use L2 instead of cosine

11.4.text: Diagram in sub-step 2: Right triangle with two points q and d in 2D. A horizontal dashed leg labeled (q1 - d1) shows the x-axis difference, a vertical dashed leg labeled (q2 - d2) shows the y-axis difference, and the blue hypotenuse is highlighted as the L2 distance. Two legs squared, sum, take sqrt - same trick scales to d = 768.
11.4.summary: Right-triangle picture of L2 as the hypotenuse, illustrating Pythagoras in 2D extended to high dimensions.
queries: x and y differences as legs
square root of squared differences picture

11.4.text: Inner product: the fastest of all. Inner product is the raw dot product - just multiply component-wise and add. No normalization, no sqrt, no division. A CPU can do hundreds of dot products per microsecond using SIMD instructions that fuse multiply-and-add into a single op. Formula: q · d = Σ_i q_i · d_i. Range: unbounded (depends on magnitudes). Inner product at d = 768: 768 multiplies + 767 adds = ~1535 FLOPs. One fused multiply-add per pair; SIMD does 8 at a time. Cosine at d = 768: 768 multiplies (q · d) + 1536 mults (||q||, ||d||) + 2 sqrt calls + 1 divide = ~4600 FLOPs + 2 sqrt + 1 div. 3x more math, plus expensive sqrt and divide. At billions of vectors, that 3x difference is the difference between 100 ms and 300 ms per query. Inner product wins by being stripped to the bone - just a tight loop of multiply-add that GPU and CPU SIMD units love.
11.4.summary: Why inner product is the fastest similarity - a FLOP-by-FLOP comparison versus cosine showing the 3x compute gap.
queries: why is inner product the fastest similarity
inner product vs cosine compute cost
FLOPs for cosine vs dot product
how many FLOPs for cosine at d=768
dot product no sqrt no divide
why no normalization in inner product
cost of sqrt and divide in cosine

11.4.text: When vectors are L2-normalized, all three are equivalent. The identity production systems exploit every day: if you divide every vector by its own length so ||q|| = ||d|| = 1, then cosine, inner product, and L2 all rank documents in the same order. Normalize once at ingest, then use the fastest metric - inner product - forever after. Cosine: (q · d) / (||q|| ||d||) with ||q|| = ||d|| = 1 → q · d. Inner product: q · d, already bounded in [-1, 1]. L2 squared: ||q||^2 + ||d||^2 - 2(q · d) = 1 + 1 - 2(q · d) = 2 - 2(q · d). All three collapse to the same ranking: max cos(q, d) = max (q · d) = min L2(q, d). Larger inner product = closer in L2 = higher cosine. Concrete check: q = [0.85, 0.14, 0.44, 0.21], d = [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, 0.22]. Before normalization: ||q|| = 0.9898, ||d|| = 0.9599, q · d = 0.9495, cos = 0.9993. After normalization: q' = [0.8587, 0.1414, 0.4445, 0.2122], d' = [0.8438, 0.1250, 0.4688, 0.2292], q' · d' = 0.9993 = cosine. Normalize once at ingest. Store unit vectors. Use inner product forever. FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate all let you do exactly this.
11.4.summary: Algebraic proof and concrete check that L2-normalization makes cosine, inner product, and L2 produce identical rankings - the production trick.
queries: are cosine and inner product the same after normalization
unit vector trick for vector search
L2 squared identity 2 minus 2 dot product
prove cosine equals dot product unit vectors
how to use inner product instead of cosine
do all three give same top-k

11.4.text: Which metric for which workload. Pick the metric to match how the embedding model was trained. Text embeddings (SBERT, OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere) → cosine. Trained with cosine-similarity loss; magnitude carries no signal, only direction. Vision / face embeddings (FaceNet, some CNN retrieval heads) → L2. Length can encode confidence, saliency, or image quality, and L2 preserves those magnitude differences. Pre-normalized anything (any model after ||v|| = 1 at ingest) → inner product. Fastest possible similarity, same ranking as cosine, no sqrt or divide in the hot loop. The production default for text embeddings from SBERT, OpenAI, or Cohere: normalize at ingest, store unit vectors, use inner product at query time. Same recall as cosine, but the ranking loop is the fastest code your hardware can run.
11.4.summary: Decision table mapping embedding family (text vs vision vs pre-normalized) to the right metric and the production default for text retrieval.
queries: what is the production default metric

11.5.text: Chapter 11.5 The ANN Family Tree surveys the four algorithm families that tried to deliver sub-linear nearest-neighbor search at billion scale. Brute force is O(N) and melts down at 1B vectors; the goal is O(log N). Trees (KD-tree, ball tree, M-tree, VP-tree) work at d=2 but collapse under the curse of dimensionality at d=768. LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing) hashes vectors with random hyperplanes into bit codes; needs 50-100 hash tables for 95% recall on high-d data, so it loses on memory. IVF (Inverted File Index) clusters into Voronoi cells with k-means and probes nearest cells; cheap to build, weak at cell boundaries. Graphs (HNSW 2016, Vamana 2019/DiskANN) build proximity graphs and greedy-hop to the query, reaching top-k in roughly log N hops. Graph indexes sit on the Pareto frontier of ann-benchmarks.com, beating trees, LSH, and pure IVF at every recall target. HNSW ships in FAISS, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, pgvector, Pinecone, Chroma, Redis. Vamana ships in Azure AI Search and Milvus disk mode for graphs that don't fit in RAM.
11.5.summary: Overview of the four ANN algorithm families - trees, LSH, clustering/IVF, graphs - and why graph indexes (HNSW, Vamana) won production.
queries: what are the families of approximate nearest neighbor algorithms
why did graph indexes win in vector databases
KD-tree vs LSH vs IVF vs HNSW
which ANN algorithm should I use
why is brute force search infeasible at billion scale
history of approximate nearest neighbor algorithms
why do KD-trees fail in high dimensions
when to use HNSW vs Vamana
what are the production ANN indexes
why graphs beat trees and LSH

11.5.text: Brute force at 1 billion vectors melts down because it touches every single stored vector for every query - a linear cost in N. To make queries fast at billion scale, the algorithm has to skip most of the corpus. The target is sub-linear: growing with something much slower than N, like log N or sqrt(N). brute force: cost proportional to N times d (linear). Goal: cost proportional to log(N) times d (sub-linear, often O(log N)). At N = 1,000,000,000: linear is a billion ops, log N is about 30 ops. Four families of algorithms have tried to deliver this. Three of them had their moment and lost. One family won.
11.5.summary: Why brute-force linear search fails at billion scale and why the goal is sub-linear cost like O(log N).
queries: why is brute force search slow at billion scale
why do we need O(log N) search
cost of brute-force kNN at 1 billion vectors
what is the target complexity for ANN
how many ops at log N for 1 billion vectors
why skip most of the corpus
why we need approximate nearest neighbor
comparing N vs log N at 1B
cost proportional to N times d
how fast is log of one billion

11.5.text: Trees: beautiful at d = 2, broken at d = 768. The KD-tree partitions space by alternating axis splits - split on x at the median, then y at the median of each half, then x again, recursively. At d = 2 with 10 docs, it gives a clean log N search. At high dimensions it collapses: the curse of dimensionality erases the partitioning's advantage. d = 2 (drawable): each split halves the search region; log(10) steps to find the neighbor. d = 768 (real): in high dim, almost every pair sits at a similar distance, so partitions lose meaning and KD-tree scans almost all nodes. At d = 768 typical for BERT/OpenAI embeddings, KD-trees degrade to brute force. Ball trees, M-trees, cover trees, VP-trees all share the same fate. Space-partitioning trees are the wrong tool for high-dimensional dense vectors.
11.5.summary: KD-trees and other space-partitioning trees give log N search at low dimensions but degrade to brute force at d=768 due to the curse of dimensionality.
queries: why do KD-trees fail in high dimensions
why are space-partitioning trees bad for vectors
what happens to KD-tree at d=768
why does KD-tree degrade to brute force

11.5.text: LSH: hash similar vectors to the same bucket. Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) picks a few random hyperplanes through the embedding space. Each vector gets a bit for each hyperplane: 1 if it falls on the positive side, 0 if on the negative. The resulting bit string is a hash code, and vectors close in space tend to share most bits. Three random hyperplanes cut the space; each region is one hash bucket; similar vectors land in the same bucket. The catch: recall sags at high d. To hit 95% recall on 768-dim data, LSH needs many hash tables (often 50-100) and each table holds long hash codes. That pushes memory back up and erodes the speed advantage. Graph indexes beat LSH on the same recall target with much less memory. LSH had its moment in the 2000s. Modern production systems rarely reach for it - the follow-up work on graphs turned out to be simpler and more accurate.
11.5.summary: Locality-Sensitive Hashing uses random hyperplanes to bucket similar vectors but needs many hash tables for high-d recall and lost to graph indexes.
queries: how does LSH work for vectors
why LSH needs many hash tables
is LSH still used in production

11.5.text: Clustering (IVF): partition the space, probe the nearest cell. IVF (Inverted File Index) does partition-and-probe: cluster the corpus into nlist Voronoi cells ahead of time, and at query time scan only the nprobe nearest cells. Cheap to build, easy to update, decent recall at nprobe = 8 to 32. Its weakness is neighbors that sit just across a cell boundary - the partition hides them from the probe. Strengths: low memory overhead, fast to rebuild, trivial to add or remove docs; production default for huge static corpora when paired with PQ compression. Weaknesses: recall cliffs at cell boundaries, needs nprobe tuning per workload, struggles with mixed distributions where k-means produces unbalanced cells.
11.5.summary: IVF clusters vectors into Voronoi cells with k-means and probes only the nearest nprobe cells - cheap and easy but prone to recall cliffs at boundaries.
queries: what is IVF in vector search

11.5.text: Graphs: navigate the metric space directly. A graph index stores the corpus as a proximity graph: every vector is a node, every edge connects a vector to one of its nearest neighbors. At query time, we start somewhere in the graph and greedily hop to whichever neighbor is closer to the query. Each hop drops the distance. A well-built graph reaches the true top-k in roughly log N hops. HNSW (2016) - Hierarchical Navigable Small World: in-memory graph with a multi-layer hierarchy. Production default for FAISS, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, pgvector, Pinecone. Vamana (2019, DiskANN) - Microsoft Research: single-layer graph with alpha-pruning, designed so the graph can live on SSD and still answer queries in milliseconds. Used by Azure AI Search and Milvus disk mode.
11.5.summary: Graph-based ANN indexes (HNSW, Vamana/DiskANN) build proximity graphs and greedy-hop to the query in log N steps - the production default.
queries: log N hops in graph index
how do graph indexes find top-k
why graphs are fast for ANN

11.5.text: Why graphs won in production. Every year a volunteer benchmark called ann-benchmarks.com measures the best ANN algorithms on standard datasets, plotting recall against queries-per-second. HNSW and Vamana sit on the Pareto frontier: at every recall target they run faster than trees, LSH, and pure IVF. Graph indexes are now the default in every major vector database. Where you see HNSW: FAISS (Meta), Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, pgvector, Pinecone, Chroma, Redis-Search - effectively every vector DB ships an HNSW index. Where you see Vamana: Azure AI Search, Milvus disk mode, some self-hosted DiskANN deployments - the go-to when the graph cannot fit in RAM.
11.5.summary: Graph indexes dominate the ann-benchmarks Pareto frontier and ship as default in nearly every production vector database.
queries: why did HNSW win in production
when to use Vamana over HNSW

11.5.text: KD-tree recursive partition of a 2D point cloud: a vertical split down the middle, horizontal splits in each half, and one more vertical split in a quadrant. Each split halves the search region, illustrating clean logarithmic partitioning at low dimensions before the curse of dimensionality kicks in.
11.5.summary: Diagram of recursive KD-tree axis splits showing log N partitioning at d=2.
queries: what does a KD-tree look like

11.5.text: Two-dimensional space with three random hyperplane lines slicing across it; vectors sitting in the same intersection of half-planes share a 3-bit LSH hash code like 011 or 100. Each region of the plane corresponds to a bucket; nearby vectors tend to land in the same bucket.
11.5.summary: Diagram showing how three random LSH hyperplanes carve 2D space into bit-code buckets.

11.5.text: Small IVF recap scatter showing three Voronoi cells with the query landing inside and probing the nearest cell. Centroid squares mark each cell; the query's arrow points to the closest centroid; only that cell's docs are scanned.
11.5.summary: Mini IVF recap diagram with three Voronoi cells and a query probing one.

11.5.text: Proximity graph of the 10 cat-corpus documents with edges connecting each node to its two or three nearest neighbors. A highlighted green path crosses several hops from a starting node in the lower-right (birds/fish docs) to the cat cluster in the upper-left, illustrating greedy hop-by-hop search across a graph index.
11.5.summary: Diagram of a proximity graph with a highlighted greedy search path from one cluster to another.

11.5.text: Illustrative recall vs queries-per-second scatter inspired by ann-benchmarks: HNSW and Vamana sit on the upper-right Pareto frontier (high recall, high QPS), while IVF+PQ, LSH, and KD-tree fall below and to the left of the frontier. Recall@10 is on the x-axis (log) and QPS on the y-axis (log).
11.5.summary: Pareto frontier scatter showing HNSW and Vamana dominating IVF+PQ, LSH, KD-tree on recall vs QPS.
queries: which ANN is fastest at high recall

11.6.text: Brute force is correct but slow because it reads every stored vector for every query. In a 2D scatter of 10 cat-corpus docs, the query sits in the middle and an arrow goes from the query to every vector - nothing is skipped. Every line is a distance computation. At N = 10 this is nothing; at N = 1 billion it balloons to the 3 TB of scans per query that brute force cannot afford. Cost = N times d per query. The IVF trick: group vectors ahead of time so at query time only the group the query landed in is scanned.
11.6.summary: Brute-force kNN computes a distance from the query to every stored vector, costing N*d per query and motivating IVF's grouping trick.
queries: why is brute force slow at scale
what does IVF improve over brute force
cost of comparing query to every vector
N times d per query meaning
brute force kNN limitation 1 billion vectors
what is the IVF idea in one line
how does IVF avoid scanning everything
every line is a distance computation
3 TB scan per query brute force

11.6.text: Before accepting any queries, run k-means on the stored vectors. Pick the number of clusters nlist = 3 and let the algorithm find three centroids that minimize the average distance from each doc to its closest centroid. Each doc gets assigned to exactly one cluster. In the cat corpus: cluster A (purple centroid) holds the 5 cat docs {1,3,4,5,7}; cluster B (yellow) holds the 2 dog docs {2,8}; cluster C (cyan) holds {6,9,10}. nlist is a tuning knob - small nlist means few large clusters, large nlist means many tiny clusters. 3 is drawable; production picks roughly sqrt(N).
11.6.summary: IVF preprocesses the corpus by running k-means with nlist centroids, assigning every doc to exactly one cluster.
queries: how does IVF cluster the corpus
production rule sqrt N for nlist
what does k-means do for IVF

11.6.text: Draw the region of space closer to centroid A than to any other centroid - that is centroid A's Voronoi cell. Repeat for B and C and the whole plane is divided into three cells. Every stored doc sits in exactly one cell, and the cell it sits in is its cluster assignment. Four names for one thing: Cluster (k-means view: docs landed near same centroid), Cell or Voronoi cell (geometric view: region of space closest to centroid A), Partition (set-theory view: dataset split into nlist disjoint groups), Posting list or inverted list (storage view: on-disk array of doc IDs assigned to centroid A - this is why it's called Inverted File index). One centroid owns one cell owns one cluster owns one posting list. Centroid = representative. Cell = territory. Cluster = group. Posting list = stored IDs.
11.6.summary: Voronoi cells partition space by nearest centroid, and the same group of docs is called a cluster, cell, partition, or posting list depending on viewpoint.
queries: what is a Voronoi cell IVF
why is it called inverted file
cluster vs cell vs partition vs posting list
what is a posting list in IVF

11.6.text: Cell(p) = argmin over i of ||p - centroid_i||. In words: cell of point p is the index of the closest centroid. ||p - centroid_i|| is the distance from p to centroid i; argmin picks the i that makes that distance smallest. Every doc gets its cluster assignment fixed after training (the k-means step that picked centroids). New queries do not move docs around; assignments only change if you retrain centroids. What IVF actually stores on disk: only two tiny things - (1) Centroid list: A: [1.3, 1.1], B: [3.1, 1.0], C: [3.5, 2.3]; (2) Inverted file (posting lists): A -> [1,3,4,5,7], B -> [2,8], C -> [6,9,10]. No polygons, no boundary geometry. Boundaries are implicit - run the argmin formula on the fly. Centroids alone fully define the partition.
11.6.summary: The Cell(p) = argmin formula assigns each point to its nearest centroid, and IVF stores only the centroid list plus posting lists - boundaries are implicit.
queries: what does IVF store on disk
posting list example A B C

11.6.text: A query arrives. Compute distance from query to each of the nlist centroids - just 3 distances, not 10. Pick the nearest centroid (A) and scan only docs inside its cell. The other 2 cells are skipped entirely. Work with nprobe = 1: 3 + 5 = 8 dot products (3 to pick the nearest centroid, 5 to scan cluster A). Brute force: 10 dot products (scan every doc every query). At N = 10, 8 vs 10 looks tiny. At N = 1M with nlist = 1000, IVF at nprobe = 1 scans about N/1000 = 1000 docs instead of a million - a 1000x speedup, where IVF earns its keep.
11.6.summary: With nprobe = 1 IVF probes only the nearest centroid's cell, costing nlist + N/nlist comparisons - about a 1000x speedup at million scale.
queries: IVF query algorithm step by step

11.6.text: nprobe = 1 is fastest but fragile: if the true neighbor sits across a cell boundary, the probe misses it. Raising nprobe tells IVF to scan the top-nprobe closest cells. More cells scanned = higher recall + higher latency - a direct move on the tradeoff triangle. Measured at N = 1M, nlist = 1000: nprobe=1 -> Recall@10 = 0.80, 1000 docs scanned, 1000x vs brute; nprobe=4 -> 0.93, 4000 scanned, 250x; nprobe=8 -> 0.97, 8000 scanned, 125x; nprobe=32 -> 0.995, 32000 scanned, 31x. nprobe is the single most important IVF knob.
11.6.summary: nprobe controls how many of the closest cells IVF scans, directly trading recall against latency.

11.6.text: Two rules of thumb cover 90% of IVF deployments. Pick nlist to keep each cell small enough that scanning is fast, and pick nprobe from a recall curve measured on your own data. nlist ~= sqrt(N) (FAISS default). N = 1,000,000 -> sqrt(N) ~= 1000 -> FAISS rounds up to 4096; N = 100,000,000 -> sqrt(N) ~= 10,000 -> 32,768. nprobe = 8 is a sensible starting point that typically reaches 97% recall on text embeddings; raise to 16 or 32 for recall-critical workloads, drop to 4 or less for tight latency. Memory overhead: each centroid is d=768 float32 = 3 KB, so nlist*3KB. At nlist=4096 that is 12 MB - negligible next to the dataset. IVF trades a tiny bit of recall (boundary misses) and a one-time clustering cost for a massive per-query speedup. This is why IVF is the FAISS default for million-scale static corpora.
11.6.summary: Production IVF sizing uses nlist ~= sqrt(N), nprobe = 8 as a starting default, with negligible centroid memory overhead.
queries: how to size IVF in production
nlist sqrt N rule of thumb

11.6.text: IVF (Inverted File Index) speeds up nearest-neighbor search by partitioning the corpus into nlist clusters via k-means and storing each cluster as a posting list. At query time, distance is computed only to the nlist centroids, then only the top-nprobe nearest cells are scanned. The Cell(p) = argmin formula assigns docs to centroids; only centroid list + posting lists are stored on disk. Four interchangeable names: cluster, Voronoi cell, partition, posting list. nprobe is the recall-latency knob: more cells scanned = higher recall, higher latency. Production rule of thumb: nlist ~= sqrt(N), nprobe = 8 default. Trades small recall (boundary misses) and one-time k-means cost for a 100x-1000x per-query speedup.
11.6.summary: IVF partitions vectors by k-means, stores posting lists per centroid, and scans only nprobe nearest cells per query - trading small recall for huge speedup.
queries: what is IVF in one paragraph
how IVF works end to end
IVF speedup vs brute force overview

11.7.text: Chapter 11.7 introduces HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) by building intuition from a flat proximity graph up to a multi-layer hierarchy. A flat graph connects each of the 10 cat-corpus docs to its M nearest neighbors (M = 3 for the visual, M = 16 in production), but greedy search from a random start takes too many hops because there are only local edges. Adding a sparse hub layer with long-range edges lets queries jump across the dataset in one hop, then drop down for the local fine-grained search. Stacking layers exponentially - each holding roughly 1/M of the nodes below - turns search from O(N) hops on a flat graph into O(log N) hops total. The airport analogy makes it concrete: local airstrips (layer 0), regional airports (layer 1, ~6% of nodes), international hubs (layer 2+, <1%) - same hierarchical strategy planes use to cross continents.
11.7.summary: HNSW turns slow O(N) flat-graph search into O(log N) by stacking sparse upper layers of long-range hub edges over a dense bottom proximity graph.
queries: how does HNSW achieve log N search
why hierarchical graph beats flat graph

11.7.text: HNSW starts with one simple idea: build a graph over the docs where each node links to its M nearest neighbors. M is a tuning knob; the visual uses M = 3 so the picture stays readable, while M = 16 is the production standard. One nuance: because the graph is undirected, a node ends up with at least M edges, not exactly M. If another node picks this one among its M nearest, the link is added both ways - so some nodes have 4 edges instead of 3. Real HNSW caps this at a hard upper bound called M_max (usually 2M), pruning older neighbors if a node gets too crowded. Pseudocode: for each doc d, neighbors(d) = M nearest docs by distance. Every doc is reachable from every other doc by hopping along edges. The question is how fast we can reach the right one.
11.7.summary: A flat HNSW proximity graph connects each node to its M nearest neighbors, with bidirectional edges and a M_max cap (usually 2M) to prevent overcrowding.
queries: how does HNSW build a flat graph
what does M_max mean in HNSW
why do some HNSW nodes have more than M edges
how many neighbors per node in HNSW
what is the M parameter for

11.7.text: Greedy search starts somewhere and moves to whichever neighbor is closer to the query. Simple and correct - but if the starting node is far from the query, the walk across the flat graph takes many short hops. On N = 1,000,000 with a random start, greedy-on-flat averages about 1,000 hops. Slow. Concrete example: start at doc 10 (birds/fish), target doc 1 (cats). Hops on this flat graph: ~8 hops for 10 docs. Extrapolated to N = 1,000,000: many hundreds of hops. The reason: the flat graph has only local edges. There is no way to jump across the space in one step - we have to trudge through every intermediate doc.
11.7.summary: Greedy search on a flat proximity graph is correct but slow because only local edges exist, forcing many short hops from a random start - about 1000 hops at N = 1M.
queries: why is greedy search slow on flat graph
how many hops does greedy search take
what is greedy nearest neighbor search
why does flat graph need many hops

11.7.text: Add a sparse second layer above the flat graph. Only a handful of nodes exist on it - we call them hubs. Hubs are connected to each other by long-range edges that span the whole dataset. A query enters the graph at the hub layer, jumps across the space in one or two long hops, then drops down to the flat layer for the final fine-grained search. Long-range edges: hub-to-hub edges reach across the dataset in one hop. Enter at a hub, pay one long-haul, and we are already near the target's neighborhood. Drop down once close: when no hub is closer to the query, drop to layer 0 and do the last mile on the flat graph's short edges. One sparse hub layer already drops search from N-scale hops to a handful. Stacking one more layer on top takes it to log N.
11.7.summary: Adding a sparse hub layer with long-range edges lets queries jump across the dataset in one hop, then drop down to the flat layer for final local search.
queries: what is a hub layer in HNSW
why add a second layer to HNSW
hub to hub edges span dataset
drop down to lower layer HNSW
long haul hops in graph search
what are hubs in vector search

11.7.text: Stack a third layer on top, holding only a hub-of-hubs node or two. Searches enter at the top layer, descend through each tier with a constant number of hops per layer, and land at layer 0 already right next to the target. Because each layer holds roughly 1/M of the nodes beneath it, the number of layers grows with log(N) and so does the total hop count. Formula: layers = ceil(log_M(N)). At M = 16, N = 1,000,000: log_16(1,000,000) ~ 5 layers. Hops per layer ~ constant (~6 with ef_search = 50). ef_search = how many candidates the search keeps in its shortlist at each step (bigger = better recall, slower). Total hops ~ 30 to find the top-k vs ~1000 on flat. Flat graph: O(N) hops. Hierarchical graph: O(log N) hops. Exponential speedup for a small memory bump from the upper layers.
11.7.summary: Stacking layers exponentially gives layers = ceil(log_M(N)) and total hops ~ 30 at N = 1M, M = 16, turning O(N) flat search into O(log N) hierarchical search.
queries: how many layers does HNSW have
why is HNSW O log N
HNSW layer count for 1 million vectors
flat O(N) vs hierarchical O(log N)
memory cost of HNSW upper layers

11.7.text: Airport analogy: getting from a small town in India to a small town in Portugal is not one direct flight. You take a local cab to the regional airport, a regional flight to an international hub, a long-haul flight across continents to another international hub, a regional flight down, and a local cab in. HNSW search does the same thing for vectors. Three tiers: Local (layer 0) - every city has an airstrip, many connections to nearby towns, 100% of nodes; on a flat graph you are stuck using only these. Regional (layer 1) - a handful of regional airports reachable from any local airstrip and connected to each other by medium-range flights, ~6% of nodes (1/M = 1/16). International (layer 2+) - a few global hubs with long-haul flights between continents, search enters here and drops down as it gets closer to the target, <1% of nodes. The route a query takes: long-haul at international layer -> regional hops once you are over the right continent -> short local hops to the final neighbor. Same strategy planes use. Same strategy HNSW uses.
11.7.summary: The airport analogy maps HNSW's three tiers to local airstrips (layer 0, 100%), regional airports (layer 1, ~6%), and international hubs (layer 2+, <1%) for long-haul-first search.
queries: real world analogy for hierarchical search
what percent of nodes per HNSW layer
how does a query route through HNSW layers
long haul flight analogy vector search

11.7.text: Flat proximity graph diagram of the 10 cat-corpus documents with each node connected by cyan edges to its two or three nearest neighbors. This is the starting point before any hierarchy is added - shows that every doc is reachable but only via local short edges, with no long-range jumps across the dataset.
11.7.summary: Diagram of the flat baseline proximity graph with each of 10 docs connected to its M nearest neighbors via short cyan edges.

11.7.text: Same flat proximity graph with a dashed red greedy-search path meandering across many nodes before reaching the cat cluster. Illustrates that random-start greedy search on a flat graph is slow because the path has to trudge through many intermediate nodes since only local edges exist.
11.7.summary: Diagram showing a long dashed-red greedy path meandering across the flat graph, illustrating why random-start greedy search is slow.

11.7.text: Two-layer HNSW illustration: bottom cyan layer is the flat proximity graph of all 10 docs; top yellow layer holds three hub nodes (docs 1, 6, 10) with yellow long-range edges between them spanning the space. Shows how a query can jump across the dataset on the hub layer before dropping down to layer 0.
11.7.summary: Diagram of a two-layer HNSW with three yellow hub nodes (docs 1, 6, 10) above the flat cyan layer, connected by long-range hub-to-hub edges.

11.7.text: Three-layer HNSW illustration: bottom cyan all-nodes layer, middle yellow hub layer with long-range edges, top red hub-of-hubs layer with a single global entry-point node. Shows how stacking layers exponentially produces a global entry that descends through tiers to the target.
11.7.summary: Diagram of a three-layer HNSW with a top red hub-of-hubs entry node, a middle yellow hub layer, and the bottom cyan flat layer holding every doc.

11.8.text: HNSW Construction: build the hierarchical graph by inserting vectors one at a time. Each insert (1) rolls a layer L from L = floor(-ln(u)*mL) where mL = 1/ln(M) ≈ 0.36, (2) greedy-descends from the top entry node down to layer L+1 with one candidate per layer, (3) runs beam search at layers L..0 with ef_construction candidates (default 200), (4) connects the new node bidirectionally to its M nearest from the pool (default M=16). The exponential layer distribution puts ~94% of nodes at L=0, ~5.7% at L=1, decaying by 1/M per layer. M sets edge density and memory: ~70 bytes/vector graph overhead at M=16. ef_construction is a one-time build cost; M is fixed at build time and requires a rebuild to change.
11.8.summary: How HNSW builds its graph: layer roll, greedy descent, beam search with ef_construction, and bidirectional M-nearest connection per insert.

11.8.text: Start with an empty graph and insert one vector at a time. The very first doc has nothing to connect to, so it becomes the graph's entry point at whatever layer the layer formula assigns it. The entry point matters: every future query starts from whichever doc currently sits in the top layer. As more docs are inserted, upper-layer residents can get replaced.
11.8.summary: HNSW build is incremental: insert vectors one at a time; the first vector becomes the top-layer entry point.
queries: What is the HNSW entry point?
Does the HNSW entry point change?
How does HNSW handle the first inserted vector?
Why does HNSW need an entry point?
What layer does the first HNSW node land on?
Can entry-point residents be replaced in HNSW?
Where do queries start in HNSW?

11.8.text: Every inserted vector is placed at a random layer L drawn from an exponential distribution so upper layers stay sparse. Formula from the original HNSW paper: u = uniform(0, 1); L = floor(-ln(u) * mL), where mL = 1/ln(M) ≈ 0.36 and M = 16. Example u = 0.7: -ln(0.7) = 0.357, 0.357 * 0.36 = 0.128, floor = L = 0. Example u = 0.01: -ln(0.01) = 4.605, 4.605 * 0.36 = 1.658, floor = L = 1. Only rare extreme u values get promoted; this asymmetric distribution is the only reason upper layers stay small. No tuning needed beyond M.
11.8.summary: Layer L is sampled by L = floor(-ln(u) * mL) with mL = 1/ln(M); the exponential distribution keeps upper layers sparse.
queries: What is the HNSW layer assignment formula?
How is the HNSW layer L sampled?
What does mL mean in HNSW?
Why is HNSW layer assignment exponential?
How does u = uniform(0,1) map to a layer?
What L do most random samples produce?
Does layer assignment in HNSW need tuning?

11.8.text: Simulate the layer formula a million times at M = 16. Expected node counts per layer at N = 1,000,000: L=0 ≈ 93.75% (937,500), L=1 ≈ 5.86% (58,600), L=2 ≈ 0.37% (3,700), L=3 ≈ 0.023% (230), L=4 ≈ 0.001% (14). Exponential decay: P(L >= k+1) / P(L >= k) = 1/M, so each layer is 1/16 the size of the one below. Upper layers hold a handful of hub nodes - cheap in storage, expensive in importance because every query enters through them.
11.8.summary: ~94% of HNSW nodes land at L=0 and counts shrink by factor 1/M per layer, leaving tiny but critical upper tiers.

Why query enters through top layer

11.8.text: Insert one new doc into a graph holding docs 1-10, with M = 3 and ef_construction = 4. Frame 1: roll layer. u = 0.45 -> L = floor(-ln(0.45) * 0.36) = floor(0.288) = 0. Frame 2: greedy descend from top entry doc 1 down to layer L+1, keeping just one candidate per layer above target (cheap). Frame 3: at layer L = 0, run beam search with ef_construction = 4 candidate pool. Distances from new node at (260, 110): doc 7 = 58.3, doc 4 = 94.9, doc 3 = 111.8, doc 2 = 117.0. Frame 4: pick M = 3 nearest from pool (docs 7, 4, 3) and add bidirectional edges N<->7, N<->4, N<->3. Doc 2 stays in the pool but is not selected. HNSW Algorithm 1: roll L -> greedy descend top to L+1 -> beam search at L..0 with ef_construction -> connect M nearest. Production uses ef_construction = 200 for better neighbor choice.
11.8.summary: Worked 4-frame insert: roll L=0, greedy descend, beam search top-4 candidates, connect bidirectional edges to M=3 nearest.

11.8.text: Insert all 10 cat-corpus docs in order using deterministic u values. Doc 1 lands at L = 2 with u = 0.01, becomes top entry. Doc 6 lands at L = 1 with u = 0.07, adding a hub. Docs 7, 3, 5, 4, 2, 8, 10, 9 all land at L = 0. Three snapshots: after doc 1 only L2 holds doc 1; after docs 1, 6 the L1 hub layer connects 6 to 1; after all 10 docs L0 has every node connected via the dense flat-graph proximity edges, L1 = {1, 6, 10}, L2 = {1}. Insertion log shows each doc, u value, layer L, and edges (e.g., doc 4 at L=0 connects to 5, 3). Real layer rolls are random; the distribution still produces this shape: one or two top, a handful in the middle, the rest at the bottom.
11.8.summary: Walking 10 inserts: doc 1 to L=2, doc 6 to L=1, the rest to L=0; the graph grows hub-by-hub into its final 3-tier shape.

11.8.text: M is the cap on neighbors per node at layer 0 and is the most important construction parameter. Larger M = more edges per node = more starting points for search = better recall, but more memory and slightly slower per-hop queries. Visualizing M = 4 (sparse, degree 4), M = 8 (mid, degree 8), M = 16 (production, degree 10 capped at N-1 = 11) on a 12-node ring. M = 16 production rules: edges/node at L0 <= M = 16 bidirectional; at upper layers <= M/2 = 8; memory ≈ M * 4 bytes + upper layer overhead ≈ 70 bytes per vector. Graph overhead: 70 MB at N = 1M (vs 3 GB vectors), 7 GB at N = 100M (vs 300 GB vectors). Profiles: M = 8 memory-tight, M = 16 typical default, M = 48 recall-critical. M is the one knob you set up front and leave alone - changing it requires a graph rebuild.
11.8.summary: M caps L0 neighbors per node; M=16 default gives ~70 bytes/vec graph overhead and is fixed at build time.

11.8.text: First vector inserted into an empty HNSW graph: a single cyan dot labeled doc 1, marked as the entry point. No edges, no neighbors, no other layers populated. Demonstrates the initial seed state of an HNSW build.
11.8.summary: Diagram of HNSW build's empty-graph seed: one node, no edges.

11.8.text: Greedy descent during HNSW insertion: starting at the top entry doc 1 at L2, drop along green vertical edges to L1 doc 1, then to L0 doc 1. Above the target layer L = 0, every layer keeps just one candidate (cheap). The descent stops when it hits the target layer where beam search begins.
11.8.summary: Diagram of cheap one-candidate-per-layer greedy descent from top entry to L+1.

11.8.text: Layer-0 beam search during HNSW insertion: the new doc sits as a green star at (260, 110) and dashed orange lines connect it to its top-4 ef_construction candidates - docs 7 (58.3), 4 (94.9), 3 (111.8), 2 (117.0). The candidate pool size = ef_construction = 4 in this visualization (production default 200).

11.8.text: Final HNSW insertion frame: from the ef_construction = 4 pool, M = 3 nearest (docs 7, 4, 3) are chosen and connected to the new node by solid green bidirectional edges. Doc 2 - in the pool but not in the top M - is dimmed and gets no edge.

11.8.text: Build snapshot 1: HNSW graph state after only doc 1 has been inserted. A single L2 node, no edges, no L1 or L0 populated. Doc 1 rolled L = 2 and becomes the top entry on its own.
11.8.summary: Diagram of HNSW after the first insert: one isolated top-layer node.

11.8.text: Build snapshot 2: HNSW graph state after docs 1 and 6. L2 holds doc 1; L1 hub layer adds doc 6 connected by a yellow edge to doc 1; both nodes also sit on L0. Demonstrates how the L1 hub layer first gains a connection.

11.8.summary: Diagram of final 3-tier HNSW after all 10 inserts: full L0 plus L1 hubs plus L2 entry.

11.8.text: Ring of 8 (or 12) nodes with each node connected to its M = 4 nearest neighbors along the ring. Sparse edge density - degree 4 per node. Illustrates a memory-tight HNSW configuration.

11.10.text: HNSW exposes exactly three tuning parameters: M (graph shape, build-time, default 16), ef_construction (insertion candidate pool size, build-time, default 200), and ef_search (query-time beam width, default 50). M trades recall for memory and is fixed at build; ef_construction trades recall for build time as a one-off cost; ef_search is the only knob you can change without rebuilding and is the production dial for recall vs latency. Pick M first, then tune ef_search per workload. Memory at production scale is dominated by vectors (N x d x 4 bytes) plus a smaller graph overhead (N x M x 8 bytes). Playbook: low recall -> raise ef_search; high memory -> lower M + apply PQ; slow build -> lower ef_construction; slow queries -> lower ef_search.
11.10.summary: Chapter 11.10 covers the three HNSW tuning knobs (M, ef_construction, ef_search), their defaults, tradeoffs, and a production playbook for which to adjust when.
queries: how to tune HNSW for recall
which HNSW knob to change at runtime
how to fix low recall in HNSW

11.10.text: HNSW exposes exactly three parameters. M controls the graph shape and is a build-time structural choice. ef_construction controls how hard we work to pick good neighbors during insert (build-time effort, one-off). ef_search controls how wide a net we cast at query time (per-query recall dial). Defaults: M = 16, ef_construction = 200, ef_search = 50. M trades recall vs memory. ef_construction trades recall vs build time. ef_search trades recall vs latency. Change one knob at a time and measure - changing two at once makes it impossible to tell which one mattered.
11.10.summary: Introduces the three HNSW knobs M, ef_construction, ef_search with default values and which tradeoff each one governs.
queries: what are the three HNSW parameters
default value of M in HNSW
why change one HNSW knob at a time

11.10.text: M is the cap on neighbors per node at layer 0. Doubling M roughly doubles the per-vector graph memory and gives a small but measurable recall bump; returns flatten at high M. Measured at ef_search = 50, N = 1M, d = 768: M=8 gives recall@10 = 0.93 with ~35 bytes/vec graph overhead = 35 MB total; M=16 gives 0.97 at ~70 bytes/vec = 70 MB; M=32 gives 0.99 at ~140 bytes/vec = 140 MB; M=48 gives 0.995 at ~210 bytes/vec = 210 MB. M = 16 is the sweet spot for text. Drop to 8 when RAM is tight; raise to 32 or 48 when recall is critical and memory is available.
11.10.summary: Concrete table of how M affects recall@10 and per-vector graph memory, with M=16 as the sweet spot for text embeddings.
queries: what does M do in HNSW
HNSW memory per vector by M
M=8 vs M=16 vs M=32 recall
HNSW graph overhead bytes per vector
when to use M=8 in HNSW
when to use M=32 or M=48
graph memory at N=1M with M=16
M parameter sweet spot text embeddings
HNSW M tradeoff recall vs RAM

11.10.text: ef_construction is the candidate pool size during insertion. Larger pool finds better neighbors for every new node, yielding a better graph and better recall at query time, at the cost of longer builds. Once the index is built, ef_construction no longer matters - it is a one-time up-front cost. Measured at N = 1M, d = 768, M = 16: ef_construction=100 builds in ~5 min with recall ceiling 0.95; 200 in ~9 min for 0.97; 500 in ~20 min for 0.98; 1000 in ~35 min for 0.985. Recommended starting point: ef_construction = 200. Returns diminish sharply past 500 on most text datasets.
11.10.summary: Shows how ef_construction trades build time for a recall ceiling on the resulting graph; recommends starting at 200 with diminishing returns past 500.

11.10.text: ef_search is the only knob you change after deployment. Raising it expands the beam at layer 0 - more candidate nodes explored per query, higher recall, higher latency. Lowering it does the opposite. This is the knob production teams reach for when they need a quick recall bump. Query-time sweep at M = 16, N = 1M, d = 768, single CPU core: ef_search=10 gives recall 0.85 at 0.5 ms; 50 gives 0.97 at 1.2 ms; 200 gives 0.995 at 3.0 ms; 500 gives 0.999 at 6.0 ms. Start at ef_search = 50. Bump to 100 or 200 if recall is low. Drop to 10-20 only when sub-millisecond latency matters more than a 1-2% recall drop.
11.10.summary: Demonstrates the ef_search dial: a per-query knob that trades latency for recall and is the only HNSW parameter changeable without rebuilding.
queries: can I change ef_search without rebuild

11.10.text: Each M value traces its own recall@10 curve as ef_search varies on a log x-axis. Three curves shown: M=8 (cyan) plateaus lowest, M=16 (yellow) is the standard compromise, M=32 (green) converges closest to 1.0. Higher M lets the curve flatten closer to 1.0 before latency gets painful. Numbers are empirical, measured on a laptop with SIFT-1M. Moving along one curve is cheap (change ef_search). Switching curves is expensive (rebuild the graph with new M). That asymmetry is why M is a fixed architectural choice while ef_search is the runtime tuning dial.
11.10.summary: Recall@10 vs ef_search curves for M = 8, 16, 32 illustrate why M is fixed at build time while ef_search is the runtime dial.
queries: HNSW M=8 vs M=16 vs M=32 curve
why is ef_search the tuning dial

11.10.text: At production scale, total HNSW memory is dominated by vectors plus a smaller graph overhead. Memory ~= N * (d * 4 + M * 8) bytes. At N = 100M, d = 768, M = 16: 100M * (3072 + 128) ~= 320 GB total (300 GB vectors + 20 GB graph). Playbook: Recall too low -> raise ef_search first, then raise M (rebuild required). Memory too high -> lower M (rebuild) then apply PQ compression. Build time too slow -> lower ef_construction first, then lower M. Query latency too high -> lower ef_search (recall drops, queries get faster). ef_search is the only knob changeable without a rebuild, making it the default tool for incident response. Everything else requires planning ahead.
11.10.summary: Memory formula and a four-row symptom-to-action playbook for which HNSW knob to raise or lower in production.
queries: total RAM for HNSW at 100M vectors
N times d plus M times 8
when to apply PQ to HNSW
what to do if HNSW too slow

11.10.text: Line chart plotting recall@10 against ef_search on a log horizontal axis. Three curves shown: M = 8 (cyan), M = 16 (yellow), M = 32 (green). All curves rise from low recall at ef_search = 10 toward 1.0 as ef_search grows; higher M curves converge closer to 1.0 but require more memory. M = 16 is the standard compromise. The chart visualizes that moving along one curve (changing ef_search) is cheap while switching curves (changing M) requires a rebuild.
11.10.summary: Diagram description: recall@10 vs ef_search line chart with curves for M = 8, 16, 32 showing higher M reaches higher recall ceilings.

11.9.text: Chapter 11.9 walks through HNSW search end-to-end on the 10-doc cat corpus. Step 0: query enters at the fixed top-layer entry node (doc 1 at L2). Steps 1-2: greedy descent through upper layers - at each layer hop to a closer neighbor or drop down. L2 has no neighbors, so drop. L1's only neighbor (doc 6 at distance 267.1) is farther than doc 1 (87.1), so drop again. Step 3: at layer 0, switch from pure greedy to beam search with a priority queue of capacity ef_search (production default 50, visualized at 3). Step 4: repeatedly pop the closest unexplored candidate, evaluate its neighbors, insert any closer than worst-in-queue, evict the worst; converge when the beam top stops changing. Three frames trace popping doc 1 then doc 3 then doc 7. Step 5: return the top-k from the beam - docs 3, 7, 1 at distances 40.0, 86.4, 87.1. Same algorithm scales to a billion vectors; only layer count (~log N) and ef_search change.
11.9.summary: End-to-end HNSW search: enter at top-layer entry, greedy-descend to layer 0, run beam search of capacity ef_search until the top of the priority queue stabilizes, then return top-k.
queries: how does HNSW search work step by step
what happens when you query an HNSW index
HNSW greedy descent then beam search
why does HNSW use a priority queue at layer 0
how many hops does HNSW take to find nearest neighbors
do HNSW searches start from the same node
how does HNSW handle layer drops during search
what is the production default ef_search value
HNSW search returns top-k from beam
step by step HNSW query on cat corpus

11.9.text: Step 0: query lands; enter at the top-layer entry point. The yellow query star sits in 2D space inside the cat cluster near docs 1, 3, 7. Brute force would compute 10 distances right away. HNSW skips that. It starts at one fixed node: doc 1 in the top layer (L2). The entry point is fixed - every query starts from the same top-layer node. Upper-tier long-range edges close the gap fast.
11.9.summary: Every HNSW query starts at the same fixed top-layer entry node rather than computing distances to every doc up front.
queries: what is the entry point in HNSW
do all HNSW queries start at the same node
HNSW step 0 enter at top layer
why not compute all distances at start in HNSW
fixed start node hierarchical graph search
what does HNSW do when query arrives

11.9.text: Step 1: greedy descent at L2 - no neighbors, drop. Greedy rule: at each layer, look at every neighbor of the current node; if any neighbor is closer to the query than the current node, hop there; otherwise drop one layer down. At L2, doc 1 has zero neighbors, so we drop straight down to L1 doc 1. Distance from current doc 1 to query: 87.1. L2 neighbors of doc 1: none. Greedy step in code: while any neighbor n has dist(n, q) < dist(current, q): current = n.
11.9.summary: L2 holds only doc 1 with no neighbors at that layer, so greedy descent drops vertically to L1 doc 1.
queries: how does greedy descent work in HNSW
what is the greedy rule in HNSW
no neighbors at top layer HNSW
when does HNSW drop a layer
greedy hop or drop in HNSW
greedy search neighbor closer than current
HNSW top layer single node behavior

11.9.text: Step 2: greedy descent at L1 - neighbors farther, drop again. At L1 doc 1 has one neighbor: doc 6. Compare distances to the query. Doc 1: 87.1. Doc 6: 267.1. Doc 6 is far. Stay at doc 1 and drop to L0. Drops are free - the same node id sits in every layer it was assigned to; only the adjacency list changes. Decision: doc 6 farther → stay at doc 1 → drop to L0.
11.9.summary: At L1, doc 1's only neighbor doc 6 sits at distance 267.1 versus doc 1's 87.1, so the search stays put and drops to L0.
queries: what happens when L1 neighbor is farther
HNSW does not hop sideways when neighbor farther
drop from L1 to L0 HNSW
same node in multiple HNSW layers

11.9.text: Step 3: at L0, switch to beam search with a priority queue. Layer 0 is dense - pure greedy gets stuck. Instead, keep a priority queue of the closest ef_search candidates seen so far. ef_search defaults to 50 in production. The visualization uses ef_search = 3 so the queue fits on screen. Search starts at doc 1 in the layer-0 graph, with the beam initialized to [doc 1, distance 87.1].
11.9.summary: On the dense layer 0, HNSW switches from pure greedy to a beam search backed by a priority queue of size ef_search (default 50, illustrated with 3).
queries: why does HNSW switch to beam search at layer 0
why pure greedy fails at HNSW layer 0
how HNSW limits search at bottom layer

11.9.text: Step 4: expand the beam - pop, evaluate neighbors, update queue. Loop: pop the unexplored candidate with smallest distance, look at every neighbor, insert any neighbor closer than worst-in-queue, evict the worst. Stop when a full pass fails to update the top of the queue. Frame A: pop doc 1 (87.1), expand neighbors {3, 5, 7}; insert doc 3 (40.0) and doc 7 (86.4); reject doc 5 (135.4) - farther than worst-in-queue (87.1). Frame B: pop doc 3 (smallest, 40.0); expand {1, 4, 5, 7}; reject doc 4 (136.6); docs 1 and 7 already in queue. Frame C: pop doc 7 (86.4); expand {1, 2, 3, 4}; none beat worst-in-queue, beam top unchanged - converged. Beam has capacity ef_search; expand until best in queue stops changing.
11.9.summary: Beam expansion repeatedly pops the closest unexplored candidate, inserts neighbors closer than worst-in-queue, evicts worst, and converges when the queue top stabilizes - illustrated by popping docs 1, 3, 7.
queries: how does HNSW beam search expand
when does HNSW beam search converge
neighbor closer than worst in queue
doc 5 rejected from HNSW beam why

11.9.text: Step 5: return top-k. Take the top k entries of the converged beam. For query 'information about cats' with k = 3, the returned docs are 3, 7, 1 - exactly the cat docs. Distances: doc 3 = 40.0, doc 7 = 86.4, doc 1 = 87.1. Doc 3 text: 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa'. Doc 7: 'Kittens grow up to be cats'. Doc 1: 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores'. End-to-end path: L2 doc 1 → L1 doc 1 → L0 beam → top-3. Same algorithm scales to a billion docs; only layer count (~log N) and ef_search change.
11.9.summary: HNSW returns the top-k from the converged beam - for the cat query the result is docs 3, 7, 1 with distances 40.0, 86.4, 87.1 - via the path L2→L1→L0 beam.
queries: top-3 nearest docs cat corpus HNSW
L2 to L1 to L0 traversal
Lions cats Africa doc retrieved HNSW
Kittens grow up to be cats nearest neighbor
does HNSW algorithm scale to billion vectors
what changes for HNSW at billion scale
ef_search and layer count at scale

11.9.text: Diagram: Layer-0 scatter of the 10 cat-corpus docs with cyan flat-graph edges drawn faintly; the yellow query star sits in the cat cluster near docs 1, 3, 7. Shows where the query lands in 2D space at the start of HNSW search.
11.9.summary: 2D layer-0 scatter showing the query landing inside the cat cluster near docs 1, 3, 7.
queries: diagram of query landing in 2D HNSW
where does the query sit cat cluster
HNSW query sits in cluster picture

11.9.text: Diagram: Three-tier HNSW hierarchy with a yellow query arrow landing at the top-layer entry node (doc 1 at L2) drawn in green; lower layers untouched. Illustrates that HNSW search begins at a fixed top-layer entry point.
11.9.summary: Three-tier hierarchy diagram showing the query arriving at the green doc-1 L2 entry node while L1 and L0 are untouched.
queries: query arrow into HNSW top layer

11.9.text: Diagram: Greedy descent at L2 has no neighbors, so the current node drops vertically along a green edge from L2 doc 1 to L1 doc 1. Illustrates the layer drop when no closer neighbor exists at the current layer.
11.9.summary: Vertical green drop edge from L2 doc 1 to L1 doc 1 shows the layer-drop step when greedy descent has no neighbors to evaluate.
queries: no neighbors at top layer drop

11.9.text: Diagram: L1 decision panel showing distance bars - doc 1 at 87.1 vs doc 6 at 267.1 - illustrating that the only L1 neighbor is farther so the search drops to L0.
11.9.summary: Distance-bar panel comparing doc 1 (87.1) to doc 6 (267.1) at L1; doc 6 is farther, so the search drops to L0.
queries: neighbor farther triggers layer drop diagram
why drop from L1 to L0 visualization

11.9.text: Diagram: Layer-0 scatter with all flat edges visible and doc 1 highlighted as the entry into the L0 beam search. Marks the start of beam search with the priority queue initialized at doc 1.
11.9.summary: Layer-0 scatter highlighting doc 1 as the entry node where beam search begins with the priority queue.
queries: L0 beam search starting point diagram

11.9.text: Diagram: Frame A of beam expansion - doc 1 popped (purple), purple lines to neighbors 3, 5, 7; dashed distance lines show docs 3 and 7 close, doc 5 far. Visualizes inserting docs 3 and 7 into the beam and rejecting doc 5.
11.9.summary: Frame A shows doc 1 popped and its neighbors 3, 5, 7 evaluated; docs 3 and 7 are inserted while doc 5 is rejected as too far.
queries: dashed distance lines HNSW frame A

11.9.text: Diagram: Frame B of beam expansion - doc 3 popped; neighbors 1, 4, 5, 7 evaluated; doc 4 distance 136.6 farther than worst-in-queue so rejected. Shows the second beam-expansion step where the new closest candidate is popped.
11.9.summary: Frame B pops doc 3 and evaluates its neighbors; doc 4 is rejected at 136.6 because it is farther than worst-in-queue.

11.9.text: Diagram: Frame C of beam expansion - doc 7 popped; neighbors 1, 2, 3, 4 evaluated; none beat the queue worst, beam top unchanged so search converges. Marks the end of beam expansion.
11.9.summary: Frame C pops doc 7 and finds no neighbor better than worst-in-queue, so the beam top is stable and the search converges.

11.9.text: Diagram: Final HNSW search result on layer-0 scatter - docs 1, 3, 7 highlighted in green with their distance dashes from the query, marking the converged top-3.
11.9.summary: Final-result diagram highlights docs 1, 3, 7 in green on the layer-0 scatter as the converged top-3 neighbors.
queries: cat corpus HNSW final answer diagram

11.11.text: Chapter 11.11 introduces Vamana / DiskANN, Microsoft Research's 2019 graph-index algorithm built for billion-scale vector search where the graph cannot fit in RAM. HNSW assumes everything lives in RAM and breaks past ~100M vectors (320 GB at d=768, M=16). Vamana drops HNSW's hierarchical pyramid and uses one flat graph because every SSD hop costs the same ~10 microseconds, so layered shortcuts no longer pay. Each node has R=64 edges chosen by alpha-pruning to mix short-range edges (local precision) and long-range edges (cross-space jumps). Adjacency lists plus the vector itself are stored as 4 KB blocks aligned to NVMe pages; a small RAM cache holds the high-degree entry-layer nodes.
