A query greedy-walks the cache for free then issues 40-80 NVMe reads (~10 us each) for ~1 ms total. One 128 GB + 10 TB NVMe server can hold 100 billion vectors at ~5 ms latency. Used in Azure AI Search, Milvus disk mode, Weaviate, with FreshDiskANN (2021) adding inserts/deletes.
11.11.summary: Vamana/DiskANN: flat-graph SSD-resident ANN index for billion-scale search beyond HNSW's RAM wall.
queries: How does Vamana differ from HNSW
Why does HNSW hit a RAM wall
How does DiskANN scale to a billion vectors
When should I use DiskANN instead of HNSW
How does Vamana store the graph on SSD
Why is Vamana single-layer not hierarchical
How fast is a Vamana query
Why drop the pyramid in DiskANN

11.11.text: HNSW assumes the entire graph plus every vector lives in RAM. That assumption breaks at production scale. A single machine with 384 GB of RAM can just barely hold 100 million 768-dim float32 vectors plus their HNSW graph. At N = 100M, d = 768, M = 16: vectors = 300 GB, HNSW graph = 20 GB, total = 320 GB (barely fits one server). At N = 1,000,000,000 the total is 3.2 TB and needs multi-node sharding. Microsoft hit this wall on Bing. Their answer, published in 2019, is Vamana - a graph index designed from the ground up to work when the graph lives on SSD and only a cache slice lives in RAM.
11.11.summary: HNSW's all-in-RAM design breaks past ~100M vectors, motivating Vamana/DiskANN.
queries: Why does HNSW fail at billion scale
How much RAM does HNSW need at 100M vectors
When does HNSW hit a RAM wall
Memory cost of HNSW at 1 billion vectors
How big is the HNSW graph at 100 million vectors
Why can't HNSW scale to a billion vectors on one server
When to abandon HNSW for SSD-resident index
When sharding is needed for HNSW

11.11.text: HNSW builds a pyramid of layers so it can make fast long-range jumps through the upper hubs. Vamana throws the pyramid away and uses a single flat graph - every node sits on one plane with no layer stack above it. HNSW: 3 layers, long jumps live on top. Vamana: 1 layer, every edge lives here.
11.11.summary: Vamana replaces HNSW's hierarchical pyramid with one flat graph layer.
queries: Why does Vamana have only one layer
Difference between HNSW pyramid and Vamana flat graph
How many layers does Vamana have
Comparison of HNSW and Vamana structure
What replaces upper layers in Vamana

11.11.text: HNSW's pyramid only earns its keep because top-layer hops in RAM are essentially free. Once the graph lives on SSD, every hop - whether through a top-layer hub or a bottom-layer neighbor - costs the same 10 microseconds. The hierarchy stops paying for itself, so Vamana skips it. HNSW in RAM: top-layer jump ~0.1 us, bottom hop ~0.1 us. Vamana on SSD: any hop ~10 us, every layer same cost. A 100x speed difference per hop is why HNSW wins in RAM and Vamana wins on SSD.
11.11.summary: On SSD every hop costs ~10 us so layering yields no benefit; in RAM HNSW's free top hops do.
queries: Why drop the pyramid for SSD-resident graph
SSD hop latency vs RAM hop latency
Why does hierarchy not help on SSD
Cost of one hop in DiskANN
When does graph hierarchy stop paying off

11.11.text: Without a pyramid to provide long-range jumps, each Vamana node's 64 neighbors have to cover both jobs at once: some are short-range (for final local precision near the target) and some are long-range (for fast cross-space jumps that HNSW used to do on the top layer). R = 64 (default max neighbors per node), single flat layer, no hierarchy, each node's edges span short + long ranges. Vamana's build algorithm, called alpha-pruning, picks these 64 edges to be diverse - a mix of short for precision and long for cross-space jumps - instead of the 64 closest neighbors which would all clump together.
11.11.summary: Each Vamana node's R=64 edges, picked by alpha-pruning, mix short- and long-range to replace HNSW's layers.
queries: Short-range vs long-range edges in Vamana
What does alpha do in DiskANN

11.11.text: NVMe stands for Non-Volatile Memory Express. It is the modern protocol that plugs an SSD directly into the PCIe bus, skipping the slow older SATA path. Inside the drive, flash is organised into fixed-size pages - almost always 4 KB each. One SSD read returns one page; asking for fewer bytes still costs a whole page. Older SATA SSD: ~500 MB/s, ~100 us per random read. NVMe SSD (modern): ~3-7 GB/s, ~10 us per random read. ~10x faster than SATA. Vamana only works as an online search engine because of NVMe speeds.
11.11.summary: NVMe is the PCIe SSD protocol with 4 KB pages and ~10 us random reads that makes DiskANN viable.
queries: How fast is an NVMe SSD
How fast is NVMe random read
Random read latency NVMe vs SATA

11.11.text: Vamana stores each node's adjacency list on SSD as a 4 KB block aligned to the NVMe page. Every block holds one node's neighbor list AND the vector itself, so a single 4 KB read delivers both the edges and the data needed to compute distance - one IO per hop, no waste. A small subset of the graph (typically the central high-degree entry-layer nodes, ~1-5% of nodes) is kept in RAM as a cache. Disk block = 4 KB = 1 vector + neighbor ids; NVMe random read latency ~10 us per block; memory cache holds entry-layer nodes (most-traversed). The 4 KB choice is deliberate - it matches NVMe page granularity so we pay for one IO per hop, no more.
11.11.summary: Vamana packs each node's vector + adjacency into one 4 KB SSD block, with a RAM cache of entry-layer hubs.
queries: How does Vamana store data on SSD
What is in a Vamana disk block
How big is one Vamana node on disk
What lives in the RAM cache for DiskANN
How much of the graph is cached
Why store vector with adjacency list

11.11.text: A Vamana search starts in the RAM cache and greedy-descends there for free (no disk IO). When the greedy walk drops off the cached subgraph, every hop costs one SSD read. With a good graph and a well-chosen cache, total SSD reads per query stay under 80 - about 800 microseconds of IO plus compute. Per-query budget at N = 100M, d = 768: RAM hops ~20 (free, nanoseconds each); SSD reads 40-80 (10 us each on NVMe); distance ops ~80 (~60 ns each). Total latency = 80 * 10 us + compute = ~1 ms per query. A single-digit-millisecond vector search over a graph that never fits in RAM - that is the whole point of DiskANN.
11.11.summary: Vamana queries take ~20 free RAM hops then 40-80 NVMe reads at 10 us each for ~1 ms total.
queries: How fast is a Vamana query
How many SSD reads per Vamana query
How many hops in a DiskANN query
How long does one DiskANN search take

11.11.text: With R = 64, d = 768, and 1.2 alpha-pruning, a single server with about 128 GB of RAM and 10 TB of NVMe holds 100 billion vectors and still answers queries in a few milliseconds. That is more vectors than Google indexes for web search. 1 server: 128 GB RAM + 10 TB NVMe holds 100,000,000,000 vectors; query latency ~5 ms at recall@10 = 0.95; cost ~1/10 of HNSW multi-node cluster at the same scale. Where DiskANN is deployed: Azure AI Search (core engine for Bing), Milvus disk mode, Weaviate with Vamana backend, self-hosted DiskANN at Microsoft, Snowflake, Databricks. FreshDiskANN (2021 follow-up) adds incremental inserts and deletes on top of Vamana, handling the one thing the original couldn't, so production avoids periodic full rebuilds. Vamana is the answer when the graph does not fit in RAM; HNSW is still the production default under 100M vectors.
11.11.summary: One server with 128 GB RAM + 10 TB NVMe runs DiskANN over 100 billion vectors at ~5 ms; FreshDiskANN adds updates.
queries: How many vectors fits on one DiskANN server
Where is DiskANN used in production
When to choose Vamana over HNSW
Recall and latency for DiskANN at 100B

11.11.text: HNSW hierarchical graph with three stacked layers: the top layer (L2) has a single hub node, the middle layer (L1) has three hub nodes connected by long orange arcs, and the bottom layer (L0) has eight nodes connected by short green edges. Dashed vertical lines link the same node across layers, showing how upper-layer hubs sit above their L0 copies. Caption: HNSW: 3 layers, long jumps live on top.
11.11.summary: Diagram of HNSW's 3-layer pyramid showing top hub, middle hub row, and dense bottom layer with cross-layer links.

11.11.text: Single flat Vamana graph with eight nodes on one plane connected by a mix of short green edges (local precision) and long orange edges stretching across the plane (cross-space jumps), doing the work HNSW's upper layers used to do. Caption: Vamana: 1 layer, every edge lives here.
11.11.summary: Diagram of Vamana's single flat layer with mixed short and long edges replacing HNSW's hierarchy.

11.11.text: Focal Vamana node p at the center surrounded by a mix of short green edges fanning to close neighbors and long orange edges shooting across the plane to distant nodes, showing how one node's 64 edges do double duty as both local-precision and long-range hub edges. Legend: short edges = local precision; long edges = cross-space jumps. The short greens do what HNSW's bottom layer did; the long oranges do what its top layer did - both baked into one node on one flat graph.
11.11.summary: Diagram of one Vamana node p whose 64 edges mix short-range precision with long-range cross-space jumps.
queries: How does one Vamana node connect
Why mix short and long edges
How are 64 edges drawn for one node

11.11.text: Storage hierarchy diagram: the top layer shows a RAM cache (green) holding a small subset of high-degree entry nodes labeled '~1-5% of nodes, kept hot for every query'. The bottom layer shows the SSD (orange, NVMe) storing every graph node as 4 KB blocks aligned to NVMe pages, labeled '4 KB blocks, one per node (vector + adjacency list)'. Side annotation: NVMe random read latency ~10 us per block.
11.11.summary: Diagram showing DiskANN's two-tier storage: small RAM cache of entry hubs over NVMe SSD with 4 KB per-node blocks.
queries: RAM cache and SSD layout Vamana

11.20.text: Filtering combines vector similarity search with metadata predicates like tenant_id = 42 AND published_year >= 2024. Three strategies: pre-filter (evaluate predicate first, brute-force survivors - wins on tight filters, loses on loose), post-filter (ANN top-k then drop non-matches - silent empty results on tight filters), and inline filtering (evaluate predicate during HNSW graph traversal via bitset - Qdrant's payload index, best across all selectivities). Every production vector DB ships a side index for metadata: bitmaps, inverted indexes, column stores, or JSONB GIN. Inline filtering is the rare no-edge-case answer.
11.20.summary: Chapter 11.20 covers three filtering strategies (pre, post, inline) for combining vector similarity with metadata predicates and the metadata indexes each requires.
queries: What is filtering in vector search?
How do you combine WHERE clauses with vector similarity?
Pre-filter vs post-filter vs inline filter
Why does post-filter return empty results?
How does Qdrant filter HNSW search?
What is the production gotcha with vector search filters?
Bitmap vs inverted index for metadata
Why pre-filter fails on loose filters
Metadata index choices in vector DBs

11.20.text: Production retrieval is rarely pure vector similarity. Callers attach metadata predicates: 'find docs similar to this one AND tenant_id = 42 AND published_after = 2024-01-01'. An ANN index built over raw embeddings has no idea what tenant_id or published_year mean - it ranks by geometry. Without help, top-k can return semantically close docs that violate the predicate. The query the app sends: SELECT id FROM docs WHERE tenant_id = 42 AND published_year >= 2024 ORDER BY embedding <=> query_embedding LIMIT 3. In the 10-doc cat corpus with synthetic tenant and year metadata, only docs 1, 3, 7 pass the predicate. A naive ANN lookup ranks nearest-by-geometry and has no way to know those filters exist. Three strategies divide this problem differently, each with a failure mode.
11.20.summary: Vector search must combine similarity ranking with metadata predicates; naive ANN ignores filters and returns geometry-close but predicate-violating results.
queries: What does a real vector search query look like?
Why doesn't ANN respect metadata filters?
Vector search with WHERE clause example
Why is filtering the number-one production gotcha?
Find similar AND filter by tenant
ORDER BY embedding distance with WHERE

11.20.text: Pre-filter: evaluate the predicate first against every document, keep only survivors, brute-force similarity over that smaller set. Filter funnel at N = 1M with 0.1% selectivity: 1M docs full corpus -> 1,000 pass predicate -> top-3 brute-force ranked. Tight filter wins: 0.1% selectivity on 1M = 1,000 survivors, brute-force over 1k is cheap (~1 ms), recall = 100% (exact search). Loose filter loses: 50% selectivity on 1M = 500k survivors, brute-force over 500k is slow (~500 ms), ANN speed is abandoned entirely. Pre-filter swaps approximate-fast for exact-slow. The strategy only pays off when the predicate shrinks the corpus to a few hundred or thousand survivors.
11.20.summary: Pre-filter strategy evaluates predicates first then brute-forces survivors; wins at tight selectivity (~1k survivors, 1ms) but collapses on loose filters (500k survivors, 500ms).
queries: How does pre-filter vector search work?

11.20.text: Post-filter: flip the order. Run ANN normally, take top-100 candidates, then apply the predicate. Keeps ANN speed on any workload, but on tight predicates it silently returns fewer than k results - sometimes zero - because survivors may all sit at position 500 in the ANN ranking. Post-filter on a tight 0.1% predicate, k = 10: Step 1 ANN returns top-100 nearest by geometry (fast). Step 2 evaluate predicate on each - 1 of 100 passes (insufficient). Step 3 return 1 result instead of 10 (fewer than k). The post-filter trap: app asks for 10 results, gets 1 or 0 back - the UI suddenly looks broken. Raising ANN top-k to 1000 to compensate makes latency climb proportionally, and still no guarantee with very selective filters. Easy to implement, fast on loose filters, silent failure mode at high selectivity.
11.20.summary: Post-filter runs ANN first then drops non-matches, keeping ANN speed but silently returning fewer than k (often zero) results on tight predicates.
queries: How does post-filter vector search work?
Why does post-filter return empty results?
Why is my vector search returning 0 results?

11.20.text: Inline filtering fuses pre and post: at every HNSW graph hop, evaluate the predicate before counting a node as a candidate. The traversal still explores the graph but only counts passing nodes. This is how Qdrant's payload index works - best-in-class across almost every selectivity level. In the example HNSW graph, nodes 1, 3, 5, 7 pass the predicate (orange) and the traversal path visits them while crossing dimmed failing nodes. How Qdrant does it - the payload index: every payload field (tenant, year, tags) has its own structured index (inverted, range tree). At each graph hop the predicate is evaluated via a bitset lookup - O(1) per node. Non-passing nodes still get traversed (you may have to cross them to reach passing ones). Only passing nodes are added to the top-k candidate heap. Inline filtering keeps O(log N) graph navigation and pays only a per-hop bitset lookup. Selectivity barely matters - the traversal reaches passing nodes either way.
11.20.summary: Inline filtering evaluates predicates during HNSW graph traversal via per-hop bitset lookups, keeping O(log N) navigation regardless of selectivity (Qdrant payload index).

11.20.text: Selectivity changes the winner. Strategy by filter selectivity at N=1M, k=10: Pre-filter tight 0.1% fast+exact (win), medium 5% acceptable, loose 50% 500ms, universal 100% 1s brute force. Post-filter tight empty results (bad), medium missing some (bad), loose 50% good, universal 100% best ANN speed (win). Inline (Qdrant) great across tight, medium, loose; matches post-filter on universal. Pre-filter dominates at extreme tightness, post-filter at extreme looseness, inline filtering wins everywhere in between - where most production workloads sit. Inline filtering is the rare 'no edge case' answer; every production system that cares about filters converges on it. Post-filter is the production footgun: looks fine in tests on uniform data, then ships and quietly returns empty top-ks to the tenant with the tightest filter.
11.20.summary: Comparison table shows pre-filter wins at 0.1% selectivity, post-filter wins at 100%, and inline filtering dominates the middle where production workloads live.
queries: Which filter strategy wins at what selectivity?
Pre vs post vs inline filter comparison
Why inline filter has no edge cases

11.20.text: Any filter strategy needs a way to evaluate the predicate quickly - a full table scan defeats the purpose. Every production vector DB ships a side index for metadata. Qdrant: inverted index + bitmap on payload, integrates tightly with HNSW traversal, per-field tunable. Pinecone: namespaces + metadata index, namespaces partition physically, metadata via filter DSL. Weaviate: column-store inverted index learned from Lucene, BM25 and filter share the column layout. pgvector: JSONB GIN or btree on columns, plain Postgres indexing familiar to SQL teams. Index complexity: bitmap O(1) set membership tiny memory; inverted index O(log N) lookup per term great for high-cardinality fields; column store fastest for range predicates bigger on disk; JSONB GIN most flexible schema slowest at scale. The filter index is the hidden cost behind every filtered vector query - benchmarks that leave it out always look good, production always finds it.
11.20.summary: Production vector DBs ship metadata side indexes (bitmap, inverted, column store, JSONB GIN) with different complexity tradeoffs that determine filtered query speed.
queries: What index does Qdrant use for filters?
How does Pinecone do metadata filtering?

11.20.text: Three-stage filter funnel for the pre-filter strategy. Wide yellow trapezoid represents 1 million vectors in the corpus; middle narrowing trapezoid represents the ~1,000 docs that pass the tenant_id and published_year predicates; rightmost green trapezoid represents the final top-3 returned after brute-force similarity search over the survivors. Stages labeled 1. Filter (metadata index read) and 2. Rank.
11.20.summary: Funnel diagram visualizing pre-filter narrowing 1M corpus to 1k survivors then top-3 via brute-force.

11.20.text: Small HNSW-style graph with 10 nodes where the four that pass the predicate (1, 3, 5, 7) are filled in orange and the six that fail are dimmed. Gray edges connect near neighbors and an orange traversal path with arrows visits only the passing nodes 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 7, illustrating Qdrant's inline filtered-HNSW approach that evaluates the predicate during graph traversal instead of pre- or post-filtering.
11.20.summary: Diagram of inline filtered HNSW: traversal path 1-3-5-7 across passing orange nodes while bypassing dimmed failing nodes.

11.21.text: Inserts in HNSW are cheap. Adding a new vector: embed the doc to vector v, assign a layer L = floor(-ln(uniform) * mL), search the existing graph for M = 16 nearest neighbors, append the node and add M edges. Work per insert is O(log N), 16 edges added, memory grows linearly in N. Append-only systems stay coherent forever - the pain begins when someone asks to delete.
11.21.summary: HNSW insert path: layer assignment, find M=16 nearest neighbors, append node with M edges at O(log N) cost.
queries: what is M parameter HNSW insert
how many edges added on HNSW insert

11.21.text: Removing a node breaks every edge incident to it. If the deleted node was a hub - the only short route between two regions - queries that used to cross it take longer detours or land on suboptimal neighbors. Greedy search has no idea the shortcut is gone; it silently returns worse results. Diagram: 8-node graph where central hub D has edges to A,B,C,E,F,G,H. D is struck through in red, every edge touching D drawn dashed red as broken routes. Five shortest-path routes lost when D disappears. Hub nodes have high betweenness, queries route through them - removing one breaks the index even though no errors are thrown.
11.21.summary: Deleting a hub node shatters multiple routing paths and silently degrades recall without throwing errors.
queries: what happens when you delete from HNSW
removing nodes from approximate nearest neighbor graph

11.21.text: Tombstone strategy: instead of physically removing the node, set node.tombstone = true. The vector and edges stay in place. At query time the greedy descent still hops through tombstoned nodes (routing preserved) but tombstoned candidates are dropped before the top-k heap. Delete pipeline: set bit, append 'deleted' to WAL, O(1) per delete, edges untouched. Query filter is a 1-bit per-node check. Trades immediate path-break problem for slow bloat. Recall stays fine for the first few deletes, then the graph wastes work on dead nodes.
11.21.summary: Tombstone deletion marks nodes as deleted while keeping them in the graph for traversal, dropping them at the top-k stage.
queries: what is a tombstone in vector DB
how do vector indexes handle deletes

11.21.text: Tombstones bloat the graph as more accumulate. Past ~20% tombstoned, queries traverse many dead nodes without benefit; hub nodes still exist but half their candidates are tombstoned. Recall@10 vs delete percentage at M=16, ef_search=50: 0% deletes -> recall 0.97, 10% -> 0.965, 20% -> 0.95, 30% -> 0.92, 40% -> 0.88, 50% -> 0.85. Dashed red line at 30% marks typical rebuild trigger. The graph degrades smoothly but the drop accelerates past ~20% deletes. Most systems set rebuild alarms at 20-30% tombstoned. Past that, recall loss becomes user-visible and wasted-traversal cost appears in P99 latency.
11.21.summary: Recall@10 degrades from 0.97 at 0% deletes to 0.85 at 50% deletes - rebuild alarms typically fire at 20-30% tombstoned.

11.21.text: Three rebuild strategies trade complexity vs recall restoration speed. Full rebuild: simplest, recall back to 0.97, but downtime and full CPU burn - good for small indexes and scheduled windows. Segment rotation: no downtime, new segment swaps in atomically, but memory overhead during swap and operational complexity - default for large indexes and 24/7 uptime. Incremental repair: continuous, no downtime, but subtle code and only partial recall recovery - good for streaming workloads with steady delete pressure. Every approach trades downtime against operational complexity. Production systems mix all three across tiers. Segment rotation is the industry default above 100M vectors: build the next segment in background, atomically swap, free the old.
11.21.summary: Three rebuild strategies (full, segment rotation, incremental repair) trade downtime against operational complexity.

11.21.text: Index family determines delete pain. IVF / IVF-PQ: easy - cell-level rebuild at ~50% dead, used by FAISS IVF_PQ and Milvus IVF. HNSW: moderate - full-graph rebuild at ~30% dead, used by Qdrant HNSW and Weaviate. HNSW + PQ: hardest - graph plus codebook retrain, used by Qdrant HNSW+PQ and Milvus HNSW_PQ. pgvector HNSW: historically hard - VACUUM + REINDEX, tracked since pgvector 0.5. IVF quantizes space into cells; deleting only affects one cell which can be rebuilt independently. Graph-based indexes pay more because the removed node participates in traversal. Layered indexes (HNSW + PQ) get the worst: graph holes plus stale codebooks. Pick the index family AFTER you know your write/delete pattern. Static corpora: IVF-PQ. Churn-heavy: HNSW or segment-rotated pgvector. Capacity plans must include delete pressure, not just insert rate - a system fine at 10% monthly churn will grind at 40%.
11.21.summary: IVF rebuilds cells independently while graph indexes need full rebuilds; HNSW+PQ is hardest because both graph and codebook stale.
queries: which vector index handles deletes best

11.21.text: Updates and deletes in vector indexes: inserts are cheap (O(log N), append node with M=16 edges) but deletes are hard. Physical deletion breaks routing paths through hub nodes, silently dropping recall. The standard workaround is the tombstone - mark deleted, leave the node and edges, filter at query time. Tombstones bloat the graph; recall@10 drops from 0.97 at 0% deletes to 0.92 at 30% and 0.85 at 50%. Rebuild triggers fire at 20-30% tombstoned. Three rebuild strategies trade off: full (downtime), segment rotation (memory overhead, the industry default above 100M), incremental repair (continuous, partial recovery). Index family matters: IVF rebuilds one cell, HNSW needs full rebuild, HNSW+PQ also retrains codebooks, pgvector relies on VACUUM + REINDEX. Capacity planning must include delete pressure, not just insert rate.
11.21.summary: Chapter overview: HNSW inserts are O(log N), deletes break paths so use tombstones, rebuild at 20-30% bloat using full/segment/incremental strategies tuned to the index family.

11.21.text: Hub-deletion graph: 8 nodes where central hub D has edges to A, B, C, E, F, G, and H. Node D is struck through in red to indicate deletion. Every edge touching D is drawn with a dashed red line marking a broken route. Outer-ring edges between non-hub nodes are dimmed but still present. Visualizes how removing a single high-betweenness hub shatters multiple routing paths simultaneously and forces detours.
11.21.summary: Diagram showing how deleting hub node D in an 8-node graph breaks every edge that touched it, shattering multiple routing paths.

11.21.text: Recall vs delete-rate line chart. Vertical axis: recall@10 from 0.80 to 1.00. Horizontal axis: delete percentage from 0 to 50%. Orange curve plots: 0% -> 0.97, 10% -> 0.965, 20% -> 0.95, 30% -> 0.92, 40% -> 0.88, 50% -> 0.85. Dashed red vertical line at 30% marks the typical rebuild trigger. Curve dips gently then accelerates past 20% deletes. Configuration: M=16, ef_search=50.
11.21.summary: Line chart of recall@10 dropping from 0.97 at 0% deletes to 0.85 at 50% deletes, with a 30% rebuild-trigger marker.

11.22.text: About 100M vectors fit on one node. Before thinking about shards, know the single-node ceiling. At d = 768, one float32 vector is 3 KB. An AWS r7i.24xlarge has 768 GB of RAM. With HNSW overhead (~100 bytes/vector) and working headroom for caches and fragmentation, roughly 100M vectors is the comfortable ceiling on one server. Beyond that, the index has to split. Single-node fit math on r7i.24xlarge (768 GB RAM): Vectors 100M x 3 KB = 300 GB. HNSW graph 100M x 100 B = 10 GB. Cache + fragmentation headroom ~60 GB. Total ~370 GB of 768 GB RAM - comfortable. Scale guide: 10M trivial on a laptop, 100M one r7i.24xlarge, 1B+ must shard. The single-node ceiling is the point where the operational cost of one big box flips to coordinating many smaller boxes.
11.22.summary: Why a single node tops out around 100M vectors and the memory math behind that ceiling.
queries: how many vectors fit on one server
when do I need to shard a vector database
memory per vector at 768 dim float32
how big can HNSW be on one machine
vector size in bytes for d=768
do I need sharding at 10M vectors
HNSW graph memory overhead per vector
cache and fragmentation headroom for vector index
when to split a vector index across nodes

11.22.text: Random sharding: round-robin by vector id. The simplest split. Hash each vector id into one of S shards; shard i holds every vector whose id hashes to i. Writes distribute evenly; memory per shard is predictable. The catch: every query must fan out to every shard because any shard can contain the nearest neighbor. Shard placement: shard(id) = hash(id) mod S. Query cost = S x single-shard query. Every shard must fan out to reach every query. Random sharding gives the best possible load balance and the worst possible query fan-out. For throughput-bound workloads the fan-out is fine; for latency-critical workloads it can blow the P99 budget.
11.22.summary: Random sharding hashes ids into shards giving perfect load balance but forces queries to fan out to every shard.
queries: why does random sharding fan out to every shard
load balance vs fan out tradeoff
fanout cost of random shard placement
is hash-mod sharding good for vector search

11.22.text: Semantic sharding: one IVF cluster per shard. Cluster the entire corpus with IVF first; each cluster becomes a shard. Now each shard holds vectors from one region of the embedding space. A query arrives, computes distance to each centroid (there are only S of them), and routes to the nearest nprobe shards. Dramatically fewer shards touched per query. Example: query routes to 2 of 4 shards (nprobe = 2), with shards labeled cats, animals, dogs, misc. Shard placement: shard(v) = argmin dist(v, centroid_i). Query cost = nprobe shards scanned, not S. The corpus is partitioned by semantic region, not by id. Load balance is worse than random sharding (popular clusters get hotter), but the per-query cost is much lower. Most production deployments accept the hotspot risk for the latency gain.
11.22.summary: Semantic sharding uses IVF clusters as shards so queries only hit nprobe nearest shards instead of all of them.
queries: how to route query to fewer shards

11.22.text: Every shard returns top-k; coordinator merges. Regardless of placement strategy, the actual query-time protocol is the same: the coordinator sends the query to the chosen shards in parallel, each shard runs its local ANN search and returns its top-k, and the coordinator merges the lists into the final top-k. Total latency is bound by the slowest shard (fan-out tail), not the sum. Fan-out: 8 shards, each returns top-10, coordinator merges to top-10. Step 1: coordinator broadcasts query to 8 shards in parallel. Step 2: each shard runs local ANN, returns top-10. Step 3: coordinator receives 80 candidates, merges by score, keeps top-10. Total latency = fan-out tail + merge cost. Tail-latency amplification: if each shard has 1% chance of being slow, 8 shards have roughly 8% chance the query waits.
11.22.summary: The coordinator scatters the query to shards in parallel, gathers each shard's local top-k, and merges into the final top-k.
queries: coordinator merge top k from shards
fan out fan in query protocol
how does multi-shard ANN query work
tail latency probability with more shards

11.22.text: Merged top-k is not single-node top-k. The coordinator can only merge what each shard sends it. If the true top-10 are spread across shards, each shard's local top-10 may miss a true top-10 that sits at local position 11. Result: merged recall is lower than a hypothetical single-node top-10 would be. The fix is simple: ask each shard for a buffer - top-20 or top-50 - so the merge has more candidates to pick from. Recall of merged top-10 vs per-shard buffer size: top-10 -> 0.88 (buffer too small, misses spread hits), top-20 -> 0.94 (typical production buffer), top-50 -> 0.97 (matches single-node within 0.1%), top-100 -> 0.98 (diminishing returns, higher network cost). Rule of thumb: per-shard buffer = k x 2 to k x 5 depending on how clustered the data is. Merged recall is not equal to single-node recall unless the buffer is large enough. "Sharded recall is lower than benchmarks" is almost always a buffer issue.
11.22.summary: Sharded recall drops unless each shard returns a buffer of k times 2 to 5 candidates so the coordinator merge has enough to pick from.
queries: sharded recall lower than single node
per shard buffer size for merge
k times 2 or k times 5 buffer rule
sharded benchmarks vs single node benchmarks
common cause of low sharded recall
how big should per-shard top-k be

11.22.text: Prune shards by filter predicate when possible. Sharding and filtering interact. When the shard key aligns with a common filter field - for example tenant_id is the shard key and the query filters by tenant_id - the coordinator can skip the irrelevant shards entirely. When the filter is orthogonal to the shard key, every shard still has to be scanned. Aligned filter (skip most shards): shard key tenant_id, filter tenant_id = 42, fan-out to 1 of 8 shards, coordinator prunes the other 7. Orthogonal filter (scan all shards): shard key vector_id (random), filter tenant_id = 42, fan-out to all 8 shards, cannot prune because tenant spans shards. Pick the shard key to match the hottest filter field. Multi-tenant apps almost always shard by tenant_id; multi-region apps almost always shard by region. Shard key choice is a one-way door - change it later and every vector has to move.
11.22.summary: Aligning shard key with the dominant filter field lets the coordinator prune irrelevant shards; orthogonal filters force full fan-out.
queries: shard key vs filter field alignment
shard by region for geo queries
pick shard key for hottest filter
how to choose shard key vector database

11.22.text: Sharding & Partitioning: a single node tops out near 100M vectors at d=768 on 768 GB RAM, then the index must split. Two placement strategies dominate. Random sharding (shard(id) = hash(id) mod S) gives perfect load balance but forces every query to fan out to every shard. Semantic sharding builds an IVF cluster per shard, so queries only hit nprobe shards but popular clusters create hotspots. At query time the coordinator scatters in parallel and merges per-shard top-k results - merged recall drops unless each shard returns a buffer of k x 2 to k x 5. Tail latency amplifies with more shards. Picking the shard key to match the hottest filter (tenant_id, region) lets the coordinator prune irrelevant shards entirely; orthogonal filters force full fan-out. Shard key is a one-way door.
11.22.summary: Chapter overview of how to split a vector index across nodes: single-node ceiling, random vs semantic placement, scatter-gather protocol, buffer-driven recall, and shard key choice.
queries: sharding and partitioning vector database overview
how to scale vector search beyond one node
when to shard a vector index

11.22.text: Random sharding diagram with a query icon q at the top center sending fan-out lines straight down to four shard rectangles arranged in a horizontal row. Each shard holds a mixed scatter of colored dots and is labeled Shard 0 through Shard 3 underneath the box. Illustrates that random sharding forces every query to hit every shard because any shard may contain the nearest neighbor.
11.22.summary: Diagram of random sharding showing query q fanning out to all four shards.
queries: query fans out to all shards picture
every shard hit by query diagram
diagram why random sharding scans all shards

11.22.text: Semantic sharding diagram with a query icon q at the top center sending routing lines straight down to only two of four shards arranged in a horizontal row. The cats and animals shards are highlighted; the dogs and misc shards are dimmed. Each box shows its cluster letter inside and the region label underneath. Illustrates that when shards align with IVF clusters the query can skip the unrelated regions of the embedding space and touch only the shards likely to contain matches.
11.22.summary: Diagram of semantic sharding showing query q routing to only the cats and animals shards while dogs and misc are skipped.
queries: query routes to 2 of 4 shards illustration
cats animals dogs misc shard diagram
diagram of shard pruning by cluster

11.23.text: Chapter 11.23 covers replication and high availability for vector databases. Vector search is read-heavy (10-100 reads per write), so production systems run N read replicas behind a load balancer to scale QPS linearly. A leader-follower pattern handles writes: the leader accepts writes and ships them asynchronously to followers, producing replication lag (50 ms steady, 200 ms during bursts, 2 s during heavy spikes/rebuilds). Reads in the lag window see stale results. On leader failure, Raft consensus promotes a follower; async replication risks losing the in-flight write window, while sync replication achieves zero data loss at ~2x write latency. Durability comes from layering WAL on disk, periodic snapshots, and source-of-truth rehydration. Recovery time for 100M vectors: snapshot+WAL replay 10 min - 2 hours, WAL-only ~12 hours, full re-embed from source 1-14 days. RTO budget = snapshot cadence + WAL replay rate.
11.23.summary: Full overview of read replicas, leader-follower writes, replication lag, failover semantics, durability layers, and recovery time tradeoffs.
queries: replication and high availability vector database
how do read replicas work for vector search
leader follower replication for ANN index
what happens when leader dies in vector DB
RTO recovery time objective vector database
data loss on failover vector DB
ensure no data loss on leader crash
vector DB cluster recovery from RAM loss

11.23.text: Vector search is read-heavy: most production workloads ship 10-100 queries per write. Run several identical copies of the index (read replicas) behind a load balancer and let queries spread out. Each replica holds the full index in RAM and answers independently. Throughput scales linearly with replica count up to the write rate the leader can distribute. QPS capacity ≈ replicas · per-replica QPS. Memory cost ≈ replicas · per-replica RAM. Reads scale cheaply; you pay in memory rather than latency. Every managed vector DB exposes replicas as a knob.
11.23.summary: Read replicas are N identical full copies of the index behind a load balancer, scaling read QPS linearly at the cost of memory.
queries: what are read replicas in vector database
how do read replicas scale QPS
load balancer in front of vector replicas
ratio of reads to writes in vector search
memory cost of replicating vector index
do replicas hold full copy of index
load balancing ANN queries across copies
how many replicas do I need for vector search
are vector replicas independent or coordinated

11.23.text: Writes go to a single leader (primary). The leader applies the write and ships it asynchronously to followers, who apply it and become eventually consistent. Replication lag is the gap between leader-applied and follower-applied. Typical lag: steady state 50 ms (P50 normal load), moderate spike 200 ms (write burst, healthy pipeline), heavy spike or rebuild 2 s (followers fall behind). Leader accepts at t=0; followers apply at t=50 ms to 2 s. Queries in the lag window see stale results. "I just added a document but search doesn't find it" is almost always lag, not a bug. Either wait out the window, or route the read-after-write to the leader.
11.23.summary: Leader-follower writes with async replication produce 50 ms to 2 s lag, causing stale reads inside the lag window.
queries: why do my new docs not show up immediately
read after write consistency vector search
how long until follower catches up
fix stale read in vector DB
route reads to leader for consistency

11.23.text: If the leader fails, a follower is promoted to primary via a consensus algorithm (Raft in most modern vector DBs). Failover timeline: t=0 ms leader processes write W_{n+1}; t=10 ms leader crashes - W_{n+1} never replicated; t=50 ms followers notice, Raft election starts; t=500 ms new leader promoted, cluster accepts writes again. Lost window: writes in-flight at t=0 to t=10 ms. Async replication (default) gives fast writes (ack at leader), at-most-50-ms lag, but possible data loss on failover. Sync replication gives slower writes (ack only after one follower applies), zero data loss on failover, but write latency ≈ 2x async. Most vector DBs default to async; pick sync only when a few lost writes in a rare failover would cost more than steady-state latency.
11.23.summary: On leader crash, Raft promotes a follower in ~500 ms; async replication risks losing in-flight writes while sync replication trades 2x write latency for zero data loss.
queries: what happens when leader crashes vector DB
data loss on failover vector database
when should I use sync replication
ack on leader vs ack on follower

11.23.text: Vector indexes live in RAM for speed. RAM is volatile - if every node restarts at once, durability depends on the persistence model. Three patterns, usually layered: WAL on disk - every write appended to a log before ack, RPO = 0 (synchronous) or 1 batch. Periodic snapshots - dump the index to disk every N minutes, RPO = interval, faster startup. Rehydrate from source - re-read every doc from the app DB and re-embed, RPO = 0 but slow. If every RAM copy dies, the index is only as durable as its WAL + snapshot. The source-of-truth DB is the ultimate recovery path. Many teams never test full cluster loss; the first time it happens they discover snapshot cadence is 24 hours and WAL retention is 1 hour, leaving them stuck re-embedding for days.
11.23.summary: Durability comes from layering WAL, periodic snapshots, and source-of-truth rehydration, each with different RPO and recovery characteristics.
queries: how is vector index made durable
WAL write ahead log vector database
RPO recovery point objective vector DB
what if every RAM copy dies
source of truth database for vectors
test full cluster loss vector DB

11.23.text: Worst case: entire cluster dies. Recovery time depends on what is cached. For a 100M-vector corpus: snapshot + WAL replay takes 10 min to 2 hours (disk I/O bound, graph pages stream in); WAL-only replay with no snapshot takes ~12 hours (every insert replayed from scratch); re-embed from source DB takes 1 to 14 days (embedding API throughput plus dollars billed per token). RTO budget = snapshot cadence + WAL replay rate. Pick snapshot interval that matches your RTO target. Re-embed is the fallback of last resort, measured in days. Put this math into the runbook before a real outage; the cost of a one-day outage at 10K QPS usually dwarfs the cost of snapshots every 30 minutes.
11.23.summary: Recovery from total cluster loss for 100M vectors ranges from 10 minutes (snapshot+WAL) to 14 days (full re-embed); pick snapshot cadence to hit your RTO.
queries: how long to recover 100M vector index
recovery time after total cluster loss
cost of one day vector search outage

11.23.text: Diagram: Read-replica topology. Five green client circles on the left feed into a yellow load-balancer box labeled LB. Four arrows fan from LB out to four cyan replica boxes labeled R1 through R4 on the right. Each replica is an identical copy of the index. Caption: "Every replica is an identical copy of the index." Illustrates how reads spread across replicas while each holds the full index, and how the load balancer distributes traffic. Writes go elsewhere (to the leader); replicas serve reads only.
11.23.summary: Diagram of clients hitting a load balancer that distributes reads across four identical full-index replicas R1-R4.
queries: read replica topology diagram vector database
load balancer fanning to replicas illustration
diagram of N replicas behind LB
how clients reach replicas through load balancer
visual of read scaling with replicas
R1 R2 R3 R4 replicas diagram
client to load balancer to replica flow

11.24.text: Semantic embeddings reward meaning - two docs saying the same thing with different words end up close in embedding space. The flip side: docs containing unique identifiers like SKU-A4291, proper nouns like 'Dr. Whiskers', or rare tokens like 'kernel panic 0x7B' get no special treatment - the exact token is smoothed into its neighborhood. Semantic search silently misses lookups where the word itself is the signal. What vectors lose: SKUs and IDs (SKU-A4291, ORD-88213) need exact match; proper nouns (Rajul, Mumbai, AlphaFold) are rare in training; rare terms (Tabby, kernel panic, RoPE) get blurred into neighbors. Vector search excels at meaning, misses literal tokens. Hybrid search combines vectors with keyword search to recover both. The rescue is classic BM25 lexical search running alongside the vector index - merge the two ranked lists and you keep the best of each.
11.24.summary: Why pure vector search fails on exact-token lookups like SKUs, proper nouns, and rare technical terms.
queries: why does vector search miss exact terms
why semantic search fails on SKUs
why dense vectors lose rare tokens
why I need keyword search alongside vectors
why pure vector retrieval misses identifiers
embeddings vs keyword search exact match
what is hybrid search and why use it
why we need BM25 plus vectors

11.24.text: BM25 is the lexical-search workhorse, shipped in every production search engine since Lucene. For a query of terms t, a document d scores by summing the IDF of each term (rare terms count more) times a saturating term-frequency contribution (more appearances help, with diminishing returns). Formula: BM25(q, d) = sum over t in q of IDF(t) * TF_norm(t, d). IDF(t) = log(N / df(t)) - inverse document frequency. TF_norm(t, d) = tf(t, d) * (k1 + 1) / (tf(t, d) + k1 * length-adjustment). Standard Lucene defaults: k1 ~ 1.2, b ~ 0.75. Worked example on cat corpus, query 'cats domesticated': Doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' BM25=4.82 rank 1; Doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' BM25=2.30 rank 2; Doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' BM25=2.10 rank 3; Doc 8 'The dog chased the cat' BM25=1.90 rank 4; Doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' BM25=1.70 rank 5. Doc 1 wins because it contains both query terms and is the shortest match. BM25's length-normalization keeps it fair across docs of different sizes.
11.24.summary: BM25 lexical scoring formula combines IDF and saturating term frequency with length normalization.

11.24.text: Given a query, fire both retrieval systems simultaneously. The vector index returns its top-N by embedding similarity; the BM25 index returns its top-N by lexical score. Neither list needs to know the other exists at this stage - they each do what they are good at. Merging is a separate step. Vector returns top-N by similarity, BM25 returns top-N by lexical score, then merge them. The two systems can even live on different nodes. The only shared state is the query itself and the final merge point. Architecture: query splits into two parallel lanes - upper lane to Vector ANN producing top-N ranked list, lower lane to BM25 producing top-N ranked list, both feeding into an RRF merge node. This is the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search.
11.24.summary: Hybrid search runs vector ANN and BM25 in parallel as independent rankers, each producing its own top-N list.
queries: how does hybrid search architecture work
vector ANN and BM25 in parallel
how do you combine vector and keyword search
can vector and BM25 run on separate nodes
fan out to vector and BM25
how to merge two ranked lists

11.24.text: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines two ranked lists without needing their raw scores - BM25 and cosine live on different scales. For each doc, compute 1 / (k + rank) on every ranker that returned it, then sum. The constant k dampens the contribution of low-ranking docs; the Lucene-standard value is k = 60 from the 2009 TREC paper. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over r in rankers of 1 / (k + rank_r(d)). RRF on cat corpus example: Doc 1 vec rank 2, BM25 rank 1, calc 1/62 + 1/61 = 0.0326 (winner); Doc 3 vec rank 1, BM25 rank 3, 1/61 + 1/63 = 0.0323; Doc 5 vec rank 4, BM25 rank 2, 1/64 + 1/62 = 0.0317; Doc 7 vec rank 3, BM25 rank 5, 1/63 + 1/65 = 0.0313; Doc 8 vec rank 5, BM25 rank 4, 1/65 + 1/64 = 0.0310. Doc 1 wins because it ranked well on both. RRF rewards doc consensus; docs only one ranker likes fall below the consensus winners.
11.24.summary: Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges ranked lists by summing 1/(k+rank) with k=60 to reward documents both rankers agree on.
queries: how to merge ranked lists without raw scores
how to combine different score scales

11.24.text: Concrete side-by-side for query 'tabby cat genetics'. Pure vector finds semantically cat-ish docs and misses 'tabby': 1) Lions are big cats (near miss), 2) Tigers are striped cats (near miss), 3) Cats are small carnivores, 4) Kittens grow up to be cats, 5) My cat sat on the mat. No hit on tabby - the actual topic. Pure BM25 finds the exact 'tabby' hit but ranks unrelated fluff with the word in it: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Genetics 101 textbook (unrelated), 4) Random tabby mention in a tweet, 5) Tabby bar and grill menu. Off-topic exact matches rank high. Hybrid (RRF) merges: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Tigers are striped cats, 4) Cats are small carnivores, 5) Kittens grow up to be cats. Tabby + genetics concentrated at the top. Hybrid wins when the query has both exact and semantic intent. Pure vector loses 'tabby', pure BM25 loses 'cat genetics'. If product queries ever include a specific term that must match exactly, hybrid is almost always worth enabling.
11.24.summary: Worked comparison showing pure vector misses 'tabby', pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches, hybrid RRF concentrates relevant results at top.
queries: hybrid search vs vector search example
when does hybrid search beat vector
side by side hybrid vs pure vector
why hybrid wins on mixed queries
comparison hybrid pure vector pure BM25

11.24.text: Production deployments often weight the two rankers. Standard form: score(d) = alpha * RRF_vec(d) + beta * RRF_bm25(d). Typical defaults: alpha = 0.7, beta = 0.3 when corpus is mostly natural language; flip to 0.3/0.7 for code or catalog data where literal tokens dominate. Tune via held-out recall on your own data via A/B tests. When hybrid wins: mixed natural-language plus SKU queries, multi-lingual or technical corpora, long-tail identifier lookups, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). When hybrid is not worth it: pure chat or FAQ where vectors are already great, very short queries with no identifiers, strict latency budgets under 10 ms, BM25 index is itself unmaintained. Weighted hybrid adds operational complexity - two indexes, two query paths, one merge. Turn it on only after you measure a specific quality gap vectors alone cannot close.
11.24.summary: Weighted RRF tunes alpha/beta per data type and hybrid is only worth its operational complexity for specific workload patterns.
queries: when is hybrid search worth it
when not to use hybrid search

11.24.text: Hybrid search recovers the literal-token signal that pure vector embeddings smooth away by running BM25 keyword search and vector ANN in parallel and merging their ranked lists. BM25 scores docs by IDF times saturating term-frequency with Lucene defaults k1=1.2, b=0.75. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists score-free as sum of 1/(k+rank). Hybrid wins when queries mix exact terms (SKUs, proper nouns, rare tokens like 'tabby') with semantic intent ('cat genetics'); pure vector misses literals, pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches. Production deployments use weighted RRF score = alpha*RRF_vec + beta*RRF_bm25, default alpha=0.7 beta=0.3 for natural language, flipped for code/catalog. Worth it for mixed queries, multi-lingual, RAG; skip for pure chat or strict <10ms latency.
11.24.summary: Hybrid search merges vector ANN with BM25 via RRF to recover exact-token retrieval that pure embeddings lose.
queries: when to use hybrid search recap
hybrid search architecture in one place
BM25 RRF weighted alpha beta summary

11.24.text: Hybrid-search flow diagram with a green query icon on the left that splits into two parallel lanes. The upper cyan lane flows to a Vector ANN box emitting a top-N ranked list. The lower yellow lane flows to a BM25 box emitting another top-N ranked list. Both lists arrive at a green merge node on the right labeled RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion). Illustrates the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search: two independent retrievers run side by side and a score-free rank fusion step merges their outputs into a single final ranking.
11.24.summary: Diagram of hybrid-search architecture: query fans out to parallel Vector ANN and BM25 lanes, each producing top-N, merged at an RRF node.
queries: diagram of vector and BM25 in parallel
fan out to vector and lexical diagram

11.25.text: Chapter 11.25 covers rerankers in vector search. Two-stage retrieval: stage 1 ANN pulls top-100 candidates fast (~5 ms over 1B vectors with HNSW); stage 2 cross-encoder rescorer reranks those 100 to top-10 (~100 ms on A10 GPU). Cross-encoder concatenates query + doc tokens ([CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]) and runs one transformer pass with full attention, emitting a single relevance score. Beats bi-encoder cosine because token-level interactions are preserved instead of pooled. Quality jump: +10 to +20 MRR@10 over bi-encoder. Latency depends on GPU: T4 5 ms/pair, A10 1 ms, A100 0.5 ms, H100 0.3 ms. Production models: Cohere Rerank 3 (managed API, ~400M params), BGE-reranker v2 (open weights, 268M base / 560M large from BAAI), MS-MARCO cross-encoders (MiniLM 33M, BERT-base 110M, classic baseline), RankGPT/LLM-as-judge (highest quality, 10x latency). Reranker cannot fix bad stage-1 recall - it can only reorder existing candidates.
11.25.summary: Chapter overview of two-stage retrieval with cross-encoder rerankers, costs, models, and limits.
queries: what is a reranker in vector search
why use a reranker after ANN
how much does reranking add to latency

11.25.text: Two-stage retrieval: ANN returns top-100, rerank returns top-10. Accurate retrieval does not need to be accurate everywhere - only at the top. Stage 1 uses ANN to quickly pull 100 roughly-relevant candidates from a billion-vector index. Stage 2 is a slower but smarter model that re-scores those 100 candidates. Total cost: fast over the billion, slow over the hundred. Stage-2 accuracy matters only on k=100, not on N=1B. Stage 1 retrieval: corpus N = 1B vectors, ANN latency ~5 ms per query (HNSW), output top-100 candidates, may include some near-misses. Candidate quality is fine at k=100, not at k=10. Stages: Stage 1 retrieval - 5 ms - HNSW/IVF-PQ - 1B to 100. Stage 2 rerank - 100 ms - cross-encoder - 100 to 10. Final - ~105 ms - combined - 10. The two-stage pattern is standard in search engines, ads systems, and RAG pipelines.
11.25.summary: Two-stage retrieval splits work: fast ANN narrows 1B to 100, slow reranker picks final 10.
queries: what does ANN return before rerank
how many candidates does a reranker score
why not run reranker on whole corpus
how does retrieval scale to 1 billion vectors

11.25.text: Cross-encoder: query and doc concatenated into one transformer pass. A cross-encoder takes the query and the candidate doc, concatenates their tokens into one input stream (often [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]), runs the whole thing through a transformer, and emits a single relevance score. Because query and doc tokens share attention inside the stack, the model can condition on exactly which part of the doc matches which part of the query. Input = [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]. One transformer pass, one scalar output. Score = P(doc is relevant to query). The architecture is dead simple. The expense is running it per candidate: each doc is a full forward pass through the model.
11.25.summary: Cross-encoder concatenates query and doc tokens through one transformer to emit a relevance score.
queries: how does a cross encoder work
what is the input format for cross encoder
why one forward pass per document
full attention query and doc tokens
what is the output of a cross encoder

11.25.text: Cross-encoder architecture diagram. Bottom row: input token boxes labeled [CLS], Query, Query, [SEP], Doc, Doc, Doc, Doc, [SEP] - query tokens cyan, doc tokens yellow, separators dim. Arrows go up from each token into a single wide yellow rounded rectangle labeled 'Transformer (full attention across all tokens)' that spans all tokens. A green Score circle sits at the top connected by an arrow from the transformer output. Illustrates that cross-encoder rerankers run full attention over the concatenated query and doc tokens in one forward pass, emitting a single scalar score.
11.25.summary: Diagram of cross-encoder showing concatenated query+doc tokens flowing into one transformer that emits a scalar score.
queries: CLS query SEP doc SEP token layout
what does a cross encoder look like
single transformer over query and doc tokens
full attention over query doc tokens

11.25.text: Bi-encoder cosine cannot see query-doc token interactions. A bi-encoder produces one vector per query and one per doc, then measures cosine similarity. All token-level information is collapsed into those two vectors before they meet. The cross-encoder keeps the tokens separate and lets full attention decide which interactions matter. Bi-encoder (stage 1): Query -> encoder -> q_vec; Doc -> encoder -> d_vec; Score = cosine(q_vec, d_vec). No token-level interaction - everything pooled into a single vector first. Cross-encoder (stage 2): Concat(query, doc) -> transformer; full attention across all tokens; Score = one scalar. Query tokens attend to doc tokens and vice versa - maximally informed score. Typical quality jump: +10 to +20 MRR@10 over bi-encoder alone. MS-MARCO leaderboards: cross-encoders win on almost every benchmark. Price: cannot pre-compute cross-encoder scores like embeddings - every (query, doc) pair is a fresh forward pass.
11.25.summary: Cross-encoder beats bi-encoder by preserving token-level interactions instead of pooling, gaining +10 to +20 MRR@10.
queries: why is cross encoder more accurate
why cant you pre-compute cross encoder scores
quality gap bi encoder cross encoder
why rerank instead of just embeddings

11.25.text: Rerank: score each candidate, sort, return top-10. Once stage 1 has supplied 100 candidates, run the cross-encoder once per candidate with the same query. Sort by score, return top-10 to user. The rerank does not change which candidates exist - only their ordering. A bad stage-1 candidate set cannot be fixed at stage 2. Algorithm: for candidate d in top-100: score(d) = cross_encoder(query, d); sort candidates by score desc; return top-10. Example before rerank (stage 1 ANN rank) vs after rerank (stage 2): Doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' ANN rank 4 -> rerank 1. Doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' ANN 2 -> rerank 2. Doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' ANN 1 -> rerank 3. Doc 4 'My cat sat on the mat' ANN 7 -> rerank 4. Doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' ANN 3 -> rerank 5. The reranker is not psychic - stage 1 must include the right doc in its top-100. Recall at stage 1 is the upstream bottleneck.
11.25.summary: Reranker scores each of the 100 candidates with the cross-encoder and reorders them; bad stage-1 recall caps the result.
queries: how does rerank actually work step by step
rerank score sort top k algorithm
example of rerank changing rank order
ANN rank vs reranker rank example
why doc 1 jumps from rank 4 to rank 1
rerank does not add new candidates
if ANN misses can rerank recover

11.25.text: Latency cost: 100 rerank runs is about 100 ms on an A10 GPU. Cross-encoders are small transformers - BERT-base or BERT-large sized. On a modern GPU, one (query, doc) forward pass is ~1 ms. 100 candidates = ~100 ms latency added on top of stage 1. Big enough to notice, small enough to justify for quality-critical paths. Reranker latency on production GPUs: T4 (older) 5 ms per-pair, 500 ms for 100 candidates, 2/sec QPS cap. A10: 1 ms per-pair, 100 ms for 100, 10/sec. A100: 0.5 ms per-pair, 50 ms for 100, 20/sec. H100: 0.3 ms per-pair, 30 ms for 100, 33/sec. Reranker latency = candidates · per-pair cost. Batch all 100 pairs on one GPU to hit these numbers. Dropping to top-50 halves latency. In RAG, this extra 100 ms usually trades against an LLM call that already costs 500 ms - rerankers fit comfortably in that budget.
11.25.summary: Reranking 100 candidates costs ~100 ms on A10, scaling linearly with candidates and per-pair GPU time.

11.25.text: Production models: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, MS-MARCO cross-encoders. Three families dominate production. Cohere's managed API ships general-purpose rerankers via simple REST call. BGE and similar open-weight models from BAAI run locally on GPU. MS-MARCO cross-encoders are the classic open-source baseline and still competitive. Production models: Cohere Rerank 3 - managed API, ~400M params, multi-lingual, context 4K tokens, simple REST. BGE-reranker v2 - open weights, base 268M / large 560M, BAAI release, self-host on A10/A100, BGE-reranker-m3 multilingual. MS-MARCO cross-encoder - open weights, MiniLM ~33M, BERT-base 110M, Sentence-Transformers, classic baseline, smallest latency. RankGPT / LLM-as-judge - managed LLM, listwise LLM-driven, highest quality, 10x latency, expensive. Strategy: start with MS-MARCO MiniLM as cheap baseline, move to BGE-reranker or Cohere when quality matters, reserve LLM-as-judge for offline evals. Swap reranker without touching stage 1 - interface is always (query, candidates) -> reordered candidates, making A/B tests straightforward.
11.25.summary: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, MS-MARCO MiniLM, and RankGPT are the four production reranker families with different cost/quality trades.
queries: what reranker should I use in production

11.26.text: Multi-vector retrieval (ColBERT-style) keeps one contextual embedding per token instead of pooling each document into a single dense vector. ColBERT (Stanford 2020, Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT) encodes a ~200-token doc as ~200 vectors and a query as ~5 token vectors, then scores via late interaction max-sim: for each query token, take the maximum cosine/dot similarity against any doc token, sum those maxes. This rewards docs that match every query token somewhere, recovering rare-term and long-document fidelity that single-vector cosine averages away. Cost: storage balloons 20-100x (e.g. 1M docs at d=768, 200 tokens/doc => 600 GB float32, ~60 GB with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression vs 3 GB single-vector). Production support: Vespa native tensor max-sim, Qdrant native multi-vector since v1.10 (Jul 2024), Elasticsearch nested dense_vector 8.13+, Weaviate preview. Pinecone and pgvector do not support it natively. Use for RAG over long docs and dense technical corpora.
11.26.summary: Chapter 11.26 overview of ColBERT-style multi-vector retrieval: per-token embeddings, max-sim late interaction, storage tradeoffs, and production support.
queries: why per-token embeddings beat pooled embeddings on long docs
how much memory does multi-vector retrieval need
when to use multi-vector retrieval over single vector
RAG over long documents retrieval quality

11.26.text: One vector per doc blurs token-level signal. A single dense embedding per document is the workhorse of vector search: compact, fast to compare, great on short text. For long docs (paragraphs, articles, chapters) the single-vector representation loses fidelity because every token is averaged into one 768-dim summary. Rare terms and specific phrasing get blurred into bulk meaning. Example: 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores that originated in the fertile crescent...' encoded into [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, ..., 0.22] (768 dims, one per doc) - every token's contribution is averaged into that one vector. Long doc + single vector = lossy compression. Multi-vector retrieval keeps one vector per token instead. This motivates ColBERT and the whole multi-vector family: keep more information, rank better, pay in storage.
11.26.summary: Single-vector embeddings average all tokens into one 768-dim vector, blurring rare terms in long docs - motivation for multi-vector retrieval.
queries: why does single vector embedding lose information on long documents
why average pooling hurts long doc retrieval
how do dense vectors blur rare terms
tradeoff between compactness and fidelity in embeddings
why one vector per document is not enough
what does mean pooling lose in BERT embeddings
single vector blur problem long passages
why rare terms disappear in pooled embeddings

11.26.text: ColBERT idea: one vector per token. ColBERT (Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT, Stanford 2020) keeps every token's contextual embedding instead of pooling to a single doc vector. A 200-token document becomes 200 vectors. Queries are also expanded to per-token embeddings (~5 query tokens for 'where do cats live'). Scoring happens at token granularity - the 'late interaction' - rather than after pooling. Single-vector retrieval: 1 vector per doc, cosine similarity, scalar output, fast and compact. Multi-vector ColBERT: ~200 vectors per doc (per token), max-sim aggregation across tokens, more accurate on long text. ColBERT is still a dense-embedding model; only the pooling step is dropped. The pipeline otherwise looks like any other BERT encoder.
11.26.summary: ColBERT keeps every BERT token's contextual embedding (~200 per doc, ~5 per query) instead of pooling, enabling token-granularity late interaction.
queries: how many vectors per document in ColBERT
what is late interaction in retrieval
how does ColBERT differ from a bi-encoder
BERT token embeddings retained for retrieval
what makes ColBERT a multi-vector model

11.26.text:
Max-sim: for each query token, find the best matching doc token, then sum. Given Q query vectors and D doc vectors, compute pairwise cosine (or dot) similarities - a Q x D matrix. For each query token, take the maximum similarity across all doc tokens. Sum those maxes over the query tokens. This is 'max-sim over late interaction'. It rewards a doc that matches each query token somewhere, even if no single doc token is a perfect match for everything. Formula:

score(q, d) = sum over i in query tokens of  max over j in doc tokens of  q_i · d_j

Sum of per-query-token max similarities against all doc tokens. Max-sim keeps the best signal per query token. A doc that matches 'cats' perfectly but does not mention 'genetics' at all scores lower than one that hits both, even if neither match is perfect.
11.26.summary: Max-sim score = sum over query tokens of the maximum similarity to any doc token; rewards docs that match each query token somewhere.
queries: what is the late interaction scoring formula
sum of max similarities over query tokens
why use max not average across doc tokens
Q x D similarity matrix scoring
how does multi-vector retrieval rank documents
max over j of q_i dot d_j
advantages of max aggregation over mean pooling for retrieval

11.26.text:
Max-sim walkthrough on the cat corpus. Query 'cats live outside' (3 tokens). Doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' (5 tokens). Compute the 3x5 similarity matrix, highlight the max in each row, sum them.

Grid (rows = query tokens, cols = doc tokens):
              Cats   Are   Small  Carnivores  That    Max
Cats          0.94   0.22  0.18   0.35        0.21    0.94
Live          0.11   0.17  0.15   0.25        0.28    0.28
Outside       0.19   0.14  0.21   0.17        0.26    0.26

Score = 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48. Strong pick on 'cats' drags the overall score up. No doc token is perfect for 'live' or 'outside', but each query token is still scored against its nearest neighbor. This robustness is what single-vector cosine throws away.
11.26.summary: Worked example: query 'cats live outside' vs doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' yields max-sim score 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48.
queries: how to calculate max-sim score by hand
query doc token similarity grid example
how max picks per row in late interaction
showing max-sim aggregation on cat corpus
how late interaction handles imperfect matches
max per row sum scoring example

11.26.text: Storage cost: N x tokens x d bytes (20 to 100x larger). The tradeoff is memory. Single-vector retrieval stores one 768-dim vector per doc. Multi-vector stores one per token. At ~200 tokens/doc that is 200x more vectors. Compression (PQ on the token vectors) and shorter vector widths reduce overhead to 20-50x in practice, but ColBERT-style indexes are always much bigger. Memory at N = 1M, d = 768, 200 tokens/doc: Single-vector float32 = 3 GB (1x baseline). ColBERT raw float32 = 600 GB (~200x larger). ColBERT compressed via PQ = ~60 GB (~20x larger). Multi-vector storage = N · tokens · d bytes, typical 20-100x single-vector footprint. PQ and ColBERTv2's residual compression take it to ~20x at good quality. Multi-vector is a great pick when retrieval quality matters more than cost; for 1B-scale workloads expect to pay for extra nodes.
11.26.summary: Multi-vector indexes cost N·tokens·d bytes - 200x raw, ~20x with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression - so 1M docs goes from 3 GB single-vector to 60-600 GB.
queries: how much memory does ColBERT use
N times tokens times d storage formula
is ColBERT worth the storage cost
storage tradeoff ColBERT vs single vector

11.26.text: Production support: Vespa, Qdrant native multi-vector, Elasticsearch. A few production systems handle multi-vector natively. Vespa: native tensor fields with max-sim as a built-in ranking expression, since v7 in Yahoo production. Qdrant multi-vector collections since v1.10 (July 2024), per-vector payload. Elasticsearch nested dense_vector fields with script-score max-sim, 8.13+. Weaviate multi-vector preview via RAG module experiments since 1.25. Multi-vector is table stakes in most production systems now; Vespa and Qdrant are common production picks. Pinecone and pgvector currently do not support it natively. Worth it for RAG on long documents, search over dense technical corpora, and any case where single-vector retrieval has been quality-limited. Swap is straightforward where supported.
11.26.summary: Production multi-vector support: Vespa tensor fields, Qdrant v1.10+, Elasticsearch 8.13 nested dense_vector, Weaviate preview - Pinecone and pgvector lack it.
queries: which vector databases support ColBERT natively
production support for late interaction retrieval
comparing ColBERT support across vector DBs

11.26.text: Diagram: 3x5 token-level similarity grid for ColBERT max-sim. Rows are query tokens (Cats, Live, Outside); columns are doc tokens (Cats, Are, Small, Carnivores, That). Each cell shows the cosine similarity between that query token and doc token: row 1 [0.94, 0.22, 0.18, 0.35, 0.21] max 0.94; row 2 [0.11, 0.17, 0.15, 0.25, 0.28] max 0.28; row 3 [0.19, 0.14, 0.21, 0.17, 0.26] max 0.26. The max in each row is highlighted in green. The right-most 'Max' column accumulates row maxes; their sum (0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48) is the ColBERT max-sim score for this (query, doc) pair. The visual makes clear that each query token picks its single best doc-token match independently.
11.26.summary: Visualization of the 3x5 ColBERT similarity grid - query tokens on rows, doc tokens on columns, row maxes highlighted, summed to score 1.48.
queries: how to draw a ColBERT score table
highlighted row max in similarity matrix
what does the ColBERT scoring matrix look like

11.27.text: Embedding Lifecycle & Re-embedding covers what to do when the embedding model that produced your indexed vectors becomes outdated. Example: 500M docs indexed with OpenAI ada-002 (1536 dims) two years ago, now text-embedding-3-large (3072 dims) is the default. Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned so you cannot mix dims in one index. Three migration options: (1) re-embed everything and swap (clean cut-over, ~$32,500 one-time at 500M docs * 500 tokens * $0.00013/1K, ~1 week, requires retained source text); (2) parallel indexes with dual-write, backfill, shadow-query, cutover (low-risk, double storage during overlap, can flip back); (3) pin the old model forever (short-term cheap, fails on provider deprecation, silent drift, new domains, missed capability). Drift monitoring is the prerequisite for all three: keep a 1K-10K (query, relevant-doc) ground-truth set, run weekly recall@10, monitor embedding distribution per ingest, lock model version.
11.27.summary: Migration strategies and drift monitoring for production vector indexes when embedding models go stale.
queries: how do I handle outdated embedding models in production
what happens when OpenAI deprecates ada-002
vector database migration to new embedding model
how to switch embedding models without downtime
dimension mismatch between old and new embeddings
should I pin my embedding model version
ground truth eval set for embeddings

11.27.text: You indexed 500M vectors two years ago. The model has moved on. Two years ago the team shipped a retrieval system with 500M documents indexed via OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 (d = 1536). Today that model is deprecated and text-embedding-3-large (d = 3072) is the new default. Every vector in the existing index is wrong in the sense that it was produced by an encoder that is no longer the state of the art. Timeline: 2024 - 500M docs embedded with ada-002 (d=1536). 2025 - OpenAI upgrades to text-embedding-3-large (d=3072). 2026 - ada-002 announced deprecated. Production system runs on two-year-old encoders. This is the most common silent pain in production vector search: models change, and what used to be a state-of-the-art embedding decays into yesterday's encoder.
11.27.summary: Sets up the lifecycle problem: an indexed corpus locked to a now-deprecated encoder.
queries: why does my embedding model become outdated
what is the embedding lifecycle problem
OpenAI deprecated my embedding model what now
model went out of date production retrieval
two year old embeddings are they still good
what happens when embedding model is deprecated
old embedding model new model migration trigger
when do I need to re-embed my corpus

11.27.text: Dimension mismatch is a hard migration. The new model outputs a different vector dimension. ada-002 emits 1536 dims; text-embedding-3-large emits 3072. Vector DBs pin the collection to one dimension at creation time - you cannot mix 1536-dim vectors with 3072-dim vectors in the same index. Old index: 1536 dims (ada-002). New index: 3072 dims (text-embedding-3-large). Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned. No mixing 1536-dim and 3072-dim in the same index. Any migration starts with creating a new collection. Matryoshka embeddings (chapter 11.16) partly ease this: truncating a 3072-dim Matryoshka to 1536 is still a sensible embedding, though not from the older model.
11.27.summary: Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned, so swapping models with different dims forces a new index.
queries: why can't I mix different embedding dimensions in one index
how to migrate when embedding dimension changes
dimension mismatch between old and new embeddings
do I need a new collection for new model
why is dimension change a hard migration
creating new collection for upgraded encoder

11.27.text: Option 1: re-embed everything, swap indexes. The conceptually clean option. Keep the source text around (if you still have it), run every doc through the new model, build a fresh index, atomically swap. You pay the full re-embed cost one time. Re-embed cost estimate at 500M docs, 500 tokens each: Tokens: 500M * 500 = 250 billion. Price: $0.00013 per 1K tokens (text-embedding-3-large). Total: ~$32,500 one-time bill. Time: ~1 week at API rate limits, parallelized. Requires retained source text (huge gotcha if you dropped it). Cost scales linearly with N and token budget. Clean cut-over, no two versions to maintain. Re-embedding is often the cheapest option measured over a year. The upfront bill is what blocks it - engineering teams are reluctant to spend five figures on something that looks like cleanup.
11.27.summary: Re-embed all docs through the new model, build fresh index, atomic swap; ~$32,500 for 500M docs * 500 tokens.
queries: how much does it cost to re-embed 500 million documents
how long does re-embedding 500M docs take
do I need to keep source text for re-embedding
is re-embedding the cheapest migration option
swap old index for new index strategy

11.27.text: Option 2: parallel indexes during migration, serve from old, populate new. Stand up the new index alongside the old. Dual-write every new or updated doc to both. Backfill the old corpus to the new index in the background. Compare quality on real traffic by shadow-querying. When the new index is caught up and its quality is verified, flip traffic. Then deprecate the old one. Parallel migration timeline: Week 0 - create new collection, start dual-write on new docs. Week 1-3 - backfill old corpus into new index in background. Week 4 - shadow-query both, measure recall delta. Week 5 - cutover traffic to new index. Week 6 - retire old index. Traffic stays on the old index the whole time. Double storage cost during the overlap window. Quality can be flipped back if the new index underperforms. This is the standard low-risk path. It costs more - you run two indexes for weeks - but regressions never reach production traffic.
11.27.summary: Run new index in parallel with old; dual-write, backfill, shadow-query, cutover - low risk but double storage during overlap.
queries: how to migrate vector index without downtime
backfill old corpus into new index
rollback embedding migration if quality drops
how long does parallel index migration take

11.27.text: Option 3: pin the old model forever (pin and pray). The do-nothing option. Pin the embedding model version, keep using it, never migrate. Works until the provider deprecates the model or drift quietly erodes quality. Many teams end up here because upgrading feels optional; a year later they discover retrieval quality has decayed and they now face a forced migration under deadline pressure. Failure modes of pinning: Provider deprecation - vendor sunsets the old model, forced migration under a deadline. Silent drift - model did not change but the domain did, recall decays over time. New language / domain - content types the old model never saw are served poorly. Missed capability - competitors ship features (longer context, better multilingual) that the pinned model cannot match. Pinning is short-term cheap, long-term expensive. Works fine for 6-12 months, pain compounds after. Drift is invisible unless you monitor it. Pinning is a valid choice as long as you plan the exit.
11.27.summary: Pinning the old model is cheap short-term but fails to provider deprecation, silent drift, and missed capabilities.
queries: should I pin my embedding model version
what happens if I never upgrade my embedding model
provider sunsets embedding model what to do
risks of not migrating embedding model
when does drift start hurting retrieval

11.27.text: Drift monitoring: periodic ground-truth evals. The cure for silent quality decay is explicit monitoring. Keep a held-out set of (query, relevant-doc) pairs. Every week or month, run the current index against the eval set and record recall@k. Any sudden drop signals either a model change, a content change, or an index regression - each demands a different response. Drift monitoring stack: Ground-truth eval set - 1K to 10K (query, relevant-doc) pairs, refreshed quarterly. Weekly recall@10 run - run the eval, record the curve, alert on regression. Content-change monitor - track the embedding distribution (mean, variance) per ingest batch. Model-change signal - lock embedding model version, surface drift when you bump it. Monitor recall, drift, eval - or discover regression after users notice. Ground-truth sampling is cheap; a quality regression is not. Whatever migration option you pick, drift monitoring is the common prerequisite. Without it, none of the other three can be operated safely.
11.27.summary: Held-out eval set + weekly recall@10 + distribution monitoring detects model, content, or index regressions before users do.

how to catch silent quality decay
