why LLM answers can't be verified
limits of GPT in real apps

12.1.text: Fine-tuning doesn't solve the problem. Comparison of Fine-Tuning vs RAG across three properties: Cost - fine-tuning costs tens of thousands of dollars per refresh, RAG costs cents per query. Freshness - fine-tuning is frozen at training time, RAG reflects index updates instantly. Traceability - fine-tuning has no source attribution, RAG cites the chunk used. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).
12.1.summary: Fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable; RAG flips every property by keeping knowledge outside the weights.
queries: why not just fine-tune the model
how often can you fine-tune an LLM
why is RAG better than fine-tuning
when to fine-tune vs use RAG

12.1.text: RAG grounds the answer in retrieved documents with a tiny architectural change: before the model answers, fetch the relevant documents and stuff them into the prompt. The model now reads first, answers second. The flow has three steps: User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. Instead of asking the model from memory, we ground its answer in retrieved documents. The model reads the docs, then answers, anchored to what the docs say.
12.1.summary: RAG works by retrieving relevant documents and inserting them into the prompt so the LLM reads first and answers second.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work
what does grounding mean in LLMs
how does RAG change the prompt
how does LLM use retrieved docs
what happens before LLM generates in RAG

12.1.text: Same question, two answers. Question: 'What is our refund policy?' Bare LLM (MADE UP): 'Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard.' RAG (CITED): 'Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans.' The cited source chunk: '[doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated.' Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Side-by-side example shows a bare LLM inventing a fake 30-day refund policy while RAG returns the real 14-day policy with citation.
queries: what does a citation look like in RAG
bare LLM made up answer example
how to tell if LLM is hallucinating
showing the source chunk to user
real vs invented numbers in LLM

12.1.text: 5 reasons production systems use RAG: (1) Knowledge Cutoff - Solved; index is fresh, re-embed when docs change. (2) Hallucination - Reduced; model is anchored to retrieved text. (3) No Citation - Solved; cite the exact chunk used. (4) Private Data - Solved; index your own docs, no fine-tune needed. (5) Cheap To Refresh - re-embed only changed docs; update individual chunks with no full retrain. Every failure mode from sub-step 1 gets a clean answer, and refresh becomes nearly free.
12.1.summary: RAG solves cutoff, citation, and private data, reduces hallucination, and makes refresh cheap by re-embedding only changed chunks.
queries: why companies use RAG over fine-tuning
RAG vs fine-tune for private docs

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 - Why LLMs Need Retrieval. Bare LLMs have five inherent failures: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data access, and stale facts. Fine-tuning doesn't fix this - it's costly (tens of thousands per refresh), frozen at training time, and untraceable. RAG flips each property: cents per query, instant index updates, citations to source chunks. The architecture is a small change - retrieve relevant docs first, then let the LLM read and answer. A refund-policy example shows the bare LLM inventing a 30-day guarantee while RAG returns the real 14-day policy with [doc-4] citation. Production systems use RAG because it solves cutoff, citation, and private data, reduces hallucination, and re-embedding only changed chunks makes refresh nearly free.
12.1.summary: Bare LLMs fail in production; fine-tuning is too expensive and opaque; RAG grounds answers in retrieved docs cheaply, freshly, and with citations.
queries: RAG vs fine-tuning vs bare LLM
key reasons to add retrieval to LLMs
what RAG does for production apps

12.2.text: Chapter 12.2 walks through the Naive RAG pipeline: five stages glued together where Chunk, Embed, and Store run once offline during ingest, and Retrieve and Generate run online on every user query. A document is sliced into ~80-token chunks, each chunk is turned into a dense vector, vectors are stored in an index like HNSW, the user query is embedded and used to retrieve the top-k most similar chunks by cosine similarity, and finally those chunks are packed into a prompt template with an 'if docs don't answer, say I don't know' instruction so the LLM produces a grounded, citable answer. Every advanced RAG variant (rerankers, hybrid search, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: Overview of the 5-stage naive RAG pipeline split into offline ingest (chunk, embed, store) and online query (retrieve, generate).

12.2.text: Naive RAG is five stages glued together. Three stages run once during ingest (offline): Chunk, Embed, Store. Two stages run on every user query (online): Retrieve, Generate. The phase split matters because the offline work is amortized across all future queries, while the online work determines per-query latency. Once you see this shape, every variant in the field is just a tweak to one of these five boxes. Pipeline: Chunk (Doc → Chunks) → Embed (Chunks → Vectors) → Store (Vectors → Index) → Retrieve (Query → Top-K) → Generate (Top-K → Answer).
12.2.summary: Introduces RAG as five stages with three offline ingest steps and two online query steps.

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk documents into pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit. We cut them into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page, sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens each. Chunk 1: 'To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the Reset Password link and enter your current password to verify identity.' Chunk 2: 'An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring.' Chunk 3: 'Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed.' Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) are covered in detail in chapters 12.4-12.10.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 of RAG: slice documents into ~80-token chunks so each chunk is about one topic.

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn each chunk into a vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning. Similar chunks have similar vectors. Examples from doc-1: chunk 1 → [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58]; chunk 2 → [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62]; chunk 3 → [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings were covered in Section 5.2 (turning text into dense vectors). RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings (more in chapter 12.11). Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; only 8 dims are shown here for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 of RAG: embed each chunk into a dense vector with similar chunks having similar vectors.

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store vectors in a vector database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Example index (HNSW): 3 chunks from doc-1 plus ~1042 chunks from 29 other docs, for 1045 chunks indexed total (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varies by doc length). Stored entries: [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...]; ... + 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) are covered in Section 11. The index turns brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search - milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 of RAG: store chunk vectors in an ANN index like HNSW for fast retrieval.

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve the top-K most similar chunks. The query is embedded by the same model used at ingest. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Example query: 'How do I reset my password?' Query vector: [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. Top-3 most similar chunks by cosine similarity: [doc-1, chunk 1] score 0.91, [doc-1, chunk 2] score 0.84, [doc-1, chunk 3] score 0.77.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 of RAG: embed the query, rank chunks by cosine similarity, return top-K (often 20-50 before reranking).

12.2.text:
Stage 5: Pack chunks into prompt + LLM generates answer. The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click...
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with...
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new...

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Generated grounded answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 of RAG: stuff retrieved chunks into a prompt template with an 'I don't know' fallback so the LLM produces a grounded, cited answer.

12.1.text: Fine-tuning doesn't solve the problem. The obvious first answer is 'just fine-tune the model on our data.' In practice, fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).

12.1.text: Same question, two answers. Question: 'What is our refund policy?' Bare LLM (MADE UP): 'Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard.' RAG (CITED): 'Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans.' Source chunk shown: [doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated. Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Worked refund-policy example showing the bare LLM inventing a 30-day policy while RAG cites doc-4 with the real 14-day rule.
queries: side by side LLM with and without retrieval

12.1.text: 5 reasons production systems use RAG. Every failure mode from sub-step 1 gets a clean answer, plus refresh becomes nearly free. Knowledge Cutoff -> Solved: index is fresh; re-embed when docs change. Hallucination -> Reduced: model is anchored to retrieved text. No Citation -> Solved: cite the exact chunk used. Private Data -> Solved: index your own docs; no fine-tune needed. Cheap To Refresh -> Re-embed only changed docs: update individual chunks; no full retrain.
12.1.summary: Five production reasons RAG wins: solves cutoff, reduces hallucination, enables citations, handles private data, and refreshes cheaply per-chunk.
queries: why do production systems use RAG
can RAG handle private company docs
how to refresh a RAG index cheaply
do I need to retrain for RAG updates

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 motivates RAG. Bare LLMs have five inherent limits: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private-data knowledge, and stale facts. Fine-tuning fails as a fix because it is expensive (tens of thousands per refresh), frozen at training time, and gives no source attribution. RAG flips each property: cents per query, instant index updates, and cited chunks. The architecture is User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. A refund-policy example contrasts an invented 30-day answer (bare LLM) with a cited 14-day answer (RAG, doc-4). Net: production systems use RAG because it solves cutoff, reduces hallucination, supplies citations, handles private docs, and refreshes by re-embedding only changed chunks.
12.1.summary: Bare LLMs hit cutoff, hallucination, no-citation, no-private-data, and staleness; fine-tuning is costly and frozen; RAG fixes all five by retrieving and grounding answers in cited docs.

12.2.text: Chapter 12.2 walks through Naive RAG as a five-stage pipeline: Chunk, Embed, Store (three offline ingest stages) plus Retrieve and Generate (two online query stages). It traces a single example query 'How do I reset my password?' through every stage with real chunk text, 8-dimensional vectors, HNSW indexing of 1045 chunks across 30 docs, top-3 cosine similarity retrieval with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77, prompt template assembly with an 'I don't know' refusal instruction, and a final grounded answer carrying a [doc-1] citation. The chapter ends with an end-to-end recap showing all five stages with their data transformations.
12.2.summary: Chapter overview of the naive RAG pipeline as five stages (chunk, embed, store, retrieve, generate) traced through one worked password-reset query.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work end to end
RAG offline ingest vs online query phases
chunk embed store retrieve generate explained
RAG pipeline with password reset query
how to build a basic RAG system
what are the stages of a RAG system
ingest pipeline vs query pipeline RAG
how chunks vectors and indexes fit together

12.2.text: Naive RAG is five stages glued together. Three run once during ingest (offline): Chunk, Embed, Store. Two run on every query (online): Retrieve, Generate. The diagram shows boxes labeled Chunk (Doc to Chunks), Embed (Chunks to Vectors), Store (Vectors to Index), Retrieve (Query to Top-K), Generate (Top-K to Answer), with arrows connecting adjacent stages. The first three are tinted cyan for the offline ingest phase; the last two are tinted yellow for the online query phase, separated by a dashed divider. Once you see the shape, every variant in the field is just a tweak to one of these boxes.
12.2.summary: Introduces the five-stage RAG pipeline split into three offline ingest stages and two online query stages.
queries: what are the five stages of RAG
which RAG steps run at query time
chunk embed store retrieve generate order
how many stages in a RAG system

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk documents into pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit. We cut them into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens each. Chunk 1: 'To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the Reset Password link and enter your current password to verify identity.' Chunk 2: 'An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring.' Chunk 3: 'Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed.' Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) covered in chapters 12.4-12.10.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 of the RAG pipeline slices long documents into smaller single-topic chunks, illustrated with three ~80-token chunks from a password-reset doc.
queries: how big should a RAG chunk be
what is a chunk in vector search

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn each chunk into a vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning; similar chunks have similar vectors. Worked vectors for the three password-reset chunks: chunk 1 -> [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58]; chunk 2 -> [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62]; chunk 3 -> [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings (covered in Section 5.2) turn text into dense vectors. RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings (more in chapter 12.11). Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; 8-dim shown here for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 embeds each chunk into a dense vector, shown with concrete 8-dim vectors for the three password-reset chunks.
queries: how does embedding work for retrieval

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store vectors in a vector database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Example index contents (HNSW): 3 chunks from doc-1 plus ~1042 chunks from 29 other docs, 1045 chunks indexed total (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varies by doc length). Stored entries: [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...]; ... + 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) covered in Section 11. The index turns brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search - milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 loads chunk vectors into a vector index (HNSW) so retrieval becomes approximate nearest-neighbor search instead of brute-force.
queries: what is a vector database in RAG
how many chunks in a RAG index
ANN vs brute force kNN retrieval
what does the vector index store

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve the top-K most similar chunks. Query is embedded by the same model. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Worked example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' embeds to [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. Top-3 most similar chunks by cosine similarity: [doc-1, chunk 1] score 0.91, [doc-1, chunk 2] score 0.84, [doc-1, chunk 3] score 0.77. Each retrieved chunk preview shows the first 40 characters of its text.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 embeds the query and retrieves the top-3 chunks by cosine similarity, with concrete scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77.
queries: how does query get matched to chunks
embed query same model as chunks

12.2.text: Stage 5: Pack chunks into prompt plus LLM generates answer. The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know". Documentation: [doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click... [doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with... [doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new... Question: How do I reset my password? Answer:'. Generated grounded answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 packs retrieved chunks plus the question into a prompt template with a refusal instruction and the LLM produces a grounded cited answer.
queries: I don't know instruction reduce hallucination
how does LLM use retrieved chunks

12.2.text: End to end: one query, five stages with real data flowing left to right. Query: 'How do I reset my password?' - Chunk: [doc-1] split into 3 chunks. Embed: 3 chunks -> 3 vectors (8-dim). Store: indexed in HNSW. Retrieve: query -> top-3 chunks (scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77). Generate: grounded answer with [doc-1] citation. Final answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password. [doc-1]'. That's naive RAG end to end. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: End-to-end recap of the five RAG stages running on one password-reset query with concrete data at each stage.
queries: all five stages of RAG with data
what does a RAG query look like end to end
RAG recap chunk embed store retrieve generate
rerankers hybrid query rewriting as variants of naive RAG
agents are tweaks on naive RAG
where do RAG variants fit in the pipeline
stages of RAG with concrete numbers
how do RAG enhancements relate to base pipeline

12.3.text: Naive RAG has 7 failure modes. The pipeline from 12.2 works on a happy-path query, but production traffic exposes 7 named ways it can fail: Bad Chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), Low Recall (lexical mismatch hides relevant docs), Lost In Middle (long contexts skip middle chunks), No Citation (answers without source attribution), Hallucination (fills gaps with confident invention), Stale Index (doc edits not reflected until re-embed), and Cost / Latency (per-query LLM tokens add up at scale).
12.3.summary: Overview of the 7 named failure modes that break naive RAG in production.
queries: what are the failure modes of naive RAG
why does RAG fail in production

12.3.text: Failure 1: Bad Chunking. Fixed-size chunking sliced a single sentence across two chunks, so neither chunk contains both halves of the answer. User Query: 'How long do I have to use the password reset link?' Chunk 1 (mid-sentence split): '...Click the link in the email...' Chunk 2 (mid-sentence split): '...within 24 hours to set a new password...' The 24-hour limit is split across two chunks. Neither contains both 'reset link' AND '24 hours' near each other. Retrieved chunk: '...Click the link in the email...' Model output: 'I don't see a time limit in the docs.' Wrong - the 24-hour limit IS in the doc, but bad chunking hid it. Chapters 12.4-12.10 (chunking strategies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Bad chunking splits a sentence so neither chunk has the full answer, causing the model to miss the 24-hour password reset window.
queries: what is bad chunking in RAG
how does fixed size chunking fail

12.3.text: Failure 2: Low Recall. Embedding-only retrieval is supposed to handle synonyms. In practice, 'sign in' and 'log in' don't always cluster tightly enough, and the right doc gets missed. User Query: 'I can't sign in to my account'. Expected Match: Doc 'Login Troubleshooting' - 'If you can't log in, check your password. Logging in requires a verified email...' (MISSED FROM TOP-3). Top-3 retrieved (embedding-only): [doc-12] 'Sign in to view your billing history.' [doc-19] 'Sign in required to access the API console.' [doc-27] 'Sign in with Google or Apple supported.' Embedding-only retrieval scores docs by vector similarity. 'Log in' vs 'sign in' don't cluster tightly enough. The right doc is missed entirely from top-3. Chapters 12.11-12.14 (hybrid retrieval + rerankers) and 12.15-12.18 (query transformation) fix this.
12.3.summary: Embedding-only retrieval misses the correct 'login troubleshooting' doc because 'sign in' and 'log in' don't cluster tightly in vector space.
queries: what is low recall in RAG
why does embedding retrieval miss docs
sign in vs log in retrieval problem
why is the right doc missed

12.3.text: Failure 3: Lost In The Middle. Even when the answer IS in the retrieved context, long-context models attend more to the start and end than to the middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. The Lost-In-The-Middle attention curve shows a U-shape: attention weight is high at position 1 (0.92) and position 2 (0.78), drops to lows at position 4 (0.28), position 5 (0.18), position 6 (0.20), and position 7 (0.32), then rises again at position 9 (0.82) and position 10 (0.95). The relevant chunk at position 5 is in context but the model skips it. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Long-context models attend most to the start and end of the context, ignoring middle chunks - so a relevant chunk at position 5 of 10 gets skipped.
queries: what is lost in the middle
why do LLMs ignore middle of context
why first and last chunks get attention
how to fix lost in middle

12.3.text: Failure 4: No Citation. The model answered, but the answer has no source markers. Reviewers can't tell which chunk the answer came from, or whether any chunk supports it at all. User Query: 'Why is my account suspended?' Generated answer (no citation): 'Your account may be suspended due to payment failures, terms of service violations, or unusual activity. Contact support for details.' No [doc-X] markers anywhere. Reviewer cannot verify which doc the answer came from. Without a citation, if the model hallucinated half of it, no one can tell. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (citations + groundedness) fix this.
12.3.summary: Without source markers, reviewers can't verify whether the model's answer is grounded in the retrieved docs or partly hallucinated.
queries: can't tell where answer came from

12.3.text: Failure 5: Hallucination On Partial Info. Retrieved context covers SSO for Enterprise plan but says nothing about Pro plan. The model fills the gap with a confident-sounding guess. User Query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' Retrieved context (top-3 chunks): [doc-3] 'SSO is available on Enterprise plan via SAML 2.0.' [doc-3] 'Enterprise plan includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated support.' [doc-5] 'Pricing: Basic $10/mo, Pro $25/mo, Enterprise custom.' Note: no mention of Pro + SSO anywhere. Model output (hallucinated): 'Yes, the Pro plan includes SSO.' This claim is wrong. SSO is Enterprise-only. Model filled in a gap with a plausible-sounding guess. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (refusal + groundedness instruction) and 12.28-12.32 (faithfulness eval) fix this.
12.3.summary: When retrieved context partly covers a topic, the model invents a confident wrong answer to fill the missing piece (SSO on Pro plan).
queries: why model invents facts with retrieval

12.3.text: Failures 6 & 7: Stale Index, Cost, Latency. The last two failures aren't about answer quality - they're about operations. Failure 6 - Stale Index: Doc-7 (Refund Policy) was updated 2 days ago. Sales now allows refunds within 30 days. The embedding index still has the old version (14 days). User query: 'What's our current refund window?' Answer returned (stale): '14 days.' Embedding lifecycle (Section 11.27) and chapters 12.33-12.37 (drift detection) fix this. Failure 7 - Cost & Latency: Per-query cost breakdown: Embedding $0.0001, Retrieval $0.0002, LLM Tokens (4000 ctx) $0.0120, Total Per Query $0.0123. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 800ms generation = 850ms p50. At 1000 QPS, costs $12,300/day. Chapters 12.33-12.37 (caching, cost models, observability) fix this.
12.3.summary: Operational failures: the index goes stale after doc edits, and per-query costs ($0.0123) plus 850ms latency become expensive at 1000 QPS ($12,300/day).
queries: what is stale index in RAG
why does RAG go out of date
cost of LLM tokens in RAG

12.3.text: U-shaped attention curve plotting attention weight (y-axis 0 to 1) against chunk position 1 to 10 (x-axis). Curve starts high at position 1 (0.92), drops through position 2 (0.78) and position 3 (0.52) to a trough at positions 4-7 (around 0.18-0.32), then rises back through position 8 (0.60), position 9 (0.82), and position 10 (0.95). Position 5 is highlighted in red with the label 'Position 5 (Relevant) - Missed' showing where the relevant chunk sits in the attention valley and gets ignored by the model.
12.3.summary: Diagram of the lost-in-the-middle attention curve showing a U-shape where middle chunk positions receive minimal attention.

12.3.text: Chapter 12.3 catalogs the 7 ways naive RAG breaks in production: (1) Bad Chunking splits answers across chunk boundaries; (2) Low Recall - embedding-only retrieval misses synonyms like 'sign in' vs 'log in'; (3) Lost In The Middle - models attend to start and end of context, skipping middle chunks; (4) No Citation - answers lack source markers so reviewers can't verify; (5) Hallucination On Partial Info - model invents to fill gaps in retrieved context; (6) Stale Index - doc edits don't propagate until re-embed; (7) Cost & Latency - per-query LLM tokens add up ($0.0123/query, 850ms p50, $12,300/day at 1000 QPS). Each failure is fixed by later chapters in section 12 (chunking 12.4-12.10, hybrid + rerankers 12.11-12.14, query transformation 12.15-12.18, context packing + groundedness 12.19-12.21, faithfulness eval 12.28-12.32, drift + cost + observability 12.33-12.37).
12.3.summary: Chapter-level summary of the 7 naive RAG failure modes and the later section 12 chapters that fix each one.

12.1.text: Bare LLMs Have Hard Limits. Drop a frontier model into a production app and the cracks appear fast. It does not know about events after its cutoff, makes up facts, can't show its work, and has never seen your private data. These failure modes are inherent to the model alone. Five failure modes: Knowledge Cutoff - trained on data through 2023; can't answer about 2025 events. Hallucination - will confidently invent plausible-sounding facts. No Citations - cannot point to sources for verification. Private Data Unknown - knows nothing about your internal docs. Stale Facts - public info from years ago, not last week.
12.1.summary: Bare LLMs without retrieval have five built-in failure modes: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data access, and stale facts.
queries: what are limits of bare LLMs
LLM doesn't know my private docs
what's wrong with using just an LLM in production
why can't LLM access my company docs

12.1.text: Fine-Tuning Doesn't Solve The Problem. The obvious first answer is 'just fine-tune the model on our data.' In practice, fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable. RAG flips every property. Comparison: Cost - Fine-Tuning: tens of thousands of dollars per refresh; RAG: cents per query. Freshness - Fine-Tuning: frozen at training time; RAG: reflects index updates instantly. Traceability - Fine-Tuning: no source attribution; RAG: cite the chunk used. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).
12.1.summary: Fine-tuning fails as a fix because it's expensive, frozen, and untraceable while RAG is cheap, fresh, and citeable.
queries: should I fine-tune or use RAG
when to use RAG over fine-tuning

12.1.text: RAG: Ground The Answer In Retrieved Documents. The fix is a tiny pipeline change: before the model answers, fetch the relevant documents and stuff them into the prompt. The model now reads first, answers second. Flow: User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. Instead of asking the model from memory, we ground its answer in retrieved documents. The model reads the docs, then answers, anchored to what the docs say.
12.1.summary: RAG inserts a retrieval step before generation so the LLM answers grounded in fetched documents instead of from memory.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work
user question to LLM with retrieval

12.1.text: Same Question, Two Answers. Question: 'What is our refund policy?' Bare LLM (MADE UP): 'Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard.' RAG (CITED): 'Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans.' Source chunk: [doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated. Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Worked example contrasts a bare LLM inventing a fake 30-day refund policy against a RAG answer citing the real 14-day chunk.
queries: side by side comparison LLM RAG
concrete RAG vs no RAG example

12.1.text: 5 Reasons Production Systems Use RAG. Every failure mode from sub-step 1 gets a clean answer, plus refresh becomes nearly free. Knowledge Cutoff -> Solved: index is fresh; re-embed when docs change. Hallucination -> Reduced: model is anchored to retrieved text. No Citation -> Solved: cite the exact chunk used. Private Data -> Solved: index your own docs; no fine-tune needed. Cheap To Refresh -> Re-embed Only Changed Docs: update individual chunks; no full retrain.
12.1.summary: Five production motivations for RAG: solves cutoff, reduces hallucination, enables citations, handles private data, and refreshes cheaply.

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 motivates RAG by showing five inherent failures of bare LLMs (knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data, stale facts), explains why fine-tuning is a poor fix (expensive, frozen, untraceable) versus RAG (cheap per query, fresh on index update, citeable to chunks), introduces the three-step RAG flow (user question -> retrieve docs -> LLM reads docs and answers), shows a concrete refund-policy example where the bare LLM invents a 30-day policy while RAG cites the real 14-day chunk from [doc-4], and lists five production reasons to adopt RAG including cheap re-embedding of only changed docs.
12.1.summary: Chapter overview of why production LLM systems need retrieval-augmented generation instead of bare or fine-tuned models.

12.3.text: Chapter 12.3 catalogs the seven failure modes of naive RAG that production traffic exposes: bad chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), low recall (lexical mismatch like 'sign in' vs 'log in' misses the right doc), lost-in-the-middle (long contexts skip middle chunks due to U-shaped attention), no citation (answers without source markers can't be verified), hallucination on partial info (model fills gaps with confident guesses, e.g. claiming Pro plan includes SSO when only Enterprise does), stale index (doc edits not reflected until re-embed, returning outdated 14-day refund window when policy changed to 30), and cost/latency (~$0.0123 per query, 850ms p50, $12,300/day at 1000 QPS). Each failure points forward to later chapters that fix it.
12.3.summary: Chapter 12.3 catalogs seven named failure modes of naive RAG: bad chunking, low recall, lost-in-the-middle, no citation, hallucination on partial info, stale index, and cost/latency.

where does naive RAG break down

12.3.text: Failure 1: Bad Chunking. Fixed-size chunking sliced a single sentence across two chunks. User query: 'How long do I have to use the password reset link?' Chunk 1 (mid-sentence split): '...Click the link in the email...' Chunk 2 (mid-sentence split): '...within 24 hours to set a new password...' The 24-hour limit is split across two chunks. Neither contains both 'reset link' AND '24 hours' near each other. Retrieved chunk: '...Click the link in the email...' Model output: 'I don't see a time limit in the docs.' Wrong - the 24-hour limit IS in the doc, but bad chunking hid it. Chapters 12.4-12.10 (chunking strategies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Fixed-size chunking can split a single answer sentence across chunks so the model says it doesn't know even though the doc has the info.

12.3.text: Failure 2: Low Recall. Embedding-only retrieval is supposed to handle synonyms, but in practice 'sign in' and 'log in' don't always cluster tightly enough and the right doc gets missed. User query: 'I can't sign in to my account.' Expected match (missed from top-3): Doc 'Login Troubleshooting' - 'If you can't log in, check your password. Logging in requires a verified email...' Top-3 retrieved (embedding-only): [doc-12] 'Sign in to view your billing history,' [doc-19] 'Sign in required to access the API console,' [doc-27] 'Sign in with Google or Apple supported.' Embedding-only retrieval scores docs by vector similarity; 'log in' vs 'sign in' don't cluster tightly enough, so the right doc is missed entirely from top-3. Chapters 12.11-12.14 (hybrid retrieval + rerankers) and 12.15-12.18 (query transformation) fix this.
12.3.summary: Embedding-only retrieval can miss the right doc when synonyms like 'sign in' and 'log in' don't cluster tightly enough in vector space.

12.3.text: Failure 3: Lost In The Middle. Even when the answer IS in the retrieved context, long-context models attend more to the start and end than to the middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. The U-shaped attention curve shows attention weight ~0.92 at position 1, ~0.78 at position 2, dropping to ~0.18 at position 5 (the relevant chunk, missed), back up to ~0.95 at position 10. Model attends most to first and last chunks; middle chunks (positions 4-7) get less attention. The relevant chunk at position 5 is in context, but the model skips it. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Long-context LLMs follow a U-shaped attention curve - they attend to start and end of context but skip middle chunks, so a relevant chunk buried at position 5 can be ignored.

12.3.text: U-shaped attention curve plotting attention weight (0 to 1) against chunk position (1-10). Curve starts high at position 1 (~0.92) and position 2 (~0.78), dips through the middle to a minimum at position 5 (~0.18), and rises back to position 10 (~0.95). Position 5 is highlighted with a red dot labeled 'Position 5 (Relevant)' and 'Missed' - the relevant chunk that gets ignored even though it's in context. The shape demonstrates the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon where long-context models attend most to start and end positions and skip middle chunks.

12.3.text: Failure 4: No Citation. The model answered but the answer has no source markers. Reviewers can't tell which chunk the answer came from or whether any chunk supports it at all. User query: 'Why is my account suspended?' Generated answer (no citation): 'Your account may be suspended due to payment failures, terms of service violations, or unusual activity. Contact support for details.' No [doc-X] markers anywhere. Reviewer cannot verify which doc the answer came from; without a citation, if the model hallucinated half of it, no one can tell. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (citations + groundedness) fix this.

12.3.text: Failure 5: Hallucination On Partial Info. Retrieved context covers SSO for Enterprise plan but says nothing about Pro plan. The model fills the gap with a confident-sounding guess. User query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' Retrieved context (top-3 chunks): [doc-3] 'SSO is available on Enterprise plan via SAML 2.0,' [doc-3] 'Enterprise plan includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated support,' [doc-5] 'Pricing: Basic $10/mo, Pro $25/mo, Enterprise custom.' No mention of Pro + SSO anywhere. Model output (hallucinated): 'Yes, the Pro plan includes SSO.' This claim is wrong - SSO is Enterprise-only. Model filled a gap with a plausible-sounding guess. Chapters 12.19-12.21 (refusal + groundedness instruction) and 12.28-12.32 (faithfulness eval) fix this.
12.3.summary: When retrieved chunks don't cover the question, the model fills the gap with a confident hallucination - here claiming Pro plan has SSO when only Enterprise does.

12.3.text: Failures 6 & 7: Stale Index, Cost & Latency. Failure 6 (Stale Index): doc-7 (Refund Policy) was updated 2 days ago - sales now allows refunds within 30 days - but the embedding index still has the old 14-day version. User query: 'What's our current refund window?' Answer returned (stale): '14 days.' Embedding lifecycle (Section 11.27) and chapters 12.33-12.37 (drift detection) fix this. Failure 7 (Cost & Latency): per-query cost breakdown - Embedding $0.0001, Retrieval $0.0002, LLM Tokens (4000 ctx) $0.0120, Total Per Query $0.0123. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 800ms generation = 850ms p50. At 1000 QPS, costs $12,300/day. Chapters 12.33-12.37 (caching, cost models, observability) fix this.
12.3.summary: Stale index returns outdated answers when docs change before re-embed; cost and latency add up to ~$0.0123 and 850ms per query, or $12,300/day at 1000 QPS.

12.2.text: The Naive RAG Pipeline. Naive RAG is five stages glued together. Three run once during ingest (offline): Chunk, Embed, Store. Two run on every query (online): Retrieve, Generate. Once you see the shape, every variant in the field is just a tweak to one of these boxes. Five stages, two phases. Chunk + Embed + Store are done once during ingest (offline). Retrieve + Generate run on every user query (online).
12.2.summary: Introduces the 5-stage naive RAG pipeline split into offline ingest (Chunk/Embed/Store) and online query (Retrieve/Generate) phases.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work
what are the steps in RAG
what is the shape of a RAG system

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk Documents Into Pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit. We cut them into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page. Sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens each. [doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the 'Reset Password' link and enter your current password to verify identity. [doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring. [doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed. Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) covered later.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 splits long documents into small ~80-token chunks so each chunk focuses on one idea, shown with a password reset doc sliced into 3 chunks.
queries: how big should a chunk be
what is a chunk in RAG
how to split documents for embedding

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn Each Chunk Into A Vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning. Similar chunks have similar vectors. Example vectors (8-dim shown for visibility): [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings turn text into dense vectors. RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings. Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; 8 dims shown here for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 embeds each chunk into a dense vector (typically 1024-dim in production) so semantically similar chunks land near each other.
queries: how does embedding work in RAG

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store Vectors In A Vector Database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Vector Index (HNSW): 3 chunks from doc-1 + ~1042 chunks from 29 other docs. 1045 chunks indexed total (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varies by doc length). [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...]; ... + 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) turn brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search, milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 stores all chunk vectors in a vector index (e.g., HNSW) to enable fast approximate nearest-neighbor search instead of brute-force comparison.
queries: what does the vector DB do

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve The Top-K Most Similar Chunks. Query is embedded by the same model. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Query: 'How do I reset my password?' Query vector: [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. Top-3 Most Similar Chunks (cosine similarity): [doc-1, chunk 1] score 0.91; [doc-1, chunk 2] score 0.84; [doc-1, chunk 3] score 0.77.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 embeds the query with the same model and retrieves top-K chunks ranked by cosine similarity, with example scores 0.91/0.84/0.77.

12.2.text: Stage 5: Pack Chunks Into Prompt + LLM Generates Answer. The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt Template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know". Documentation: [doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click... [doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with... [doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new... Question: How do I reset my password? Answer:' Generated Answer (Grounded): 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 packs retrieved chunks into a prompt template with an 'I don't know' guardrail and the LLM produces a grounded, cited answer.
queries: what is the generation stage RAG
how chunks are inserted into prompt
how does LLM use retrieved chunks
what does the LLM see in RAG

12.2.text: End To End: One Query, Five Stages. Same query, all five stages, real data flowing left to right. Query: 'How do I reset my password?' Chunk: [doc-1] split into 3 chunks. Embed: 3 chunks → 3 vectors (8-dim). Store: Indexed in HNSW. Retrieve: Query → Top-3 chunks (scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77). Generate: Grounded answer with [doc-1] citation. Final Answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password. [doc-1]' That's naive RAG end to end. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: Walks one query through all 5 stages end-to-end producing a cited answer, and frames every advanced RAG variant as a tweak to these five boxes.
queries: stages of RAG with example data
RAG password example end to end

12.2.text: Naive RAG is a 5-stage pipeline: Chunk documents into pieces, Embed each chunk into a dense vector, Store vectors in a vector index (HNSW etc.), Retrieve top-K chunks by cosine similarity to the query embedding, and Generate an answer by packing retrieved chunks into a prompt for the LLM. Chunk/Embed/Store run once offline during ingest; Retrieve/Generate run on every online query. Production typically uses 1024-dim embeddings, top-k 20-50 before reranking, and an 'I don't know' guardrail in the prompt. Every advanced RAG variant - rerankers, hybrid search, query rewriting, agents - is a tweak to one of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: Chapter-level summary of the 5-stage naive RAG pipeline and how all variants are tweaks to its boxes.
queries: how RAG works in one paragraph
chunk embed store retrieve generate explained
what does naive RAG look like

12.2.text: Horizontal flow diagram of the 5-stage RAG pipeline: Chunk, Embed, Store, Retrieve, Generate. First 3 stages tinted cyan and labeled 'Ingest (Offline)', last 2 stages tinted yellow and labeled 'Query Time (Online)'. A dashed divider separates the ingest and query phases. Arrows connect adjacent stages. Each stage box has an annotation: Chunk (Doc → Chunks), Embed (Chunks → Vectors), Store (Vectors → Index), Retrieve (Query → Top-K), Generate (Top-K → Answer).
12.2.summary: Diagram of the naive RAG pipeline showing 5 stages split into offline ingest (cyan) and online query (yellow) phases with annotated arrows.

what does RAG pipeline look like

12.1.text: Bare LLMs Have Hard Limits. Drop a frontier model into a production app and the cracks appear fast. It does not know about events after its cutoff, makes up facts, can't show its work, and has never seen your private data. These failure modes are inherent to the model alone. Five named failure modes: (1) Knowledge Cutoff - trained on data through 2023; can't answer about 2025 events. (2) Hallucination - will confidently invent plausible-sounding facts. (3) No Citations - cannot point to sources for verification. (4) Private Data Unknown - knows nothing about your internal docs. (5) Stale Facts - public info from years ago, not last week.
12.1.summary: Bare LLMs have five built-in failure modes that block production use: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data, and stale facts.

12.1.text: Fine-Tuning Doesn't Solve The Problem. The obvious first answer is 'just fine-tune the model on our data.' In practice, fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable. RAG flips every property. Comparison table: Cost - fine-tuning costs tens of thousands of dollars per refresh; RAG costs cents per query. Freshness - fine-tuning is frozen at training time; RAG reflects index updates instantly. Traceability - fine-tuning has no source attribution; RAG can cite the chunk used. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).
12.1.summary: Fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable; RAG flips each property by keeping knowledge in a retrievable index outside the weights.

12.1.text: RAG: Ground The Answer In Retrieved Documents. The fix is a tiny pipeline change: before the model answers, fetch the relevant documents and stuff them into the prompt. The model now reads first, answers second. Flow: User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. Instead of asking the model from memory, we ground its answer in retrieved documents. The model reads the docs, then answers, anchored to what the docs say.
12.1.summary: RAG inserts a retrieval step before generation so the LLM reads relevant documents first and grounds its answer in them.

12.1.text: Same Question, Two Answers. Question: 'What is our refund policy?' Bare LLM answer (MADE UP): 'Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard.' RAG answer (CITED): 'Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans.' Cited chunk shown: [doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated. Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Side-by-side example shows the bare LLM inventing a 30-day refund policy while RAG cites doc-4 stating the real 14-day window.

12.1.text: 5 Reasons Production Systems Use RAG. Every failure mode from sub-step 1 gets a clean answer, plus refresh becomes nearly free. (1) Knowledge Cutoff -> Solved: index is fresh; re-embed when docs change. (2) Hallucination -> Reduced: model is anchored to retrieved text. (3) No Citation -> Solved: cite the exact chunk used. (4) Private Data -> Solved: index your own docs; no fine-tune needed. (5) Cheap To Refresh -> Re-embed Only Changed Docs: update individual chunks; no full retrain.
12.1.summary: RAG solves or reduces each bare-LLM failure mode and makes refresh cheap because only changed chunks need re-embedding.

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 motivates Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Bare LLMs have five inherent failure modes: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no knowledge of private data, and stale facts. Fine-tuning is the obvious fix but is expensive (tens of thousands per refresh), frozen at training time, and untraceable. RAG flips every property: cents per query, instant freshness via index updates, and chunk-level citations. The mechanism is a tiny pipeline addition - User Question -> Retrieve Docs -> LLM Reads + Answers - that grounds the model in real text. A concrete refund-policy example contrasts a confidently invented 30-day policy from a bare LLM with a cited 14-day policy from RAG. Every original failure mode is solved or reduced and the index can be refreshed by re-embedding only changed chunks.
12.1.summary: Chapter 12.1 explains why bare LLMs fail in production, why fine-tuning doesn't fix it, and how RAG grounds answers in retrieved documents with citations.

12.3.text: Chapter 12.3 catalogs seven named failure modes of naive RAG that show up in production: bad chunking, low recall, lost-in-the-middle, no citation, hallucination on partial info, stale index, and cost/latency. Each failure is shown with a concrete user query, the broken behavior, and a pointer to the later chapters that fix it (12.7-12.13 chunking strategies, 12.16-12.21 hybrid retrieval and query transformation, 12.22-12.24 context packing and citations, 12.31-12.35 faithfulness eval, 12.36-12.40 caching, drift, and observability).
12.3.summary: Chapter 12.3 names seven ways naive RAG breaks in production and maps each to later fixes.

Why is my RAG system bad

12.3.text: Naive RAG has 7 failure modes. The pipeline from 12.2 works on a happy-path query, but production traffic exposes seven named ways it can fail: Bad Chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), Low Recall (lexical mismatch hides relevant docs), Lost In Middle (long contexts skip middle chunks), No Citation (answers without source attribution), Hallucination (fills gaps with confident invention), Stale Index (doc edits not reflected until re-embed), and Cost / Latency (per-query LLM tokens add up at scale).
12.3.summary: Seven named failure modes show how naive RAG breaks under real production traffic.
queries: What are the 7 failure modes of RAG
RAG bad chunking low recall lost in middle
What is stale index in RAG
Production failures of retrieval augmented generation

Doc edits not in RAG index

12.3.text: Failure 1: Bad Chunking. Fixed-size chunking sliced a single sentence across two chunks. User query: 'How long do I have to use the password reset link?' Chunk 1 contains '...Click the link in the email...' and Chunk 2 contains '...within 24 hours to set a new password...'. Neither chunk contains both 'reset link' AND '24 hours' near each other. Retrieved chunk: '...Click the link in the email...'. Model output: 'I don't see a time limit in the docs.' Wrong - the 24-hour limit IS in the doc, but bad chunking hid it. Chapters 12.7-12.13 (chunking strategies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Fixed-size chunking can split a single sentence so the answer disappears from any single chunk.
queries: What is bad chunking in RAG
How does mid-sentence split hurt retrieval
Why does chunk splitting break answers
How chunk size affects RAG accuracy
Why model says I don't see in docs

12.3.text: Failure 2: Low Recall. Embedding-only retrieval should handle synonyms but in practice 'sign in' and 'log in' don't always cluster tightly enough. User query: 'I can't sign in to my account'. Expected match (missed from top-3): doc 'Login Troubleshooting' - 'If you can't log in, check your password. Logging in requires a verified email...'. Top-3 retrieved instead: [doc-12] 'Sign in to view your billing history.', [doc-19] 'Sign in required to access the API console.', [doc-27] 'Sign in with Google or Apple supported.' Embedding-only retrieval scores docs by vector similarity; 'log in' vs 'sign in' don't cluster tightly enough. Chapters 12.16-12.17 (hybrid retrieval + rerankers) and 12.18-12.21 (query transformation) fix this.
12.3.summary: Embedding-only retrieval misses relevant docs when query and doc use different synonyms like 'sign in' vs 'log in'.
queries: What is low recall in RAG
Why does embedding retrieval miss synonyms
Sign in vs log in RAG example

Why right doc missing from top-3

12.3.text: Failure 3: Lost In The Middle. Even when the answer IS in the retrieved context, long-context models attend more to the start and end than to the middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. U-shaped attention curve plots attention weight (0 to 1) vs chunk position (1-10): position 1 weight 0.92, position 2 weight 0.78, position 3 weight 0.52, position 4 weight 0.28, position 5 weight 0.18 (highlighted red as relevant chunk that gets missed), position 6 weight 0.20, position 7 weight 0.32, position 8 weight 0.60, position 9 weight 0.82, position 10 weight 0.95. Middle chunks (positions 4-7) get less attention. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Long-context LLMs follow a U-shaped attention curve, ignoring middle chunks even when they contain the answer.
queries: What is lost in the middle problem

Why first and last chunks dominate

12.3.text: U-shaped attention curve plotting attention weight (y-axis, 0 to 1) against chunk position 1 through 10 (x-axis). Curve is high at start (positions 1-2, weights 0.92 and 0.78) and end (positions 9-10, weights 0.82 and 0.95), dipping in the middle (positions 4-7 with weights between 0.18 and 0.32). Position 5 is highlighted in red with label 'Position 5 (Relevant) Missed' showing that the relevant chunk at position 5 gets the lowest attention weight 0.18 and is skipped by the model.
12.3.summary: Diagram of the U-shaped attention curve over chunk position 1-10 with position 5 highlighted as the missed relevant chunk.
queries: Plot of attention by chunk position

12.3.text: Failure 4: No Citation. The model answered but the answer has no source markers. User query: 'Why is my account suspended?' Generated answer (no citation): 'Your account may be suspended due to payment failures, terms of service violations, or unusual activity. Contact support for details.' No [doc-X] markers anywhere. Reviewer cannot verify which doc the answer came from. Without a citation, if the model hallucinated half of it, no one can tell. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (citations + groundedness) fix this.
12.3.summary: Without inline citations, RAG answers cannot be verified against their source chunks.

12.3.text: Failure 5: Hallucination On Partial Info. Retrieved context covers SSO for Enterprise plan but says nothing about Pro plan. User query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' Retrieved context (top-3 chunks): [doc-3] 'SSO is available on Enterprise plan via SAML 2.0.', [doc-3] 'Enterprise plan includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated support.', [doc-5] 'Pricing: Basic $10/mo, Pro $25/mo, Enterprise custom.' No mention of Pro + SSO anywhere. Model output (hallucinated): 'Yes, the Pro plan includes SSO.' This claim is wrong - SSO is Enterprise-only. The model filled in a gap with a plausible-sounding guess. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (refusal + groundedness instruction) and 12.31-12.35 (faithfulness eval) fix this.
12.3.summary: When retrieved context is silent on the question, the model invents a confident wrong answer instead of refusing.

12.2.text: Chapter 12.2 introduces the Naive RAG Pipeline as five stages: Chunk, Embed, Store (run offline during ingest), then Retrieve and Generate (run online per query). It walks through each stage with real data - splitting a password-reset doc into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens, embedding each into 8-dim vectors, storing all 1045 chunks in an HNSW index, retrieving top-3 by cosine similarity (scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77), and packing them into a prompt template with an 'I don't know' refusal instruction so the LLM generates a grounded, cited answer. Every advanced RAG variant (rerankers, hybrid, query rewriting, agents) is framed as a tweak to one of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: Naive RAG = 5 stages (Chunk, Embed, Store offline; Retrieve, Generate online) shown end-to-end with real password-reset query.

Stage annotations: Chunk = Doc to Chunks; Embed = Chunks to Vectors; Store = Vectors to Index; Retrieve = Query to Top-K; Generate = Top-K to Answer.

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk Documents Into Pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit. We cut them into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page, sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens each. Chunk 1: 'To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the Reset Password link and enter your current password to verify identity.' Chunk 2: 'An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring.' Chunk 3: 'Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed.' Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) covered in chapters 12.7-12.13.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 chunks long docs into ~80-token pieces; doc-1 password reset page becomes 3 chunks.

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn Each Chunk Into A Vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning. Similar chunks have similar vectors. Example vectors (8-dim shown for visibility): chunk 1 = [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58]; chunk 2 = [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62]; chunk 3 = [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings (Section 5.2) turn text into dense vectors. RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings (chapter 12.14). Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; 8 dims are shown here for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 embeds each chunk into a dense vector; production uses ~1024-dim, similar chunks get similar vectors.

How does a chunk become a vector

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store Vectors In A Vector Database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Example index (HNSW): 3 chunks from doc-1 + ~1042 chunks from 29 other docs = 1045 chunks indexed total (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varies by doc length). Stored entries: [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...]; ... + 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) covered in Section 11. The index turns brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search, milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 stores chunk-vectors in a vector index (e.g. HNSW) so retrieval is milliseconds instead of brute-force seconds.
queries: What is the store stage in RAG
Why use a vector database for RAG
How are vectors indexed for retrieval
Vector index vs brute force kNN
How many chunks in a typical RAG index

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve The Top-K Most Similar Chunks. The query is embedded by the same model. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Example query 'How do I reset my password?' becomes vector [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. Top-3 most similar chunks returned: [doc-1, chunk 1] score 0.91, [doc-1, chunk 2] score 0.84, [doc-1, chunk 3] score 0.77.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 embeds the query and uses cosine similarity to pull top-K most similar chunks (typically 20-50 before rerank).

How is the query embedded in RAG

How many chunks does RAG retrieve

12.2.text:
Stage 5: Pack Chunks Into Prompt + LLM Generates Answer. Retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt Template:

```
You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation
to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click...
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with...
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new...

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:
```

Generated grounded answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 stuffs retrieved chunks into a prompt template with refusal instruction; LLM returns grounded, cited answer.

12.2.text: End To End: One Query, Five Stages. Same query 'How do I reset my password?' flows through all five stages with real data: Chunk - [doc-1] split into 3 chunks; Embed - 3 chunks become 3 vectors (8-dim); Store - indexed in HNSW; Retrieve - query returns top-3 chunks (scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77); Generate - grounded answer with [doc-1] citation. Final answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password. [doc-1]'. That's naive RAG end to end. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: End-to-end recap: one query traced through all 5 stages with real numbers, producing a cited grounded answer.

12.4.text: Chapter 12.4 covers parsing in RAG ingestion: turning raw sources (PDFs, HTML, DOCX/PPTX, Markdown, structured APIs) into clean text plus structured metadata. Bad parsing is unrecoverable - no chunker, reranker, or LLM can fix corrupted text. Production RAG needs format-specific parsers for at least five families, must handle three PDF failure modes (two-column layout, flattened tables, OCR errors), must strip HTML boilerplate (nav, sidebar, ads, footer) using tools like Readability or Trafilatura, and must extract a metadata sidecar (source_url, title, doc_type, section_path, updated_at, author, permissions, language, version) for filtering, citations, permission gating, and multilingual routing. Five named failure modes form the checklist: two-column PDF, tables flattened, OCR errors, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata.
12.4.summary: Chapter 12.4 teaches that parsing converts raw sources into clean text plus metadata, and that format-specific parsers plus a metadata sidecar are required to avoid unrecoverable recall loss.

12.4.text: Bad parsing is unrecoverable - the garbage-in problem. The cleanest chunker, the strongest reranker, and the smartest LLM cannot recover what the parser already corrupted. If the text coming out of your parser is garbage, recall is gone before chunking even starts. Worked example: a scanned password-reset PDF. Step 1: scanned PDF (image). Source page is a low-resolution scan, not selectable text. Original text reads 'Password reset: click the link in your email within 24 hours.' Step 2: naive OCR output produces 'Pgssword resel: cIick the Iink in your ernail within 24 hours.' rn becomes m, l becomes I, vowels swap. Tokens no longer match. Step 3: embedding lookup. Query 'How do I reset my password?' embedding misses the broken 'Pgssword' tokens. Cosine similarity drops below threshold. Doc is never retrieved. No chunker, reranker, or LLM fixes this. The recall is gone before chunking starts.
12.4.summary: Shows via a scanned password-reset PDF that OCR errors corrupt tokens, drop cosine similarity below threshold, and make the document unretrievable - no downstream component can recover it.

12.4.text: Production RAG ingests from 5+ format families. Before any chunking, embedding, or indexing, raw sources must be turned into clean text plus metadata. Five families and their tools: (1) PDFs - manuals, contracts, research papers - tool: PyMuPDF / Unstructured / Docling. (2) HTML - web pages, wikis, public docs - tool: Readability / Trafilatura. (3) DOCX / PPTX - internal docs, slide decks - tool: python-docx / Unstructured. (4) Markdown - engineering docs, READMEs, knowledge bases - tool: native parsers (mistletoe, marko). (5) Structured APIs - Confluence, Notion, Salesforce, Zendesk, Jira - tool: vendor SDK or REST connector. One parser per family is the minimum. A single shared 'pdf2text' connector across all formats is the most common silent killer of RAG recall in production.
12.4.summary: Lists the five source-format families production RAG must handle and the dedicated parser tools for each, warning that a single shared parser is a silent recall killer.

12.4.text: PDF: three failure modes that silently wreck recall. PDFs are the format where parsers go to die. Failure 1 - two-column PDF layout. Naive parser: '...gravitational lensing distorts || ...the Higgs boson decays into ... light from a quasar across two W bosons...' Layout-aware: 'Gravitational lensing distorts light from a quasar across galaxy clusters.' Fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling) reads columns in reading order. Failure 2 - tables flattened to prose. Naive parser: 'Q1, Q2, Q3, Revenue, 100, 110, 105, Costs, 80, 75, 78'. Layout-aware: structured rows { Q1: { Revenue: 100, Costs: 80 }, Q2: { Revenue: 110, Costs: 75 }, Q3: { Revenue: 105, Costs: 78 } }. Fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table) keeps row/column relationships. Failure 3 - scanned PDF / OCR errors. Naive: 'Pgssword resel: cIick the link. Arnount due: $14O.00'. Fixed: 'Password reset: click the link. Amount due: $140.00'. Fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract, Azure Document Intelligence) fixes rn-to-m, l-to-1, O-to-0 confusions. Tool choice matters. Production RAG uses layout-aware + table-aware + OCR-aware parsers, not pdf2text.
12.4.summary: Three PDF failure modes - two-column layout, flattened tables, and OCR errors - shown with naive-vs-layout-aware outputs and named fixes (Unstructured, Docling, LlamaParse, Tesseract 5, Textract).

12.4.text: HTML: strip the boilerplate or dilute your chunks. Every HTML page wraps its main content in nav bars, sidebars, ads, cookie banners, and footers. If your parser ingests the whole DOM, every chunk gets polluted. Web page zone map: top nav bar - DROP; sidebar ads - DROP; main article content - KEEP; footer + newsletter signup - DROP. Bad chunk (no boilerplate strip): 'Home Products Pricing Subscribe to our newsletter | How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email... © 2026 Acme Inc.' Retrieval matches 'Subscribe' instead of the real answer. The chunk is diluted with junk. Good chunk (Readability extract): 'How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email within 24 hours to set a new password...' Only the main content survives. The embedding now matches the user's intent cleanly. Tools: Readability, Trafilatura, BoilerNet. They detect main-content zones via DOM heuristics and ML. Without them, retrieval matches the boilerplate and pollutes every chunk.
12.4.summary: Explains why HTML pages must have nav, sidebar, ad, and footer boilerplate stripped before chunking, comparing polluted vs Readability-extracted chunks and naming Readability, Trafilatura, and BoilerNet.

12.4.text: Metadata extraction: the required sidecar. Parsing is not just text. Every chunk also needs a structured sidecar of metadata. Skip this and you lose filtering, citations, and permission boundaries for the whole index. Metadata schema example: { source_url: 'https://docs.habuild.com/account/password-reset', title: 'Reset Your Password', doc_type: 'kb_article', section_path: 'Account > Security > Password', updated_at: '2026-04-12T14:22:00Z', author: 'support-team', permissions: ['public', 'logged-in'], language: 'en', version: 'v3.1' }. The three fields production RAG cannot skip are source_url, updated_at, and permissions. Use cases: (1) Filtering - chapter 11.20 filtering uses updated_at > 2026-01-01 to exclude stale docs at query time. (2) Citations - chapter 12.24 uses source_url so the LLM can cite where the answer came from. (3) Permission gating - never retrieve docs whose permissions ACL list doesn't include the current user's role. (4) Multilingual routing - detect language at ingest time, route to per-language indexes for higher recall. Metadata is parsed at ingest, attached to every chunk vector, and queried at retrieval time. Skip it equals no filtering, no citations, no permission boundaries.
12.4.summary: Defines the required metadata sidecar (source_url, title, doc_type, section_path, updated_at, author, permissions, language, version) and shows its four use cases: filtering, citations, permission gating, and multilingual routing.

12.4.text: The five parsing failures every production RAG must handle. This is the checklist every team owns before they touch chunking, embedding, or retrieval. (1) Two-column PDF layout - symptom: sentences jumbled across columns - fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling). (2) Tables flattened to prose - symptom: lost row/column structure - fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table). (3) Scanned PDF / OCR errors - symptom: garbled tokens (rn becomes m, missing diacritics) - fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract). (4) HTML boilerplate not stripped - symptom: chunks polluted with nav, ads, footers - fix: Readability / Trafilatura main-content extraction. (5) Missing or sparse metadata - symptom: no filtering, citations, or permission gating possible - fix: mandatory metadata schema at ingest. Every production RAG has this checklist. Skip one row equals a silent recall killer. Bad parsing is the gap a reranker cannot fix.
12.4.summary: Final checklist of the five parsing failure modes (two-column PDF, flattened tables, OCR errors, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata) with their symptoms and named fixes.

12.5.text: The same knowledge-base article often lives in Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion at the same time. Ingest all three blindly and the same answer becomes three vectors in your index. A top-5 retrieval for 'How do I reset my password?' returns 3 copies of the same chunk plus 2 unrelated docs - no diversity, and most of the context-window budget is wasted on duplicates. Concrete numbers: each duplicate chunk is ~512 tokens, so three duplicates consume 1,536 of a 4,096-token context window, leaving only 2,560 tokens for distinct answers. 3 copies of the same answer equals wasted tokens, zero diversity, and the LLM has nothing distinct to triangulate against. Example sources: Zendesk help center article #4821, Confluence IT runbook page, Notion onboarding doc section - all containing 'Reset Your Password'.
12.5.summary: Shows how duplicate articles across Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion waste 1,536 of 4,096 context tokens and kill retrieval diversity.

12.5.text: The first line of defense is a cryptographic hash. Normalize the text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, then keep a hash table of every hash already seen. If a new doc's hash matches an existing hash, content is byte-identical, so drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize - 'Reset Your Password. Click The Link.' becomes 'reset your password click the link'. Step 2 Hash - sha256(normalized) produces hex digest like a3f2b9... Step 3 Dedup Lookup - hash table check, match equals drop, miss equals keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example: Zendesk hash a3f2b9... kept (first), Confluence hash a3f2b9... dropped, Notion hash a3f2b9... dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute plus O(1) hash-table lookup. Misses: 'Same article with one typo fixed' produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
12.5.summary: Exact-hash dedup with SHA-256 on normalized text catches byte-identical copies cheaply but misses any change as small as a typo.

12.5.text: One typo fix, one re-worded sentence, or one renamed term flips the SHA-256 digest completely. MinHash estimates Jaccard set overlap between shingle sets cheaply, and locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) groups near-duplicates into the same bucket without an O(N^2) pairwise scan. Example pair exact hash misses: Doc A (Zendesk): 'Reset your password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 a3f2b9... Doc B (Confluence): 'Reset the password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 9e1cd4... One word change ('your' to 'the') flips the hash. MinHash + LSH pipeline: Step 1 - 3-gram shingles {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Step 2 - MinHash signature with K=128 hash functions; 125 of 128 positions match equals Jaccard estimate around 0.97. Step 3 - LSH banding 16 bands x 8 rows; Doc A and Doc B hash into the SAME bucket, candidate near-duplicate pair found via sub-linear scan. MinHash + LSH equals sub-linear near-duplicate detection at scale (1B docs feasible). Jaccard threshold tunable (typically 0.85). Catches typo-fixes, re-words, copy-paste edits exact hashing cannot.
12.5.summary: MinHash signatures plus LSH banding (16 bands x 8 rows, K=128) detect near-duplicates by estimating Jaccard overlap without O(N^2) pairwise comparison.
queries: how does LSH work for near duplicates
how to catch typo fix duplicates
locality sensitive hashing for document deduplication
how does banding LSH avoid pairwise comparison

12.5.text: Two docs can say the same thing with almost no shared words. MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Only embedding cosine catches paraphrases - the same meaning in different words. Paraphrase example: KB article (formal): 'To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address.' Forum post (casual): 'Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email.' MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: Cosine >= 0.97 = definite duplicate, drop one. 0.90 <= Cosine < 0.97 = near-duplicate, human review queue. 0.85 <= Cosine < 0.90 = topically similar, keep both. Cosine < 0.85 = distinct, keep all. Cost reality: embedding a full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens times corpus size. For 10M docs at 512 tokens each = 5B tokens = about $500 just to dedup. Use embedding dedup when corpus has paraphrases (KB + forum posts, multi-author docs, translations). Skip when content is byte-stable (PDFs from authoritative sources) and exact hashing + MinHash already cover the threshold.
12.5.summary: Embedding cosine similarity is the only dedup that catches paraphrases (Jaccard 0.08 vs cosine 0.94), with banded thresholds and a ~$500 cost for 10M docs.
