12.5.text: Dedup catches duplicates. Cleaning fixes the bytes themselves. Four steps turn raw extracted text into something the embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. (1) Unicode Normalization (NFC): two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. 'café' composed (5 bytes) vs 'café' decomposed (6 bytes). NFC pass collapses both to the same 5-byte form so hash dedup and embedding lookup agree. (2) Encoding Fix-Up: mojibake from latin-1 / UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Raw bytes rendered wrong show 'naïve résumé' as 'naïve résumé'. ftfy or chardet detects the mismatch and restores 'naïve résumé'. (3) Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks: boilerplate repeated per page bloats chunks. 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47' on every chunk - layout-aware parser strips the repeating per-page header, footer, and watermark band. (4) PII Scrubbing: emails, SSNs, phone numbers, credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. 'Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789' becomes 'Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED]'. Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped - stabilizes eval by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics, keeps PII out of the index.
12.5.summary: Four cleaning steps (Unicode NFC, encoding fix-up via ftfy/chardet, per-page boilerplate stripping, PII redaction with Presidio) make text consistent for embedding and keep PII out of the index.

12.5.text: Deduplication and cleaning is the layer between parsing and chunking. Three dedup tiers: (1) Exact SHA-256 hash on normalized text catches byte-identical copies, O(N) cost, but misses one-character changes. (2) MinHash + LSH on 3-gram shingles with K=128 hash functions and 16 bands x 8 rows catches near-duplicates via Jaccard estimate ~0.97, sub-linear scale to 1B docs, typical threshold 0.85. (3) Embedding cosine catches paraphrases that share no shingles (cosine 0.94 vs Jaccard 0.08), with thresholds: >=0.97 drop, 0.90-0.97 human review, 0.85-0.90 keep both, <0.85 distinct. Cost about $500 for 10M docs. Cleaning has four required steps: Unicode NFC normalization, encoding fix-up (ftfy/chardet), per-page header/footer/watermark stripping, and PII scrubbing (regex + Presidio NER). Skip dedup and you waste context window (1,536 of 4,096 tokens on identical chunks). Skip cleaning and similarity metrics get fooled and PII leaks into the index.
12.5.summary: Chapter overview of three-tier dedup (SHA-256, MinHash+LSH, embedding cosine) plus four cleaning steps (NFC, encoding, boilerplate, PII) that prevent wasted context and leaked PII.

12.5.text: Diagram: MinHash plus LSH pipeline with three stacked stages. Stage 1 tokenizes two near-duplicate docs into 3-gram shingle sets like {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Stage 2 reduces each shingle set to a 128-position MinHash signature so 125 of 128 positions match and the Jaccard estimate lands around 0.97. Stage 3 hashes the signatures through LSH banding with 16 bands of 8 rows so both docs land in the same candidate-pair bucket without an O(N squared) pairwise scan.

PDFs have three silent failure modes: two-column layout jumbling, tables flattened to prose, and OCR errors on scans. HTML pages must be stripped of nav/sidebar/footer boilerplate using tools like Readability or Trafilatura. Every chunk needs a metadata sidecar (source_url, updated_at, permissions, language, version) for filtering, citations, and permission gating. 12.4.text: Production RAG ingests from 5+ format families. Before any chunking, embedding, or indexing, raw sources must be turned into clean text plus metadata. Every production RAG handles five or more format families, each with its own parser and its own failure modes. PDFs (manuals, contracts, research papers) - tools: PyMuPDF / Unstructured / Docling. HTML (web pages, wikis, public docs) - tools: Readability / Trafilatura. DOCX / PPTX (internal docs, slide decks) - tools: python-docx / Unstructured. Markdown (engineering docs, READMEs, knowledge bases) - tools: native parsers (mistletoe, marko). Structured APIs (Confluence, Notion, Salesforce, Zendesk, Jira) - tools: vendor SDK or REST connector. One parser per family is the minimum. A single shared 'pdf2text' connector across all formats is the most common silent killer of RAG recall in production.
12.4.summary: Production RAG must handle five or more source format families - PDF, HTML, DOCX/PPTX, Markdown, structured APIs - each with its own dedicated parser, never a single shared connector.

12.4.text: PDF: three failure modes that silently wreck recall. PDFs are the format where parsers go to die - the same file looks fine to a human and turns into nonsense when read naively. (1) Two-column PDF layout - naive output reads across columns: '...gravitational lensing distorts || ...the Higgs boson decays into ... light from a quasar across two W bosons...' Layout-aware output: 'Gravitational lensing distorts light from a quasar across galaxy clusters.' Fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling) reads columns in reading order. (2) Tables flattened to prose - naive output: 'Q1, Q2, Q3, Revenue, 100, 110, 105, Costs, 80, 75, 78'. Structured output: { Q1: { Revenue: 100, Costs: 80 }, Q2: { Revenue: 110, Costs: 75 }, Q3: { Revenue: 105, Costs: 78 } }. Fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table) keeps row/column relationships. (3) Scanned PDF / OCR errors - naive: 'Pgssword resel: cIick the link. Arnount due: $14O.00'. After: 'Password reset: click the link. Amount due: $140.00'. Fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract, Azure Document Intelligence) fixes rn-to-m, l-to-1, O-to-0 confusions. Tool choice matters - production RAG uses layout-aware + table-aware + OCR-aware parsers, not pdf2text.
12.4.summary: PDFs fail in three silent ways: two-column layouts read across columns, tables flatten to prose, and OCR garbles characters; fixes require layout-aware, table-extracting, and modern OCR parsers.

12.4.text: HTML: strip the boilerplate or dilute your chunks. Every HTML web page wraps its main content in nav bars, sidebars, ads, cookie banners, and footers. If your parser ingests the whole DOM, every chunk gets polluted with boilerplate the user did not ask about. Web page zone map: top nav bar DROP, left sidebar ads DROP, main article content KEEP, footer + newsletter signup DROP. Bad chunk (no boilerplate strip): 'Home Products Pricing Subscribe to our newsletter | How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email... (c) 2026 Acme Inc.' - retrieval matches 'Subscribe' instead of the real answer; the chunk is diluted with junk. Good chunk (Readability extract): 'How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email within 24 hours to set a new password...' - only the main content survives; the embedding now matches the user's intent cleanly. Tools: Readability, Trafilatura, BoilerNet. They detect main-content zones via DOM heuristics and ML. Without them, retrieval matches the boilerplate (nav, sidebar, footer) and pollutes every chunk.
12.4.summary: HTML parsing must strip nav, sidebar, ads, and footer boilerplate using Readability, Trafilatura, or BoilerNet, otherwise chunks get diluted with junk that pollutes retrieval matches.

12.4.text: Metadata extraction: the required sidecar. Parsing is not just text. Every chunk also needs a structured sidecar of metadata: where the doc came from, when it was updated, who is allowed to see it, what language it is in. Skip this and you lose filtering, citations, and permission boundaries for the whole index. Metadata schema: { "source_url": "https://docs.habuild.com/account/password-reset", "title": "Reset Your Password", "doc_type": "kb_article", "section_path": "Account > Security > Password", "updated_at": "2026-04-12T14:22:00Z", "author": "support-team", "permissions": ["public", "logged-in"], "language": "en", "version": "v3.1" }. The three fields production RAG cannot skip are source_url, updated_at, and permissions. Use cases: (1) Filtering - Section 11.20 filtering uses fields like updated_at > 2026-01-01 to exclude stale docs at query time. (2) Citations - Chapter 12.24 uses source_url so the LLM can cite where the answer came from. (3) Permission gating - never retrieve docs whose permissions (ACL list) don't include the current user's role. (4) Multilingual routing - detect language at ingest time, route to per-language indexes for higher recall. Metadata is parsed at ingest, attached to every chunk vector, and queried at retrieval time. Skip it equals no filtering, no citations, no permission boundaries.
12.4.summary: Every parsed chunk needs a metadata sidecar including source_url, updated_at, permissions, language, version - powering filtering, citations, ACL gating, and multilingual routing at retrieval time.

12.4.text: The five parsing failures every production RAG must handle. Five named failure modes turn clean source documents into garbage text - the checklist every team owns before they touch chunking, embedding, or retrieval. Skip one row and you have a silent recall killer. (1) Two-column PDF layout - symptom: sentences jumbled across columns. Fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling). (2) Tables flattened to prose - symptom: lost row/column structure. Fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table). (3) Scanned PDF / OCR errors - symptom: garbled tokens (rn becomes m, missing diacritics). Fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract). (4) HTML boilerplate not stripped - symptom: chunks polluted with nav, ads, footers. Fix: Readability / Trafilatura main-content extraction. (5) Missing or sparse metadata - symptom: no filtering, citations, or permission gating possible. Fix: mandatory metadata schema at ingest. Every production RAG has this checklist. Skip one row equals a silent recall killer. Bad parsing is the gap a reranker cannot fix.
12.4.summary: Five-row checklist of parsing failures - column jumbling, flattened tables, OCR errors, unstripped HTML boilerplate, missing metadata - with the parser/tool fix for each.

12.5.text: The same knowledge-base article often lives in Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion simultaneously. Ingest all three blindly and the same answer becomes three vectors in your index. A top-5 retrieval for 'How do I reset my password?' returns 3 copies of the same chunk plus 2 unrelated docs - no diversity, and most of the context-window budget is wasted on duplicates. Concrete numbers: with a 4,096-token context window and 3 duplicate chunks at ~512 tokens each, 1,536 tokens are wasted on dupes and only 2,560 tokens remain for distinct answers. Three copies of the same answer equals wasted tokens, zero diversity, and the LLM has nothing distinct to triangulate against. Example sources holding the identical 'Reset Your Password' article: Zendesk help center article #4821, Confluence IT runbook page, Notion onboarding doc section.
12.5.summary: Ingesting the same article from multiple source systems creates duplicate vectors that waste retrieval slots and context-window tokens.

12.5.text: Exact-hash dedup is the first line of defense. Normalize the text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, then keep a hash table of every hash already seen. New doc hash matches an existing hash equals byte-identical content, drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize - 'Reset Your Password. Click The Link.' becomes 'reset your password click the link'. Step 2 Hash (SHA-256) - sha256(normalized) produces hex digest a3f2b9... Step 3 Dedup Lookup - hash table check, match equals drop, miss equals keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example with three sources of the same article: Zendesk hash a3f2b9... kept (first), Confluence hash a3f2b9... dropped, Notion hash a3f2b9... dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute plus O(1) hash-table lookup. Misses: 'Same article with one typo fixed' produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
12.5.summary: Exact-hash dedup normalizes text, computes SHA-256, and drops matches in a hash table - cheap O(N) coverage of byte-identical duplicates.

12.5.text: MinHash + LSH catches near-duplicates that exact hashing misses. One typo fix, one re-worded sentence, one renamed term and the SHA-256 digest changes completely. MinHash estimates Jaccard set overlap between shingle sets cheaply, and locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) groups near-duplicates into the same bucket without an O(N^2) pairwise scan. Example pair exact hash misses: Doc A (Zendesk) 'Reset your password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 a3f2b9... versus Doc B (Confluence) 'Reset the password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 9e1cd4... (different). One word change ('your' to 'the') flips the hash. Pipeline: Step 1 3-Gram Shingles - {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Doc A and Doc B share most 3-gram shingles. Step 2 MinHash Signature with K = 128 hash functions - 125 of 128 positions match equals Jaccard estimate around 0.97. Cheap fixed-size signature replaces full shingle-set comparison. Step 3 LSH Banding 16 bands x 8 rows - Doc A and Doc B hash into the SAME bucket, candidate near-duplicate pair found. Sub-linear scan, no O(N^2) all-pairs check needed. MinHash + LSH equals sub-linear near-duplicate detection at scale (1B docs feasible). Jaccard threshold is tunable (typically 0.85). Catches typo-fixes, re-words, and copy-paste edits that exact hashing cannot.
12.5.summary: MinHash + LSH detects near-duplicates by shingling, fixed-size signatures estimating Jaccard, and band-based bucketing for sub-linear scan.
queries: how does MinHash work for near-duplicate detection
why does exact hash miss typo fixes
how to avoid O(N squared) duplicate scan

12.5.text: Embedding dedup is the only way to catch paraphrases - the same meaning in different words. MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Example: KB Article (formal) 'To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address.' versus Forum Post (casual) 'Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email.' MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: Cosine >= 0.97 definite duplicate, drop one keep the other. 0.90 <= Cosine < 0.97 near-duplicate, send to human review queue. 0.85 <= Cosine < 0.90 topically similar, keep both. Cosine < 0.85 distinct documents, keep all. Cost reality: Embedding a full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens times corpus size. For 10M docs at 512 tokens each that is 5B tokens equals about $500 just to dedup. Cheaper than re-embedding twice but a real line item. Use embedding dedup when the corpus has paraphrases (KB + forum posts, multi-author docs, translations). Skip it when content is byte-stable (PDFs from authoritative sources) and exact hashing + MinHash already cover the threshold.
12.5.summary: Embedding cosine dedup catches paraphrases MinHash misses, using a tiered cosine ladder with $500-scale cost for 10M docs.

12.5.text: Cleaning fixes the bytes themselves after dedup. Four steps - Unicode normalization, encoding fix-up, boilerplate stripping, and PII scrubbing - turn raw extracted text into something the embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. Step 1 Unicode Normalization (NFC): Two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. Before: 'café' (composed, 5 bytes) vs 'café' (decomposed, 6 bytes). After: NFC pass collapses both to the same 5-byte form. Hash dedup and embedding lookup now agree. Step 2 Encoding Fix-Up: Mojibake from latin-1 / UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Before: raw bytes rendered wrong 'naïve résumé' shown as 'naïve résumé'. After: ftfy or chardet detects the encoding mismatch and restores 'naïve résumé'. Step 3 Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks: Boilerplate repeated on every page bloats chunks with noise. Before: every chunk starts with 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47'. After: layout-aware parser strips the repeating per-page header, footer, and watermark band. Step 4 PII Scrubbing: Emails, SSNs, phone numbers, and credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. Before: 'Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789.' After: 'Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED]'. Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped. It stabilizes eval scores by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics, and it keeps PII out of the index entirely.
12.5.summary: Four cleaning steps - NFC Unicode, encoding fix-up, boilerplate stripping, PII scrubbing - normalize bytes so embedders, tokenizers, and dedup all agree.

12.5.text: Chapter 12.5 Deduplication & Cleaning covers how to remove duplicate and dirty content before chunking and embedding. The same KB article often lives in multiple systems (Zendesk, Confluence, Notion) so blind ingest wastes context-window tokens and kills retrieval diversity. Three-layer dedup ladder: (1) Exact-hash dedup with SHA-256 over normalized text catches byte-identical copies cheaply in O(N), missing the easy 80%. (2) MinHash + LSH (3-gram shingles, 128-position signatures, 16 bands x 8 rows) catches near-duplicates like typo-fixes and re-words via sub-linear Jaccard estimation. (3) Embedding cosine dedup is the only method that catches paraphrases (formal KB vs casual forum post: Jaccard 0.08 miss, cosine 0.94 hit), using a cosine ladder (>=0.97 drop, 0.90-0.97 review, 0.85-0.90 keep both, <0.85 distinct). After dedup, four cleaning steps (Unicode NFC normalization, encoding fix-up with ftfy/chardet, boilerplate stripping of headers/footers/watermarks, PII scrubbing via regex + Presidio NER) make bytes consistent for the embedder and tokenizer.
12.5.summary: Chapter 12.5 walks through the full RAG ingestion dedup-and-clean ladder: exact hash, MinHash+LSH, embedding cosine, then four cleaning steps.

12.5.text: Diagram for sub 2: MinHash + LSH pipeline shown as three stacked stages. Stage 1 (purple, 3-Gram Shingles): tokenizes two near-duplicate docs into 3-gram shingle sets such as {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Doc A and Doc B share most 3-gram shingles, with one differing. Stage 2 (purple, MinHash Signature with K=128 hash functions): reduces each shingle set to a 128-position fixed-size MinHash signature; 125 of 128 positions match, giving a Jaccard estimate around 0.97. Cheap signature replaces full shingle-set comparison. Stage 3 (green, LSH Banding 16 Bands x 8 Rows): hashes signatures through banded LSH so Doc A and Doc B land in the same candidate-pair bucket. Sub-linear scan, no O(N^2) all-pairs check needed. Arrows connect each stage top to bottom.
12.5.summary: Visualizes the three-stage MinHash+LSH pipeline: shingling, 128-position signature with Jaccard 0.97, and 16x8 LSH banding into same bucket.

12.4.text: Bad parsing is unrecoverable - the garbage-in problem. The cleanest chunker, strongest reranker, and smartest LLM cannot recover what the parser already corrupted. If text coming out of the parser is garbage, recall is gone before chunking even starts. Worked example: a scanned password-reset PDF. Step 1: scanned PDF is a low-resolution image, not selectable text. Source sentence is 'Password reset: click the link in your email within 24 hours.' Step 2: naive OCR output produces 'Pgssword resel: cIick the Iink in your ernail within 24 hours.' Broken text: rn becomes m, l becomes I, vowels swap, tokens no longer match. Step 3: embedding lookup for query 'How do I reset my password?' misses the broken Pgssword tokens, cosine similarity drops below threshold, doc is never retrieved. No chunker, reranker, or LLM fixes this. Recall is gone before chunking starts.
12.4.summary: Bad parsing corrupts text upstream so no downstream component (chunker, reranker, LLM) can recover lost recall.

12.4.text: Chapter 12.4 - Parsing: raw sources to clean text. Parsing is the upstream step that converts heterogeneous documents (PDFs, HTML, DOCX/PPTX, Markdown, structured APIs) into clean text plus a metadata sidecar before chunking, embedding, or indexing. Bad parsing is unrecoverable - no chunker, reranker, or LLM can fix garbage output (scanned password-reset PDF example: OCR mangles 'Password' into 'Pgssword' and embedding lookup misses). Production RAG handles 5+ format families with a dedicated parser per family (PyMuPDF/Unstructured/Docling for PDFs, Readability/Trafilatura for HTML, python-docx for Office, mistletoe/marko for Markdown, vendor SDKs for Confluence/Notion/Salesforce/Zendesk/Jira). PDFs have three killer failure modes - two-column layout jumbling, tables flattened to prose, and OCR confusions (rn->m, l->1, O->0) - each fixed by layout-aware, table-aware, or modern-OCR parsers (LlamaParse, Tesseract 5, AWS Textract). HTML must be boilerplate-stripped via Readability/Trafilatura/BoilerNet so nav, sidebar, ads, and footer don't pollute chunks. Every chunk also needs structured metadata (source_url, title, doc_type, section_path, updated_at, author, permissions, language, version) for filtering, citations, permission gating, and multilingual routing. The five named failure modes - two-column layout, table flattening, OCR errors, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata - form the checklist every production team owns; skip any row and recall dies silently before chunking starts.
12.4.summary: Chapter overview: parsing converts diverse sources into clean text + metadata, and five failure modes (PDF layout, tables, OCR, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata) must each have a dedicated fix or recall dies silently.

12.5.text: The same knowledge-base article often lives in Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion at the same time. Ingest all three blindly and the same answer becomes three vectors in the index. A top-5 retrieval for 'How do I reset my password?' returns 3 copies of the same chunk plus 2 unrelated docs - no diversity, and most of the context-window budget is wasted on duplicates. Example sources: Zendesk help center article #4821, Confluence IT runbook page, Notion onboarding doc section, each containing 'Reset Your Password'. Three duplicate chunks at ~512 tokens each consume 1,536 tokens of a 4,096-token context window, leaving only 2,560 tokens for distinct answers. 3 copies of the same answer equals wasted tokens, zero diversity, and the LLM has nothing distinct to triangulate against.
12.5.summary: Duplicate articles across source systems eat context-window budget and kill retrieval diversity.
queries: same article in Zendesk Confluence Notion
why ingest from multiple sources causes problems
password reset article in three systems

12.5.text: First line of defense: a cryptographic hash. Normalize the text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, keep a hash table of every hash already seen. New doc hash matches existing hash = byte-identical content, drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize: 'Reset Your Password. Click The Link.' becomes 'reset your password click the link'. Step 2 Hash: sha256(normalized) produces hex digest a3f2b9... Step 3 Dedup lookup: hash table check, match = drop, miss = keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example - Zendesk hash a3f2b9... kept (first), Confluence hash a3f2b9... dropped, Notion hash a3f2b9... dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute + O(1) hash-table lookup. Miss case: 'Same article with one typo fixed' produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
12.5.summary: Exact-hash deduplication via SHA-256 normalize-and-lookup catches byte-identical duplicates cheaply but misses typo-level variants.
queries: cost of exact hash dedup O(N)
what does exact hash dedup miss

12.5.text: One typo fix, one re-worded sentence, one renamed term and the SHA-256 digest changes completely. MinHash estimates Jaccard set overlap between shingle sets cheaply; locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) groups near-duplicates into the same bucket without an O(N^2) pairwise scan. Near-duplicate pair exact hash misses: Doc A (Zendesk) 'Reset your password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 a3f2b9... vs Doc B (Confluence) 'Reset the password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours.' SHA-256 9e1cd4... (different). One word change ('your' to 'the') flips the hash. Pipeline: Step 1 - 3-gram shingles: {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Step 2 - MinHash signature with K=128 hash functions: 125 of 128 positions match = Jaccard estimate ~0.97. Step 3 - LSH banding (16 bands x 8 rows): Doc A and Doc B hash into same bucket = candidate near-duplicate pair found, sub-linear scan, no O(N^2) check. MinHash + LSH = sub-linear near-duplicate detection at scale (1B docs feasible). Jaccard threshold tunable (typically 0.85). Catches typo-fixes, re-words, copy-paste edits exact hashing cannot.
12.5.summary: MinHash plus LSH banding catches near-duplicates via Jaccard estimates over shingle sets without an O(N squared) pairwise scan.
queries: Doc A Doc B SHA differs one word

12.5.text: Two docs can say the same thing with almost no shared words. MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Only embedding cosine catches paraphrases - same meaning, different words. Paraphrase pair MinHash misses: KB article (formal) 'To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address.' vs Forum post (casual) 'Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email.' MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: cosine >= 0.97 = definite duplicate, drop one; 0.90 <= cosine < 0.97 = near-duplicate, human review queue; 0.85 <= cosine < 0.90 = topically similar, keep both; cosine < 0.85 = distinct documents, keep all. Cost reality: embedding a full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens times corpus size. 10M docs at 512 tokens each = 5B tokens = ~$500 just to dedup. Use embedding dedup when corpus has paraphrases (KB + forum, multi-author, translations). Skip when content is byte-stable (authoritative PDFs) and exact hashing + MinHash cover threshold.
12.5.summary: Embedding cosine dedup catches paraphrases by semantic similarity where MinHash fails on disjoint shingles.
queries: embedding dedup cost per million tokens
when to use embedding dedup vs MinHash

12.5.text: Dedup catches duplicates. Cleaning fixes the bytes themselves. Four steps - Unicode normalization, encoding fix-up, boilerplate stripping, PII scrubbing - turn raw extracted text into something the embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. (1) Unicode Normalization (NFC): two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. Before: 'café' composed 5 bytes vs 'café' decomposed 6 bytes. After: NFC pass collapses both to same 5-byte form, hash dedup and embedding lookup now agree. (2) Encoding Fix-Up: mojibake from latin-1/UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Before: 'naïve résumé' shown as 'naïve résumé'. After: ftfy or chardet detects mismatch and restores 'naïve résumé'. (3) Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks: boilerplate repeated on every page bloats chunks with noise. Before: every chunk starts with 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47'. After: layout-aware parser strips per-page header, footer, watermark band. (4) PII Scrubbing: emails, SSNs, phone numbers, credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. Before: 'Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789.' After: 'Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED].' Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped. It stabilizes eval scores by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics, and keeps PII out of the index.
12.5.summary: Four cleaning steps - Unicode NFC, encoding fix-up, boilerplate stripping, PII scrubbing - stabilize bytes before embedding.
queries: strip headers footers page numbers watermarks
PII scrubbing redact email SSN phone
keeping PII out of vector index

12.5.text: Chapter 12.5 covers deduplication and cleaning in the RAG ingestion pipeline. Duplicate articles across Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion waste context-window tokens and kill retrieval diversity. Three layers of dedup: (1) exact-hash via SHA-256 on normalized text catches byte-identical copies cheaply but misses small edits; (2) MinHash + LSH banding (e.g. 128-hash signature, 16 bands x 8 rows) catches near-duplicates via Jaccard estimates on 3-gram shingles in sub-linear time; (3) embedding cosine dedup catches paraphrases (cosine ladder: >=0.97 drop, 0.90-0.97 review, 0.85-0.90 keep both, <0.85 distinct). Cleaning then fixes bytes via Unicode NFC, encoding repair (ftfy/chardet), header/footer/watermark stripping, and PII scrubbing (Presidio). Without these, the index serves duplicates, ghost chunks, and leaked PII; eval scores never stabilize.
12.5.summary: Three layers of dedup (exact hash, MinHash+LSH, embedding cosine) plus four cleaning steps make the RAG index distinct, consistent, and safe.
queries: exact hash MinHash embedding dedup ladder
why dedup and cleaning matter RAG ingestion
how to dedup RAG corpus production
context window dedup cleaning Unicode PII

12.5.text: Diagram shows three source systems - Zendesk, Confluence, Notion - each containing the same 'Reset Your Password' article, flowing through a single ingestion pipeline (no dedup) that produces three nearly-identical duplicate chunks in the vector database at ~512 tokens each. Below them, a context-window bar of 4,096 tokens shows the first 1,536 tokens consumed by three duplicate chunks tinted red ('1,536 Wasted On Dupes') while only 2,560 tokens remain green for distinct answers ('2,560 Left For Distinct Answers'). Visualizes how blind multi-source ingest converts one answer into three vectors and burns context budget.
12.5.summary: Diagram of three source systems producing duplicate chunks that consume 1,536 of 4,096 context tokens.

12.5.text: MinHash plus LSH pipeline diagram with three stacked stages. Stage 1 tokenizes two near-duplicate docs into 3-gram shingle sets (e.g. {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}). Stage 2 reduces each shingle set to a 128-position MinHash signature so the Jaccard estimate lands around 0.97 (125 of 128 positions match). Stage 3 hashes the signatures through LSH banding with 16 bands of 8 rows so both docs land in the same candidate-pair bucket - sub-linear scan, no O(N squared) pairwise check.
12.5.summary: Diagram of the MinHash+LSH three-stage pipeline: shingles, 128-hash signature, 16x8 banding into candidate bucket.
queries: three stage near duplicate detection visual
how MinHash plus LSH works diagram

12.6.text: Stale RAG hallucinates with confidence. Docs change but the index does not. When source is updated but chunks in the vector DB still reflect the old version, RAG retrieves stale content and the LLM answers with confidence - user sees a fluent, wrong answer. There is no automatic stale signal; the only way to know is to compare against the live source, which RAG never does at query time. Worked example timeline: 2026-04-12 v1 password-reset article published; 2026-05-01 v2 edited to add MFA-preservation note ("MFA setup preserved through reset"); 2026-05-10 stale query "Do I lose my MFA after password reset?" hits v1 chunk in the never-re-ingested index, LLM answers "MFA may need re-setup" (wrong). Source-of-truth (Confluence) shows v1 active 2026-04-12 to 2026-05-01, v2 active 2026-05-01 onward. Vector index contains only v1 chunks - stale from 2026-05-01 onward. Every doc has an expiry date. Stale answers feel correct because the LLM is confident.
12.6.summary: Stale indexes cause confident hallucinations because RAG never compares against the live source at query time.
queries: what is a stale RAG index
why do LLMs hallucinate from outdated docs
what happens when source doc updates but index doesn't
how does stale retrieval cause wrong answers
why is there no stale signal in RAG

why does retrieval not detect outdated content

12.6.text: Strategy 1: Full re-index on a schedule. Simplest sync strategy - the heavy hammer. Every night, rebuild the entire index from scratch. No change detection, no event plumbing, just a cron job that re-ingests everything. Mechanism: nightly cron at 2 AM fires; the job fetches all 10,000 docs from the source, re-parses, re-chunks, and re-embeds them. Output is written into a SHADOW index (parallel collection). At 6 AM an atomic pointer swap promotes shadow to primary - retrieval traffic flips instantly with zero gap. Without the shadow-index pattern, retrieval is degraded for the full 4-hour rebuild window; shadow pattern is the difference between a clean swap and a service incident. Cost math: 10K docs x 1K tokens/doc x $0.10/1M tokens = $1/night, roughly $30/month for a 10K-doc corpus (trivial). At 1M docs: $3,000/month just for re-embedding. Re-embedding dominates the budget once the corpus crosses 100K docs - above that, full re-index is the wrong default. Decision card - Use if: corpus small (<10K docs), changes infrequent (weekly batch updates), simplicity beats freshness. Avoid if: real-time updates needed, corpus large enough for re-embed to dominate budget.
12.6.summary: Full nightly re-index using a shadow collection and atomic pointer swap works under 100K docs but cost dominates above that.
queries: what is full reindex strategy in RAG
what is a shadow index pattern
why use atomic pointer swap for index
when does full reindex become too expensive
simplest sync strategy for vector DB
how often should RAG index rebuild

12.6.text: Strategy 2: Webhook-driven incremental sync. When the source supports webhooks (Confluence, Notion, GitHub, Slack), the index stays fresh in seconds instead of overnight. Each edit fires an event; the ingestion service handles only docs that actually changed - no wasted re-embedding on the 99.99% of unchanged docs. Sequence diagram timeline: T=0:00 user edits doc (support author updates "Reset Your Password" article in Confluence to add MFA-preservation note). T=0:01 webhook fires (Confluence POSTs {event: "page.updated", page_id: "doc-1", revision: 42} to the ingestion service). T=0:02 fetch latest (ingestion calls Confluence API to pull doc-1 revision 42 in full). T=0:05 re-parse + re-chunk + re-embed (parser, chunker, embedder run end-to-end; 3 chunks produced in ~150ms). T=0:08 upsert in vector DB (same doc_id, new chunks; old chunks dropped, new chunks inserted - Section 11.21 deletes-updates pattern). T=0:10 queryable (fresh chunks ready for retrieval; end-to-end propagation lag ~10 seconds). Pros: real-time freshness (~10s), no wasted re-embedding, upsert aligned with 11.21, linear cost scales with edit volume not corpus size. Cons: requires webhook support in source (Confluence yes, raw S3 no), webhook failures need retry/DLQ handling, upserts must be idempotent for retries, out-of-order events need revision-number guards. Webhook incremental sync is the default for SaaS sources with event hooks - build idempotent upserts and a retry queue from day one.
12.6.summary: Webhook-driven incremental sync keeps the index fresh in ~10 seconds by re-embedding only changed docs, but requires idempotent upserts and retry handling.
queries: how do webhooks update a vector index
why use webhooks instead of nightly reindex
out of order webhook events handling
cost of webhook sync vs full reindex
when do webhooks fail in sync
does S3 support webhooks for RAG

12.6.text: Strategy 3: Polling - the fallback for webhook-less sources. Many sources (REST APIs without event hooks, SQL databases, internal services) cannot push events. The ingestion service must pull. Pattern: track a per-source watermark, ask the source on a timer what has changed since the watermark, advance the watermark, repeat. Worked example pipeline: last_synced_at = "2026-05-10T14:00:00Z" (service tracks per-source watermark). interval = every 15 minutes (tune to freshness budget, throttle to source's rate limits). query = WHERE updated_at > 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z LIMIT 1000 (REST or SQL filter the source API exposes). result = 7 changed docs since last poll (process them, then advance last_synced_at to max(updated_at) in batch). Freshness lag edge case: doc-12 is edited at t=2 min (just after a poll fires at t=0). Change is picked up at t=15 min (next poll). Freshness lag = 13 minutes average for that doc, 15 minutes worst case across corpus. Tighter interval = lower lag, higher API cost. Looser interval = higher lag, lower API cost. Tune poll interval to match freshness budget. Decision: use polling when source has no webhooks but exposes a queryable updated_at field (most REST APIs, SQL DBs, internal services). Cost: API calls every interval whether or not anything changed. Throttle the poller to the source's rate limits. Lag is bounded by the poll interval.
12.6.summary: Polling pulls changed docs since a watermark on a fixed interval - used when sources lack webhooks; lag is bounded by poll frequency.
queries: how to sync when no webhooks
how often should RAG poll source
when to use polling vs webhook

12.6.text: Delete propagation and versioning: where 90% of in-house RAG breaks. Sync is not just "add new docs" - hard parts are propagating deletes (when source removes a doc) and versioning chunks (so retrieval never sees a gap during a re-embed). Both are silent failures - eval sets catch them, customers do not get a stack trace, they just get the wrong answer. Three-row versioning timeline: Row 1 t=0: doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active. Queries hit v1 chunks; source-of-truth and index agree. Row 2 t=+1min: v2 ingested, v1 deprecated. Chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated. status=active OR status=deprecated. Both versions live during re-embed window - no retrieval gap. Row 3 t=+15min: v1 dropped after grace period. Chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active. Old chunks tombstoned and removed. Delete propagation: source removes doc-1, webhook fires event: "page.deleted", ingestion writes a tombstone in vector DB, tombstone propagates to all replicas, all chunks for doc-1 removed (Section 11.21 covers multi-replica deletes-updates pattern). Without delete propagation: deleted docs still served as ghost chunks - retrievable but pointing to content no longer in source. Versioning + grace period: new chunks land as status=active, old chunks flip to status=deprecated, both queryable during grace period (typically 5-15 minutes), after grace period deprecated chunks dropped. Without versioning: old chunks dropped before new ones land - retrieval gap, queries during re-embed window return empty or stale results from other docs. Build delete propagation + versioning from day one. Retro-fitting after launch costs 10x the dev time because every downstream component (retrieval, eval, monitoring) already assumes chunks are immutable.
12.6.summary: Deletes must propagate via tombstones and chunks must be versioned with a grace period so retrieval never gaps during re-embed.
queries: how to propagate deletes in vector DB
what is a tombstone in vector index
how long should grace period be
why eval catches deletes but customers don't

12.6.text: Chapter 12.6 covers refresh and sync schedules for RAG indexes. Indexes go stale because docs change but chunks do not, and the LLM has no stale signal so it hallucinates confidently. Three sync strategies: (1) Full re-index on a nightly cron using a shadow collection and atomic pointer swap - simple, works under 100K docs, dominates budget above 1M docs ($3,000/month). (2) Webhook-driven incremental sync - real-time ~10-second propagation lag, only re-embeds changed docs, requires idempotent upserts and retry/DLQ for failed events. (3) Polling - fallback for sources without webhooks, tracks a last_synced_at watermark and queries WHERE updated_at > watermark on a tunable interval, lag bounded by interval. Beyond adds, delete propagation via tombstones and chunk versioning with a 5-15 minute grace period keep retrieval consistent: dual-version active+deprecated chunks during re-embed prevent retrieval gaps; tombstones replicated across nodes prevent ghost chunks. Build delete propagation and versioning from day one because retro-fitting costs 10x.
12.6.summary: Three sync strategies (full reindex, webhook incremental, polling) plus delete propagation and chunk versioning keep RAG indexes fresh without retrieval gaps.
queries: how to keep RAG index fresh
full reindex vs webhook vs polling
when to use each RAG sync strategy

12.6.text: Timeline diagram showing the stale-index failure mode. Three time markers along a horizontal axis: 2026-04-12 v1 published (green), 2026-05-01 v2 edited adding MFA-preservation note (green), 2026-05-10 stale query hits v1 (red). Top band shows source of truth (Confluence): v1 segment "No MFA mention" active 2026-04-12 to 2026-05-01, v2 segment "MFA setup preserved through reset" active 2026-05-01 onward. Bottom band shows vector index (never re-ingested) covering full width in red: only v1 chunks indexed, stale from 2026-05-01 onward. Query "Do I lose my MFA after password reset?" retrieves the v1 chunk and the LLM answers "MFA may need re-setup" (wrong). The diagram makes the gap between source and index visually obvious - the source has two versions but the index froze at v1.

12.6.text: Three-row versioning timeline diagram. Row 1 at t=0 (green): doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active. Queries hit v1 chunks; source-of-truth and index agree. Row 2 at t=+1min (yellow): v2 ingested, v1 deprecated. Chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active plus [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated. status=active OR status=deprecated. Both versions live during the re-embed window so there is no retrieval gap. Row 3 at t=+15min (cyan): v1 dropped after grace period. Chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active. Old chunks are tombstoned and removed. The diagram shows the grace-period overlap window where both versions are queryable - the key trick to avoid retrieval gaps during re-embed.

12.4.text: Bad parsing is unrecoverable - the garbage-in problem. The cleanest chunker, strongest reranker, and smartest LLM cannot recover what the parser already corrupted. If text out of the parser is garbage, recall is gone before chunking starts. Worked example - a scanned password-reset PDF: Step 1 - scanned PDF (image) source page says 'Password reset: click the link in your email within 24 hours' but is a low-resolution scan, not selectable text. Step 2 - naive OCR output: 'Pgssword resel: cIick the Iink in your ernail within 24 hours.' Broken text: rn becomes m, l becomes I, vowels swap. Tokens no longer match. Step 3 - embedding lookup: query 'How do I reset my password?' embedding misses the broken 'Pgssword' tokens, cosine similarity drops below threshold, doc never retrieved. No chunker, reranker, or LLM fixes this. Recall is gone before chunking starts.
12.4.summary: Bad parsing is unrecoverable - a scanned PDF garbled by naive OCR makes recall fail before chunking even starts.

12.4.text: Production RAG ingests from 5+ format families. Before any chunking, embedding, or indexing, raw sources must be turned into clean text plus metadata. Every production RAG handles five or more format families, each with its own parser and failure modes. PDFs (manuals, contracts, research papers) - tools: PyMuPDF / Unstructured / Docling. HTML (web pages, wikis, public docs) - tools: Readability / Trafilatura. DOCX / PPTX (internal docs, slide decks) - tools: python-docx / Unstructured. Markdown (engineering docs, READMEs, knowledge bases) - tools: native parsers (mistletoe, marko). Structured APIs (Confluence, Notion, Salesforce, Zendesk, Jira) - tools: vendor SDK or REST connector. One parser per family is the minimum. A single shared 'pdf2text' connector across all formats is the most common silent killer of RAG recall in production.
12.4.summary: Production RAG must handle five or more source format families, each with its own parser, and a one-size-fits-all parser silently destroys recall.

12.4.text: PDF: three failure modes that silently wreck recall. PDFs are the format where parsers go to die - same file looks fine to a human but turns into nonsense when read naively. Failure 1 - Two-Column PDF Layout: naive output '...gravitational lensing distorts || ...the Higgs boson decays into ... light from a quasar across two W bosons...' Layout-aware output: 'Gravitational lensing distorts light from a quasar across galaxy clusters.' Fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling) reads columns in reading order. Failure 2 - Tables Flattened To Prose: naive output 'Q1, Q2, Q3, Revenue, 100, 110, 105, Costs, 80, 75, 78'. Structured output: { Q1: { Revenue: 100, Costs: 80 }, Q2: { Revenue: 110, Costs: 75 }, Q3: { Revenue: 105, Costs: 78 } }. Fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table) keeps row/column relationships. Failure 3 - Scanned PDF / OCR Errors: naive 'Pgssword resel: cIick the link. Arnount due: $14O.00'. Modern OCR output: 'Password reset: click the link. Amount due: $140.00'. Fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract, Azure Document Intelligence) fixes rn-to-m, l-to-1, O-to-0 confusions. Production RAG uses layout-aware + table-aware + OCR-aware parsers, not pdf2text.
12.4.summary: PDFs have three silent failure modes - two-column layout jumbling, tables flattened to prose, and OCR character confusions - each fixable with a specific parser class.
queries: why does my PDF parser jumble columns
how to extract tables from PDFs for RAG
what OCR errors are common in PDFs
how to preserve table rows and columns in RAG

12.4.text: HTML: strip the boilerplate or dilute your chunks. Every HTML web page wraps main content in nav bars, sidebars, ads, cookie banners, and footers. If the parser ingests the whole DOM, every chunk gets polluted with boilerplate the user did not ask about. Web page zone map: top nav bar - DROP, left sidebar ads - DROP, main article content - KEEP, footer + newsletter signup - DROP. Bad chunk (no boilerplate strip): 'Home Products Pricing Subscribe to our newsletter | How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email... copyright 2026 Acme Inc.' Retrieval matches 'Subscribe' instead of the real answer; chunk is diluted with junk. Good chunk (Readability extract): 'How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email within 24 hours to set a new password...' Only main content survives; embedding matches user's intent cleanly. Tools: Readability, Trafilatura, BoilerNet detect main-content zones via DOM heuristics and ML. Without them, retrieval matches boilerplate (nav, sidebar, footer) and pollutes every chunk.
12.4.summary: HTML pages must have nav, sidebars, ads, and footers stripped via Readability or Trafilatura so chunks contain only main article content.

12.4.text: Metadata extraction: the required sidecar. Parsing is not just text. Every chunk also needs a structured sidecar of metadata: where the doc came from, when updated, who is allowed to see it, what language it is in. Skip this and you lose filtering, citations, and permission boundaries for the whole index. Metadata schema: { 'source_url': 'https://docs.example.com/account/password-reset', 'title': 'Reset Your Password', 'doc_type': 'kb_article', 'section_path': 'Account > Security > Password', 'updated_at': '2026-04-12T14:22:00Z', 'author': 'support-team', 'permissions': ['public', 'logged-in'], 'language': 'en', 'version': 'v3.1' }. The three fields production RAG cannot skip are source_url, updated_at, and permissions. Use cases: Filtering - Section 11.20 uses fields like updated_at > 2026-01-01 to exclude stale docs at query time. Citations - chapter 12.24 uses source_url so the LLM can cite where the answer came from. Permission Gating - never retrieve docs whose permissions (ACL list) don't include current user's role. Multilingual Routing - detect language at ingest time, route to per-language indexes for higher recall. Metadata is parsed at ingest, attached to every chunk vector, and queried at retrieval time. Skip it equals no filtering, no citations, no permission boundaries.
12.4.summary: Every chunk needs a metadata sidecar - source_url, updated_at, permissions, language and more - to enable filtering, citations, and permission gating.

12.4.text: The five parsing failures every production RAG must handle. This is the checklist every team owns before they touch chunking, embedding, or retrieval. Skip one row and you have a silent recall killer. Row 1 - Two-Column PDF Layout: symptom sentences jumbled across columns; fix layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling). Row 2 - Tables Flattened To Prose: symptom lost row/column structure; fix table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table). Row 3 - Scanned PDF / OCR Errors: symptom garbled tokens (rn becomes m, missing diacritics); fix modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract). Row 4 - HTML Boilerplate Not Stripped: symptom chunks polluted with nav, ads, footers; fix Readability / Trafilatura main-content extraction. Row 5 - Missing Or Sparse Metadata: symptom no filtering, citations, or permission gating possible; fix mandatory metadata schema at ingest. Every production RAG has this checklist. Skip one row equals silent recall killer. Bad parsing is the gap a reranker cannot fix.

12.4.text: Chapter 12.4 - Parsing: Raw Sources to Clean Text. Bad parsing is unrecoverable; no chunker, reranker, or LLM rescues garbled text. Production RAG handles 5+ format families (PDF, HTML, DOCX/PPTX, Markdown, structured APIs) each with its own parser. PDFs have three silent failure modes: two-column layout, flattened tables, and OCR errors - fixed by layout-aware parsers (Unstructured, Docling), table-extracting parsers (LlamaParse), and modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract). HTML needs boilerplate stripping via Readability or Trafilatura to keep only the main article. Every chunk also needs a metadata sidecar (source_url, updated_at, permissions, language) to enable filtering, citations, and permission gating. The five-row parsing failure checklist - two-column PDFs, flattened tables, OCR errors, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata - is the gate before chunking.

12.4.text: Bad parsing is unrecoverable - the garbage-in problem. The cleanest chunker, the strongest reranker, and the smartest LLM cannot recover what the parser already corrupted. If the text from your parser is garbage, recall is gone before chunking starts. Worked example: a scanned password-reset PDF. Step 1 - Scanned PDF (image): source page is a low-resolution scan, not selectable text, containing 'Password reset: click the link in your email within 24 hours.' Step 2 - Naive OCR output: 'Pgssword resel: cIick the Iink in your ernail within 24 hours.' Broken text: rn becomes m, l becomes I, vowels swap. Tokens no longer match. Step 3 - Embedding lookup: query 'How do I reset my password?' - embedding misses the broken 'Pgssword' tokens, cosine similarity drops below threshold, doc is never retrieved. No chunker, reranker, or LLM fixes this.
12.4.summary: Bad parser output destroys recall before any downstream stage can fix it, shown with a scanned-PDF OCR failure that makes a password-reset doc unretrievable.

12.4.text: Production RAG ingests from 5+ format families. Before any chunking, embedding, or indexing, raw sources must be turned into clean text plus metadata. Every production RAG handles five or more format families, each with its own parser and failure modes. Formats: PDFs (manuals, contracts, research papers) - tool: PyMuPDF / Unstructured / Docling. HTML (web pages, wikis, public docs) - tool: Readability / Trafilatura. DOCX / PPTX (internal docs, slide decks) - tool: python-docx / Unstructured. Markdown (engineering docs, READMEs, knowledge bases) - tool: native parsers like mistletoe, marko. Structured APIs (Confluence, Notion, Salesforce, Zendesk, Jira) - tool: vendor SDK or REST connector. One parser per family is the minimum. A single shared 'pdf2text' connector across all formats is the most common silent killer of RAG recall in production.

12.4.text: PDF: three failure modes that silently wreck recall. PDFs are where parsers go to die - the same file looks fine to a human and turns into nonsense when read naively. (1) Two-column PDF layout. Naive output: '...gravitational lensing distorts || ...the Higgs boson decays into ... light from a quasar across two W bosons...' Layout-aware output: 'Gravitational lensing distorts light from a quasar across galaxy clusters.' Fix: layout-aware parser (Unstructured, Docling) reads columns in reading order. (2) Tables flattened to prose. Naive output: 'Q1, Q2, Q3, Revenue, 100, 110, 105, Costs, 80, 75, 78'. Layout-aware output: structured rows { Q1: { Revenue: 100, Costs: 80 }, Q2: { Revenue: 110, Costs: 75 }, Q3: { Revenue: 105, Costs: 78 } }. Fix: table-extracting parser (LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table) keeps row/column relationships. (3) Scanned PDF / OCR errors. Naive output: 'Pgssword resel: cIick the link. Arnount due: $14O.00'. Fixed output: 'Password reset: click the link. Amount due: $140.00'. Fix: modern OCR (Tesseract 5, AWS Textract, Azure Document Intelligence) fixes rn-to-m, l-to-1, O-to-0 confusions. Production RAG uses layout-aware + table-aware + OCR-aware parsers, not pdf2text.
12.4.summary: Three PDF parsing failures (two-column layout jumbling, table flattening, OCR confusions) shown with before/after text and the layout/table/OCR-aware tools that fix each.

12.4.text: HTML: strip the boilerplate or dilute your chunks. Every HTML web page wraps its main content in nav bars, sidebars, ads, cookie banners, and footers. If your parser ingests the whole DOM, every chunk gets polluted with boilerplate the user did not ask about. Web page zone map: top nav bar - DROP, left sidebar ads - DROP, main article content - KEEP, footer + newsletter signup - DROP. Bad chunk (no boilerplate strip): 'Home Products Pricing Subscribe to our newsletter | How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email... © 2026 Acme Inc.' Retrieval matches 'Subscribe' instead of the real answer; the chunk is diluted with junk. Good chunk (Readability extract): 'How do I reset my password? Click the link in your email within 24 hours to set a new password...' Only the main content survives. Tools: Readability, Trafilatura, BoilerNet. They detect main-content zones via DOM heuristics and ML. Without them, retrieval matches boilerplate (nav, sidebar, footer) and pollutes every chunk.
12.4.summary: HTML pages must have nav/sidebar/footer/ads stripped before chunking; Readability/Trafilatura/BoilerNet isolate the main article so embeddings match real content instead of boilerplate.

12.4.text: Chapter 12.4 - Parsing: Raw Sources to Clean Text. Bad parsing is unrecoverable: if the parser corrupts text, no chunker, reranker, or LLM can recover recall (shown with a scanned PDF OCR example). Production RAG ingests from 5+ format families (PDFs, HTML, DOCX/PPTX, Markdown, structured APIs like Confluence/Notion/Salesforce), each needing its own parser - one shared pdf2text is the most common silent recall killer. PDFs have three failure modes: two-column layout jumbling (fix: Unstructured, Docling), tables flattened to prose (fix: LlamaParse, Unstructured.partition_table), and OCR errors like rn-to-m or O-to-0 (fix: Tesseract 5, AWS Textract, Azure Document Intelligence). HTML must have nav/sidebar/footer/ads stripped via Readability, Trafilatura, or BoilerNet so chunks match real content instead of boilerplate. Metadata sidecar (source_url, updated_at, permissions, language, version) is mandatory at ingest - it powers filtering, citations, permission gating, and multilingual routing. The five-failure checklist (column jumble, table flatten, OCR, HTML boilerplate, missing metadata) is what every production RAG owns before chunking or embedding.
12.4.summary: Chapter overview: bad parsing is unrecoverable, so production RAG needs per-format parsers (layout-aware PDF, table-aware, OCR, HTML boilerplate stripping) plus a mandatory metadata sidecar before chunking.

12.5.text: Chapter 12.5 covers deduplication and cleaning in the RAG ingestion pipeline. Duplicate articles across systems like Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion waste context budget and kill answer diversity. Three dedup tiers stack: exact SHA-256 hashing on normalized text catches byte-identical copies (the easy 80%); MinHash + LSH estimates Jaccard overlap on shingle sets to catch typo-fixes and re-wordings in sub-linear time using 128-position signatures with 16 bands of 8 rows; embedding cosine similarity catches paraphrases (formal vs casual rewordings) that share almost no shingles, using bands like >=0.97 drop, 0.90-0.97 human review, 0.85-0.90 keep both, <0.85 distinct. Cleaning then fixes bytes: Unicode NFC normalization collapses composed vs decomposed forms, ftfy or chardet repair mojibake from encoding mismatches, layout-aware parsers strip repeating headers/footers/page numbers/watermarks, and regex plus LLM NER (Presidio) scrub emails, phones, SSNs, and credit cards before indexing.
12.5.summary: Chapter overview: stack exact hash, MinHash+LSH, and embedding cosine to remove duplicates, then clean bytes with Unicode normalization, encoding fixup, boilerplate stripping, and PII scrubbing.
queries: what is deduplication in RAG ingestion
how to remove duplicate documents from vector database
why do duplicate chunks hurt RAG
exact hash vs MinHash vs embedding dedup
how does dedup save context window tokens
cosine similarity thresholds for duplicate documents

silent recall killers in RAG ingestion

12.5.text: The same knowledge-base article often lives in Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion at the same time. Ingest all three blindly and the same answer becomes three vectors in the index. A top-5 retrieval for "How do I reset my password?" returns 3 copies of the same chunk plus 2 unrelated docs - no diversity, and most of the context-window budget is wasted on duplicates. Concrete numbers: each duplicate chunk is roughly 512 tokens. With three copies in a 4,096-token context window, 1,536 tokens are wasted on dupes and only 2,560 tokens remain for distinct answers. 3 copies of the same answer equals wasted tokens, zero diversity, and the LLM has nothing distinct to triangulate against. Sources shown: Zendesk help center article #4821, Confluence IT runbook page, and Notion onboarding doc section - all containing "Reset Your Password".
12.5.summary: Same article in Zendesk, Confluence, and Notion produces three near-identical chunks that waste context tokens and destroy retrieval diversity.
queries: why are duplicate documents bad in RAG
what happens when the same article lives in multiple systems
Zendesk Confluence Notion same article duplicate
how much context window is wasted by duplicates
why ingesting from multiple SaaS tools creates duplicates
what is the cost of not deduplicating

12.5.text: Exact-hash dedup is the first line of defense. Normalize the text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, then keep a hash table of every hash already seen. If a new doc's hash matches an existing one, the content is byte-identical, so drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize - "Reset Your Password. Click The Link." becomes "reset your password click the link". Step 2 Hash - sha256(normalized) produces hex digest a3f2b9... Step 3 Dedup lookup - hash table check, match equals drop, miss equals keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example with three sources: Zendesk hash a3f2b9... kept (first), Confluence hash a3f2b9... dropped, Notion hash a3f2b9... dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute plus O(1) hash-table lookup. Misses: "same article with one typo fixed" produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
12.5.summary: Exact SHA-256 hashing on normalized text deduplicates byte-identical copies in O(N) with O(1) lookups, but misses any one-character change.
queries: how does exact hash deduplication work
hash table lookup for duplicate detection
first line of defense for dedup
worked example three sources same hash

12.5.text: One typo fix, one re-worded sentence, one renamed term and the SHA-256 digest changes completely. MinHash estimates Jaccard set overlap between shingle sets cheaply, and locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) groups near-duplicates into the same bucket without an O(N^2) pairwise scan. Two near-duplicates exact hash misses: Doc A (Zendesk) "Reset your password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours." SHA-256 a3f2b9...; Doc B (Confluence) "Reset the password by clicking the link in your email within 24 hours." SHA-256 9e1cd4... (different). One word change ("your" to "the") flips the hash. MinHash + LSH pipeline: Step 1 - 3-gram shingles {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...} - Doc A and Doc B share most shingles. Step 2 - MinHash signature with K=128 hash functions; 125 of 128 positions match equals Jaccard estimate around 0.97. Step 3 - LSH banding 16 bands x 8 rows; Doc A and Doc B hash into the SAME bucket - candidate near-duplicate pair found, sub-linear scan, no O(N^2) all-pairs check. MinHash + LSH equals sub-linear near-duplicate detection at scale (1B docs feasible). Jaccard threshold tunable, typically 0.85. Catches typo-fixes, re-words, and copy-paste edits exact hashing cannot.
12.5.summary: MinHash signatures plus LSH banding estimate Jaccard overlap on shingle sets so near-duplicates bucket together in sub-linear time without an O(N^2) pairwise scan.
queries: why does one typo break exact hashing

12.5.text: Two docs can say the same thing with almost no shared words. MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Only embedding cosine catches paraphrases - the same meaning in different words. Paraphrase pair MinHash misses: KB Article (Formal) "To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address." vs Forum Post (Casual) "Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email." MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: Cosine >= 0.97 = definite duplicate, drop one keep the other; 0.90 <= Cosine < 0.97 = near-duplicate, send to human review queue; 0.85 <= Cosine < 0.90 = topically similar, keep both; Cosine < 0.85 = distinct documents, keep all. Cost reality: embedding a full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens times corpus size. For 10M docs at 512 tokens each that is 5B tokens equals about $500 just to dedup. Cheaper than re-embedding twice, but a real line item. Use embedding dedup when corpus has paraphrases (KB + forum posts, multi-author docs, translations). Skip when content is byte-stable (PDFs from authoritative sources) and exact hashing + MinHash already cover the threshold.
12.5.summary: Embedding cosine similarity catches semantic duplicates and paraphrases that share no shingles, with tunable threshold bands and a real $500-per-10M-doc cost.
queries: KB article vs forum post paraphrase example

12.5.text: Dedup catches duplicates. Cleaning fixes the bytes themselves. Four steps turn raw extracted text into something the embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. Step 1 Unicode Normalization (NFC): two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. Before: café (composed, 5 bytes) vs café (decomposed, 6 bytes). After: NFC pass collapses both to the same 5-byte form; hash dedup and embedding lookup now agree. Step 2 Encoding Fix-Up: mojibake from latin-1 / UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Before: raw bytes rendered wrong, 'naïve résumé' shown as 'naïve résumé'. After: ftfy or chardet detects the encoding mismatch and restores 'naïve résumé'. Step 3 Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks: boilerplate repeated on every page bloats chunks with noise. Before: every chunk starts with 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47'. After: layout-aware parser strips the repeating per-page header, footer, and watermark band. Step 4 PII Scrubbing: emails, SSNs, phone numbers, credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. Before: 'Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789.' After: 'Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED].' Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped. It stabilizes eval scores by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics and keeps PII out of the index entirely.
12.5.summary: Four cleaning steps - Unicode NFC normalization, ftfy/chardet encoding repair, boilerplate stripping, and Presidio PII redaction - normalize bytes so embeddings and tokenizers behave consistently.
queries: what cleaning steps run before chunking

12.5.text: Diagram: Three source systems (Zendesk, Confluence, Notion) each containing the same Reset-Your-Password article flow into a single ingestion pipeline with no dedup, producing three nearly-identical duplicate chunks (~512 tokens each) in the vector database. Below them, a context-window bar of 4,096 tokens shows the first 1,536 tokens consumed by three duplicate chunks tinted red while only 2,560 tokens remain green for distinct answers. The diagram makes the token-waste cost of duplicates visual.
12.5.summary: Diagram shows three SaaS sources producing duplicate chunks that consume 1,536 of 4,096 context tokens, leaving 2,560 for distinct content.

12.5.text: Diagram: MinHash plus LSH pipeline with three stacked stages. Stage 1 tokenizes two near-duplicate docs into 3-gram shingle sets like {reset your password, your password by, password by clicking, ...}. Stage 2 reduces each shingle set to a 128-position MinHash signature so 125 of 128 positions match and the Jaccard estimate lands around 0.97. Stage 3 hashes the signatures through LSH banding configured as 16 bands of 8 rows so both docs land in the same candidate-pair bucket - sub-linear scan, no O(N^2) pairwise check.
12.5.summary: Diagram of three-stage MinHash plus LSH pipeline: shingle, signature with 128 hashes giving Jaccard 0.97, LSH banding 16x8 bucketing candidate near-duplicate pairs.

12.6.text: Chapter 12.6 covers Refresh & Sync Schedules for RAG: how to keep the vector index in sync with source documents that keep changing. Stale RAG hallucinates with confidence because there is no automatic stale signal — retrieval and the LLM proceed as if the index were fresh. The chapter walks through three sync strategies (full nightly re-index with shadow-index swap, webhook-driven incremental sync with ~10 second propagation, and polling with a per-source updated_at watermark) and the two silent failures that break 90% of in-house RAG: delete propagation (avoid ghost chunks pointing at removed source content) and chunk versioning with a 5-15 minute grace period where v1 chunks are deprecated and v2 chunks active simultaneously so retrieval never sees a gap during re-embed.
12.6.summary: Overview of how production RAG keeps its index in sync with changing sources via re-index, webhooks, or polling, plus delete propagation and chunk versioning.

12.6.text: Stale RAG hallucinates with confidence. Docs change but the index does not, so when the source is updated but chunks in the vector DB still reflect the old version, RAG retrieves stale content and the LLM answers with confidence. The user sees a fluent, wrong answer. There is no automatic stale signal — the only way to know is to compare against the live source, which RAG never does at query time. Three-time-marker timeline of the password-reset article: 2026-04-12 v1 published with no MFA mention; 2026-05-01 v2 edited to add 'MFA Setup Preserved Through Reset'; 2026-05-10 a stale query 'Do I lose my MFA after password reset?' retrieves the v1 chunk and the LLM answers 'MFA may need re-setup' (WRONG). Source of truth (Confluence) shows v1 active 2026-04-12 to 2026-05-01 then v2 active onward, but the vector index has only v1 chunks indexed and is stale from 2026-05-01 onward. The hallucinated answer looks confident because the LLM has no stale signal. Every doc has an expiry date; stale answers feel correct because the LLM is confident.
12.6.summary: Stale indexes cause confident hallucinations because RAG never compares against the live source at query time.
queries: why does RAG give wrong answers when documents change
what is a stale RAG index
why do LLMs hallucinate confidently with RAG
stale signal in retrieval augmented generation

12.6.text: Strategy 1: Full Re-Index On A Schedule. The simplest sync strategy is the heavy hammer. Every night a cron job rebuilds the entire index from scratch with no change detection and no event plumbing. It works, scales down, and is the most common starting point for in-house RAG. Mechanism: nightly cron at 2 AM fires; the job fetches all 10,000 docs from the source, re-parses, re-chunks, re-embeds; output is written into a SHADOW index (parallel collection); at 6 AM an atomic pointer swap promotes shadow to primary so retrieval traffic flips instantly with zero gap. Without the shadow-index pattern, retrieval is degraded for the full 4-hour rebuild window — the shadow pattern is the difference between a clean swap and a service incident. Cost math: 10K docs × 1K tokens/doc × $0.10/1M tokens = $1/night, ~$30/month for a 10K-doc corpus (trivial). At 1M docs: $3,000/month just for re-embedding; re-embedding dominates the budget once the corpus crosses 100K docs and above that full re-index is the wrong default. Decision card — Use Full Re-Index If: corpus is small (<10K docs), changes are infrequent (weekly batch updates), simplicity beats freshness. Avoid If: real-time updates needed, corpus large enough for re-embed to dominate budget.
12.6.summary: Nightly full re-index with a shadow-index atomic swap; cheap below 10K docs, prohibitive above 100K.

12.6.text: Strategy 2: Webhook-Driven Incremental Sync. When the source supports webhooks (Confluence, Notion, GitHub, Slack), the index stays fresh in seconds instead of overnight. Each edit fires an event and the ingestion service only handles docs that actually changed — no wasted re-embedding on the 99.99% of docs that did not change. Sequence diagram — 10-second propagation: T=0:00 User Edits Doc (support author updates 'Reset Your Password' article in Confluence to add the MFA-preservation note); T=0:01 Webhook Fires (Confluence POSTs {event: 'page.updated', page_id: 'doc-1', revision: 42} to the ingestion service); T=0:02 Fetch Latest (ingestion service calls Confluence API to pull doc-1 revision 42 in full); T=0:05 Re-Parse + Re-Chunk + Re-Embed (parser, chunker, embedder run end-to-end, 3 chunks produced in ~150ms); T=0:08 Upsert In Vector DB (same doc_id, new chunks; old chunks dropped, new chunks inserted using the Section 11.21 deletes-updates pattern); T=0:10 Queryable (fresh chunks ready, end-to-end propagation lag ~10 seconds). Pros: real-time freshness ~10s, no wasted re-embedding on unchanged docs, upsert lines up with Section 11.21, linear cost scales with edit volume not corpus size. Cons: requires webhook support in the source (Confluence yes, raw S3 no), webhook failures need retry/DLQ handling, upserts must be idempotent to avoid duplicate writes on retries, out-of-order events need revision-number guards. Webhook incremental sync is the default for SaaS sources with event hooks; build idempotent upserts and a retry queue from day one.
12.6.summary: Webhook-driven incremental sync delivers ~10-second freshness via per-edit upserts but needs idempotency and retry/DLQ handling.

how does incremental sync work in RAG

12.6.text: Strategy 3: Polling — The Fallback For Webhook-Less Sources. Many sources (REST APIs without event hooks, SQL databases, internal services) cannot push events, so the ingestion service must pull. The pattern: track a per-source watermark, ask the source on a timer what has changed since the watermark, advance the watermark, repeat. Polling pipeline worked example: last_synced_at = 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z (service tracks per-source watermark); interval = Every 15 minutes (tune to freshness budget, throttle to source's rate limits); query = WHERE updated_at > 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z LIMIT 1000 (REST or SQL filter the source API exposes); result = 7 changed docs since last poll (process them, then advance last_synced_at to max(updated_at) in batch). Freshness lag edge case: doc-12 edited at t=2 min (just after a poll fires at t=0) is picked up at t=15 min (next poll), so freshness lag = 13 minutes on average for that doc, 15 minutes worst case across the corpus. Tighter interval = lower lag, higher API cost; looser interval = higher lag, lower API cost — tune the poll interval to your freshness budget. Decision card: use polling when the source has no webhooks but exposes a queryable updated_at field (most REST APIs, SQL databases, internal services). Cost: API calls every interval whether or not anything changed; throttle the poller to the source's rate limits; lag is bounded by the poll interval.
12.6.summary: Polling syncs webhook-less sources by tracking a watermark and querying updated_at > watermark on a tunable interval.

12.6.text: Delete Propagation And Versioning: Where 90% Of In-House RAG Breaks. Sync is not just 'add new docs.' The hard parts are propagating deletes (when the source removes a doc) and versioning chunks (so retrieval never sees a gap during a re-embed). Both are silent failures — eval sets catch them when chunks reference ghost content or queries miss during a swap, but customers do not see a stack trace, they just get the wrong answer. Three-row versioning timeline: Row 1 at t=0 doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks=[c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active, queries hit v1 chunks and source-of-truth and index agree. Row 2 at t=+1min v2 ingested and v1 deprecated, chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated, status=active OR status=deprecated, both versions live during the re-embed window so no retrieval gap. Row 3 at t=+15min v1 dropped after grace period, chunks=[c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active, only v2 active and old chunks tombstoned and removed. Delete propagation: source removes doc-1, webhook fires event 'page.deleted', ingestion service writes a tombstone in the vector DB, tombstone propagates to all replicas, all chunks for doc-1 are removed (Section 11.21 covers the multi-replica deletes-and-updates pattern). Without delete propagation: deleted docs are still served as ghost chunks — retrievable but pointing to content that no longer exists in the source. Versioning + grace period: new chunks land as status=active, old chunks flip to status=deprecated, both queryable during the grace period (typically 5-15 minutes), after which deprecated chunks are dropped. Without versioning: old chunks dropped before new ones land = retrieval gap, queries during the re-embed window return empty or stale results from other docs. Silent failures: without delete propagation ghost chunks served as fact; without versioning retrieval gap during re-embed window; both invisible to the user, eval catches them but customers do not. Build delete propagation + versioning from day one — retro-fitting after launch costs 10x the dev time because every downstream component (retrieval, eval, monitoring) already assumes chunks are immutable.
12.6.summary: Delete propagation removes ghost chunks for deleted docs and a 5-15 min versioning grace period prevents retrieval gaps during re-embed.
