12.1.text: Bare LLMs have hard limits in production apps. Five failure modes are inherent to the model alone: (1) Knowledge Cutoff - trained on data through 2023, can't answer about 2025 events. (2) Hallucination - confidently invents plausible-sounding facts. (3) No Citations - cannot point to sources for verification. (4) Private Data Unknown - knows nothing about your internal docs. (5) Stale Facts - public info from years ago, not last week. Drop a frontier model into production and these cracks appear fast.
12.1.summary: Lists five inherent failure modes of bare LLMs without retrieval: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data, stale facts.
queries: what are limits of bare LLM
LLM doesn't know private company data
why bare model fails in production

12.1.text: Fine-tuning doesn't solve the bare-LLM problem. Comparison table: Cost - fine-tuning costs tens of thousands of dollars per refresh, RAG costs cents per query. Freshness - fine-tuning is frozen at training time, RAG reflects index updates instantly. Traceability - fine-tuning has no source attribution, RAG cites the chunk used. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).
12.1.summary: Compares fine-tuning vs RAG across cost, freshness, and traceability - RAG wins on all three.
queries: should I fine-tune or use RAG
knowledge in weights vs knowledge in index
when to use fine-tuning vs retrieval

12.1.text: RAG grounds the answer in retrieved documents. Tiny pipeline change: before the model answers, fetch the relevant documents and stuff them into the prompt. Flow: User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. The model now reads first, answers second. Instead of asking the model from memory, we ground its answer in retrieved documents anchored to what the docs say.
12.1.summary: Defines RAG as a three-step pipeline that retrieves documents before generation so the LLM reads first, answers second.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work

12.1.text: Same question, two answers. Question: "What is our refund policy?" Bare LLM answer (MADE UP): "Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard." RAG answer (CITED): "Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans." Source chunk: [doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated. Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Worked example contrasts a bare-LLM hallucinated refund policy with a RAG answer cited from doc-4.
queries: made up answer vs grounded answer

12.1.text: Five reasons production systems use RAG. (1) Knowledge Cutoff -> Solved: index is fresh; re-embed when docs change. (2) Hallucination -> Reduced: model is anchored to retrieved text. (3) No Citation -> Solved: cite the exact chunk used. (4) Private Data -> Solved: index your own docs; no fine-tune needed. (5) Cheap To Refresh -> Re-embed only changed docs; update individual chunks, no full retrain. Every failure mode gets a clean answer, plus refresh becomes nearly free.
12.1.summary: Maps each bare-LLM failure mode to how RAG fixes it, plus cheap incremental refresh by re-embedding changed chunks.
queries: RAG fixes for bare LLM problems

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 introduces why LLMs need retrieval. Bare LLMs suffer five inherent failures: knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private-data knowledge, and stale facts. Fine-tuning is the obvious but wrong fix - tens of thousands per refresh, frozen at training time, no audit trail. RAG flips every property: cents per query, fresh, traceable. The mechanism is a tiny pipeline change - retrieve relevant docs first, then let the LLM read and answer grounded in those docs. A refund-policy example shows the bare model inventing a 30-day guarantee while RAG cites [doc-4] with the real 14-day window. Production systems use RAG because it solves cutoff, reduces hallucination, enables citations, handles private data, and refreshes cheaply by re-embedding only changed chunks.
12.1.summary: Chapter overview: bare LLMs have five failure modes, fine-tuning is too expensive and untraceable to fix them, RAG grounds answers in retrieved documents and is the production answer.
queries: bare LLM problems and RAG solutions
limits of LLMs and how to fix them

12.2.text: Chapter 12.2 introduces the Naive RAG Pipeline: five stages glued together, split into two phases. Three stages run offline during ingest (Chunk, Embed, Store) and two run online for every query (Retrieve, Generate). Documents are sliced into chunks of about 80 tokens, each chunk is turned into a dense vector (production uses 1024 dims, the example uses 8 dims), vectors are stored in a vector index like HNSW (1045 chunks across 30 docs in the example), the user query is embedded by the same model and cosine similarity returns the top-K most similar chunks (top-3 with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77), and those chunks are stuffed into a prompt template with an instruction to say 'I don't know' if the docs are insufficient. The LLM then generates a grounded answer with a [doc-1] citation. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid retrieval, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: Five-stage naive RAG pipeline: Chunk, Embed, Store (offline ingest) plus Retrieve and Generate (online query).

12.2.text: Naive RAG is five stages glued together. Three run once during ingest (offline) and two run on every query (online). The five stages are Chunk (Doc to Chunks), Embed (Chunks to Vectors), Store (Vectors to Index), Retrieve (Query to Top-K), and Generate (Top-K to Answer). Chunk, Embed, and Store form the offline ingest phase. Retrieve and Generate form the online query-time phase. Once you see the shape, every variant in the field is just a tweak to one of these boxes.
12.2.summary: RAG has five stages split between offline ingest (Chunk/Embed/Store) and online query (Retrieve/Generate).

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk Documents Into Pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit, so they are cut into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page, sliced into 3 chunks of about 80 tokens each. Chunk 1: 'To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the Reset Password link and enter your current password to verify identity.' Chunk 2: 'An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring.' Chunk 3: 'Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed.' Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) are covered in detail in chapters 12.7-12.13.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 splits each document into ~80-token chunks so one chunk is mostly about one thing.

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn Each Chunk Into A Vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning. Similar chunks have similar vectors. Example vectors for doc-1: chunk 1 = [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58], chunk 2 = [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62], chunk 3 = [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings (covered in Section 5.2) turn text into dense vectors. RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings (more in chapter 12.14). Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; the example shows 8 dims for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 embeds each chunk as a dense vector so semantically similar chunks land near each other.

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store Vectors In A Vector Database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Example: an HNSW vector index holding 3 chunks from doc-1 plus about 1042 chunks from 29 other docs, for 1045 chunks total (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varying by doc length). Storage layout: [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...], [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...], [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...], plus 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) are covered in Section 11. The index turns brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search, milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 puts chunk vectors into a vector index like HNSW to enable millisecond approximate nearest-neighbor search.

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve The Top-K Most Similar Chunks. The query is embedded by the same model used at ingest. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Example query: 'How do I reset my password?' is embedded as [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. The top-3 most similar chunks by cosine similarity are [doc-1, chunk 1] with score 0.91, [doc-1, chunk 2] with score 0.84, and [doc-1, chunk 3] with score 0.77. All three come from the same doc because they all talk about password reset.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 embeds the query and uses cosine similarity to pull the top-K most relevant chunks from the index.

12.2.text: Stage 5: Pack Chunks Into Prompt + LLM Generates Answer. The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know". Documentation: [doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click... [doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with... [doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new... Question: How do I reset my password? Answer:'. Grounded generated answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 packs retrieved chunks into a prompt with an 'I don't know' instruction and the LLM generates a cited answer.

12.2.text: End To End: One Query, Five Stages. Same query 'How do I reset my password?' flowing through all five stages with real data: Chunk - [doc-1] split into 3 chunks. Embed - 3 chunks become 3 vectors (8-dim). Store - indexed in HNSW. Retrieve - Query produces Top-3 chunks with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77. Generate - Grounded answer with [doc-1] citation. Final answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password. [doc-1]'. That's naive RAG end to end. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid retrieval, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.

12.3.text: Chapter 12.3 names the seven failure modes of naive RAG that show up under production traffic: bad chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), low recall (lexical mismatch like 'sign in' vs 'log in'), lost-in-the-middle (U-shaped attention skips middle chunks), no citation (unverifiable answers), hallucination on partial info (model fills gaps with confident guesses), stale index (doc edits not re-embedded), and cost/latency (~$0.0123 per query, 850ms p50, $12,300/day at 1000 QPS). Each failure points forward to specific later chapters that fix it: 12.7-12.13 chunking strategies, 12.16-12.17 hybrid + rerankers, 12.18-12.21 query transformation, 12.22-12.24 context packing + citations + groundedness, 12.31-12.35 faithfulness eval, 12.36-12.40 caching, drift detection, cost models, observability.
12.3.summary: Names the 7 production failure modes of naive RAG and maps each to its fix chapter.

12.3.text: Naive RAG has 7 failure modes. The pipeline from 12.2 works on a happy-path query. Production traffic exposes 7 named ways it can fail: Bad Chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), Low Recall (lexical mismatch hides relevant docs), Lost In Middle (long contexts skip middle chunks), No Citation (answers without source attribution), Hallucination (fills gaps with confident invention), Stale Index (doc edits not reflected until re-embed), Cost / Latency (per-query LLM tokens add up at scale).
12.3.summary: Introduces the 7 named ways naive RAG fails once it meets production traffic.

12.3.text: Failure 1: Bad Chunking. Fixed-size chunking sliced a single sentence across two chunks. User query: 'How long do I have to use the password reset link?' Chunk 1 (mid-sentence split): '...Click the link in the email...'. Chunk 2 (mid-sentence split): '...within 24 hours to set a new password...'. The 24-hour limit is split across two chunks. Neither contains both 'reset link' AND '24 hours' near each other. Retrieved chunk: '...Click the link in the email...'. Model output: 'I don't see a time limit in the docs.' Wrong - the 24-hour limit IS in the doc, but bad chunking hid it. Chapters 12.7-12.13 (chunking strategies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Shows how fixed-size chunking splits the 24-hour password reset fact across two chunks so the model answers 'I don't know'.

how do fixed size chunks hide answers
how chunking causes wrong RAG answers

RAG missing answer due to chunking

12.3.text: Failure 2: Low Recall. Embedding-only retrieval is supposed to handle synonyms. In practice, 'sign in' and 'log in' don't always cluster tightly enough, and the right doc gets missed. User query: 'I can't sign in to my account'. Expected match: Doc 'Login Troubleshooting' - 'If you can't log in, check your password. Logging in requires a verified email...' - MISSED FROM TOP-3. Top-3 retrieved (embedding-only): [doc-12] Sign in to view your billing history; [doc-19] Sign in required to access the API console; [doc-27] Sign in with Google or Apple supported. Embedding-only retrieval scores docs by vector similarity. 'Log in' vs 'sign in' don't cluster tightly enough. The right doc is missed entirely from top-3. Chapters 12.16-12.17 (hybrid retrieval + rerankers) and 12.18-12.21 (query transformation) fix this.
12.3.summary: Shows how embedding-only retrieval misses the 'log in' doc when the query says 'sign in'.
queries: what is low recall in RAG
why does embedding retrieval miss synonyms
sign in vs log in retrieval problem
why is the right doc not in top-3
when do embeddings fail at synonyms

12.3.text: Failure 3: Lost In The Middle. Even when the answer IS in the retrieved context, long-context models attend more to the start and end than to the middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. Lost-in-the-middle attention curve: position 1 weight 0.92, position 2 0.78, position 3 0.52, position 4 0.28, position 5 0.18 (relevant chunk, missed), position 6 0.20, position 7 0.32, position 8 0.60, position 9 0.82, position 10 0.95. Model attends most to the first and last chunks. Middle chunks (positions 4-7) get less attention. The relevant chunk at position 5 is in context but the model skips it. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: U-shaped attention curve means a relevant chunk at position 5 of 10 is in context but ignored by the model.
queries: what is lost in the middle
why do LLMs ignore middle of context
why doesn't the model see chunk 5
primacy and recency in LLM attention
how attention weight changes by position
how to fix lost in the middle
where in context do LLMs focus

12.3.text: Diagram for chapter 12.3: U-shaped attention curve plotting attention weight (y-axis 0 to 1) against chunk position 1-10 (x-axis). High attention at start (positions 1-2 around 0.92 and 0.78) and end (positions 9-10 around 0.82 and 0.95), dipping in the middle (positions 4-7 around 0.28, 0.18, 0.20, 0.32). Position 5 is highlighted in red as 'Position 5 (Relevant) - Missed' showing the relevant chunk that gets ignored despite being in context.
12.3.summary: Diagram of the U-shaped lost-in-the-middle attention curve over 10 chunk positions with position 5 marked as the missed relevant chunk.
queries: attention weight by chunk position chart
graph of LLM attention over context positions
how attention falls in middle of prompt
where is the relevant chunk highlighted
diagram of why middle chunks get ignored

12.3.text: Failure 4: No Citation. The model answered, but the answer has no source markers. Reviewers can't tell which chunk the answer came from, or whether any chunk supports it at all. User query: 'Why is my account suspended?' Generated answer (no citation): 'Your account may be suspended due to payment failures, terms of service violations, or unusual activity. Contact support for details.' No [doc-X] markers anywhere. Reviewer cannot verify which doc the answer came from. Without a citation, if the model hallucinated half of it, no one can tell. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (citations + groundedness) fix this.
12.3.summary: Shows an answer with no [doc-X] markers, so a reviewer cannot tell what supports it or whether the model hallucinated.
queries: what is no citation failure in RAG
why does RAG need source citations
how to verify a RAG answer
RAG answers that cant be checked

12.3.text: Failure 5: Hallucination On Partial Info. Retrieved context covers SSO for Enterprise plan but says nothing about Pro plan. The model fills the gap with a confident-sounding guess. User query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' Retrieved top-3 chunks: [doc-3] SSO is available on Enterprise plan via SAML 2.0; [doc-3] Enterprise plan includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated support; [doc-5] Pricing: Basic $10/mo, Pro $25/mo, Enterprise custom. Note: no mention of Pro + SSO anywhere. Model output (hallucinated): 'Yes, the Pro plan includes SSO.' This claim is wrong. SSO is Enterprise-only. Model filled in a gap with a plausible-sounding guess. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (refusal + groundedness instruction) and 12.31-12.35 (faithfulness eval) fix this.
12.3.summary: Shows the model inventing 'Pro plan includes SSO' when retrieved chunks only mention Enterprise SSO.
queries: why does RAG still hallucinate with retrieved context
RAG answer not supported by docs
fix hallucination in retrieval augmented generation

12.3.text: Failures 6 & 7: Stale Index, Cost, Latency. The last two failures aren't about answer quality - they're about operations. Failure 6 Stale Index: Doc-7 (Refund Policy) was updated 2 days ago. Sales now allows refunds within 30 days. The embedding index still has the old version (14 days). User query: 'What's our current refund window?' Answer returned (stale): '14 days.' Embedding lifecycle (Section 11.27) and chapters 12.36-12.40 (drift detection) fix this. Failure 7 Cost & Latency: per-query cost breakdown - Embedding $0.0001, Retrieval $0.0002, LLM Tokens (4000 ctx) $0.0120, Total Per Query $0.0123. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 800ms generation = 850ms p50. At 1000 QPS, costs $12,300/day. Chapters 12.36-12.40 (caching, cost models, observability) fix this.
12.3.summary: Stale index returns outdated refund window; per-query cost $0.0123 and 850ms latency scale to $12,300/day at 1000 QPS.
queries: what is stale index in RAG
why does the index go stale
cost and latency in production RAG
how much does RAG cost per query

12.7.text: Chapter 12.7 explains why RAG pipelines must chunk documents before embedding, then introduces fixed-size sliding-window chunking as the baseline. A 500-page manual at 250,000 tokens cannot fit through an embedding model with an 8,192-token ceiling, and one vector for 500 pages would carry no specific signal. Three forces force chunking: context limits, retrieval granularity (top-K returns chunks, not docs), and signal dilution. Fixed-size chunking slides a token window (e.g. size=128, overlap=16) across the doc; cuts can land mid-sentence, so overlap duplicates the last N tokens into the next chunk. Overlap fixes sentence-level cuts but not long-range references and adds ~12% storage at 128/16. Fixed-size is the baseline to beat: use it for short, homogeneous docs; move to structural or semantic chunking when docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or eval shows cross-chunk recall loss.
12.7.summary: Why chunking is mandatory in RAG and how fixed-size sliding-window chunking with overlap works as the baseline strategy.
queries: why do we chunk documents in RAG
how does sliding window chunking work
why can't we embed a whole document
what is chunk overlap in RAG
when should I use fixed-size chunking
what is the baseline chunking strategy
does overlap solve chunk boundary problem
context limits retrieval granularity signal dilution
when to move beyond fixed-size chunking
why chunking is highest-leverage RAG decision

12.7.text: Why can't we just embed the whole document? Intuitive first idea: take a 500-page product manual, embed as one vector, search. Fails for two hard reasons. First, the doc is too long to fit through the embedding model: a 500-page manual is ~250,000 tokens but OpenAI ada-3 caps at 8,192 tokens. Only ~3.3% (8,192/250,000) fits; 241,808 tokens overflow. Second, even if it fit, one vector [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would mean nothing specific. Averaging the entire manual collapses every individual fact into a generic blob.
12.7.summary: Embedding a whole document fails because docs exceed model token ceilings and one vector for hundreds of pages dilutes all specific meaning.
queries: why not embed entire document as one vector
what happens if document exceeds embedding ceiling
what is the embedding model ceiling
why one vector for many pages is useless
token budget vs model ceiling diagram

12.7.text: Three reasons production RAG always chunks. (1) Context Limits: embedding models cap at 8K tokens; LLMs cap at context-window length. A 500-page manual cannot fit in either. (2) Retrieval Granularity: top-K retrieval returns chunks, not whole docs. Smaller chunks = more focused matches. (3) Signal Dilution: averaging 500 pages into one vector buries any specific fact. Smaller chunks keep semantic density. Bottom line: chunking is not a workaround, it is the fundamental design choice. Get the chunk strategy wrong and every downstream stage compounds the error.
12.7.summary: Production RAG always chunks because of context limits, retrieval granularity, and signal dilution.
queries: three reasons to chunk in RAG
why smaller chunks keep semantic density

12.7.text: Fixed-size chunking is the simplest strategy: slide a fixed token window across the document. Each window becomes one chunk. Adjacent chunks overlap by a few tokens so a sentence cut in half still appears whole somewhere. Example call: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). Applied to doc-1 (Password Reset Article, ~480 tokens) it produces four chunks: Chunk 1 tokens 0-128, Chunk 2 tokens 112-240, Chunk 3 tokens 224-352, Chunk 4 tokens 336-464. Each adjacent pair shares a 16-token overlap band (112-128, 224-240, 336-352). The example doc text covers visiting account.example.com, the 24-hour reset link expiry, password requirements (at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, symbols), and spam folder troubleshooting.
12.7.summary: Fixed-size chunking slides a token window with overlap; e.g. slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16) yields four overlapping chunks across a 464-token document.
queries: sliding window across password reset doc
what does slide function do in chunking

12.7.text: Fixed-size cuts land anywhere, including mid-sentence. The token window does not care about grammar; the 128th token is wherever the 128th token is. Boundary between chunk 1 and chunk 2 in doc-1 demonstrates: Chunk 1 ends mid-phrase '...click the reset link in the email within 24 hours to set a new'. Chunk 2 starts mid-phrase 'password. The link expires after that and you must request a new one...'. The boundary cut breaks retrieval: the chunk ending with 'set a new' has no idea what the user is setting; the chunk starting with 'password.' has no idea what action was being taken. Query 'password reset expiry' cannot match either piece alone - both lose the cross-boundary fact.
12.7.summary: Token-based fixed-size cuts ignore grammar and can split sentences mid-phrase, destroying retrieval signal for facts that span the boundary.
queries: what happens when sentence cut in half
chunk 1 ends mid-phrase chunk 2 starts mid-phrase

12.7.text: Overlap helps but only partially. The standard fix is overlap: duplicate the last N tokens of each chunk into the start of the next. A sentence cut at the boundary now appears whole in one of the two adjacent chunks. Cost: storage and redundant compute. Overlap = 0 (naive) has hard cuts that lose context entirely and drop recall on cross-chunk facts. Overlap = 16 shares 16 tokens between adjacent chunks (e.g. shared text 'set a new password. The link expires'). Tradeoffs of overlap: duplicate storage cost, ~12% more vectors at 128/16, and still breaks long-range references (entity earlier in doc, pronoun later). Overlap fixes the easy case (sentence-level cuts) but not the hard case (a definition on page 3 referenced by 'it' on page 17).
12.7.summary: Overlap duplicates tail tokens into the next chunk to preserve sentence-level cuts, costing ~12% extra vectors at 128/16 and not fixing long-range references.
queries: does overlap fix all chunk boundary issues
why overlap doesn't fix long-range references
how many extra vectors with 16 token overlap

12.7.text: Fixed-size is the baseline to beat. Use Fixed-Size If: docs are short (< 2K tokens each); content is homogeneous (uniform paragraphs); you need a baseline to measure other strategies against; budget = zero engineering time. Move to Structural / Semantic chunking if: docs have clear structural markers (markdown, HTML headings); content is heterogeneous (one doc = manual + FAQ + changelog); cross-chunk facts hurt recall in your eval set. Fixed-size is the baseline; every chunking strategy in production is measured against it. Chapters 12.8-12.12 each fix one weakness of fixed-size.
12.7.summary: Fixed-size chunking is the production baseline; use it for short homogeneous docs, move to structural or semantic chunking when docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or recall suffers.

12.7.text: Token budget vs embedding model ceiling diagram. Horizontal token-count bar showing a 500-page product manual at 250,000 tokens overflowing the 8,192-token embedding model ceiling. Green portion marks the 8,192 tokens that fit; the rest of the bar is tinted red as overflow ('Overflow zone - 241,808 tokens exceed the ceiling'). A second smaller bar below visualizes the ceiling itself, labeled 'Embedding Model Ceiling = 8,192 Tokens (OpenAI ada-3)'. A callout shows that even if the whole doc fit, a single vector [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would carry no specific signal.
12.7.summary: Bar diagram comparing a 250,000-token document against the 8,192-token ada-3 embedding ceiling, with a callout that one vector for 500 pages means nothing specific.
queries: token budget vs embedding ceiling diagram
what does the overflow zone show
why doc doesn't fit in embedding model

12.7.text: Sliding window chunk diagram across doc-1 (the password-reset article). Four colored 128-token chunk bars stacked vertically along a 0-464 token axis. Each chunk labeled with its token range: Chunk 1 (0-128), Chunk 2 (112-240), Chunk 3 (224-352), Chunk 4 (336-464). Adjacent chunks share a 16-token overlap zone (112-128, 224-240, 336-352) shaded as a darker green band. Legend shows lighter green = Chunk, darker green = Overlap (16 tokens). Axis ticks at 0, 128, 240, 352, 464.
12.7.summary: Diagram of four 128-token sliding-window chunks across a 464-token doc with 16-token darker-green overlap bands between adjacent chunks.
queries: sliding window across password reset doc visual
how overlap appears in chunk diagram

12.7.text: Hard-cut boundary diagram (Overlap = 0). Two clean rectangle chunks side by side with no overlap. A red dashed vertical line at the boundary marks the mid-sentence hard cut between chunk 1 and chunk 2. Labels 'Chunk 1' and 'Chunk 2' inside the rectangles, 'Hard Cut' label below the red line. Illustrates how naive non-overlapping fixed-size chunking severs sentences at the boundary.
12.7.summary: Two adjacent chunks with a red dashed line marking a hard mid-sentence cut, illustrating overlap=0 naive chunking.
queries: two chunks side by side no overlap

12.7.text: Overlap=16 chunk diagram. Two yellow chunk rectangles with a darker yellow overlap band shared between them, labeled as the shared 16-token region. A green 'Shared 16 Tokens' label below the band. Illustrates that the cut sentence appears whole in both adjacent chunks. Example shared text shown: 'set a new password. The link expires'. Demonstrates how overlap preserves sentence-level continuity at the cost of duplicate storage.
12.7.summary: Two chunks sharing a darker overlap band, showing how a 16-token overlap places the cut sentence whole in both adjacent chunks.
queries: what does overlap look like visually

12.1.text: Bare LLMs have hard limits in production. Drop a frontier model into a real app and cracks appear fast: it does not know about events after its training cutoff, makes up plausible-sounding facts (hallucination), cannot point to sources for verification, has never seen your private internal docs, and its public info is years stale. Five inherent failure modes of the model alone: Knowledge Cutoff (trained on data through 2023, can't answer about 2025 events), Hallucination (confidently invents plausible-sounding facts), No Citations (cannot point to sources), Private Data Unknown (knows nothing about internal docs), Stale Facts (public info from years ago, not last week).
12.1.summary: Bare LLMs fail in production due to knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data, and stale facts.
queries: why do LLMs fail in production
what are limits of bare LLMs
can LLMs answer about private company docs
why does ChatGPT not know recent events
what failure modes do frontier models have
why can't GPT answer questions about my company
why do LLMs make things up
limits of GPT in real apps

12.1.text: Fine-tuning doesn't solve the problem. The obvious first answer is 'just fine-tune the model on our data,' but fine-tuning is slow, expensive, and untraceable. RAG flips every property. Comparison: Cost: fine-tuning costs tens of thousands of dollars per refresh vs RAG cents per query. Freshness: fine-tuning is frozen at training time vs RAG reflects index updates instantly. Traceability: fine-tuning has no source attribution vs RAG can cite the chunk used. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge INTO the weights (expensive to update, no audit trail). RAG keeps knowledge OUTSIDE the model in a retrievable index (cheap, fresh, traceable).
12.1.summary: Fine-tuning is expensive, frozen, and untraceable; RAG is cheap, fresh, and citable because it keeps knowledge outside the model.
queries: why not fine-tune instead of RAG
should I fine-tune or use RAG

12.1.text: RAG grounds the answer in retrieved documents. The fix is a tiny pipeline change: before the model answers, fetch the relevant documents and stuff them into the prompt. The model reads first, answers second. Flow: User Question -> Retrieve Relevant Docs -> LLM Reads Docs + Answers. Instead of asking the model from memory, we ground its answer in retrieved documents. The model reads the docs, then answers, anchored to what the docs say.
12.1.summary: RAG fetches relevant documents and adds them to the prompt so the LLM reads first then answers grounded in retrieved text.
queries: how does retrieval augmented generation work
what does grounding mean in LLMs
what does retrieve relevant docs mean
how does LLM use retrieved documents

12.1.text: Same question, two answers. Question: 'What is our refund policy?' Bare LLM (MADE UP): 'Our standard refund policy offers a 30-day money-back guarantee on all subscriptions. You can request a refund through your account dashboard.' RAG (CITED): 'Per [doc-4] (refunds.md): Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days of payment, prorated for annual plans.' Source chunk: [doc-4] Refunds are issued for cancellations within 14 days. Annual plans prorated. Both answers sound confident. Only one is checkable. The bare model invented numbers that look reasonable; the RAG answer is pinned to a real chunk we can show the user.
12.1.summary: Worked example shows a bare LLM inventing a fake 30-day refund policy while RAG cites doc-4 with the true 14-day policy.
queries: example bare LLM vs RAG answer
what does a RAG citation look like
RAG vs bare LLM same question

12.1.text: 5 reasons production systems use RAG. Every failure mode from the bare LLM gets a clean answer, plus refresh becomes nearly free. Knowledge Cutoff -> Solved: index is fresh; re-embed when docs change. Hallucination -> Reduced: model is anchored to retrieved text. No Citation -> Solved: cite the exact chunk used. Private Data -> Solved: index your own docs; no fine-tune needed. Cheap To Refresh -> Re-embed Only Changed Docs: update individual chunks; no full retrain.
12.1.summary: RAG fixes all five bare-LLM failures plus makes refresh cheap by re-embedding only changed chunks instead of full retraining.
queries: how does RAG solve knowledge cutoff
how does RAG handle private data

12.1.text: Chapter 12.1 explains why LLMs need retrieval. Bare LLMs have five inherent failures (knowledge cutoff, hallucination, no citations, no private data, stale facts). Fine-tuning is too slow, expensive, and untraceable to fix them. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) inserts a retrieval step before generation so the model reads relevant docs first and answers grounded in them, with citations. A refund-policy example contrasts a bare LLM's invented 30-day answer with RAG's cited 14-day answer from doc-4. Production uses RAG because it solves knowledge cutoff, reduces hallucination, enables citations, handles private data, and refreshes cheaply by re-embedding only changed chunks.
12.1.summary: Chapter 12.1 motivates RAG by showing bare LLMs fail in 5 ways, fine-tuning is worse than RAG on cost/freshness/traceability, and RAG grounds answers in retrieved docs with citations.
queries: summary of why LLMs need RAG
RAG vs bare LLM vs fine-tuning summary
five failure modes of LLMs and RAG fixes

12.2.text: Chapter 12.2 walks through the naive RAG pipeline end to end: five stages glued together where three run offline during ingest (Chunk, Embed, Store) and two run online per query (Retrieve, Generate). It uses a password-reset help doc (doc-1) sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens, embedded into 8-dim vectors, indexed in HNSW alongside 1042 other chunks from 30 docs, then queried with 'How do I reset my password?' which gets embedded and matched via cosine similarity to top-3 chunks with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77, finally stuffed into a prompt template that produces a grounded answer with [doc-1] citation.
12.2.summary: Overview of the 5-stage naive RAG pipeline: Chunk, Embed, Store (offline) then Retrieve, Generate (online), traced with a password-reset example.
queries: what is the naive RAG pipeline
how does retrieval augmented generation work
chunk embed store retrieve generate pipeline
how does RAG work end to end
what are the steps in RAG

12.2.text: The Naive RAG Pipeline. Naive RAG is five stages glued together. Three run once during ingest (offline): Chunk, Embed, Store. Two run on every query (online): Retrieve, Generate. Once you see the shape, every variant in the field is just a tweak to one of these boxes. The flow diagram shows: Chunk (Doc to Chunks) -> Embed (Chunks to Vectors) -> Store (Vectors to Index) -> Retrieve (Query to Top-K) -> Generate (Top-K to Answer). First three stages tinted cyan (offline ingest), last two tinted yellow (online query time).
12.2.summary: Introduces the five RAG stages split into offline ingest (Chunk, Embed, Store) and online query (Retrieve, Generate).
queries: what are the 5 stages of RAG
why is RAG split into two phases
what runs once vs every query in RAG
what are RAG variants tweaks of

12.2.text: Horizontal flow diagram of the 5-stage RAG pipeline: Chunk, Embed, Store, Retrieve, Generate. First 3 stages tinted cyan (offline ingest), last 2 stages tinted yellow (online query). Arrows connect adjacent stages. Annotations under each stage: Chunk (Doc to Chunks), Embed (Chunks to Vectors), Store (Vectors to Index), Retrieve (Query to Top-K), Generate (Top-K to Answer). A dashed vertical divider separates the ingest phase from query phase.
12.2.summary: Diagram showing the 5-stage RAG pipeline with ingest stages in cyan and query stages in yellow, separated by a dashed divider.
queries: diagram of chunk embed store retrieve generate
what does RAG look like visually

12.2.text: Stage 1: Chunk Documents Into Pieces. Docs are usually too long to retrieve as a single unit. We cut them into chunks small enough that one chunk is mostly about one thing. Example: doc-1 is the password reset help page, sliced into 3 chunks of ~80 tokens each. Chunk 1: 'To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security in your account dashboard. Click the Reset Password link and enter your current password to verify identity.' Chunk 2: 'An email will be sent to your registered address with a one-time reset link. The link is valid for 24 hours and can only be used once before expiring.' Chunk 3: 'Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new password. Choose a password of at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols mixed.' Chunking strategies (fixed-size, sentence-aware, semantic, hierarchical) are covered in detail in chapters 12.7-12.13.
12.2.summary: Stage 1 chunks a long doc into ~80-token pieces, shown with three concrete chunks from a password-reset doc.
queries: what is document chunking in RAG
how big should a chunk be
what does a chunk look like

12.2.text: Stage 2: Turn Each Chunk Into A Vector. Each chunk becomes a dense vector encoding its meaning. Similar chunks have similar vectors. Example vectors for the 3 doc-1 chunks (8-dim shown for visibility): chunk 1 = [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, 0.43, 0.07, 0.91, 0.22, 0.58]; chunk 2 = [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, 0.51, 0.13, 0.86, 0.27, 0.62]; chunk 3 = [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, 0.48, 0.10, 0.88, 0.24, 0.60]. Embeddings (covered in Section 5.2) turn text into dense vectors. RAG uses retrieval-tuned embeddings (more in chapter 12.14). Production typically uses 1024-dim vectors; we show 8 here for visibility.
12.2.summary: Stage 2 embeds each chunk into a dense vector; concrete 8-dim vectors shown for the three doc-1 chunks.
queries: what dimensionality do RAG embeddings use
how similar chunks get similar vectors

12.2.text: Stage 3: Store Vectors In A Vector Database. Once embedded, each chunk-vector goes into a vector index. The index is what makes retrieval fast - without it, every query would compare against every chunk. Example: Vector Index (HNSW) holds 3 chunks from doc-1 plus ~1042 chunks from 29 other docs, totaling 1045 chunks indexed (30 docs x ~35 chunks each, varies by doc length). Storage layout: [doc-1, chunk 1] [0.81, 0.12, 0.65, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 2] [0.74, 0.18, 0.59, ...]; [doc-1, chunk 3] [0.79, 0.15, 0.61, ...]; ... + 1042 more vectors. Vector indexes (HNSW, IVF, hybrid, compression) are covered in Section 11. The index turns brute-force kNN into approximate nearest-neighbor search, milliseconds instead of seconds.
12.2.summary: Stage 3 stores chunk vectors in a vector index (HNSW shown with 1045 chunks) for fast approximate nearest-neighbor search.
queries: how are vectors stored for RAG
how big is a typical RAG index

12.2.text: Stage 4: Retrieve The Top-K Most Similar Chunks. Query is embedded by the same model. Cosine similarity ranks all chunks; the top-3 score highest. In production, top-k is usually 20-50 before reranking. Example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' is embedded to query vector [0.78, 0.14, 0.63, 0.46, 0.09, 0.89, 0.25, 0.61]. Top-3 most similar chunks by cosine similarity: [doc-1, chunk 1] score 0.91, [doc-1, chunk 2] score 0.84, [doc-1, chunk 3] score 0.77. The query must be embedded by the SAME model used during ingest so the vectors live in the same space.
12.2.summary: Stage 4 embeds the query and uses cosine similarity to fetch the top-3 chunks with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77.
queries: same embedding model for query and chunks
how does RAG find relevant chunks

12.2.text: Stage 5: Pack Chunks Into Prompt + LLM Generates Answer. The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template along with the user's question. The 'I don't know' instruction reduces hallucination when retrieved docs are insufficient. Prompt Template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the following documentation to answer the user's question. If the docs don't answer, say "I don't know". Documentation: [doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, navigate to Settings > Security and click... [doc-1, chunk 2] An email will be sent to your registered address with... [doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link in the email within 24 hours to set a new... Question: How do I reset my password? Answer:'. Generated grounded answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password of at least 12 characters. [doc-1]'
12.2.summary: Stage 5 packs the top chunks into a prompt template with an 'I don't know' instruction and the LLM produces a grounded, cited answer.
queries: what is the generate stage in RAG
how chunks are inserted into prompt
how does the LLM use retrieved chunks

12.2.text: End To End: One Query, Five Stages. Same query 'How do I reset my password?' flows through all five stages with real data. Chunk: [doc-1] split into 3 chunks. Embed: 3 chunks become 3 vectors (8-dim). Store: indexed in HNSW. Retrieve: query yields top-3 chunks with scores 0.91, 0.84, 0.77. Generate: grounded answer with [doc-1] citation. Final answer: 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security and click Reset Password. You'll receive a one-time link by email valid for 24 hours. Click it and set a new password. [doc-1]'. That's naive RAG end to end. Every variant in the field (rerankers, hybrid, query rewriting, agents) is a tweak to one or more of these five boxes.
12.2.summary: End-to-end recap: one query traced through all five stages producing a grounded final answer with citation.

12.3.text: Chapter 12.3 catalogs seven failure modes of naive RAG that surface in production: bad chunking, low recall, lost-in-the-middle, no citation, hallucination on partial info, stale index, and cost/latency. Each failure is shown with a concrete example query and broken output, then mapped to later chapters that fix it.
12.3.summary: Naive RAG breaks in seven named ways in production - chunking, recall, lost-in-middle, citations, hallucination, staleness, cost.
queries: why does naive RAG fail in production
what are the failure modes of RAG
what goes wrong with retrieval augmented generation
list of RAG problems to fix

12.3.text: The naive RAG pipeline from chapter 12.2 works on a happy-path query, but production traffic exposes seven named ways it can fail: Bad Chunking (mid-sentence splits hide answers), Low Recall (lexical mismatch hides relevant docs), Lost In Middle (long contexts skip middle chunks), No Citation (answers without source attribution), Hallucination (fills gaps with confident invention), Stale Index (doc edits not reflected until re-embed), and Cost / Latency (per-query LLM tokens add up at scale).
12.3.summary: Seven failure modes of naive RAG previewed as a grid.
queries: what are the 7 failure modes of RAG
bad chunking low recall lost in middle
why naive RAG isn't production ready

12.3.text: Failure 1: Bad Chunking. User query: 'How long do I have to use the password reset link?' Fixed-size chunking sliced one sentence across two chunks. Chunk 1 ends mid-sentence: '...Click the link in the email...' Chunk 2 starts mid-sentence: '...within 24 hours to set a new password...' Neither chunk contains both 'reset link' AND '24 hours' near each other. Retrieved chunk: '...Click the link in the email...' Model output: 'I don't see a time limit in the docs.' Wrong - the 24-hour limit IS in the doc, but bad chunking hid it. Chapters 12.7-12.13 (chunking strategies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Mid-sentence fixed-size splits hide the 24-hour password-reset answer, so the model says it doesn't know.
queries: how does fixed size chunking hurt RAG
how to fix bad chunking in RAG

12.3.text: Failure 2: Low Recall. User query: 'I can't sign in to my account'. Expected match (missed from top-3): doc 'Login Troubleshooting' - 'If you can't log in, check your password. Logging in requires a verified email...' Top-3 retrieved (embedding-only): [doc-12] Sign in to view your billing history. [doc-19] Sign in required to access the API console. [doc-27] Sign in with Google or Apple supported. Embedding-only retrieval scores docs by vector similarity. 'Log in' vs 'sign in' don't cluster tightly enough. The right doc is missed entirely from top-3. Chapters 12.16-12.17 (hybrid retrieval + rerankers) and 12.18-12.21 (query transformation) fix this.
12.3.summary: Embedding-only retrieval misses the Login doc when the query says 'sign in' - synonym mismatch tanks recall.

12.3.text: Lost-In-The-Middle Attention Curve. U-shaped attention curve plotting attention weight against chunk position 1-10. Position 1: 0.92, Position 2: 0.78, Position 3: 0.52, Position 4: 0.28, Position 5: 0.18 (highlighted red - relevant chunk, missed), Position 6: 0.20, Position 7: 0.32, Position 8: 0.60, Position 9: 0.82, Position 10: 0.95. High attention at start and end, dipping in the middle. Even when the answer IS in retrieved context, long-context models attend more to start and end than middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: U-curve diagram showing attention weight per chunk position, with position 5 relevant but skipped.

why LLMs ignore middle of context

12.3.text: Failure 3: Lost In The Middle. Even when the answer IS in the retrieved context, long-context models attend more to the start and end than to the middle. A chunk buried at position 5 of 10 can be ignored entirely. The model attends most to the first and last chunks; middle chunks (positions 4-7) get less attention. The relevant chunk at position 5 is in context, but the model skips it. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (context packing + lost-in-middle remedies) fix this.
12.3.summary: Long-context LLMs underweight middle chunks, so a relevant chunk at position 5 of 10 gets skipped.

why position matters in RAG context
how to fix lost in middle

12.3.text: Failure 4: No Citation. User query: 'Why is my account suspended?' Generated answer (no citation): 'Your account may be suspended due to payment failures, terms of service violations, or unusual activity. Contact support for details.' No [doc-X] markers anywhere. Reviewer cannot verify which doc the answer came from. Without a citation, if the model hallucinated half of it, no one can tell. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (citations + groundedness) fix this.
12.3.summary: Answer without [doc-X] markers leaves reviewers unable to verify source or detect hallucination.
queries: how to add citations to RAG output

12.3.text: Failure 5: Hallucination On Partial Info. User query: 'Does the Pro plan include SSO?' Retrieved context (top-3 chunks): [doc-3] SSO is available on Enterprise plan via SAML 2.0. [doc-3] Enterprise plan includes SSO, audit logs, dedicated support. [doc-5] Pricing: Basic $10/mo, Pro $25/mo, Enterprise custom. Note: No mention of Pro + SSO anywhere. Model output (hallucinated): 'Yes, the Pro plan includes SSO.' This claim is wrong - SSO is Enterprise-only. The model filled a gap with a plausible-sounding guess. Chapters 12.22-12.24 (refusal + groundedness instruction) and 12.31-12.35 (faithfulness eval) fix this.
12.3.summary: When retrieved chunks don't cover the question, the model invents a plausible wrong answer about Pro plan SSO.
queries: how to prevent hallucination in RAG

12.3.text: Failures 6 & 7: Stale Index, Cost & Latency. Failure 6 - Stale Index: doc-7 (Refund Policy) was updated 2 days ago; sales now allows refunds within 30 days, but the embedding index still has the old version (14 days). User query: 'What's our current refund window?' Answer returned (stale): '14 days.' Embedding lifecycle (Section 11.27) and chapters 12.36-12.40 (drift detection) fix this. Failure 7 - Cost & Latency per-query breakdown: Embedding $0.0001, Retrieval $0.0002, LLM Tokens (4000 ctx) $0.0120, Total Per Query $0.0123. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 800ms generation = 850ms p50. At 1000 QPS, costs $12,300/day. Chapters 12.36-12.40 (caching, cost models, observability) fix this.
12.3.summary: Stale index returns outdated refund window; per-query cost $0.0123 and 850ms p50 latency balloon to $12,300/day at 1000 QPS.

12.7.text: Why can't we just embed the whole document? Intuitive first idea: take a 500-page product manual, embed as one vector, search. Fails for two hard reasons. (1) Document too long to fit through embedding model - 500-page manual is ~250,000 tokens but embedding models cap around 8,192 tokens (e.g., OpenAI ada-3 ceiling = 8,192). Overflow zone: 241,808 tokens exceed the ceiling. (2) Even if it fit, one vector [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would mean nothing specific - signal gets washed out. Token budget vs embedding model ceiling: 500-page manual = 250,000 tokens; fits portion = 8,192; the rest overflows.
12.7.summary: Explains why embedding an entire document as one vector fails - context limits and signal dilution.
queries: why can't I embed a whole document at once
what is the token limit for embedding models
one vector per document why bad
what happens if document exceeds embedding model context
how many tokens in a 500 page manual
can I embed an entire book as one vector
why does one vector per document lose specificity

12.7.text: Three reasons production RAG always chunks. Chunking is not a workaround - it is the fundamental design choice. (1) Context Limits: embedding models cap at 8K tokens; LLMs cap at context-window length. A 500-page manual cannot fit in either. (2) Retrieval Granularity: top-K retrieval returns chunks, not whole docs. Smaller chunks = more focused matches. (3) Signal Dilution: averaging 500 pages into one vector buries any specific fact. Smaller chunks keep semantic density. Bottom line: chunking is the highest-leverage decision in RAG. Get the chunk strategy wrong and every downstream stage compounds the error.
12.7.summary: Three forces - context limits, retrieval granularity, signal dilution - make chunking unavoidable in production RAG.
queries: why is chunking necessary in RAG
why smaller chunks give better matches
is chunking the most important RAG decision
chunks vs whole documents in retrieval
RAG design choices ranked by impact

why not return whole documents in RAG
context window limit LLM vs embedding model

12.7.text: Fixed-size chunking: a sliding window with overlap. Simplest possible strategy: slide a fixed token window across the document. Each window becomes one chunk. Adjacent chunks overlap by a few tokens so a sentence cut in half still appears whole somewhere. Pseudocode: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). Example on doc-1 (Password Reset Article, ~480 tokens): Chunk 1 = tokens 0-128, Chunk 2 = tokens 112-240, Chunk 3 = tokens 224-352, Chunk 4 = tokens 336-464. Each chunk is 128 tokens wide; adjacent chunks share a 16-token overlap band (e.g., tokens 112-128 appear in both chunk 1 and chunk 2).
12.7.summary: Defines fixed-size chunking as a fixed-token sliding window with a small overlap between adjacent chunks.
queries: how does sliding window chunking work
what is the simplest chunking strategy
how to chunk a 480 token document into 128 token pieces
RAG chunk size and overlap defaults
sliding window across a document tokens
what's a typical chunk overlap value
how many chunks for a 480 token document at size 128

12.7.text: Fixed-size cuts land anywhere - including mid-sentence. The token window does not care about grammar. The 128th token is wherever the 128th token is. Zoom into the boundary between chunk 1 and chunk 2 in doc-1: Chunk 1 ends mid-phrase: '...click the reset link in the email within 24 hours to set a new'. Chunk 2 starts mid-phrase: 'password. The link expires after that and you must request a new one...'. The boundary cut breaks retrieval - the chunk ending with 'set a new' has no idea what the user is setting; the chunk starting with 'password.' has no idea what action was being taken. Retrieval cannot match the query 'password reset expiry' to either piece alone - both lose the cross-boundary fact.
12.7.summary: Shows a concrete example where a fixed-size cut splits 'set a new password' across two chunks and destroys retrieval.
queries: how do fixed-size chunks break sentences
what happens when a chunk ends mid-phrase
why does fixed-size hurt retrieval recall
chunk 1 ends with 'set a new' problem
can a query miss both adjacent chunks
retrieval fails when answer splits across chunks
why fixed-size chunking has low recall on long answers
example of bad chunk boundary in RAG

12.7.text: Overlap helps - but only partially. The standard fix is overlap: duplicate the last N tokens of each chunk into the start of the next. A sentence cut at the boundary now appears whole in one of the two adjacent chunks. Comparison: Overlap = 0 (naive) - hard cut, cuts can lose context entirely, recall drops on cross-chunk facts. Overlap = 16 tokens - shared text in both adjacent chunks (e.g., 'set a new password. The link expires'). Cost: overlap = duplicate storage cost; overlap = ~12% more vectors at 128/16 (16/128 = 12.5%); still breaks long-range references (entity earlier in doc, pronoun later). Overlap fixes the easy case (sentence-level cuts) but not the hard case (a definition on page 3 referenced by 'it' on page 17).
12.7.summary: Overlap duplicates the boundary tokens to recover short sentence splits, at the cost of ~12% more vectors, but does not fix long-range references.
queries: does chunk overlap fix the boundary problem
how much extra storage does overlap add
overlap fixes sentence cuts but not coreference
how big should chunk overlap be

12.7.text: Fixed-size: the baseline to beat. Fixed-size chunking is the right default in a narrow band of cases. Outside that band, every other strategy beats it. Use Fixed-Size If: docs are short (< 2K tokens each); content is homogeneous (uniform paragraphs); you need a baseline to measure other strategies against; budget = zero engineering time. Move To Structural / Semantic If: docs have clear structural markers (markdown, HTML headings); content is heterogeneous (one doc = manual + FAQ + changelog); cross-chunk facts hurt recall in your eval set. Fixed-size is the baseline. Every chunking strategy in production is measured against it. Chapters 12.8-12.12 each fix one weakness of fixed-size.
12.7.summary: Decision rules: when fixed-size chunking is good enough versus when to upgrade to structural or semantic chunking.
queries: when should I use fixed-size chunking
when to upgrade from fixed-size chunking
how to choose chunking method for RAG
when does fixed-size fail in production
criteria for moving past fixed-size chunks

12.7.text: Chapter 12.7 - Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline. Embedding a full document fails because (1) embedding models cap at ~8,192 tokens (500-page manual = 250,000 tokens, 241,808 overflow) and (2) one vector for 500 pages carries no specific signal. Production RAG always chunks because of context limits, retrieval granularity, and signal dilution. The simplest strategy is fixed-size chunking: a sliding window like slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16), producing chunks at token ranges 0-128, 112-240, 224-352, 336-464 with 16-token overlap bands. The token window ignores grammar, so cuts can land mid-sentence ('set a new' | 'password.') and destroy retrieval of cross-boundary facts. Overlap duplicates the last N tokens of each chunk into the next, fixing sentence-level cuts at ~12% extra storage but not long-range coreference. Fixed-size is the right default for short, homogeneous docs and as a baseline; move to structural/semantic chunking when docs have markdown/HTML structure, heterogeneous content, or cross-chunk recall failures.
12.7.summary: Chapter overview: why chunking is mandatory, the fixed-size sliding-window-with-overlap baseline, its mid-sentence failure mode, and when to upgrade.
